topics.title: Sources of Power Dissipation in CMOS Circuits: Dynamic Power Dissipation - Charging and Discharging Capacitance Power Dissipation; Short Circuit Power Dissipation - Short Circuit Current of Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sources of Power Dissipation in CMOS Circuits: Dynamic Power Dissipation - Charging and Discharging Capacitance Power Dissipation; Short Circuit Power Dissipation - Short Circuit Current of Inverter # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Sources of Power Dissipation in CMOS Circuits — Formal Definition In CMOS Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) design, the **total power dissipation** ($P_{total}$) in a complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) circuit is the electrical energy consumed per unit time. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAET752)**, the power dissipation in a static CMOS logic gate is classified into two principal categories: $$ P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} $$ Where: - $P_{dynamic}$ = Power consumed due to **switching activity** (transient behavior) - $P_{static}$ = Power consumed when the circuit is **idle** (leakage currents) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic specifically focuses on the two sub-components of **Dynamic Power Dissipation**: > 1. **Charging/Discharging Capacitance Power Dissipation** ($P_{switching}$) > 2. **Short Circuit Power Dissipation** ($P_{short\_circuit}$) Thus, the working equation for dynamic power is: $$ P_{dynamic} = P_{switching} + P_{short\_circuit} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model Imagine a CMOS inverter as a **leaky water bucket system**: - **Charging/Discharging Capacitance Power** is like repeatedly **filling a bucket** with water from a tap (VDD) and then **dumping it down the drain** (GND). Every time you pour, half the water is "wasted" as heat in the pipe (transistor resistance), and the other half goes into the bucket (stored in capacitor). When you dump it, all the stored water is "wasted" as heat. You are billed (power consumed) for the water that passes through the tap. - **Short Circuit Power** is like the brief moment when **both the tap and the drain are open simultaneously**. Water flows directly from supply to ground without filling the bucket — pure waste. This happens only during the **switching transition** when both the PMOS and NMOS transistors are partially ON. > [!NOTE] > **The Switch Analogy for Short Circuit Current:** A light switch in a staircase wiring is OFF at one end, ON at the other, and during movement — both contacts are physically connected, allowing current to flow directly. Similarly, during a CMOS transition, both PMOS and NMOS conduct briefly, creating a direct VDD-to-GND path. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):** Typically **1.2 V to 1.8 V** for modern nanometer CMOS (45 nm/32 nm/14 nm nodes). - **Load Capacitance ($C_L$):** Sum of gate, diffusion, and interconnect capacitance, typically in **femtofarads (fF)**. - **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$):** Typically **0.3 V to 0.5 V** for modern processes. - **Switching Frequency ($f$):** Clock frequency in **Hertz (Hz)** or **MHz/GHz**. - **Activity Factor ($\alpha$):** Probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle, **$0 \le \alpha \le 1$**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Component 1 — Charging and Discharging Capacitance Power Dissipation ($P_{switching}$) ### 2.1.1 Operational Concept (Bulleted Logic Steps) - **Step 1:** The CMOS inverter output node is connected to a **load capacitance** $C_L$ (gate capacitance of fan-out + parasitic diffusion + interconnect capacitance). - **Step 2:** During a **0 → 1 transition** at the output: - The **PMOS transistor turns ON**, the **NMOS turns OFF**. - $C_L$ is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS. - **Energy drawn from supply** = $C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$. - **Energy stored in $C_L$** = $\frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$. - **Energy dissipated as heat in PMOS** = $\frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$. - **Step 3:** During a **1 → 0 transition** at the output: - The **NMOS transistor turns ON**, the **PMOS turns OFF**. - $C_L$ is discharged from $V_{DD}$ to $0$ through the NMOS. - **Energy stored in $C_L$** ($\frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$) is **completely dissipated as heat in the NMOS** — no energy is returned to the supply. - **Step 4:** For a clock signal toggling at frequency $f$, the number of full charging cycles per second is $f$ (assuming 50% duty cycle and $\alpha = 1$). - **Step 5:** For real circuits, the probability of a 0→1 transition is captured by the **activity factor** $\alpha$ ($0 \le \alpha \le 0.5$ for random data, $\alpha = 1$ for clock). ### 2.1.2 Why This Power Dominates Modern CMOS - It is **proportional to $V_{DD}^2$** — this is why voltage scaling is the most effective power-reduction technique. - It is **proportional to $f$** — explains why high-performance processors have huge power budgets. - It accounts for **~60-70%** of total power in well-designed CMOS circuits (before aggressive leakage optimization). ### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - Used in **synthesis tools** (Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus) to estimate power **before layout**. - Forms the basis of the **dynamic power optimization loop** in **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling)** used in smartphones (e.g., ARM big.LITTLE architecture). - Critical for **battery-life estimation** in IoT devices and wearable electronics. ## 2.2 Component 2 — Short Circuit Power Dissipation ($P_{short\_circuit}$) ### 2.2.1 Operational Concept (Bulleted Logic Steps) - **Step 1:** During the input transition of a CMOS inverter, there is a **finite time window** $\Delta t$ when the input voltage $V_{in}$ lies in the range $[V_{th,n}, V_{DD} - \mid V_{th,p} \mid]$. - **Step 2:** In this window, **both NMOS and PMOS are simultaneously ON**, creating a **direct resistive path** from $V_{DD}$ to GND. - **Step 3:** A **short circuit current** ($I_{sc}$) flows through this path, dissipating power as $I_{sc}^2 \cdot R_{on}$. - **Step 4:** This current is **non-zero only during transitions** — hence it is a "dynamic" component of dynamic power. - **Step 5:** The magnitude of $I_{sc}$ depends on: - **Input rise/fall time ($\tau$):** Slower input → longer window of simultaneous conduction → higher $I_{sc}$. - **Transistor sizes** ($k_n$, $k_p$): Larger transistors → higher current drive → higher $I_{sc}$. - **Output load capacitance ($C_L$):** Larger $C_L$ → output moves slowly → transistors stay in saturation longer → higher $I_{sc}$. - **Threshold voltages** ($V_{th,n}$, $V_{th,p}$): Lower $V_{th}$ → wider conduction window. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Design Insight:** If the input rise/fall time is **very fast** and the load capacitance is **very small**, the short circuit current is **minimal**. The worst case occurs when the input transition is **slow** AND the output load is **heavy** (a classic pitfall in poorly designed clock distribution networks). ### 2.2.2 Why Short Circuit Power is Often Neglected in Deep Sub-Micron - In modern nanometer CMOS, $V_{DD} \approx 1.0\text{ V}$ and $V_{th} \approx 0.3\text{ V}$, making $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})$ very small. - The **cubed dependence** on $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})$ makes short-circuit power negligible compared to switching power. - However, in **I/O pads** and **level shifters** with high $V_{DD}$ (3.3 V or 5 V), it is still significant. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Notational Convention:** $\vert x \vert$ denotes the absolute value of $x$ (used here for the PMOS threshold voltage, which is negative). | **Formula** | **Description** | **Boundary Conditions / Units** | |:---|:---|:---| | $P_{switching} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Switching power of CMOS gate | $\alpha \in [0,1]$; $f$ in Hz; $C_L$ in F; $V_{DD}$ in V → Power in W | | $E_{transition} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy dissipated per full 0→1→0 cycle | Joules (J) | | $E_{charge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy stored in $C_L$ after charging | Joules (J) — stored, not dissipated | | $E_{pmos} = E_{nmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Heat dissipated in each transistor per half-cycle | Joules (J) | | $I_{peak} = \frac{k}{2} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^2$ | Peak short circuit current (symmetric inverter at $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$) | $k$ in A/V²; both transistors in saturation | | $P_{short\_circuit} = \frac{k \cdot \tau \cdot f}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ | Short circuit power (symmetric inverter) | $\tau$ = input rise/fall time in s; $f$ in Hz | | $P_{sc,asym} = \frac{(k_n + k_p) \cdot \tau \cdot f}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ | Short circuit power (general asymmetric) | $k_n, k_p$ are process transconductances | | $I_{sc,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T I_{sc}(t) \, dt$ | Average short circuit current over one period | Amperes (A) | | $P_{total} = P_{switching} + P_{short\_circuit} + P_{static}$ | Total power dissipation | Watts (W) | | $\alpha = \frac{N_{transitions}}{N_{clock\_cycles}}$ | Switching activity factor (definition) | Dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charging/Discharging Capacitance Power Dissipation ### 3.1.1 Setup & Assumptions - Consider a CMOS inverter with output node connected to load capacitance $C_L$. - Input switches at frequency $f$ with a 50% duty cycle square wave. - Initially, $V_{out} = 0$. At $t=0$, input goes low → PMOS turns ON, $C_L$ charges from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Energy Analysis (Charging Half-Cycle) The instantaneous current charging the capacitor is: $$ i(t) = C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} $$ The energy drawn from the $V_{DD}$ supply during charging is the integral of power over time: $$ E_{drawn} = \int_0^{T_{charge}} P_{supply}(t) \, dt = \int_0^{T_{charge}} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt $$ Substituting $i(t) = C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}$: $$ E_{drawn} = \int_0^{T_{charge}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \, dt $$ Since $V_{DD}$ is constant, we change the integration variable from $t$ to $V_{out}$ (which varies from $0$ to $V_{DD}$): $$ E_{drawn} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV_{out} $$ Evaluating this definite integral: $$ E_{drawn} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot [V_{out}]_0^{V_{DD}} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - 0) $$ $$ \boxed{E_{drawn} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2} $$ The energy **stored in the capacitor** at the end of charging is: $$ E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 $$ By **conservation of energy**, the energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor during charging is: $$ E_{pmos} = E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{pmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2} $$ ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Energy Analysis (Discharging Half-Cycle) During the 1→0 transition, the NMOS turns ON and the charged capacitor discharges to GND. **No energy is returned to the supply** because the NMOS is a passive switch to ground, not a source. The energy stored in the capacitor at the start of discharge is $\frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$. All of this energy is dissipated as heat in the NMOS channel resistance: $$ \boxed{E_{nmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2} $$ ### 3.1.4 Total Energy Per Switching Cycle Adding the heat dissipated in both transistors per complete 0→1→0 cycle: $$ E_{total} = E_{pmos} + E_{nmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{total} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2} $$ ### 3.1.5 Final Power Equation For a clock frequency $f$, there are $f$ full switching cycles per second. The average dynamic (switching) power is: $$ P_{switching} = E_{total} \cdot f = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f $$ Introducing the **activity factor** $\alpha$ to account for non-switching or partially switching nodes: $$ \boxed{P_{switching} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway:** The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ from the capacitor energy storage is **canceled** by the factor of 2 from the two half-cycles. The final expression is **proportional to $V_{DD}^2$** — the most power-sensitive design parameter. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Short Circuit Current in CMOS Inverter ### 3.2.1 Setup & Assumptions - Symmetric CMOS inverter: $k_n = k_p = k$, $V_{th,n} = \vert V_{th,p} \vert = V_{th}$. - Input rises **linearly** from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ with rise time $\tau$: $$ V_{in}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{\tau} \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le \tau $$ - Output is unloaded (or has negligible capacitance) — **worst case** for short circuit current. ### 3.2.2 Identifying the Conduction Window Both NMOS and PMOS conduct when: $$ V_{th} \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - V_{th} $$ The duration of this window is: $$ \Delta t = \tau \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})}{V_{DD}} $$ ### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Current Analysis For a **symmetric inverter** with input at voltage $V_{in}$: - NMOS: $V_{gs,n} = V_{in}$ - PMOS: $V_{sg,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ **Case A:** $V_{th} \le V_{in} \le V_{DD}/2$ → NMOS in **saturation**, PMOS in **triode** region. **Case B:** $V_{DD}/2 \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - V_{th}$ → NMOS in **triode**, PMOS in **saturation** region. **Special Point:** $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$ → Both in **saturation** → **Peak current**. At $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$, both transistors have overdrive voltage $V_{ov} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}$, and the saturation current is: $$ I_{peak} = \frac{k}{2} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{th}\right)^2 $$ Expanding the square: $$ I_{peak} = \frac{k}{2} \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2}{4} = \frac{k}{8} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2 $$ ### 3.2.4 Derivation of Average Short Circuit Current The instantaneous short circuit current (limited by the saturation current of the driver in the triode-mode partner) is modeled approximately as a **triangular pulse** with peak $I_{peak}$ and base duration $\Delta t = \tau \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})}{V_{DD}}$. The total charge delivered as short circuit current per transition is: $$ Q_{sc} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot \Delta t $$ Substituting the expressions for $I_{peak}$ and $\Delta t$: $$ Q_{sc} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{k}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2 \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})}{V_{DD}} $$ $$ Q_{sc} = \frac{k \cdot \tau}{16 \cdot V_{DD}} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 $$ Assuming $V_{DD} \gg V_{th}$ (typical for older processes), we approximate $V_{DD} \approx V_{DD} - 2V_{th}$ for the denominator, giving: $$ Q_{sc} \approx \frac{k \cdot \tau}{16} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2 $$ Multiplying by frequency $f$ for power, and noting the conventional result integrates the cubic form: $$ P_{short\_circuit} = V_{DD} \cdot Q_{sc} \cdot f $$ Performing a **rigorous integration** of $I_{sc}(t)$ over the symmetric triangular envelope (using $\int_0^{\Delta t} I_{sc}(t) \, dt$ with $I_{sc}$ increasing then decreasing linearly) yields the standard result: $$ \boxed{P_{short\_circuit} = \frac{1}{12} \cdot k \cdot \tau \cdot f \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Rigorous Integration Path (For Advanced Students):** The factor $\frac{1}{12}$ arises from integrating $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{\tau} t - V_{th}\right)^2 dt$ where $t_1 = \frac{V_{th} \cdot \tau}{V_{DD}}$ and $t_2 = \frac{(V_{DD}-V_{th}) \cdot \tau}{V_{DD}}$. Evaluating this definite integral in the variable $u = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{\tau} - V_{th}$ yields the closed form containing the $\frac{1}{3} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ term, scaled by the linear $\tau/V_{DD}$ factor of the substitution, producing the final $\frac{1}{12}$ coefficient after combining with the symmetric half-cycle contribution. ### 3.2.5 Physical Interpretation of the Cubic Dependence The $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ term is the **single most important design insight** in this derivation: - When $V_{DD} = 2V_{th}$, the conduction window closes completely ($I_{sc} = 0$). - This is the principle behind **subthreshold circuits** and **near-threshold computing** used in ultra-low-power IoT chips. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem **Problem:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 100 \text{ fF}$ at $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$, switching frequency $f = 250 \text{ MHz}$, and activity factor $\alpha = 0.15$. Calculate: (a) Switching Power, (b) Energy per transition. If $k = 200 \text{ μA/V}^2$, $V_{th} = 0.4 \text{ V}$, and $\tau = 100 \text{ ps}$, calculate: (c) Short Circuit Power, (d) Ratio of $P_{sc}/P_{switching}$. **Solution:** **(a) Switching Power:** $$ P_{switching} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f $$ $$ P_{switching} = 0.15 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^2 \times 250 \times 10^6 $$ $$ P_{switching} = 0.15 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \times 250 \times 10^6 $$ $$ P_{switching} = 0.15 \times 100 \times 3.24 \times 250 \times 10^{-9+6} $$ $$ P_{switching} = 12{,}150 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 12.15 \text{ μW} $$ **(b) Energy per transition:** $$ E_{transition} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 = 324 \text{ fJ} $$ **(c) Short Circuit Power:** $$ P_{sc} = \frac{k \cdot \tau \cdot f}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 $$ $$ P_{sc} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 250 \times 10^6}{12} \cdot (1.8 - 0.8)^3 $$ $$ P_{sc} = \frac{200 \times 100 \times 250 \times 10^{-6-12+6}}{12} \cdot (1.0)^3 $$ $$ P_{sc} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{12} \cdot 1 = 416.67 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} \approx 0.417 \text{ nW} $$ **(d) Ratio:** $$ \frac{P_{sc}}{P_{switching}} = \frac{0.417 \times 10^{-9}}{12.15 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-5} $$ **Observation:** The short circuit power is **negligible** (< 0.01%) compared to switching power in this 1.8 V design — confirming the earlier note about deep sub-micron dominance of $P_{switching}$. # 4. Psw plus Psc]:::total A --> C C --> E B --> C B --> D D --> E D --> F E -.charges CL.-> G E -.short circuit path.-> H G --> I H --> I classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef input fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pmos fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nmos fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef psw fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef psc fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef total fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart — Energy Flow During a Switching Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Vin low
PMOS ON, NMOS OFF]:::s1 S2[Step 2: Current flows from VDD to CL
through PMOS]:::s2 S3[Step 3: CL charges from 0 to VDD
Energy in CL equals half CL VDD squared]:::s3 S4[Step 4: Heat in PMOS equals half CL VDD squared]:::s4 S5[Step 5: Vin high
PMOS OFF, NMOS ON]:::s5 S6[Step 6: CL discharges to GND
through NMOS]:::s6 S7[Step 7: All stored energy dissipated
as heat in NMOS
half CL VDD squared]:::s7 S8[Step 8: Total per cycle
CL times VDD squared]:::s8 S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 classDef s1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s6 fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef s7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef s8 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 State Diagram — Short Circuit Conduction Window ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Vin_Below_Vth: Initial State Vin equals 0 Vin_Below_Vth --> PMOS_Only_ON: Vin rises above 0 PMOS_Only_ON --> Both_ON_Symmetric: Vin reaches Vth
NMOS turns on Both_ON_Symmetric --> Peak_Sc_Current: Vin equals VDD by 2
Both in saturation Peak_Sc_Current --> Both_ON_Symmetric: Vin continues rising Both_ON_Symmetric --> NMOS_Only_ON: Vin exceeds VDD minus Vth
PMOS turns off NMOS_Only_ON --> Vin_Above_VDD: Vin reaches VDD Vin_Above_VDD --> [*]: Steady state high note right of Both_ON_Symmetric CRITICAL WINDOW Short circuit current flows from VDD to GND Duration depends on tau end note ``` ## 4.4 Power Trade-off Decision Tree (For Design Engineers) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Circuit Type?}:::q A1[Clock Tree or I/O Pad
Slow edges, High VDD]:::a A2[Internal Core Logic
Fast edges, Low VDD]:::a Q2{Critical Parameter?}:::q B1[Minimize Switching Power
Reduce VDD, Reduce CL, Reduce f]:::b B2[Minimize Short Circuit Power
Sharpen input edges
Balance transistor sizes]:::b B3[Minimize Both
Use tapered buffers
Voltage scaling]:::b Q1 --> A1 Q1 --> A2 A1 --> Q2 A2 --> Q2 Q2 --> B1 Q2 --> B2 Q2 --> B3 classDef q fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef a fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef b fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]` **Q: List the major sources of power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Which one dominates in modern nanometer technologies?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The major sources of power dissipation in a CMOS inverter are: 1. **Dynamic Switching Power** — Energy dissipated in charging and discharging the load capacitance ($P_{sw} = \alpha f C_L V_{DD}^2$). 2. **Short Circuit Power** — Power dissipated due to direct current flow from $V_{DD}$ to GND during input transitions ($P_{sc} = \frac{1}{12} k \tau f (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$). 3. **Static (Leakage) Power** — Power consumed even when the circuit is idle, due to subthreshold and gate leakage currents. **Dominant component in modern nanometer CMOS:** **Dynamic Switching Power** dominates in well-designed modern circuits due to its $V_{DD}^2$ dependence being significant despite low $V_{DD}$. However, at very advanced nodes (below 22 nm), **static leakage power** becomes comparable due to thin-oxide tunneling. (1 mark for identification) ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define the activity factor ($\alpha$) in CMOS circuits. What is its typical value for a clock signal and for random data?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The **activity factor** $\alpha$ is defined as the probability that the output of a logic gate transitions (0→1 or 1→0) in a single clock cycle. It is a dimensionless quantity in the range $[0, 1]$. (1.5 marks) - For a **clock signal** (which toggles every cycle): $\alpha = 1$ (every cycle is a 0→1 transition). (0.75 marks) - For **random data** with equal probability of 0 and 1: $\alpha = 0.5$ for a transition-only count, but for power calculations using $P = \alpha f C V_{DD}^2$, the value used is $\alpha = 0.5$ representing full toggling equivalent. More precisely, the average number of transitions per cycle is $0.5$. (0.75 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question B.A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]` **Q: (a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, derive the expression for the dynamic power dissipation due to charging and discharging of capacitance in a CMOS inverter. Discuss the dependence of this power on supply voltage and operating frequency. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** A CMOS inverter in a 65 nm process has the following specifications: - $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$, $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$, $\alpha = 0.25$. Calculate: (i) The dynamic switching power. **(2 marks)** (ii) The energy dissipated per full switching cycle. **(2 marks)** (iii) If the supply voltage is scaled down to 0.9 V while keeping the frequency same, what is the percentage reduction in switching power? **(3 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Dynamic Switching Power:** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The complete step-by-step derivation must be reproduced in the answer script, covering: - Charging phase: $E_{drawn} = C_L V_{DD}^2$, $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$, $E_{pmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (3 marks for the energy balance) - Discharging phase: $E_{nmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (1 mark) - Total energy per cycle: $E_{total} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ (1 mark) - Final power: $P_{sw} = \alpha f C_L V_{DD}^2$ (1 mark) - Discussion of $V_{DD}^2$ and $f$ dependence with at least 2 real-world examples (e.g., mobile CPU power gating, DVFS in Snapdragon processors) (1 mark) **(b) Numerical Computation:** **(i) Dynamic Switching Power:** $$ P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f $$ $$ P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2 \times 500 \times 10^6 $$ $$ P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^8 $$ $$ P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^{-15+8} = 90 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{sw} = 90 \text{ μW}} $$ **(ii) Energy per full switching cycle:** $$ E_{cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 = 72 \text{ fJ} $$ **(iii) Power at 0.9 V (frequency unchanged):** $$ P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.9)^2 \times 500 \times 10^6 $$ $$ P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 0.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 50.625 \text{ μW} $$ **Percentage Reduction:** $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{P_{sw} - P_{sw,new}}{P_{sw}} \times 100 = \frac{90 - 50.625}{90} \times 100 $$ $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{39.375}{90} \times 100 = 43.75\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B.A:** > **Common mistake #1:** Students often write the total energy per cycle as $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (forgetting to add the energy dissipated in BOTH the PMOS and NMOS). The correct answer is $C_L V_{DD}^2$ because the $\frac{1}{2}$ stored energy and the $\frac{1}{2}$ PMOS heat sum to give a full $C_L V_{DD}^2$. > **Common mistake #2:** In the percentage reduction problem, students sometimes forget to **square the voltage ratio** correctly. The reduction is **NOT** $\frac{1.2 - 0.9}{1.2} = 25\%$ — it is $1 - (0.9/1.2)^2 = 1 - 0.5625 = 43.75\%$. This quadratic dependence is the **core reason** why voltage scaling is the most powerful low-power technique. > **Common mistake #3:** Not mentioning that this 43.75% reduction in power comes at the cost of increased delay (lower $V_{DD}$ → slower transistors), illustrating the **energy-delay trade-off**. --- ### **Question B.B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain the mechanism of short circuit power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for the short circuit current. Under what condition is this power dissipation zero? **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** Compare dynamic switching power and short circuit power dissipation in CMOS circuits in terms of: (i) Order of dominance in modern technologies. **(2 marks)** (ii) Dependence on supply voltage. **(3 marks)** (iii) Two design techniques to reduce each. **(2 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Short Circuit Power Mechanism and Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes. The complete derivation must cover: - Identification of the conduction window $[V_{th}, V_{DD} - V_{th}]$ (1 mark) - State diagram or explanation of both transistors being ON simultaneously (1 mark) - Linear input ramp assumption and peak current calculation at $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$: $$ I_{peak} = \frac{k}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2 $$ (1 mark) - Triangular approximation of current pulse and integration yielding: $$ P_{sc} = \frac{1}{12} k \tau f (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 $$ (2 marks) - **Condition for zero short circuit power:** $V_{DD} \le 2V_{th}$. When $V_{DD} = 2V_{th}$, the conduction window collapses — there is no input voltage range where both transistors conduct simultaneously. (1 mark) - Example: For $V_{th} = 0.4$ V, if $V_{DD} \le 0.8$ V, $P_{sc} = 0$. (1 mark) **(b) Comparison:** **(i) Order of dominance in modern technologies (2 marks):** - In modern nanometer CMOS (45 nm and below with $V_{DD} \approx 1.0$ V and $V_{th} \approx 0.3$ V), **dynamic switching power dominates** (60-80% of total). The $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ term in $P_{sc}$ becomes very small $(0.4)^3 = 0.064$, suppressing short circuit power. - Short circuit power is significant only in **I/O drivers, level shifters, and clock distribution** with higher $V_{DD}$ (3.3 V or 5 V). **(ii) Dependence on supply voltage (3 marks):** - **Switching Power:** Quadratic dependence, $P_{sw} \propto V_{DD}^2$. - **Short Circuit Power:** Cubic dependence with effective overdrive, $P_{sc} \propto (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \approx V_{DD}^3$ for $V_{DD} \gg V_{th}$. - Short circuit power falls **faster** than switching power with voltage scaling. This is why modern low-power design focuses on reducing $P_{sw}$ via voltage scaling, which automatically suppresses $P_{sc}$ as well. **(iii) Design techniques to reduce each (2 marks):** | **Technique** | **Reduces** | **Mechanism** | |:---|:---|:---| | Voltage Scaling ($V_{DD}$ reduction) | Both $P_{sw}$ and $P_{sc}$ | Quadratic and cubic reduction respectively | | Clock Gating | $P_{sw}$ | Disables clock to idle blocks, sets $\alpha \to 0$ | | Reducing Load Capacitance | $P_{sw}$ | Smaller transistors, shorter wires, multi-VDD | | Sharpening Input Edges (low $\tau$) | $P_{sc}$ | Reduces conduction window $\Delta t$ | | Staircase (Tapered) Buffers | Both | Gradually sized buffers minimize $\tau$ at each stage | | Transistor Sizing (balanced $k_n$, $k_p$) | $P_{sc}$ | Equal rise/fall times minimize peak current | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B.B:** > **Common mistake #1:** Students often claim that short circuit power can be eliminated by making the inverter "perfectly symmetric" (equal $k_n$ and $k_p$). This is **FALSE** — symmetry actually *maximizes* the short circuit current because the peak current formula $\frac{k}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2$ uses $k$ in the denominator. Symmetric inverters are designed for **noise margin**, not for short circuit reduction. > **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting the **cubic dependence** of $P_{sc}$ on $(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})$ — students sometimes write it as squared or linear, leading to incorrect quantitative answers in numerical problems. > **Common mistake #3:** Stating that $P_{sc} = 0$ when the input is a perfect step function. The correct statement is $P_{sc} \to 0$ **as** $\tau \to 0$. With $\tau = 0$, the conduction window has **zero duration** and zero charge passes through, but the analysis assumes $\tau > 0$ for a meaningful integral. > **Common mistake #4:** Confusing short circuit current with the **leakage current**. Short circuit current is a *dynamic* phenomenon occurring only during transitions, while leakage is *static* and persists even when the input is stable. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Quick-Reference Summary** ### **Key Conceptual Points** - ☐ Power dissipation in CMOS = **Dynamic Power** (switching + short circuit) + **Static Power** (leakage). - ☐ Switching power is caused by **charging/discharging load capacitance** at the output node. - ☐ Short circuit power is caused by **simultaneous conduction** of PMOS and NMOS during input transitions. - ☐ Static power is caused by **leakage currents** when the circuit is idle (not in this module's focus). ### **Critical Formulas (Memorize These)** - ☐ $P_{switching} = \alpha f C_L V_{DD}^2$ - ☐ $E_{transition} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ (energy per 0→1→0 cycle) - ☐ $E_{pmos} = E_{nmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ - ☐ $I_{peak,sc} = \frac{k}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2$ (symmetric inverter) - ☐ $P_{short\_circuit} = \frac{k \tau f}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ - ☐ $P_{sc} = 0$ when $V_{DD} \le 2V_{th}$ ### **Numerical Problem-Solving Strategy** - ☐ Step 1: Identify the **given parameters** ($V_{DD}$, $C_L$, $f$, $\alpha$, $k$, $\tau$, $V_{th}$). - ☐ Step 2: Compute $P_{sw}$ using $\alpha f C_L V_{DD}^2$ first (this is usually dominant). - ☐ Step 3: Compute $P_{sc}$ using $\frac{1}{12} k \tau f (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$ next. - ☐ Step 4: Total dynamic power = $P_{sw} + P_{sc}$. - ☐ Step 5: For voltage scaling problems, compute the **ratio** rather than absolute values to avoid arithmetic errors. ### **Design Intuition (For Higher-Order Questions)** - ☐ **Voltage scaling is the most powerful** low-power technique due to $V_{DD}^2$ dependence. - ☐ **Frequency scaling** has a **linear** effect on power but causes performance loss. - ☐ **Capacitance reduction** requires architectural changes (shorter wires, smaller gates, multi-VDD domains). - ☐ **Activity factor reduction** is achieved through **clock gating, operand isolation, and encoding schemes** (e.g., Gray code for counters). ### **Frequently Asked KTU Question Patterns** - ☐ "Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation" → 7-mark derivation. - ☐ "Calculate the switching power for given parameters" → 3-5 mark numerical. - ☐ "What is short circuit current? When does it occur?" → 3-4 mark short answer. - ☐ "Compare switching power and short circuit power" → 7-10 mark comparison. - ☐ "Discuss techniques to reduce power in CMOS circuits" → 7-10 mark design question. - ☐ "What happens to power when $V_{DD}$ is halved?" → Trick question — answer depends on whether frequency is also scaled (constant-performance vs. energy-delay product minimization). ### **Units & Dimensions (Sanity Check)** - ☐ $\alpha$: dimensionless - ☐ $f$: Hz (or MHz = $10^6$ Hz, GHz = $10^9$ Hz) - ☐ $C_L$: F (or fF = $10^{-15}$ F, pF = $10^{-12}$ F) - ☐ $V_{DD}$: V - ☐ $k$: A/V² (or μA/V² = $10^{-6}$ A/V²) - ☐ $\tau$: s (or ps = $10^{-12}$ s, ns = $10^{-9}$ s) - ☐ $V_{th}$: V - ☐ $P$: W (or μW = $10^{-6}$ W, mW = $10^{-3}$ W) - ☐ $E$: J (or fJ = $10^{-15}$ J, pJ = $10^{-12}$ J) > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the **unit** with every numerical answer. Many students lose 0.5-1 mark per sub-part for writing "90" instead of "90 μW" or "324" instead of "324 fJ". The KTU valuation scheme explicitly awards partial marks for correct units.

topics.title: Short Circuit Current Dependency with Input and Output Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Short Circuit Current Dependency with Input and Output Load ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition In CMOS digital circuits, **Short Circuit Current (I_sc)** is the direct current that flows from the supply rail (V_DD) to the ground rail (GND) through the simultaneously conducting PMOS and NMOS transistors of a logic gate during the **switching transition** interval. Mathematically, this transient leakage path exists when the input voltage V_in satisfies the condition: $$V_{tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ where **V_tn** is the NMOS threshold voltage, **V_tp** is the PMOS threshold voltage, and **V_DD** is the supply voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Short-circuit power dissipation (P_sc) is the second major component of dynamic power in CMOS, alongside switching (capacitive) power. It cannot be ignored in low-power VLSI design because it is directly proportional to the **input signal slope** and the **output loading conditions**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **two-lane toll booth on a highway** where the green light (PMOS = ON) and the red light (NMOS = ON) are momentarily both green during the "yellow" transition phase. While both lights are green, cars (current) pass directly from the entry lane (V_DD) to the exit lane (GND) without paying the toll at the destination (no useful work done at the output load). The longer the yellow phase, the more "wasted" current flows. In digital CMOS: - **V_DD** = Entry toll booth - **GND** = Exit toll booth - **Output Capacitor (C_L)** = Actual destination of useful work - **Input transition time (t_r / t_f)** = Duration of the "yellow phase" ### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Supply Voltage (V_DD):** Modern KTU-syllabus CMOS operates at **0.7 V to 3.3 V** (typical values). - **Threshold Voltages:** V_tn ≈ 0.3V–0.5V, V_tp ≈ -0.4V–0.6V for 180nm/130nm nodes. - **Short-Circuit Power Ratio:** P_sc / P_switching typically ranges from **10% to 30%** in poorly designed circuits. - **Miller Capacitance (C_M):** Critical parameter affecting the interaction between input slope and output load. > [!NOTE] > **Why does I_sc exist?** It is a fundamental property of complementary CMOS logic. Unlike dynamic (capacitive) power, it can never be completely eliminated — only minimized through careful design of input slew rate, output load, and transistor sizing. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanism of Short Circuit Current Flow The short circuit current path is established through the following sequence of events during a **low-to-high input transition**: 1. **Initial State (V_in = 0):** NMOS is OFF (V_gs < V_tn), PMOS is ON (V_sg > |V_tp|). Output = V_DD. 2. **Transition Begins (V_in = 0 → V_DD):** As V_in rises past V_tn, NMOS begins to conduct. PMOS is still ON. 3. **Critical Region (V_tn ≤ V_in ≤ V_DD - |V_tp|):** **BOTH transistors are ON simultaneously.** A direct current path V_DD → PMOS → NMOS → GND exists. This is the **short circuit current I_sc**. 4. **End of Transition (V_in > V_DD - |V_tp|):** PMOS turns OFF. Short circuit current path is broken. 5. **Final State (V_in = V_DD):** NMOS fully ON, PMOS OFF. Output = 0V. ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Dependency #### (A) Input Rise/Fall Time (t_r, t_f) Dependency - **Slow input slope** → Input voltage stays in the [V_tn, V_DD - |V_tp|] region for a **longer duration** → More I_sc. - **Fast input slope** → Input quickly crosses the critical region → Less I_sc. - **Ideal step input (t_r = 0)** → Theoretically, I_sc = 0 because the input instantly jumps past the critical region. - **Real-world:** Due to finite transistor capacitances and the Miller effect, I_sc is never zero. #### (B) Output Load Capacitance (C_L) Dependency - **Heavy Load (Large C_L):** Output voltage changes slowly. During the input transition, the output is still near its previous state (e.g., still near V_DD when input is rising). This keeps V_ds of NMOS high, sustaining I_sc for longer. - **Light Load (Small C_L):** Output switches quickly. As input rises, output falls rapidly, reducing V_ds across NMOS and hence reducing I_sc. - **Key Insight:** The output load determines the **discharge time of the output node**, which directly affects the magnitude of V_ds across the conducting NMOS, thereby modulating I_sc. #### (C) Transistor Sizing Dependency - Larger W/L ratios → Higher drive current → Higher I_sc peak. - Wider transistors allow more current to flow during the brief "both ON" window. #### (D) Supply Voltage (V_DD) Dependency - Higher V_DD → Higher overdrive voltage (V_gs - V_t) → **Dramatically higher I_sc** (cubic dependency). - This is why low-power VLSI aggressively scales down V_DD. #### (E) Threshold Voltage Dependency - Lower V_t → Wider critical region (V_tn to V_DD - |V_tp|) → More I_sc. - Higher V_t → Narrower critical region → Less I_sc. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Short Circuit Power (Veendrick Model) | $P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f$ | Empirical short circuit power model | **Watts (W)** | | Average Short Circuit Current | $I_{sc,avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{T}$ | Time-averaged I_sc over one period | **Amperes (A)** | | Peak Short Circuit Current | $I_{peak} = \dfrac{\beta}{2} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^2$ | Maximum current during transition | **Amperes (A)** | | Output Time Constant | $\tau_{out} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\beta \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{th})^2}$ | RC time constant of output node | **Seconds (s)** | | Input/Output Slope Ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | **Unitless** | | Total Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = P_{switching} + P_{sc}$ | Sum of capacitive + short circuit power | **Watts (W)** | | Switching Power | $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Activity-factor-based switching power | **Watts (W)** | | Critical Voltage Window | $V_{win} = V_{DD} - V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$ | Range where both transistors conduct | **Volts (V)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Relationship:** When **τ_in >> τ_out** (slow input, fast output): I_sc approaches its **maximum value**. When **τ_in << τ_out** (fast input, slow output): I_sc approaches **zero**. The ratio **τ_in / τ_out** is the **master tuning knob** for short-circuit power optimization. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mobile/IoT Design:** Smartphones and IoT sensors use sleep modes where I_sc during wake-up transitions dominates battery drain. - **High-Speed ASICs:** At GHz clock rates, transitions occur billions of times per second, making I_sc a significant contributor to total power. - **FPGA Interconnect:** Long wires create large C_L and slow input slopes simultaneously, exacerbating I_sc on global routes. - **KTU Industry Connection:** Companies like Intel, Qualcomm, and Texas Instruments explicitly optimize the input slew rate of clock buffers and I/O pads to minimize short circuit losses. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Veendrick Short-Circuit Power Model The Veendrick model is the **gold standard** for short-circuit power estimation in KTU-style exams. We will derive it from first principles. #### Step 1: Establish the Short-Circuit Current During Linear Transition Consider a symmetric CMOS inverter (W_n/L_n = W_p/L_p = β) with input rising linearly with slope 1/τ_in from 0 to V_DD: $$V_{in}(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{\tau_{in}} \cdot t \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \leq t \leq \tau_{in}$$ The current through the NMOS transistor during the sub-threshold-to-saturation transition can be modeled using the **alpha-power law** approximation. In the saturation region, the drain current is: $$I_D = \dfrac{\beta_n}{2} (V_{in} - V_{tn})^2$$ The PMOS is in the linear (triode) region for most of the transition since V_sd is small. By KCL at the output node (ignoring output capacitance charging initially for I_sc analysis): $$I_{sc}(t) \approx I_{NMOS}(t) \approx \dfrac{\beta}{2} \left( V_{in}(t) - V_{tn} \right)^2$$ #### Step 2: Substitute the Linear Input Ramp Substituting the input ramp expression: $$I_{sc}(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{2} \left( \dfrac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{\tau_{in}} - V_{tn} \right)^2$$ #### Step 3: Determine the Time Bounds The short-circuit current flows only when V_tn ≤ V_in ≤ V_DD - |V_tp|. By symmetry, the peak occurs at t = τ_in/2 (where V_in = V_DD/2). **Time at start of I_sc (NMOS turns on):** $$t_1 = \dfrac{V_{tn} \cdot \tau_{in}}{V_{DD}}$$ **Time at end of I_sc (PMOS turns off):** $$t_2 = \dfrac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert) \cdot \tau_{in}}{V_{DD}}$$ For a symmetric inverter, V_tn = |V_tp| = V_th, so t_2 = τ_in - t_1. #### Step 4: Integrate to Find the Total Charge Delivered The total charge Q_sc delivered to ground per transition is: $$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I_{sc}(t) \, dt$$ Substituting: $$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \dfrac{\beta}{2} \left( \dfrac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{\tau_{in}} - V_{tn} \right)^2 dt$$ Let u = (V_DD · t / τ_in) - V_th, then du = (V_DD / τ_in) dt, hence dt = (τ_in / V_DD) du. When t = t_1, u = 0. When t = t_2, u = V_DD - 2V_th. $$Q_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{V_{DD}} \int_{0}^{V_{DD} - 2V_{th}} u^2 \, du$$ Evaluating the integral: $$Q_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{V_{DD}} \cdot \dfrac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3}{3}$$ $$Q_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \tau_{in} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3}{6 \cdot V_{DD}}$$ #### Step 5: Account for Output Loading Effect The above derivation assumed the output node is **stuck** at V_DD (no load discharge). In reality, C_L discharges during the transition. Define the output discharge time constant: $$\tau_{out} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\beta (V_{DD} - V_{th})^2}$$ The fraction of input transition time during which the output remains high (i.e., V_ds is significant) is approximately **min(1, τ_in/τ_out)**. The effective I_sc is reduced when the output transitions quickly. The corrected Veendrick formula incorporates this: $$I_{sc,avg} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{V_{DD}}$$ #### Step 6: Convert to Power Multiplying by V_DD (to get power) and by switching frequency f: $$\boxed{P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Limiting Cases of the Veendrick Formula:** > - **τ_in → 0** (step input): P_sc → 0 ✓ (No time for both transistors to conduct) > - **τ_in → ∞** (very slow input): P_sc saturates at a maximum value ✓ > - **C_L → 0** (no load, τ_out → 0): P_sc → 0 ✓ (Output swings fast, killing I_sc) > - **C_L → ∞** (huge load, τ_out → ∞): P_sc → 0 ✓ (Output never moves, V_ds collapses) ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: I_sc Behavior in Different Operating Regimes | **Operating Regime** | **Input Slope** | **Output Slope** | **I_sc Magnitude** | **Physical Reason** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Step input, light load | Very fast (τ_in ≈ 0) | Very fast (τ_out ≈ 0) | **Zero** | Input jumps past critical region instantly | | Step input, heavy load | Very fast (τ_in ≈ 0) | Slow (τ_out large) | **Zero** | No time spent in critical region | | Slow input, light load | Slow (τ_in large) | Very fast (τ_out ≈ 0) | **Low** | Output drops quickly, choking I_sc | | **Slow input, heavy load** | **Slow (τ_in large)** | **Slow (τ_out large)** | **MAXIMUM** | **Both conditions favor I_sc** | | **Balanced input/output** | **τ_in = τ_out** | **τ_in = τ_out** | **~50% of maximum** | **Most common design scenario** | > [!WARNING] > **Common Exam Pitfall:** Students often assume I_sc is purely a function of V_DD. The derivation above proves that I_sc is a **strong function of the ratio τ_in/τ_out**. A fast, lightly-loaded gate can have I_sc ≈ 0 even at high V_DD, while a slow, heavily-loaded gate can have significant I_sc even at low V_DD. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CMOS Inverter Short Circuit Current Path ```mermaid flowchart TB VDD["V_DD Supply Rail"] GND["Ground Rail GND"] PMOS["PMOS Transistor
W_p / L_p"] NMOS["NMOS Transistor
W_n / L_n"] INPUT["Input V_in
Ramp Signal"] OUTPUT["Output Node V_out"] CL["Load Capacitor C_L"] ISC["Short Circuit
Current I_sc"] VDD --> PMOS PMOS --> OUTPUT NMOS --> OUTPUT NMOS --> GND INPUT -.-> PMOS INPUT -.-> NMOS OUTPUT --- CL PMOS -. "Both ON
during transition" .-> ISC NMOS -. "Both ON
during transition" .-> ISC ISC -->|"Direct V_DD to GND path"| GND style VDD fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff style GND fill:#4a4a4a,color:#ffffff style PMOS fill:#5b8def,color:#ffffff style NMOS fill:#5b8def,color:#ffffff style ISC fill:#ffd93d,color:#000000 style CL fill:#6bcf7f,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 I_sc vs. Input Rise Time Dependency Plot ```mermaid flowchart LR A["τ_in very small
Step Input"] -->|"I_sc ≈ 0"| B["B1 Region
Negligible I_sc"] B -->|"τ_in increases"| C["B2 Region
I_sc grows linearly"] C -->|"τ_in >> τ_out"| D["B3 Region
I_sc saturates
at maximum value"] D -->|"τ_in → ∞"| E["B4 Region
I_sc = I_sc_max
constant"] style A fill:#90ee90 style B fill:#ffd93d style C fill:#ff9933 style D fill:#ff6b6b style E fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Short Circuit Power Minimization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_DOMAIN["INPUT DOMAIN CONTROL"] I1["Buffer Chain
with Tapered Sizing"] I2["Slew Rate
Controller"] I3["Input Signal
V_in"] end subgraph CORE_GATE["CMOS LOGIC GATE CORE"] C1["PMOS Network
Pull-Up"] C2["NMOS Network
Pull-Down"] C3["Output Node
V_out"] end subgraph OUTPUT_DOMAIN["OUTPUT LOAD DOMAIN"] O1["Wire Capacitance
C_wire"] O2["Gate Capacitance
C_gate"] O3["Total Load C_L"] end subgraph POWER_ANALYZER["POWER DISSIPATION UNIT"] P1["P_switching
α·C_L·V_DD²·f"] P2["P_short_circuit
β/12 · (V_DD-2V_th)³ · τ_in/τ_out · V_DD · f"] P3["Total P_dyn = P_sw + P_sc"] end I3 --> C1 I3 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> O1 C3 --> O2 O1 --> O3 O2 --> O3 O3 -->|"C_L value"| P1 I2 -->|"τ_in value"| P2 O3 -->|"τ_out value"| P2 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 style INPUT_DOMAIN fill:#e3f2fd style CORE_GATE fill:#fff3e0 style OUTPUT_DOMAIN fill:#f3e5f5 style POWER_ANALYZER fill:#ffebee style P2 fill:#ffd93d style P3 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.4 I_sc Dependency Hierarchy (Conceptual Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT["I_sc Total
Short Circuit Current"] ROOT --> A["Input Parameters"] ROOT --> B["Output Parameters"] ROOT --> C["Device Parameters"] ROOT --> D["Environmental"] A --> A1["τ_in: Rise time"] A --> A2["Input Signal Shape"] A1 --> A1a["Slow → High I_sc"] A1 --> A1b["Fast → Low I_sc"] B --> B1["C_L: Load Cap"] B --> B2["τ_out: Output RC"] B1 --> B1a["Heavy → Sustains I_sc"] B1 --> B1b["Light → Kills I_sc"] C --> C1["W/L Ratios"] C --> C2["V_th: Threshold"] C --> C3["V_DD: Supply"] C3 --> C3a["High V_DD → Cubic increase"] D --> D1["Temperature"] D --> D2["Process Corner"] style ROOT fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff style A fill:#5b8def,color:#ffffff style B fill:#5b8def,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5b8def,color:#ffffff style D fill:#5b8def,color:#ffffff style A1a fill:#ff9933 style B1a fill:#ff9933 style C3a fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** *Define short circuit current in a CMOS inverter. Under what input voltage condition does this current flow?* **Model Answer:** Short circuit current (I_sc) is the direct current that flows from V_DD to GND through the PMOS and NMOS transistors of a CMOS inverter when both transistors are simultaneously ON during a switching transition. This current flows when the input voltage satisfies the condition: $$V_{tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ It contributes to short-circuit power dissipation: $P_{sc} = I_{sc,avg} \cdot V_{DD}$ and is independent of the load capacitance in its pure form, but is modulated by the output discharge dynamics. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** *List any three factors that affect the short circuit power dissipation in CMOS circuits.* **Model Answer:** The three primary factors affecting short circuit power dissipation are: 1. **Input Rise/Fall Time (τ_in):** Slower input transitions increase the duration both transistors are ON, increasing I_sc. 2. **Output Load Capacitance (C_L):** Larger C_L slows output transitions, sustaining V_ds across the NMOS/PMOS and increasing I_sc. 3. **Supply Voltage (V_DD):** I_sc has a cubic dependency on (V_DD - 2V_th), making V_DD the most sensitive parameter. Other acceptable factors: Threshold voltage, transistor sizing (W/L), temperature, and switching frequency. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Module Internal Choice Pattern) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Derive the expression for short-circuit power dissipation in a CMOS inverter using the Veendrick model. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CMOS inverter in a 0.18μm process has the following parameters: V_DD = 1.8V, V_tn = |V_tp| = 0.5V, β = 200 μA/V², τ_in = 100 ps, τ_out = 50 ps, f = 500 MHz. Calculate the short-circuit power dissipation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Assumptions:** 1. Symmetric inverter: W_n/L_n = W_p/L_p = β 2. Input is a linear ramp with rise time τ_in 3. Output load is purely capacitive C_L 4. NMOS and PMOS threshold voltages are equal in magnitude **Derivation Steps:** **Step 1:** Input voltage as a linear function of time: $$V_{in}(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{\tau_{in}} \cdot t$$ **Step 2:** Short circuit current (NMOS in saturation, PMOS in triode): $$I_{sc}(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{2} \left( V_{in}(t) - V_{tn} \right)^2$$ **Step 3:** Charge delivered per transition: $$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I_{sc}(t) \, dt = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \tau_{in} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3}{6 \cdot V_{DD}}$$ **Step 4:** Apply output loading correction factor (τ_in/τ_out): $$Q_{sc,eff} = Q_{sc} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}}$$ **Step 5:** Average current over period T = 1/f: $$I_{sc,avg} = Q_{sc,eff} \cdot f = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \tau_{in}^2 \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot f}{6 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \tau_{out}}$$ **Step 6:** Short circuit power: $$\boxed{P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f}$$ **(7 marks — full marks for complete derivation with all steps shown)** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given Data:** - V_DD = 1.8 V - V_tn = |V_tp| = 0.5 V - β = 200 μA/V² = 200 × 10⁻⁶ A/V² - τ_in = 100 ps = 100 × 10⁻¹² s - τ_out = 50 ps = 50 × 10⁻¹² s - f = 500 MHz = 500 × 10⁶ Hz **Step 1:** Calculate (V_DD - 2V_th): $$V_{DD} - 2V_{th} = 1.8 - 2(0.5) = 0.8 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate (V_DD - 2V_th)³: $$(0.8)^3 = 0.512 \text{ V}^3$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the slope ratio: $$\dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} = \dfrac{100 \text{ ps}}{50 \text{ ps}} = 2$$ **Step 4:** Substitute into the Veendrick formula: $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{12} \cdot (0.512) \cdot (2) \cdot (1.8) \cdot (500 \times 10^6)$$ **Step 5:** Multiply term by term: $$P_{sc} = (16.67 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.512) \cdot (2) \cdot (1.8) \cdot (500 \times 10^6)$$ $$P_{sc} = 16.67 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.512 \times 1.8 \times 10^9$$ $$P_{sc} = 16.67 \times 0.512 \times 1.8 \times 10^3$$ $$P_{sc} = 15.36 \times 10^3 \text{ μW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{sc} \approx 15.36 \text{ mW}}$$ **(7 marks — 2 marks for formula identification, 3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer with units)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model - Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how short circuit current depends on (i) input rise time and (ii) output load capacitance. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a CMOS inverter, the input signal is a ramp with rise time τ_in = 200 ps, and the output load capacitance is C_L = 50 fF. The transistor parameter β = 150 μA/V², V_DD = 1.5 V, V_th = 0.4 V. If the switching frequency is 250 MHz, find: (i) the output time constant τ_out, and (ii) the short circuit power. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):** - Draw the CMOS inverter with V_in (ramp input), V_out (capacitive load C_L). - Show I_sc vs. τ_in curve: Starts at zero for step input (τ_in = 0), rises linearly, then saturates. - Show I_sc vs. C_L curve: Increases with C_L up to a point, then saturates when τ_in/τ_out << 1. **Dependency (i) - Input Rise Time:** - I_sc is directly proportional to τ_in (for τ_in << τ_out). - For **τ_in → 0** (step input): Both transistors are ON for negligible time → **I_sc → 0**. - For **τ_in >> τ_out**: Output discharges fully during input transition, I_sc reaches its **maximum saturation value**. **Dependency (ii) - Output Load Capacitance:** - Larger C_L → Larger τ_out → Output transitions slowly → I_sc is sustained longer. - For **C_L → 0** (no load): Output switches instantly, V_ds collapses, → **I_sc → 0**. - For **C_L → ∞** (huge load): Output doesn't switch, but V_ds of NMOS also doesn't grow, → **I_sc → 0**. - **Maximum I_sc occurs at intermediate C_L values** where τ_in ≈ τ_out. **(7 marks — 2 marks for sketch, 3 marks for input dependency, 2 marks for load dependency)** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given Data:** - τ_in = 200 ps = 200 × 10⁻¹² s - C_L = 50 fF = 50 × 10⁻¹⁵ F - β = 150 μA/V² = 150 × 10⁻⁶ A/V² - V_DD = 1.5 V, V_th = 0.4 V, f = 250 MHz **Part (i): Output Time Constant** $$\tau_{out} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\beta \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{th})^2}$$ Calculate (V_DD - V_th): $$V_{DD} - V_{th} = 1.5 - 0.4 = 1.1 \text{ V}$$ Calculate (V_DD - V_th)²: $$(1.1)^2 = 1.21 \text{ V}^2$$ Substitute: $$\tau_{out} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (1.5)}{(150 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1.21)}$$ $$\tau_{out} = \dfrac{75 \times 10^{-15}}{181.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\tau_{out} = 0.4132 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{out} \approx 413.2 \text{ ps}}$$ **Part (ii): Short Circuit Power** Calculate (V_DD - 2V_th): $$V_{DD} - 2V_{th} = 1.5 - 0.8 = 0.7 \text{ V}$$ Calculate (V_DD - 2V_th)³: $$(0.7)^3 = 0.343 \text{ V}^3$$ Slope ratio: $$\dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} = \dfrac{200}{413.2} = 0.484$$ Substitute into Veendrick formula: $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{-6}}{12} \cdot (0.343) \cdot (0.484) \cdot (1.5) \cdot (250 \times 10^6)$$ $$P_{sc} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.343 \cdot 0.484 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 250 \times 10^6$$ $$P_{sc} = 12.5 \times 0.343 \times 0.484 \times 1.5 \times 250 \times 10^{0}$$ $$P_{sc} = 12.5 \times 62.3 \text{ μW (intermediate)}$$ $$P_{sc} \approx 778.6 \text{ μW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{sc} \approx 0.779 \text{ mW}}$$ **(7 marks — 3 marks for τ_out, 4 marks for P_sc calculation)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 12** in the Veendrick formula is the most common error. Always write the full formula before substituting. > 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** ps ↔ s, fF ↔ F, μA ↔ A. KTU examiners specifically check for dimensional consistency. > 3. **Symmetric inverter assumption:** Most exam problems assume V_tn = |V_tp|. If they differ, you must use V_DD - V_tn - |V_tp| explicitly. > 4. **Forgetting the slope ratio:** Students often write P_sc ∝ τ_in alone, missing the critical τ_in/τ_out term. This leads to a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. > 5. **Not drawing the waveform:** For 7-mark descriptive questions, a missing input/output waveform sketch costs **at least 1-2 marks**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Short Circuit Current** - ✅ **Definition:** I_sc flows when V_tn ≤ V_in ≤ V_DD - |V_tp| (both transistors ON). - ✅ **Two Critical Dependencies:** (1) Input rise/fall time (τ_in), (2) Output load capacitance (C_L / τ_out). - ✅ **Master Formula (Veendrick):** $P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \dfrac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot f$ - ✅ **Cubic V_DD Dependency:** I_sc ∝ (V_DD - 2V_th)³ — the single most sensitive parameter. - ✅ **Limiting Cases:** I_sc → 0 when τ_in → 0, τ_in → ∞, or C_L → 0 or C_L → ∞. - ✅ **Maximum I_sc:** Occurs at intermediate C_L where τ_in ≈ τ_out. - ✅ **Design Mitigation:** Use buffer chains to sharpen input edges, minimize long wires, and avoid high-V_DD slow-signal interfaces. - ✅ **Relationship to P_sw:** P_sc is *additional* to P_sw, not a replacement. Total P_dyn = P_sw + P_sc. - ✅ **Examination Tip:** Always quote the full Veendrick formula, mention "symmetric inverter" assumption, and draw the I_sc vs. τ_in curve. - ✅ **Common Mistake:** Confusing short-circuit current (transient, during switching) with subthreshold leakage (static, when OFF). They are different phenomena.

topics.title: Glitching Power; Static Power Dissipation - Leakage Power Dissipation; Gate Level Power Analysis - Capacitive, Internal, and Static Power Dissipation of Gate Level Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Glitching Power; Static Power Dissipation - Leakage Power Dissipation; Gate Level Power Analysis - Capacitive, Internal, and Static Power Dissipation of Gate Level Circuit ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Glitching Power **Definition:** Glitching power is the unwanted dynamic power dissipated due to *spurious (spike-like) transitions* at the output of a logic gate, which occur *before* the signal settles to its final correct logic value. These temporary, false transitions cause redundant charging and discharging of load capacitances. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Glitches are NOT useful transitions. They are parasitic transitions caused by unequal propagation delays along different signal paths reaching the same logic gate. **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a classroom where a student is being called to the office. Five different messengers (signal paths) are sent to fetch the student with *different walking speeds*. The student starts moving toward the office, then is told "wait, come back" by a faster messenger who got a different instruction, and then finally moves again correctly. The student *wasted energy* walking back and forth. In CMOS circuits, the gate output "wastes energy" toggling multiple times for a *single intended logical change*. ### 1.2 Static Power Dissipation (Leakage Power) **Definition:** Static power is the power consumed by a CMOS circuit when it is in a *steady (quiescent) state* — i.e., when inputs are NOT switching. In modern deep-submicron CMOS, this is dominated by **leakage currents** that flow even when the transistor is nominally OFF. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition:** Leakage power is the power dissipated due to unwanted currents flowing through the transistor in the OFF state, primarily subthreshold leakage, gate-oxide tunneling leakage, and reverse-biased junction leakage. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a *leaking water tap* in your home. Even when you tightly close the tap (OFF transistor), a small amount of water (current) still drips out continuously. The electricity bill keeps increasing even when no water is being intentionally used. Similarly, leakage currents drain power even when the chip is "idle." ### 1.3 Gate Level Power Analysis **Definition:** Gate level power analysis is the *hierarchical estimation* of total power dissipation at the granularity of a single logic gate. The total power is the sum of three orthogonal components: $$P_{total} = P_{capacitive} + P_{internal} + P_{static}$$ | Component | Cause | When Active | |---|---|---| | **Capacitive Power** | Charging/discharging of load capacitance | During transitions | | **Internal Power** | Short-circuit current during switching | During transitions | | **Static Power** | Leakage currents | Always (ON or OFF) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** In sub-180nm technologies, **static leakage ≈ 30–50% of total power**. In older micron-scale designs, leakage was negligible compared to capacitive power. The examiner expects students to know *which leakage mechanism dominates* at which technology node. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Glitching Power — Mechanism (Why it Happens) **Logic Flow (Why and How):** 1. **Multiple paths** converge at a multi-input gate (e.g., a 4-input AND). 2. Each input arrives at a *different time* due to unequal path delays. 3. The output *transitions multiple times* before stabilizing. 4. Each false transition charges/discharges the output capacitance — wasting energy. **How to quantify glitching power:** Glitching power is computed by counting the *extra (redundant) transitions* and treating each as an additional capacitive switching event. $$P_{glitch} = \alpha_{glitch} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ where $\alpha_{glitch}$ is the *glitching activity factor* (a fraction > 0 that captures spurious toggles beyond the ideal $\alpha$). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Glitching power can account for **20–70% of total dynamic power** in poorly balanced arithmetic circuits (adders, multipliers). KTU questions frequently ask: "Why does a balanced adder consume less power?" — Answer: *because balanced paths reduce glitches*. ### 2.2 Static Power Dissipation — Three Leakage Mechanisms **A. Subthreshold Leakage (Dominant in 90nm and above)** Current flowing between source and drain when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$. $$I_{sub} = \mu_0 C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \, e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{nV_T}} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)$$ **B. Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage (Dominant below 90nm)** Electrons tunnel through the thin gate oxide. $$I_{gate} = A \left(\frac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}}\right)^2 \, e^{-\frac{B \cdot t_{ox}}{V_{ox}}}$$ **C. Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage** Minority carrier leakage from source/drain to body. $$I_{rev} = A_j \cdot J_s \left(e^{\frac{V_{bi}}{V_T}} - 1\right)$$ **Total Static Power:** $$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot (I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{rev})$$ ### 2.3 Gate Level Power Analysis — The Three Components #### 2.3.1 Capacitive (Switching/Dynamic) Power Energy stored on a load capacitor during a 0→1 transition: $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. Discharged during 1→0: another $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated. Per cycle (one 0→1 and one 1→0): full energy $C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated. $$P_{cap} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ where: - $\alpha$ = switching activity (probability of 0→1 transition per clock) - $C_L$ = load capacitance (Farads) - $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage (Volts) - $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency (Hz) #### 2.3.2 Internal (Short-Circuit) Power During the finite transition time of the input, both NMOS and PMOS conduct simultaneously, creating a direct path from $V_{DD}$ to GND. $$P_{int} = \frac{1}{12} \cdot k \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot \tau$$ where $\tau$ is the rise/fall time of the input and $k = \mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}$. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Internal"?** It is power dissipated *inside* the gate due to internal crow-bar currents, NOT visible at the output node. #### 2.3.3 Static (Leakage) Power $$P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{cap} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Switching power | Watts (W) | | 2 | $P_{int} = \frac{k}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3 f \tau$ | Short-circuit power | Watts (W) | | 3 | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage power | Watts (W) | | 4 | $P_{glitch} = \alpha_g C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Glitch-induced power | Watts (W) | | 5 | $I_{sub} = \mu_0 C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}V_T^2 e^{\frac{V_{GS}-V_{TH}}{nV_T}}$ | Subthreshold current | Amperes (A) | | 6 | $E_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per full cycle | Joules (J) | | 7 | $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per 0→1 transition | Joules (J) | | 8 | $I_{gate} = A\left(\frac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}}\right)^2 e^{-B t_{ox}/V_{ox}}$ | Gate tunneling current | Amperes (A) | | 9 | $P_{total} = P_{cap} + P_{int} + P_{stat} + P_{glitch}$ | Total gate power | Watts (W) | | 10 | $f_{max} \propto \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{\alpha}}{V_{DD}}$ | Delay–voltage relation | Hz | > **Boundary Conditions (KTU frequently asked):** > - For $V_{DS} \geq 3V_T$: $\left(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T}\right) \approx 1$, so $I_{sub}$ saturates. > - For $V_{GS} = 0$ (OFF transistor): $I_{sub} = I_0 \, e^{-V_{TH}/(nV_T)}$ — *this is non-zero*, hence leakage exists. > - For $\tau = 0$ (ideal step input): $P_{int} = 0$ (no overlap of PMOS/NMOS conduction). > - For balanced logic: $\alpha_{glitch} \rightarrow 0$, so $P_{glitch} \approx 0$. **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations form the *backbone* of power estimation tools like **Synopsys PrimeTime PX, Cadence Genus, and Mentor ModelSim Power Aware**. Every modern ASIC design flow uses these exact formulas at the gate level for power budgeting before fabrication. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Capacitive Switching Power **Starting premise:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L$ at its output node. **Step 1 — Energy delivered from supply during 0→1 transition:** When the output switches from 0 to $V_{DD}$, charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to the capacitor. The energy supplied by the source is: $$E_{supplied} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor at the end of charging:** $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 3 — Energy dissipated as heat in PMOS during charging:** $$E_{dissipated,PMOS} = E_{supplied} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 4 — During 1→0 transition:** The capacitor discharges to GND through the NMOS. The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat in the NMOS (none of it returns to the supply — the diode-connected PMOS is OFF). $$E_{dissipated,NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 5 — Total energy dissipated per full clock cycle (one 0→1 + one 1→0):** $$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 6 — Power (energy per unit time):** For a clock of frequency $f_{clk}$, there are $f_{clk}$ cycles per second. If the gate switches every cycle (activity $\alpha = 1$): $$P_{cap} = C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ **Step 7 — Generalize for switching activity $\alpha$ (probability of 0→1 per cycle):** $$P_{cap} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ This is the **fundamental dynamic power equation** that every KTU examiner expects you to derive from first principles. ### 3.2 Derivation of Short-Circuit (Internal) Power **Starting premise:** The input rises linearly with slope $1/\tau$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ over time $\tau$. **Step 1 — Identify the conduction window:** Both NMOS and PMOS conduct when $V_{TH} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - V_{TH}$. The duration of overlap: $$\Delta t = \tau \cdot \frac{V_{DD} - 2V_{TH}}{V_{DD}}$$ **Step 2 — Average short-circuit current (using saturated current model):** For a saturated NMOS at $V_{in}$, the current is approximately: $$I_{sc}(t) \approx k \cdot (V_{in}(t) - V_{TH})^2$$ Averaging over the ramp: $$\bar{I}_{sc} = \frac{k}{3} \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3}{V_{DD}}$$ **Step 3 — Power dissipated as heat in the short-circuit path:** $$P_{int} = \bar{I}_{sc} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{\tau / f} = \frac{k}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot \tau$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** If $V_{DD} < 2V_{TH}$, the short-circuit window disappears ($P_{int} = 0$). This is the basis of **subthreshold / near-threshold designs** for ultra-low-power applications. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) **Problem:** A CMOS gate drives a load $C_L = 50 \, fF$ at $V_{DD} = 1.2V$, clock $f = 500 \, MHz$, with switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$. Compute $P_{cap}$. **Solution:** $$P_{cap} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$ Substitute values (convert fF to F): $C_L = 50 \times 10^{-15} \, F$ $$P_{cap} = 0.2 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.2)^2 \times (500 \times 10^6)$$ $$P_{cap} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^8$$ $$P_{cap} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$P_{cap} = 72 \times 10^{-7} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6} \, W = 7.2 \, \mu W$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Technology Node | $V_{DD}$ | Dominant Leakage | Dominant Dynamic | |---|---|---|---| | **500 nm** | 5.0 V | Negligible (≪ 1%) | Capacitive (~95%) | | **180 nm** | 1.8 V | Subthreshold (~10%) | Capacitive (~80%) | | **90 nm** | 1.2 V | Subthreshold + Gate (~30%) | Capacitive (~60%) | | **45 nm** | 1.0 V | Gate tunneling (~45%) | Capacitive (~50%) | | **22 nm** | 0.85 V | Gate + Junction (~55%) | Capacitive (~40%) | > **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Questions of the form *"At 45nm, which power component dominates?"* require students to recognize that **leakage becomes comparable to dynamic power** at sub-100nm nodes. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Power Dissipation Components in a CMOS Inverter (Block-Level Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["V_DD Supply Rail"] --> nodeB["PMOS Transistor"] nodeB --> nodeC["Output Node with Load Capacitance C_L"] nodeC --> nodeD["NMOS Transistor"] nodeD --> nodeE["GND Rail"] nodeF["Input Signal V_in"] --> nodeB nodeF --> nodeD nodeB -. "Charging C_L
Capacitive Power P_cap" .-> nodeC nodeC -. "Discharging C_L
Capacitive Power P_cap" .-> nodeE nodeF -. "Finite rise/fall time
Short-Circuit Power P_int" .-> nodeB nodeF -. "Finite rise/fall time
Short-Circuit Power P_int" .-> nodeD nodeB -. "Subthreshold + Gate Leakage
Static Power P_stat" .-> nodeE nodeC -. "Junction Leakage
Static Power P_stat" .-> nodeE nodeG["Unequal Path Delays from Logic Cone"] -. "Multiple transitions
Glitching Power P_glitch" .-> nodeC nodeC --> nodeH["P_total = P_cap + P_int + P_stat + P_glitch"] ``` ### 4.2 Glitch Generation Mechanism Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Input A
arrives early"] --> nodeD["AND Gate
4-Input"] nodeB["Input B
arrives late"] --> nodeD nodeC["Input C
arrives medium"] --> nodeD nodeE["Input D
arrives early"] --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeF["Output: 0 to 1 to 0 to 1
GLITCH observed"] nodeF --> nodeG["Extra charging of C_L
Wasted energy"] ``` ### 4.3 Leakage Current Paths in an OFF NMOS (Transistor Cross-Section View) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Gate V_GS = 0V (OFF)"] --> nodeB["Channel: weak inversion layer
exists due to subthreshold"] nodeB --> nodeC["I_sub: Subthreshold Leakage
Source to Drain"] nodeA --> nodeD["Gate Oxide: thin layer
Electrons tunnel through"] nodeD --> nodeE["I_gate: Gate Tunneling Leakage
Gate to Channel"] nodeF["Source/Drain to Body
Reverse biased PN junction"] --> nodeG["I_rev: Junction Leakage
Drain to Body"] nodeC --> nodeH["Total Leakage I_leak = I_sub + I_gate + I_rev"] nodeE --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI["P_static = V_DD x I_leak"] ``` ### 4.4 Gate Level Power Analysis Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Total Power P_total at Gate Level"] --> nodeB["P_dynamic (Switching)"] nodeA --> nodeC["P_internal (Short-Circuit)"] nodeA --> nodeD["P_static (Leakage)"] nodeA --> nodeE["P_glitch (Spurious)"] nodeB --> nodeF["alpha x C_L x V_DD^2 x f"] nodeC --> nodeG["k/12 x V_DD-2V_TH^3 x f x tau"] nodeD --> nodeH["V_DD x I_sub"] nodeD --> nodeI["V_DD x I_gate"] nodeD --> nodeJ["V_DD x I_rev"] nodeE --> nodeK["alpha_glitch x C_L x V_DD^2 x f"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]** *Define glitching power. Why does it occur in CMOS combinational logic?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Glitching power is the unwanted dynamic power dissipated due to spurious transitions at the output of a logic gate before the signal settles to its final correct value. **It occurs because of unequal propagation delays along different signal paths converging at a multi-input gate**, causing the output to toggle multiple times for a single input change. Each false transition charges/discharges the output load capacitance $C_L$, wasting energy proportional to $C_L V_{DD}^2$ per glitch. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List the three components of gate level power dissipation. Which one dominates in 90nm CMOS technology?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three components of gate level power dissipation are: 1. **Capacitive (Switching) Power** — $P_{cap} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ 2. **Internal (Short-Circuit) Power** — $P_{int}$ during input transition overlap 3. **Static (Leakage) Power** — $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ In **90nm CMOS technology**, capacitive power still dominates (~60%), but leakage power (subthreshold + gate tunneling) becomes significant (~30–40%) and cannot be ignored as in older nodes. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently: > 1. **Forget to derive** the $P = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ per transition (loses 3 marks). > 2. **Confuse $\alpha$ with frequency** (loses 2 marks). > 3. **Omit the activity factor** when writing the dynamic power formula (loses 2 marks). > 4. **Write $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ without justification** of the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ per direction (loses 2 marks). > 5. **State leakage components without naming them** (subthreshold, gate, junction) (loses 2 marks). --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **average dynamic power dissipation** in a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **three leakage mechanisms** that contribute to static power dissipation in a deep-submicron CMOS transistor. **(7 marks)** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A** **(a) Derivation of $P_{cap} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$:** **(7 marks)** **Assumptions (1 mark):** - The CMOS inverter is driving a lumped load capacitance $C_L$ at the output. - $V_{DD}$ is constant and the input rise/fall time is zero (ideal step) for now. - $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor (probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle). **Derivation (6 marks):** **Step 1:** During a 0→1 transition at the output, charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to the capacitor. The energy supplied by the source: $$E_{supplied} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 2:** The energy stored in the capacitor at the end of the charging phase: $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 3:** The remaining energy is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor: $$E_{PMOS} = E_{supplied} - E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 4:** During the subsequent 1→0 transition, the capacitor discharges through the NMOS. The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat in the NMOS. None returns to the supply. **Step 5:** Total energy dissipated per complete cycle (one 0→1 + one 1→0): $$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 6:** For a clock of frequency $f_{clk}$ and switching activity $\alpha$: $$\boxed{P_{cap} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}}$$ --- **(b) Three Leakage Mechanisms in Deep-Submicron CMOS:** **(7 marks)** **Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram — physical cross-section with arrows showing leakage paths] **Explanation (5 marks):** 1. **Subthreshold Leakage Current ($I_{sub}$):** - In a nominally OFF transistor ($V_{GS} < V_{TH}$), the channel is in *weak inversion*. - Carriers diffuse from source to drain due to the concentration gradient. - For $V_{DS} \geq 3V_T$: $$I_{sub} = \mu_0 C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \, e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}}$$ - **Dominant at nodes ≥ 90nm.** - Reduced by: high-$V_{TH}$ transistors, multi-$V_{TH}$ design, power gating, reverse body bias. 2. **Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage ($I_{gate}$):** - When the gate oxide is very thin (< 2nm), electrons quantum-mechanically *tunnel* through the oxide. - Becomes significant below 90nm. - Exponentially dependent on oxide thickness $t_{ox}$ and oxide voltage $V_{ox}$. - **Dominant at nodes 45nm and below.** - Reduced by: high-K dielectric gate materials (e.g., HfO₂ replacing SiO₂). 3. **Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage ($I_{rev}$):** - Reverse-biased PN junctions at the source/body and drain/body. - Minority carriers drift across the depletion region. - Increases with temperature and reverse bias. - Reduced by: lower doping concentration, SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator) technology. **Total Static Power:** $$\boxed{P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot (I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{rev})}$$ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A] **(a)** What is **glitching power**? Explain with a waveform diagram how glitches are generated in a multi-input logic gate. Discuss two techniques to reduce glitching power. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CMOS circuit operates at $V_{DD} = 1.8V$, $f = 200 \, MHz$, with $C_L = 100 \, fF$ and $\alpha = 0.15$.
