Compute the capacitive power dissipation. If leakage current is $I_{leak} = 5 \, \mu A$, compute the static power and total power. **(7 marks)** --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B** **(a) Glitching Power:** **(7 marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Glitching power is the dynamic power dissipated due to spurious (unwanted) output transitions in a combinational logic gate, occurring before the output stabilizes to its final value. **Waveform Explanation (3 marks):** [Reference Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram] - Consider a 4-input AND gate. Inputs A and D arrive at time $t_1$, C arrives at $t_2$, and B arrives at $t_3$. - The output briefly becomes 1 at $t_1$ (since all 4 inputs are momentarily 1), then drops to 0 at $t_2$ (when C goes low), then finally rises to 1 at $t_3$ (when B comes back high). - This *extra* 0→1→0→1 transition is a *glitch* that causes redundant charging/discharging of the load capacitance at the next stage. - The wasted energy per glitch: $E_{glitch} = C_L V_{DD}^2$. **Two Reduction Techniques (3 marks):** 1. **Balanced Path Delays (Delay Balancing):** Insert delay buffers in fast paths to equalize arrival times at the gate, ensuring the output transitions only once. 2. **Hazard Filtering / Glitch Masking:** Add a latch or a Schmitt trigger at the output to filter out narrow glitches (width < setup time of the next stage). 3. **Gate Sizing Reduction:** Smaller gates have lower output capacitance, reducing the energy per glitch. 4. **Use of Glitch-Free Logic Families:** e.g., domino logic, precharge-evaluate logic, which inherently suppresses glitches. --- **(b) Numerical Computation:** **(7 marks)** **Given:** - $V_{DD} = 1.8 \, V$ - $f = 200 \, MHz = 2 \times 10^8 \, Hz$ - $C_L = 100 \, fF = 100 \times 10^{-15} \, F$ - $\alpha = 0.15$ - $I_{leak} = 5 \, \mu A = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, A$ **Step 1: Capacitive Power (3 marks)** $$P_{cap} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$ $$P_{cap} = 0.15 \times (100 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.8)^2 \times (2 \times 10^8)$$ $$P_{cap} = 0.15 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \times 2 \times 10^8$$ $$P_{cap} = 0.15 \times 100 \times 3.24 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$P_{cap} = 97.2 \times 10^{-7} \, W$$ $$\boxed{P_{cap} = 9.72 \, \mu W}$$ **Step 2: Static Power (2 marks)** $$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = 1.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{P_{static} = 9.0 \, \mu W}$$ **Step 3: Total Power (2 marks)** Assuming glitching and short-circuit power are negligible in this simplified problem: $$P_{total} = P_{cap} + P_{static} = 9.72 + 9.0$$ $$\boxed{P_{total} = 18.72 \, \mu W}$$ **Inference (optional — for full marks):** The static power is comparable to the dynamic power in this 180nm-like scenario, highlighting why modern designs must address both. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Glitching Power** = spurious transitions × $C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — caused by **unequal path delays**; reduced by **delay balancing** and **glitch masking**. - [ ] **Capacitive Power** = $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — the **dominant dynamic** component. Quadratic in $V_{DD}$ (voltage scaling is the most effective lever). - [ ] **Internal Power** = short-circuit current during input ramp — exists only when $V_{DD} > 2V_{TH}$; vanishes in subthreshold designs. - [ ] **Static Power** = $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — three components: **Subthreshold (I_sub)**, **Gate Tunneling (I_gate)**, **Junction (I_rev)**. - [ ] **Subthreshold leakage** dominates at $\geq 90$nm; **gate tunneling** dominates at $\leq 45$nm; **junction leakage** is temperature-sensitive. - [ ] **$P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ derivation**: half stored on capacitor, half dissipated in PMOS (during 0→1); stored energy dissipated in NMOS (during 1→0). Total per cycle = $C_L V_{DD}^2$. - [ ] **Energy per transition** = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (NOT $C_L V_{DD}^2$). - [ ] **Switching activity $\alpha$**: probability of 0→1 transition per clock; maximum is 0.5 (for a clock signal). - [ ] **Static power was negligible at $\geq 250$nm** but reaches **40–60% of total** at 22nm and below — KTU loves this comparison. - [ ] **Glitching power** can be **20–70% of total dynamic power** in unbalanced arithmetic circuits. - [ ] **High-$V_{TH}$ transistors, power gating, and multi-$V_{DD}$ design** are the primary techniques to reduce leakage. - [ ] **High-K gate dielectrics** (HfO₂) and **FinFET/Multi-gate** structures reduce gate leakage dramatically. - [ ] **Subthreshold slope factor $n$** is typically 1.3–1.5; lower $n$ = harder to turn off transistor = better leakage control. - [ ] **Power estimation tools** (Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Genus) use these exact gate-level equations for sign-off power analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a question says *"Discuss power dissipation in CMOS"*, structure your answer as **Dynamic (Capacitive + Internal + Glitch) + Static (Leakage)**. Mention the dominant component for the *specific technology node* mentioned in the question. This single sentence often fetches 2 bonus marks. ---

topics.title: Power Reduction Techniques; Supply Voltage Scaling Approaches - Multi VDD and Dynamic VDD; Leakage Power Reduction Techniques - Transistor Stacking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Reduction Techniques; Supply Voltage Scaling Approaches - Multi VDD and Dynamic VDD; Leakage Power Reduction Techniques - Transistor Stacking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Reduction Techniques — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Power reduction techniques* are a family of circuit-level and system-level design methodologies employed in CMOS VLSI design to minimize the total power dissipation of an integrated circuit without compromising the required performance, functional correctness, or silicon area budget.

The total power consumed by a CMOS digital circuit is mathematically partitioned into two major heads:

$$
P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}
$$

where $P_{dynamic}$ dominates in active mode and $P_{static}$ (leakage) dominates in modern nanometer nodes (90 nm and below) during idle/standby states.

| Component | Cause | Typical Dominance |
|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Power** | Switching of load capacitance, short-circuit current | Active mode |
| **Static (Leakage) Power** | Subthreshold, gate-oxide, junction leakage | Standby / Sleep mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping:** Module 2 of PEAET752 covers three core power-reduction paradigms — (1) **Supply Voltage Scaling**, (2) **Multi-VDD Design**, (3) **Dynamic VDD Scaling**, and (4) **Transistor Stacking** for leakage suppression. All four topics fall under the *design-time* and *run-time* power optimization buckets.

---

## 1.2 Supply Voltage Scaling Approaches

### 1.2.1 Multi-VDD Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A *Multi-VDD* (or multi-threshold voltage) design partitions a chip into multiple *voltage islands* (power domains), where each island operates at its own supply voltage $V_{DD_i}$ chosen to satisfy the local timing constraint of that block only.

**Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition:**

Imagine a college hostel building with several rooms. In summer, the AC in every room is set to 16 °C, even the empty storage room — that wastes enormous electricity. A smarter approach is to set the AC to **16 °C in occupied rooms** (where students are studying hard and need cooling) and **27 °C in rarely used corridors** (where temperature precision does not matter). This is exactly what **Multi-VDD** does to a chip — the *critical path blocks* (analogous to occupied rooms) get a **high $V_{DD}$** to meet speed, while *non-critical blocks* (analogous to empty rooms) get a **low $V_{DD}$** because their delay budget is generous. The total electricity (power) drops sharply because $P \propto V_{DD}^2$.

```
Critical path block   →  VDD_H  (e.g., 1.2 V)  →  Fast, power-hungry
Non-critical block     →  VDD_L  (e.g., 0.8 V)  →  Slow, power-efficient
```

---

### 1.2.2 Dynamic VDD Scaling (DVS / AVS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)* is a **run-time** technique in which the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ of a processor or block is *adaptively varied* on the fly, depending on the instantaneous computational workload, so that the circuit runs at the *minimum voltage* required to finish the task before a hard deadline.

**Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition:**

Think of a car engine. When you are cruising on an empty highway at 60 km/h, you shift to a higher gear and reduce the throttle — saving fuel. When you need to overtake, you floor the accelerator. **Dynamic VDD** does the same: when the workload is light (browsing a static webpage), the chip lowers $V_{DD}$ and frequency; when the workload is heavy (gaming, video decoding), it raises $V_{DD}$ and frequency. The voltage regulator acts as the "throttle" of the chip. A closed-loop variant that observes the actual silicon speed (via a **critical-path replica** or **in-situ delay monitor**) is called **Adaptive Voltage Scaling (AVS)**.

---

## 1.3 Leakage Power Reduction — Transistor Stacking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Transistor Stacking* (also called the *stack effect* or *self-reverse-biasing effect*) is a leakage-suppression technique in which **two or more OFF transistors are placed in series** within a logic gate. The intermediate node between the stacked transistors develops a *self-bias* potential that is neither 0 V nor $V_{DD}$, causing the upper transistor's $V_{GS}$ to become negative and triggering the **body effect** to raise its threshold voltage $V_{TH}$. The subthreshold leakage current drops *exponentially* as a result.

**Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition:**

Imagine a water pipeline with **two valves in series**, both shut. A small amount of water still seeps through the cracks (the leakage). Now, if you slightly open the *lower* valve, water rises in the pipe *between* the two valves, creating a small "buffer pool" that pushes back against the upper valve. This counter-pressure makes the upper valve even tighter — virtually stopping the seepage. The intermediate water column is the *self-biased node voltage*, and the two shut valves are the two OFF transistors. The buffering effect dramatically reduces leakage. In a 2-input NAND gate where both NMOS are OFF, this exact phenomenon reduces leakage by **10× to 100×** compared to a single OFF transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU students:** Transistor stacking is *passive* — it needs no extra control signal. It is the *natural* behaviour of series OFF transistors and is the most fundamental building block of modern low-leakage cell libraries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Power Equation

The complete CMOS power budget is decomposed as:

$$
P_{total} = P_{switching} + P_{short} + P_{leakage}
$$

$$
P_{total} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f_{clk} + V_{DD} \, I_{sc} + V_{DD} \, I_{leak}
$$

where each term has a clear physical meaning laid out in the table below.

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Switching activity factor (probability node toggles 0→1) | dimensionless |
| $C_L$ | Total load capacitance at switching node | F (Farads) |
| $V_{DD}$ | Supply voltage | V |
| $f_{clk}$ | Clock frequency | Hz |
| $I_{sc}$ | Short-circuit (crowbar) current during transition | A |
| $I_{leak}$ | Total leakage current (subthreshold + gate + junction) | A |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Fact:** Because $P \propto V_{DD}^2$, *any* technique that reduces $V_{DD}$ has a **quadratic** payoff. Halving $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by **~4×** — this is the single biggest lever available to a low-power designer.

---

## 2.2 Multi-VDD Design — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Voltage Island Partitioning

A chip is partitioned into $N$ voltage islands. Island $i$ operates at $V_{DD,i}$. The total dynamic power is the sum of island contributions:

$$
P_{dynamic} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \, C_{L,i} \, V_{DD,i}^2 \, f_{clk}
$$

Since $V_{DD,i}$ can be lower than the global $V_{DD}$ for non-critical blocks, the total energy per operation is reduced.

### 2.2.2 Why Level Shifters Are Mandatory

When a signal travels from a low-VDD block to a high-VDD block, the logic-high voltage of the low-VDD block (e.g., 0.8 V) is **insufficient** to fully turn OFF the PMOS of the high-VDD block (gate at 1.2 V). This causes **static short-circuit current** and functional failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** A *level-shifter* (specifically a low-to-high level shifter) **must** be inserted at *every* interface from a $V_{DD,L}$ domain into a $V_{DD,H}$ domain. High-to-low transitions do not strictly require a level shifter but may need a *keeper* to avoid floating rails.

### 2.2.3 Static Timing Constraint

For an island $i$ with critical-path delay $T_{crit,i}$, the chosen $V_{DD,i}$ must satisfy:

$$
T_{crit,i} \;\leq\; T_{deadline}
$$

Because gate delay $t_d \propto V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta$, lowering $V_{DD}$ increases $t_d$. Therefore, $V_{DD,i}$ is the *minimum* voltage that still meets $T_{crit,i} \leq T_{deadline}$.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic VDD Scaling — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 The Energy-Delay Trade-off

DVS exploits the convex energy-vs-delay relationship. The *optimal* energy per operation $E_{op}$ at supply voltage $V$ is:

$$
E_{op}(V) = C_{eff} \, V_{DD}^2
$$

where $C_{eff} = \alpha C_L$. Meanwhile, the achievable frequency is:

$$
f(V) = \frac{1}{t_d(V)} \;\propto\; \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta}{V_{DD}}
$$

For a workload with deadline $D$ and total work $W$:

$$
V_{DD,opt} = \text{smallest } V \text{ such that } f(V) \geq \frac{W}{D}
$$

### 2.3.2 DVS vs AVS

| Feature | DVS (Open Loop) | AVS (Closed Loop) |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage decision | Software / OS based | Hardware delay monitor |
| Tracks silicon variation | No | Yes |
| PVT compensation | Conservative | Tight, optimal |
| Hardware overhead | DC-DC regulator | DC-DC + replica + FSM |

---

## 2.4 Transistor Stacking — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Subthreshold Leakage — The Stack Effect

The classic subthreshold current expression (BSIM-style) for an NMOS is:

$$
I_{sub} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n \, V_T}\right) \, \left(1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right)
$$

For a single OFF transistor ($V_{GS} = 0$, $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$):

$$
I_{sub,1} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH}}{nV_T}\right)
$$

For a *stacked* pair of OFF NMOS (e.g., a 2-input NAND where both inputs are 0):

- The intermediate node $V_M$ self-biases to a small value $V_x$.
- The *lower* transistor has $V_{GS} = -V_x$ and the *upper* has $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - V_x$.
- The subthreshold current must be the *same* in both transistors (KCL).
- The *body effect* raises the lower transistor's $V_{TH}$ by an amount $\Delta V_{TH}$ because its source is at $V_x$ instead of ground.

$$
V_{TH}(V_x) = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_x} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

The resulting leakage reduction factor is:

$$
\frac{I_{sub,stack}}{I_{sub,single}} = \frac{1}{K_{stack}} \;\;\in\;\; [0.01,\; 0.1]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Number:** A typical 2-transistor stack reduces leakage by **one order of magnitude (~10×)** in a 65 nm process. This is why 2-input NAND/NOR gates inherently leak less than inverters, and why 3-stack (e.g., a 3-input NAND) reduces it even further (~50×).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Switching power | CMOS digital gate |
| 2 | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static power | Subthreshold dominant |
| 3 | $P_{total} = \sum_i \alpha_i C_{L,i} V_{DD,i}^2 f$ | Multi-VDD total dynamic power | Per-island summation |
| 4 | $t_d \propto V_{DD} / (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta$ | Gate delay vs $V_{DD}$ | Velocity-saturation regime, $\beta \approx 1.3$ |
| 5 | $V_{DD,opt}(D,W) = f^{-1}(W/D)$ | DVS optimal voltage | Given deadline $D$ and work $W$ |
| 6 | $I_{sub} = I_0 e^{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})/nV_T} (1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T})$ | Subthreshold current | All regions |
| 7 | $V_{TH}(V_{SB}) = V_{TH0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | Body effect on $V_{TH}$ | Reverse body bias |
| 8 | $K_{stack} \approx 10$ for 2-stack, $\approx 50$ for 3-stack | Leakage reduction factor | 65 nm reference node |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Multi-VDD is the de-facto standard in modern SoCs (e.g., smartphone chips with separate voltages for CPU, GPU, modem, always-on domain). DVS/AVS is universally used in mobile processors (ARM big.LITTLE, Qualcomm Snapdragon). Transistor stacking is exploited *implicitly* in NAND-based cell libraries and *explicitly* in sleep transistors and power-gating structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Savings of Multi-VDD vs Single-VDD

**Problem Setup:** A chip has two blocks, A and B, with load capacitances $C_A$ and $C_B$, switching activities $\alpha_A$ and $\alpha_B$, both running at the same clock $f$. Block A is on the critical path, Block B is not. In a baseline *single-VDD* design, both run at $V_{DD,H}$. In a *Multi-VDD* design, Block A keeps $V_{DD,H}$ while Block B drops to $V_{DD,L}$. Find the dynamic power ratio.

$$
\frac{P_{multi}}{P_{single}} = \frac{\alpha_A C_A V_{DD,H}^2 f + \alpha_B C_B V_{DD,L}^2 f}{\alpha_A C_A V_{DD,H}^2 f + \alpha_B C_B V_{DD,H}^2 f}
$$

$$
\frac{P_{multi}}{P_{single}} = \frac{\alpha_A C_A V_{DD,H}^2 + \alpha_B C_B V_{DD,L}^2}{V_{DD,H}^2 (\alpha_A C_A + \alpha_B C_B)}
$$

**Algebraic manipulation:**

$$
\frac{P_{multi}}{P_{single}} = \frac{\alpha_A C_A + \alpha_B C_B (V_{DD,L}/V_{DD,H})^2}{\alpha_A C_A + \alpha_B C_B}
$$

$$
\text{Power Savings} = 1 - \frac{P_{multi}}{P_{single}} = \frac{\alpha_B C_B \left(1 - (V_{DD,L}/V_{DD,H})^2\right)}{\alpha_A C_A + \alpha_B C_B}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Savings grow when $\alpha_B C_B$ is large (i.e., the non-critical block has high activity/capacitance) and when $V_{DD,L} \ll V_{DD,H}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal $V_{DD}$ in DVS for a Given Deadline

**Problem Setup:** A processor must complete $W$ operations within deadline $D$. Each operation takes $t_d(V_{DD})$ time. Find the minimum $V_{DD}$ that meets the deadline.

$$
\text{Required frequency: } f_{req} = \frac{W}{D}
$$

$$
\text{Required delay per op: } t_d^{max} = \frac{D}{W}
$$

Using the alpha-power law delay model:

$$
t_d(V_{DD}) = \frac{K \cdot V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta}
$$

Setting $t_d(V_{DD}) = t_d^{max}$:

$$
\frac{K \cdot V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta} = \frac{D}{W}
$$

Solving iteratively (no closed form in general), we get $V_{DD,opt}$. The corresponding energy is:

$$
E_{op} = C_{eff} V_{DD,opt}^2
$$

> **Substitution of typical numbers:** For $W/D = 100$ MHz, $V_{TH} = 0.3$ V, $K = 30$ ps/V, $\beta = 1.3$:
> Iterating, $V_{DD,opt} \approx 0.7$ V for 100 MHz and $V_{DD,opt} \approx 1.1$ V for 500 MHz.
> Energy scales **cubically** in practice because both $V_{DD}$ and frequency must scale together.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transistor Stacking Leakage Reduction

**Problem Setup:** A 2-input NAND has both NMOS OFF. We want the leakage current as a fraction of a single OFF NMOS leakage.

Let the intermediate node voltage be $V_x$. Apply KCL — the current through both transistors must be equal:

$$
I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH1}(V_x)}{nV_T}\right) \left(1 - e^{-V_x/V_T}\right) = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH0}}{nV_T}\right) \left(1 - e^{-(V_{DD}-V_x)/V_T}\right)
$$

For $V_x \gg V_T$ and $V_{DD} - V_x \gg V_T$, the $(1 - e^{-\cdot}) \to 1$ approximation holds, simplifying to:

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH1}(V_x)}{nV_T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_{TH0}}{nV_T}\right)
$$

This forces $V_{TH1}(V_x) = V_{TH0}$, which is impossible — meaning $V_x$ must be *finite* so that one of the exponentials collapses. Numerically, $V_x$ settles around **100–200 mV** in typical 65 nm processes.

Using the body-effect expression:

$$
V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_x} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

with $\gamma \approx 0.4$ V$^{0.5}$, $\phi_F \approx 0.35$ V, $V_x = 0.1$ V:

$$
V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + 0.4 \left(\sqrt{0.7 + 0.1} - \sqrt{0.7}\right)
$$

$$
V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + 0.4 \left(\sqrt{0.8} - \sqrt{0.7}\right)
$$

$$
V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + 0.4 \left(0.8944 - 0.8367\right)
$$

$$
V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + 0.4 \times 0.0577
$$

$$
V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + 0.0231 \text{ V}
$$

The leakage reduction factor for the lower transistor:

$$
\frac{I_{lower}}{I_{single}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{TH1} - V_{TH0} - V_{GS,eff}}{nV_T}\right)
$$

With $V_{GS,eff} = -V_x = -0.1$ V, $V_{TH1} - V_{TH0} = 0.023$ V, $n = 1.4$, $V_T = 0.026$ V:

$$
\frac{I_{lower}}{I_{single}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{0.023 - (-0.1)}{1.4 \times 0.026}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{0.123}{0.0364}\right) = \exp(3.38)
$$

$$
\frac{I_{lower}}{I_{single}} \approx 29.4
$$

> **Result:** The stacked transistor pair conducts **~30× less leakage** than a single OFF transistor in this 65 nm example. KTU's typical accepted answer is **~10×** because $\gamma$ and $V_x$ vary with process; the *order of magnitude* is the key takeaway.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stacking in 3-Transistor Stack (3-input NAND OFF case)

For three OFF NMOS in series, two intermediate nodes appear. Numerical SPICE-like analysis shows:

$$
K_{stack,3} \approx 50 \text{ to } 100
$$

That is, leakage drops by nearly **two orders of magnitude**. This is why 3-input NAND gates are used as "leakage-friendly" sleep elements in some cell libraries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it not grow linearly with stack height?** Each additional transistor's $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative, but the body-effect gain on $V_{TH}$ has diminishing returns because the $\sqrt{\cdot}$ function flattens out. The total reduction is roughly *exponential* in the number of stacked transistors, but with a saturation knee around 3–4 stacks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multi-VDD Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VDD_H 1.2V Power Rail] --> B[Critical Path Block ALU]
    C[VDD_L 0.8V Power Rail] --> D[Non Critical Block Decoder]
    E[VDD_H 1.2V Power Rail] --> F[Memory Controller]
    G[Global Clock and Reset] --> B
    G --> D
    G --> F
    B --> H[Level Shifter Low to High]
    D --> H
    H --> F
    H --> I[Output Pad]
    F --> I
    J[PMU Power Management Unit] --> A
    J --> C
    J --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three voltage islands — High for the ALU and memory controller, Low for the decoder. Any signal crossing from Low to High must pass through a level shifter, enforced by the PMU.

---

## 4.2 Dynamic VDD / DVS Closed-Loop Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workload Monitor] --> B[Required Frequency Estimator]
    B --> C[Vdd Lookup Table or PID Controller]
    C --> D[DC DC Buck Converter]
    D --> E[Target Voltage Island]
    E --> F[Critical Path Replica Monitor]
    F --> G{Speed Margin OK}
    G -->|Yes| H[Hold Vdd Constant]
    G -->|No Decrease| I[Increase Vdd via DC DC]
    G -->|No Increase| J[Decrease Vdd via DC DC]
    H --> C
    I --> C
    J --> C
    K[Operating System Scheduler] --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The OS hints the workload. A hardware delay replica measures actual silicon speed. A closed loop (PID or LUT) trims $V_{DD}$ via a DC-DC converter so the chip runs at the *bare-minimum* voltage for the *bare-minimum* frequency — eliminating the traditional design margin.

---

## 4.3 Transistor Stacking — 2-Input NAND Leakage Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VDD Supply] --> B[PMOS 1 OFF when A is High]
    A --> C[PMOS 2 OFF when B is High]
    B --> D[Output Node Y]
    C --> D
    D --> E[NMOS 1 OFF when A is Low]
    E --> F[Intermediate Node Vx Self Biased]
    F --> G[NMOS 2 OFF when B is Low]
    G --> H[Ground Rail]
    F -.-> I[Body Effect increases VTH of NMOS 1]
    F -.-> J[Vx approx 100 to 200 mV typical]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** When both inputs A and B are LOW, both NMOS are OFF. The intermediate node Vx self-biases (neither 0 V nor VDD). This Vx appears as a *negative* $V_{GS}$ for NMOS-1 and a *positive* $V_{SB}$ triggering body effect, raising $V_{TH}$. The resulting subthreshold leakage through the series pair is roughly **10x lower** than a single OFF NMOS — purely from passive physics, no extra control signal required.

---

## 4.4 Sleep Transistor + Stacking (Combined Leakage Suppression)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Logic Block Internal Gates] --> B[Footer NMOS Sleep Transistor]
    B --> C[Ground]
    D[VDD] --> E[Header PMOS Sleep Transistor]
    E --> A
    F[Sleep Signal Active Low] --> B
    F --> G[Inverted Sleep Signal] --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** During sleep, the footer NMOS is OFF, stacking with the OFF NMOS inside the logic gates, yielding a **double-stack effect** — leakage is suppressed by stacking between the natural OFF stack *and* the explicit sleep transistor. This is the practical mechanism behind power-gating libraries.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET752]`

**Q: Differentiate between Multi-VDD and Dynamic VDD design with respect to when the voltage change occurs and what triggers the change.**

**Model Answer:**

> *Multi-VDD* is a **static / design-time** technique. The supply voltage of each voltage island is **fixed at design time** based on the critical-path delay requirement of that block and is not altered during normal chip operation. Voltage transitions occur only when the chip is powered on/off or enters deep-sleep.
>
> *Dynamic VDD Scaling* (DVS) is a **run-time** technique. The supply voltage is **varied dynamically during operation** based on the instantaneous workload. A heavy workload (e.g., video decoding) raises $V_{DD}$ and frequency; an idle workload (e.g., screen standby) lowers them. A closed-loop variant, AVS, uses a delay replica to track process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variation. (3 marks: 1 each for trigger, timing, and example)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET752]`

**Q: Why does a 2-input NAND gate with both inputs at logic '0' conduct less subthreshold leakage than a single OFF NMOS transistor connected between $V_{DD}$ and GND?**

**Model Answer:**

When both NMOS of a 2-input NAND are OFF, the intermediate node between them is **not** at 0 V; it self-biases to a small potential $V_x$ (typically 100–200 mV). This happens because the small subthreshold current through the lower transistor charges the parasitic capacitance of the intermediate node until the lower transistor's $V_{GS}$ becomes *negative* enough to balance the upper transistor's drain current.
This negative $V_{GS}$ and the resulting *source-to-body* voltage $V_{SB} = V_x$ trigger the **body effect**, raising the effective threshold voltage $V_{TH}$ of the lower transistor. Since subthreshold leakage depends exponentially on $-V_{TH}/nV_T$, the leakage is reduced by approximately **one order of magnitude** compared to a single OFF transistor. (3 marks: 1 for $V_x$ self-biasing, 1 for body effect, 1 for leakage reduction)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET752]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Multi-VDD design technique for low-power VLSI. With a neat block diagram, show how level shifters are inserted between voltage islands. Discuss the conditions under which Multi-VDD offers maximum power savings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS) and Adaptive Voltage Scaling (AVS). Derive the expression for the optimal $V_{DD}$ to meet a given performance deadline. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Multi-VDD

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Multi-VDD partitions the chip into multiple voltage islands. Critical-path blocks get a high $V_{DD,H}$; non-critical blocks get a low $V_{DD,L}$.

2. **Block diagram (2 marks):** See SECTION 4.1. Two (or more) voltage rails feed different blocks, with level shifters at all low-to-high crossings.

3. **Power saving condition (2 marks):** From the derivation in SECTION 3.1:

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{\alpha_B C_B \left(1 - (V_{DD,L}/V_{DD,H})^2\right)}{\alpha_A C_A + \alpha_B C_B}
$$

Maximum savings occur when:
- The non-critical block has **high switching activity** ($\alpha_B$ large) **and large capacitance** ($C_B$ large).
- The ratio $V_{DD,L}/V_{DD,H}$ is **small** (e.g., 0.6–0.7).

4. **Level shifter necessity (2 marks):** A low-VDD logic-high (e.g., 0.8 V) cannot turn OFF the PMOS of a high-VDD block (1.2 V rail) — without a level shifter, permanent short-circuit current flows and the chip burns/leaks.

#### Model Solution — (b) DVS / AVS

1. **DVS definition (1 mark):** DVS adjusts $V_{DD}$ and frequency at run-time based on workload.

2. **AVS definition (1 mark):** AVS is the closed-loop version using a critical-path delay replica to remove PVT design margin.

3. **Derivation (4 marks):** From SECTION 3.2 — required frequency $f_{req} = W/D$, gate delay $t_d = K V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta$. Setting $t_d(V_{DD}) = D/W$ gives the optimal $V_{DD}$ satisfying:

$$
\frac{K V_{DD,opt}}{(V_{DD,opt} - V_{TH})^\beta} = \frac{D}{W}
$$

The corresponding energy per operation is $E_{op} = C_{eff} V_{DD,opt}^2$.

4. **Block diagram (1 mark):** See SECTION 4.2.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET752]`

**Q: (a)** With suitable expressions, explain the subthreshold leakage mechanism in CMOS transistors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the transistor stacking effect. Show mathematically why a 2-transistor stack reduces subthreshold leakage by approximately one order of magnitude. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Subthreshold Leakage

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Subthreshold leakage is the weak inversion current that flows from drain to source even when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$.

2. **Equation (3 marks):**

$$
I_{sub} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}\right) \, \left(1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right)
$$

where $I_0 = \mu C_{ox} (W/L) V_T^2 e^{1.8} \, e^{-V_{TH}/(nV_T)}$, $n \approx 1.4$ is the subthreshold swing factor, $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at room temperature.

3. **Origin and dominance (2 marks):** In nanometer nodes (≤ 90 nm), $V_{TH}$ is lowered to maintain drive current, causing $I_{sub}$ to grow exponentially. Combined with billions of transistors, leakage power can match or exceed dynamic power.

4. **Process dependence (1 mark):** $I_{sub}$ depends exponentially on $V_{TH}$, linearly on $W/L$, and increases by **~10× per 100 mV reduction in $V_{TH}$** — the famous "100 mV per decade" rule.

#### Model Solution — (b) Transistor Stacking

1. **Concept (2 marks):** Two OFF transistors in series (e.g., NMOS of a 2-input NAND with both inputs LOW). The intermediate node $V_x$ self-biases to a value where the two subthreshold currents balance.

2. **Self-bias formation (2 marks):** KCL forces the same current through both transistors. Since the upper transistor sees the full $V_{DD} - V_x$ across it, the lower transistor must develop a $V_{GS} < 0$ to match — this happens by charging the intermediate parasitic capacitance to $V_x \approx 100$–200 mV.

3. **Body effect and leakage drop (2 marks):** With $V_{SB} = V_x$, the body effect raises $V_{TH}$ of the lower transistor:

$$
V_{TH,eff} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_x} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)
$$

Plugging $V_x = 0.1$ V, $\gamma = 0.4$ V$^{0.5}$, $2\phi_F = 0.7$ V (from SECTION 3.3) gives $V_{TH,eff} - V_{TH0} \approx 23$ mV. The combined effect of negative $V_{GS}$ and elevated $V_{TH}$ reduces leakage by **~10–30×**, i.e., approximately **one order of magnitude**.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Stacking is a free, passive technique. Cell libraries use NAND-based implementations for low-leakage cells.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Multi-VDD with DVS:** Many students write that Multi-VDD "varies voltage at run-time" — *wrong*. It is fixed at design time. DVS is the run-time technique.
> 2. **Forgetting level shifters:** When drawing Multi-VDD diagrams, students often omit level shifters at low-to-high boundaries. Examiner deducts **2 marks** explicitly.
> 3. **Stacking derivation pitfall:** Students often write $V_{TH}$ increases without showing the body-effect equation. Examiner expects the $\gamma(\sqrt{\cdot} - \sqrt{\cdot})$ form.
> 4. **DVS derivation:** Writing only "lower $V_{DD}$" without the deadline $D$ and work $W$ formulation loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Units in tables:** Always quote $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at 300 K. Omitting the temperature reference loses **0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total power** = dynamic + static. $P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$ is the dominant lever.
- **Multi-VDD** = multiple static $V_{DD}$ islands on one chip; requires level shifters at low→high boundaries.
- **Dynamic VDD (DVS)** = run-time voltage scaling based on workload. **AVS** = closed-loop variant using a delay replica for PVT compensation.
- **Optimal $V_{DD}$ in DVS** satisfies $K V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^\beta = D/W$, where $D$ is deadline and $W$ is total work.
- **Transistor stacking** = series OFF transistors self-bias their intermediate node, producing a negative $V_{GS}$ and body-effect-induced $V_{TH}$ rise.
- **Stack leakage reduction** ≈ 10× for 2-stack, ~50× for 3-stack in 65 nm process.
- **Subthreshold current** $I_{sub} = I_0 e^{(V_{GS} - V_{TH})/nV_T} (1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T})$; $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at 300 K.
- **Body effect formula:** $V_{TH}(V_{SB}) = V_{TH0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- **Why NAND leaks less than INV:** NAND has a natural 2-stack NMOS path; INV has a single NMOS.
- **Power gating + stacking** = double-leakage-suppression technique widely used in modern sleep cells.
- **Energy scales cubically** with frequency in DVS because both $V_{DD}$ and $f$ must change together.
- **KTU numerical tip:** The typical "10× per 100 mV" rule for subthreshold leakage and "10× per 2-stack" rule for the stack effect are the two most-tested numbers in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VTCMOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** VTCMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Variable Threshold CMOS (VTCMOS)** is an adaptive, dynamic low-power circuit technique used in CMOS digital design in which the **threshold voltage (Vth)** of MOS transistors is *deliberately and dynamically modulated* during circuit operation by applying a controlled **reverse body-bias (substrate bias, VBS)** to the transistor bulk terminal.

The fundamental principle is built upon the **Body Effect** of a MOS transistor. By manipulating the Source-to-Body voltage (VSB), the effective threshold voltage seen by the device changes, which in turn exponentially changes the sub-threshold leakage current (Ileak).

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, VTCMOS is classified as a **Standby Leakage Reduction Technique** belonging to the family of *static* and *adaptive* power management methods, used in conjunction with, or in place of, multi-Vth design strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> "VTCMOS is a leakage power reduction technique in which the threshold voltage of MOS transistors is dynamically varied by applying a controllable substrate bias. A low Vth is used in active mode for high speed, and a high Vth is used in standby mode for low leakage current."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water Tap" Intuition

Imagine a water tap (faucet) in your home. You have two operating conditions:

- **When cooking (active mode):** You need water to flow *fast*. You open the tap wide — this is like a **low Vth**, where current flows easily and gates switch quickly.
- **When you leave the house for work (standby mode):** You want the tap to *leak as little as possible* so you don't waste water (and don't drown your house). You tighten the tap as much as possible — this is like a **high Vth**, where leakage current is minimal but the tap cannot deliver fast flow.

**VTCMOS is the "smart plumber"** that automatically tightens or loosens the tap (i.e., adjusts Vth) based on whether the chip is working hard or resting — without you having to replace the tap physically.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical 0.18 µm Value |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | **26 mV** at 300 K |
| Body-Effect Coefficient | $\gamma$ | **0.3 – 0.5 V$^{1/2}$** |
| Zero-Bias Threshold | $V_{th0}$ | **0.4 – 0.5 V** |
| Standby Reverse Bias | $V_{SB}$ | **−0.5 to −1.5 V** |
| Leakage Reduction Factor | — | **10× to 1000×** |
| Active Mode Bias | $V_{SB}$ | **0 V** (or slightly positive) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** VTCMOS primarily targets **subthreshold leakage (I<sub>sub</sub>)** and is complementary to techniques like MTCMOS (Multi-Threshold CMOS) and Power Gating. It is most effective in deep submicron technologies (≤ 0.25 µm) where leakage dominates dynamic power.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind VTCMOS — Body Effect

The threshold voltage of a short-channel MOSFET in the presence of a source-to-body bias $V_{SB}$ is given by:

$$
V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)
$$

Where:
- $V_{th0}$ = Zero-bias threshold voltage (when $V_{SB} = 0$)
- $\gamma$ = Body-effect coefficient (depends on doping and oxide capacitance)
- $\phi_F$ = Fermi potential of the substrate ($\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\frac{N_A}{n_i}$)
- $V_{SB}$ = Source-to-Body voltage (positive for reverse body bias)

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Two Operating Modes

**Step 1 — Identify Mode of Operation**
The circuit detects whether the chip/system is in **ACTIVE** or **STANDBY** mode via a control signal (often derived from a clock-gating or sleep signal).

**Step 2 — Active Mode (High Performance)**
- $V_{SB} \approx 0$ V (zero or small forward bias)
- Effective $V_{th}$ remains **LOW** ($V_{th} \approx V_{th0}$)
- Drain current $I_D$ is **HIGH** → fast switching speed, low gate delay
- Leakage $I_{leak}$ is **HIGH** (acceptable, since chip is not idle)

**Step 3 — Standby Mode (Low Power)**
- $V_{SB}$ is made **positive** (i.e., substrate biased *negatively* relative to source for NMOS)
- This is **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)**
- Effective $V_{th}$ **INCREASES** by $\Delta V_{th}$
- Subthreshold leakage decreases **exponentially**:
$$
I_{leak} \propto e^{-V_{th}/(nV_T)}
$$
- A 100 mV increase in $V_{th}$ typically reduces leakage by **~10×** at room temperature.

**Step 4 — Dynamic Switching**
A **substrate bias controller** (a charge pump + feedback circuit) generates the appropriate $V_{SB}$ within microseconds of mode transition.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Body-effect equation for Vth | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{gs} - V_{th}}{nV_T}}\left(1 - e^{-V_{ds}/V_T}\right)$ | Subthreshold leakage current | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $\frac{I_{leak,standby}}{I_{leak,active}} = 10^{\Delta V_{th}/S}$ | Leakage ratio, where $S$ = subthreshold swing | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S = nV_T \ln 10$ | Subthreshold swing (≈ 90 mV/dec at 300 K) | V/decade |
| 5 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static leakage power | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $t_{delay} \propto \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^\alpha}$ | Gate propagation delay (α ≈ 1.3 for short channel) | seconds (s) |
| 7 | $V_{SB,standby} \approx -1$ to $-2$ V | Typical RBB for NMOS in 0.18 µm | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V$^{1/2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** The equation $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\cdots)$ is valid only for $V_{SB} \ge 0$ (reverse bias or zero). If $V_{SB} < 0$ (forward bias), the source-body junction becomes forward-biased, causing **excessive current and latch-up risk** — this is the absolute boundary that must not be violated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

VTCMOS is widely used in:

- **Mobile SoCs** (smartphones, tablets) — to extend battery life during idle/screen-off states.
- **Microcontrollers** in IoT sensor nodes — where the chip spends 99% of time in standby.
- **Wearable devices** — fitness bands, smartwatches with always-on modes.
- **DRAM Peripheral Circuits** — word-line drivers and sense amplifiers.
- It was historically used in **Toshiba's MPEG-4 codec LSI**, **NTT's DSP**, and various low-power processors from the early 2000s onward.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Effect of Body Bias on Threshold Voltage

**Given:**
- $V_{th0} = 0.45$ V
- $\gamma = 0.45$ V$^{1/2}$
- $2\phi_F = 0.7$ V
- Standby mode: $V_{SB} = 1.0$ V (reverse bias applied)

**To Find:** New threshold voltage $V_{th,standby}$

**Step 1: Write the body-effect equation**

$$
V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $V_{SB} = 0$ (active mode baseline)**

$$
V_{th,active} = 0.45 + 0.45 \left( \sqrt{0.7 - 0} - \sqrt{0.7} \right)
$$

Since the second term cancels out (both are $\sqrt{0.7}$):

$$
V_{th,active} = V_{th0} = 0.45 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V_{SB} = 1.0$ V (standby mode)**

$$
V_{th,standby} = 0.45 + 0.45 \left( \sqrt{0.7 - 1.0} - \sqrt{0.7} \right)
$$

**Step 4: Compute the square roots**

$$
\sqrt{0.7} \approx 0.8367 \text{ V}^{1/2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.7 - 1.0} = \sqrt{-0.3} = j\sqrt{0.3} \quad \text{(mathematically imaginary!)}
$$

**Step 5: Important Correction — Sign Convention**

The standard convention uses $V_{BS} = -V_{SB}$. For reverse body bias on NMOS, **$V_{BS}$ is negative**, so we write:

$$
V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + \vert V_{BS} \vert} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)
$$

**Step 6: Re-evaluate with correct sign convention** ($V_{BS} = -1$ V, so $\vert V_{BS}\vert = 1$)

$$
V_{th,standby} = 0.45 + 0.45 \left( \sqrt{0.7 + 1.0} - \sqrt{0.7} \right)
$$

$$
= 0.45 + 0.45 \left( \sqrt{1.7} - 0.8367 \right)
$$

$$
= 0.45 + 0.45 \left( 1.3038 - 0.8367 \right)
$$

$$
= 0.45 + 0.45 \times 0.4671
$$

$$
= 0.45 + 0.2102
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{th,standby} = 0.6602 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the threshold shift**

$$
\Delta V_{th} = V_{th,standby} - V_{th,active} = 0.6602 - 0.45 = 0.2102 \text{ V}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Leakage Reduction Ratio

**Given:** $\Delta V_{th} = 0.21$ V, subthreshold swing $S = 90$ mV/decade

**Step 1: Apply the leakage ratio formula**

$$
\frac{I_{leak,active}}{I_{leak,standby}} = 10^{\Delta V_{th} / S}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
= 10^{0.21 / 0.09} = 10^{2.333}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate**

$$
10^{2.333} = 10^2 \times 10^{0.333} = 100 \times 2.154 = 215.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Leakage reduction} \approx 215\times}
$$

This means standby leakage is reduced by **~99.5%** — a powerful result achievable simply by applying ~1 V of body bias.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed Penalty in Active Mode (Lower Bound)

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $C_L = 50$ fF, $V_{th,active} = 0.4$ V, $V_{th,standby} = 0.6$ V, $\alpha = 1.4$

**Step 1: Active mode delay**

$$
t_{d,active} = K \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th,active})^\alpha} = K \cdot \frac{50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.8}{(1.8 - 0.4)^{1.4}}
$$

$$
= K \cdot \frac{90 \times 10^{-15}}{(1.4)^{1.4}}
$$

$$
(1.4)^{1.4} \approx 1.626 \Rightarrow t_{d,active} \approx K \cdot 55.3 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 2: If mistakenly operated at high Vth:**

$$
t_{d,high\_Vth} = K \cdot \frac{90 \times 10^{-15}}{(1.8 - 0.6)^{1.4}} = K \cdot \frac{90 \times 10^{-15}}{(1.2)^{1.4}}
$$

$$
(1.2)^{1.4} \approx 1.274 \Rightarrow t_{d,high\_Vth} \approx K \cdot 70.6 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 3: Speed penalty**

$$
\frac{t_{d,high\_Vth}}{t_{d,active}} = \frac{70.6}{55.3} \approx 1.28
$$

**Conclusion:** Using the *wrong* threshold would slow the circuit by **~28%**. VTCMOS avoids this by switching $V_{th}$ back to low only during active mode.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: VTCMOS vs MTCMOS

| Parameter | VTCMOS | MTCMOS (Power Gating) |
|---|---|---|
| Leakage Reduction Mechanism | Modulates Vth via body bias | Disconnects supply via sleep transistor |
| Active Mode Performance | Excellent (Vth = low) | Slight degradation (sleep transistor resistance) |
| Wake-up Time | Very short (µs) | Long (µs to ms, charge restoration) |
| Area Overhead | Low (only bias generator) | Higher (sleep transistors everywhere) |
| Control Signal | Continuous bias adjustment | Single SLEEP signal |
| Best Use Case | Long standby periods, fine-grained control | Aggressive power-down, idle blocks |
| Fabrication Complexity | Triple-well or dual-well required | Standard CMOS, larger cell library |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — VTCMOS Architecture & Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    modeInput[Mode Detector Active or Standby] --> biasCtrl[Substrate Bias Controller Charge Pump + Feedback]
    biasCtrl --> nwell[Apply VSB to NMOS Substrate Triple Well]
    biasCtrl --> pwell[Apply VBS to PMOS Substrate N Well]
    nwell --> logicBlock[Core CMOS Logic Gates]
    pwell --> logicBlock
    modeInput -->|Standby Signal High| logicBlock
    logicBlock -->|Standby Leakage Ileak Reduced| outputNode[Output Node Reduced Power]
    
    style modeInput fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style biasCtrl fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style logicBlock fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style outputNode fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Two-Mode Threshold Switching Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACTIVE_MODE[ACTIVE MODE]
        A1[CLK toggling] --> A2[VSB equals 0V]
        A2 --> A3[Vth equals Vth0 LOW]
        A3 --> A4[High Speed High Leakage]
    end
    
    subgraph STANDBY_MODE[STANDBY MODE]
        S1[No clock activity] --> S2[VSB equals plus 1V RBB]
        S2 --> S3[Vth equals Vth0 plus Delta Vth HIGH]
        S3 --> S4[Low Speed Very Low Leakage]
    end
    
    style A1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style S1 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Sectional View (Functional Equivalence)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CROSS_SECTION[NMOS in Triple Well P Substrate Architecture]
        c1[P plus Substrate Deep Bulk] --> c2[P Well Active Body of NMOS]
        c2 --> c3[Source N plus]
        c2 --> c4[Drain N plus]
        c2 --> c5[Gate Oxide and Poly]
        c6[Deep N Well Isolation Layer] --> c2
        c7[Bias Contact on P Well] -.->|Controls Vth| c2
    end
    
    style c7 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style c5 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style CROSS_SECTION fill:#f5f5f5,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Flow — VTCMOS Operation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Chip Power ON]) --> detect{Operating Mode}
    detect -->|High Activity Detected| active[Active Mode Path]
    detect -->|Low Activity Timeout| standby[Standby Mode Path]
    
    active --> act1[Set VSB to 0V]
    act1 --> act2[Set Vth to Vth0 Low]
    act2 --> act3[Normal High Speed Operation]
    act3 --> monitor{Timer Check}
    monitor --> detect
    
    standby --> st1[Activate Charge Pump]
    st1 --> st2[Set VSB to plus 1V RBB]
    st2 --> st3[Set Vth to Vth0 plus Delta Vth High]
    st3 --> st4[Leakage Drops by Two Orders of Magnitude]
    st4 --> wake{Wake Up Signal}
    wake -->|Yes| detect
    wake -->|No| st4
    
    style start fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
    style detect fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style active fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style standby fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style act3 fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style st4 fill:#ff6f00,color:#ffffff
```
