## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET752]**
*Define Dynamic Threshold CMOS (DTCMOS). State any two advantages of DTCMOS over conventional CMOS.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Dynamic Threshold CMOS (DTCMOS) is a low-power VLSI circuit technique in which the **body (substrate/well) terminal of the MOSFET is tied to the gate terminal**. This dynamic connection causes the threshold voltage to vary automatically with the input — **decreasing** when the transistor is ON (for higher drive current) and **increasing** to its nominal value when the transistor is OFF (for lower subthreshold leakage).

**Advantages (1 Mark — any two):**

1. **High performance in active mode:** Threshold voltage drops automatically at high $V_{GS}$, boosting $I_{on}$ and improving switching speed without any process changes.
2. **Low leakage in standby mode:** In the OFF state, the threshold returns to its nominal value, exponentially reducing subthreshold leakage.
3. **Single-mask simplicity:** No additional process steps are required — only a circuit-level change in the body connection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEAET752]**
*Why is DTCMOS limited to low supply voltages? What is the typical upper limit of $V_{DD}$ for safe DTCMOS operation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In DTCMOS, when the gate is HIGH ($V_{gate} = V_{DD}$), the body of the NMOS is also pulled to $V_{DD}$, while the source remains at ground. This means the **body-to-source pn-junction** of the NMOS experiences a forward bias of $V_{DD}$ volts. If $V_{DD}$ exceeds the cut-in voltage of the pn-junction (**~0.7 V**), the junction turns ON, causing large parasitic currents, latch-up risk, and destruction of the dynamic threshold effect.

**Typical upper limit:** **$V_{DD} \leq 0.6$ V** (with a hard absolute ceiling of **0.7 V**) for safe DTCMOS operation in bulk CMOS technology.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEAET752]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the operation of a **DTCMOS inverter**. Derive the expression for the threshold voltage in terms of the body-effect parameters when the body is tied to the gate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **DTCMOS, MTCMOS, and VTCMOS** techniques in terms of leakage reduction, active-mode performance, area overhead, and $V_{DD}$ scalability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Schematic Description (2 Marks):**

In a DTCMOS inverter, the **NMOS bulk is tied to its gate** and the **PMOS n-well is tied to its gate**. The input $V_{in}$ drives both gates and both bulks simultaneously. The output $V_{out}$ is taken from the common drain node of the NMOS and PMOS.

**Threshold Voltage Derivation (5 Marks):**

The standard body-effect equation is:

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

For the NMOS in DTCMOS, the source is at ground ($V_S = 0$), and the body is at $V_{gate}$. Therefore:

$$V_{SB} = V_S - V_B = -V_{gate}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{th}(V_{gate}) = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{gate}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

**Two operating cases:**

- When $V_{gate} = 0$: $V_{th} = V_{th0}$ (max threshold → low leakage).
- When $V_{gate} = V_{DD}$: $V_{th} = V_{th0} - \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F} - \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{DD}} \right)$ (reduced threshold → high drive current).

The threshold drop is:

$$\Delta V_{th} = \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F} - \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{DD}} \right)$$

This completes the derivation.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

| **Criterion** | **DTCMOS** | **MTCMOS** | **VTCMOS** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Leakage Reduction** | Moderate–High; auto via dynamic $V_{th}$ | Very High; sleep transistor cuts off rail | Very High; reverse body bias in standby |
| **Active-Mode Performance** | High (auto-low $V_{th}$) | Slightly degraded (series sleep device) | High (zero or forward body bias) |
| **Area Overhead** | Minimal (only routing change) | Moderate (sleep transistors per block) | High (body-bias generators + routing) |
| **$V_{DD}$ Scalability** | Poor ($V_{DD} \leq 0.6$ V) | Good (works at nominal $V_{DD}$) | Good (works at nominal $V_{DD}$) |
| **Control Complexity** | None (self-adjusting) | Sleep signal needed | Body-bias control loops needed |
| **Dynamic Power Penalty** | High (body cap charging) | Low | Moderate |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** DTCMOS is best suited for **ultra-low-voltage, sub-threshold** applications where simplicity and self-adaptation outweigh the body-capacitance penalty.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PEAET752]**

**(a)** A DTCMOS NMOS transistor has the following parameters: $V_{th0} = 0.4$ V, $\gamma = 0.5$ V$^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.6$ V, $V_{DD} = 0.5$ V. Compute **(i)** the threshold voltage when the device is fully ON, **(ii)** the threshold voltage drop $\Delta V_{th}$, and **(iii)** the leakage reduction ratio compared to a fixed low-$V_{th}$ device designed for equal drive, assuming $n = 1.4$ and $V_T = 0.025$ V. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major disadvantages** of DTCMOS. Under what conditions would you **prefer DTCMOS over MTCMOS** in a low-power design? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_{th0} = 0.4$ V, $\gamma = 0.5$ V$^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.6$ V, $V_{DD} = 0.5$ V, $n = 1.4$, $V_T = 0.025$ V.

**(i) Threshold voltage in active mode (2 Marks):**

With body tied to gate and $V_S = 0$, $V_{SB} = -V_{DD} = -0.5$ V.

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{DD}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

$$V_{th} = 0.4 + 0.5 \left( \sqrt{0.6 - 0.5} - \sqrt{0.6} \right)$$

$$V_{th} = 0.4 + 0.5 \left( \sqrt{0.1} - \sqrt{0.6} \right)$$

$$V_{th} = 0.4 + 0.5 \left( 0.3162 - 0.7746 \right)$$

$$V_{th} = 0.4 + 0.5 \times (-0.4584)$$

$$V_{th} = 0.4 - 0.2292$$

$$\boxed{V_{th} = 0.1708 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Threshold voltage drop (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta V_{th} = V_{th0} - V_{th} = 0.4 - 0.1708 = 0.2292 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{th} = 229.2 \text{ mV}}$$

**(iii) Leakage reduction ratio (3 Marks):**

The leakage reduction compared to a fixed low-$V_{th}$ device of equal drive:

$$\frac{I_{leak,DTCMOS}}{I_{leak,low-Vth}} = e^{\Delta V_{th} / (n V_T)}$$

$$= e^{0.2292 / (1.4 \times 0.025)}$$

$$= e^{0.2292 / 0.035}$$

$$= e^{6.549}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{I_{leak,DTCMOS}}{I_{leak,low-Vth}} \approx 699.5}$$

**Interpretation:** DTCMOS reduces leakage by a factor of **~700×** compared to a fixed low-$V_{th}$ design of equivalent performance.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Major Disadvantages (4 Marks):**

1. **Body-Source Forward-Bias Limit:** $V_{DD}$ is hard-capped at ~0.7 V; DTCMOS cannot be used at standard supply voltages (1.0 V, 1.2 V, 1.8 V).
2. **Increased Dynamic Power:** The body capacitance $C_b$ must be charged and discharged every cycle. Since $C_b$ is comparable to $C_g$, dynamic power can nearly **double**.
3. **Latch-up Risk:** The forward-biased body-source junction injects carriers into the substrate, potentially triggering CMOS latch-up.
4. **Body-Effect on Analog Circuits:** If used in mixed-signal blocks, the dynamic body bias modulates the device parameters unpredictably, causing distortion and gain errors.
5. **No Threshold Flexibility:** $V_{th}$ is fully coupled to the input — there is no independent control knob for fine-tuning speed vs. leakage.

**When to prefer DTCMOS over MTCMOS (3 Marks):**

DTCMOS is preferred over MTCMOS when:

1. **$V_{DD}$ is very low** (sub-0.5 V range) and MTCMOS sleep transistors fail to operate correctly.
2. **No separate sleep signal** is available, and area/complexity overhead of a sleep controller must be avoided.
3. **Active-mode performance is the top priority** and the body-capacitance dynamic power penalty is acceptable.
4. The design is **purely digital** (no sensitive analog blocks) and latch-up risk is managed by careful layout (guard rings, deep n-well).
5. The application is **always-on, low-duty-cycle** (e.g., wake-up detectors) where standby leakage is critical and MTCMOS wake-up latency is unacceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse DTCMOS (Dynamic Threshold) with Dual-Threshold CMOS. They are different techniques. Dual-Threshold uses two fixed $V_{th}$ devices; DTCMOS modulates one device dynamically.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to mention the **$V_{DD} \leq 0.7$ V** limitation explicitly in your answers — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this.
> 3. **Do NOT** write $V_{SB} = +V_{gate}$ (this is the most common sign error). The correct relation is $V_{SB} = V_S - V_B = 0 - V_{gate} = -V_{gate}$ for an NMOS.
> 4. **Do NOT** ignore the **body-capacitance dynamic-power penalty** when comparing DTCMOS with MTCMOS. Marks are reserved for this trade-off.
> 5. **Do NOT** confuse the leakage reduction ratio formula — it is $e^{\Delta V_{th}/(nV_T)}$, not $e^{-\Delta V_{th}/(nV_T)}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **DTCMOS = Body tied to Gate.** This is the single most important fact.
- **Active state:** $V_{th}$ **decreases** → high $I_{on}$ → high speed.
- **Standby state:** $V_{th} = V_{th0}$ (nominal, maximum) → low $I_{off}$ → low leakage.
- **Voltage limit:** $V_{DD} \leq 0.7$ V (body-source junction forward bias). Practical: $V_{DD} \leq 0.5$ V.
- **Key equation:** $V_{th,DTCMOS} = V_{th0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{gate}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- **Threshold drop in active mode:** $\Delta V_{th} = \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F} - \sqrt{2\phi_F - V_{DD}})$.
- **Leakage reduction factor:** $e^{\Delta V_{th}/(nV_T)}$ — typically **50× to 1000×** depending on $\Delta V_{th}$.
- **Main penalty:** Doubled dynamic power due to body-capacitance switching.
- **Main risk:** CMOS latch-up from forward-biased body-source junction.
- **Best application:** Ultra-low-voltage (sub-0.5 V) digital logic — IoT nodes, biomedical implants, energy-harvesting systems.
- **NOT suitable for:** Standard $V_{DD}$ (1.0 V+) designs, analog/mixed-signal blocks, high-performance processors.
- **Comparison trio to remember:** DTCMOS (self-adapting) vs. MTCMOS (sleep transistor) vs. VTCMOS (body-bias generator) — all three are in Module 2 of KTU syllabus.
- **Single-line KTU-style answer:** *DTCMOS ties the body to the gate so that the threshold voltage dynamically lowers in the ON state for higher drive current and rises to its nominal value in the OFF state for reduced subthreshold leakage, but it is limited to $V_{DD} \leq 0.7$ V and incurs a body-capacitance dynamic-power penalty.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Power Dissipation - Power Gating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Power Dissipation - Power Gating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Gating** is a leakage power reduction technique in CMOS VLSI design wherein a circuit block is disconnected from the power supply rail (V<sub>DD</sub>) or the ground rail using a high-V<sub>t</sub> "sleep transistor" (also called a *header* or *footer* transistor). This effectively eliminates the sub-threshold and gate-oxide leakage currents of the gated logic during idle/states, trading off a small wake-up latency for massive standby power savings.

The standard implementation is known as **Multi-Threshold CMOS (MTCMOS)** technology, where low-V<sub>t</sub> transistors are used in the logic block for high performance, while high-V<sub>t</sub> sleep transistors are used to cut off the leakage path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Power gating is fundamentally a *static (leakage) power reduction* technique, but it is taught under the "Dynamic Power Dissipation" module because of its intimate relationship with switching activity, threshold voltage scaling, and supply voltage trade-offs that govern both dynamic and static power.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **hospital ward** at night. To save electricity, the ward is not kept "half on" with dim lights (which would still draw current and burn out bulbs). Instead, a master switch at the main panel **completely cuts off electricity** to that ward. When a patient needs care, a nurse flips the switch back on (this takes a moment — the bulbs warm up), and the ward functions normally.

The **circuit block** = the hospital ward.  
The **sleep transistor** = the master switch at the main panel.  
The **sleep signal** = the nurse flipping the switch.  
The **wake-up latency** = the time the bulbs take to glow at full brightness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Power gating is universally used in modern mobile SoCs (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos) where idle blocks (WiFi radio when Bluetooth is on, GPU when displaying static UI) consume otherwise wasted milliwatts of leakage. It is a cornerstone of *Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)* systems.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **V<sub>th</sub>** (Threshold Voltage): typically **0.3 V to 0.7 V** in modern processes.
- **I<sub>off</sub>** (Off-state leakage): ranges from **nA/µm to µA/µm** depending on V<sub>t</sub>.
- **Wake-up time** metric: typically **10 ns to 100 ns** for a 65 nm node.
- **Energy overhead** of wake-up: in the order of **picojoules** per cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Power Gating is Needed — The "Why"

As CMOS technology scales below **90 nm**, the supply voltage V<sub>DD</sub> is reduced to limit dynamic power ($\alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$). However, V<sub>th</sub> must also be reduced to maintain performance, leading to an **exponential increase in sub-threshold leakage current**:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{(V_{gs} - V_{th})}{nV_T}} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{ds}}{V_T}}\right)$$

When V<sub>th</sub> is small, I<sub>sub</sub> becomes large — meaning a transistor that is *off* still leaks current. In modern nodes, leakage can account for **30%–50%** of total chip power, hence the critical need for power gating.

## 2.2 Power Gating Modes of Operation

| Mode | Sleep Transistor State | Purpose |
|------|----------------------|---------|
| **Active Mode** | ON (full V<sub>DD</sub> to logic) | Normal computation |
| **Drowsy/Standby Mode** | Partially ON (reduced V<sub>DD</sub>) | Trivial leakage reduction |
| **Shutdown/Sleep Mode** | OFF (rail collapsed) | Maximum leakage cut-off |
| **Wake-up Transition** | Reactivating | Brief surge current phase |

## 2.3 Types of Power Gating Architectures

1. **Fine-Grained Power Gating:** A sleep transistor is inserted into every standard cell. Maximum flexibility, but large area overhead.
2. **Coarse-Grained Power Gating:** A single large sleep transistor gates an entire logic block. Area-efficient, used in industry.
3. **Cluster-Based (Hierarchical) Power Gating:** A hybrid where blocks are divided into clusters, each with its own sleep device.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic switching power | Watts (W) |
| $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static/leakage power | Watts (W) |
| $I_{leak,sleep} \approx 0$ | Leakage in shutdown mode (ideal) | Amperes |
| $W_{sleep} = \dfrac{I_{max}}{k_n \cdot V_{dsat,min}}$ | Sleep transistor sizing (linear region) | meters (m) |
| $\tau_{wake} = R_{sleep} \cdot C_{block}$ | Wake-up time constant | seconds |
| $E_{wake} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{block} V_{DD}^2$ | Energy to charge virtual rail | Joules (J) |
| $P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot d + P_{sleep} \cdot (1-d)$ | Average gated power (d = duty cycle) | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Notice the trade-off — a *larger* sleep transistor reduces wake-up latency but increases area and adds parasitic capacitance. KTU questions frequently test this **area vs. latency trade-off**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power gating is mandatory in:
- **Mobile phones** — to extend battery life during idle states.
- **IoT edge devices** — to achieve years-long battery operation.
- **Data center accelerators** — to meet thermal design power (TDP) budgets.
- **Wearable biomedical chips** — to minimize heat near skin.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dynamic Power Dissipation Recap

A CMOS inverter charges/discharges load capacitance C<sub>L</sub> at switching activity $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f$.

**Step 1:** Energy drawn from supply per switching event is $V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**Step 2:** Half of this energy is stored on C<sub>L</sub>, half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor.

**Step 3:** Power = Energy per transition × transitions per second = $C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot \alpha f$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn} &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f \\
\text{Units: } & \text{Farads} \times \text{Volts}^2 \times \text{Hz} = \text{Watts}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Power with Power Gating

Let a circuit block be **active** for a fraction $d$ of the time (duty cycle) and **sleeping** for $(1-d)$.

**Step 1:** Power during active mode = $P_{active} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{leak}$.

**Step 2:** Power during sleep mode ≈ $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,sleep}$ (where $I_{leak,sleep} \to 0$ with ideal gating).

**Step 3:** Average power = time-weighted sum.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= P_{active} \cdot d + P_{sleep} \cdot (1-d) \\
P_{avg} &= \left(\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{leak}\right) \cdot d + V_{DD} I_{leak,sleep} \cdot (1-d)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Power savings ratio = $\dfrac{P_{avg,\text{no gating}} - P_{avg,\text{gated}}}{P_{avg,\text{no gating}}}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sleep Transistor Sizing

The sleep transistor must supply the maximum current $I_{max}$ demanded by the logic block during active mode while keeping the voltage drop $\Delta V$ across it small.

**Step 1:** In linear region, the current through the sleep transistor is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{ds} &= \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{gs} - V_{th})V_{ds} - \frac{V_{ds}^2}{2} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** For small $V_{ds} = \Delta V$ (the acceptable virtual rail drop), we approximate:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{ds} &\approx \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{gs} - V_{th}) \Delta V
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for width $W$ to deliver $I_{max}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{sleep} &= \frac{I_{max} \cdot L}{\mu_n C_{ox} (V_{gs} - V_{th}) \Delta V} \\
W_{sleep} &= \frac{I_{max}}{k_n \cdot V_{dsat}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}$ and $V_{dsat} = V_{gs} - V_{th}$.

**Step 4:** Rule of thumb: $W_{sleep} \approx (2 \text{ to } 3) \times W_{total,logic}$ for coarse-grained gating.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wake-up Energy and Latency

When the sleep transistor turns ON, the virtual supply rail (C<sub>block</sub>) must be charged from 0 to V<sub>DD</sub>.

**Step 1:** Stored energy on virtual rail capacitor:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{wake} &= \frac{1}{2} C_{block} V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** RC time constant of wake-up (R<sub>sleep</sub> is ON-resistance of sleep device):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{wake} &= R_{sleep} \cdot C_{block} \\
t_{90\%} &\approx 2.3 \, \tau_{wake} \quad \text{(for 90\% rail recovery)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Rush current at the instant of wake-up:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{rush,peak} &= \frac{V_{DD}}{R_{sleep}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This rush current causes IR-drop noise on the global supply grid. KTU questions often ask how to mitigate it — the answer is **staggered wake-up** (turning on sleep transistors gradually using daisy-chained control signals).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

A logic block has $C_{block} = 50$ pF, $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $I_{leak,active} = 100$ µA, $I_{leak,sleep} = 1$ µA, and operates with duty cycle $d = 0.2$.

**Step 1: Wake-up energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{wake} &= \frac{1}{2} (50 \times 10^{-12}) (1.2)^2 \\
E_{wake} &= 36 \text{ pJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Power savings with gating (assume $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f = 10$ mW active dynamic):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{active} &= 10 \text{ mW} + (1.2)(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 10.12 \text{ mW} \\
P_{sleep} &= (1.2)(1 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.2 \text{ µW} \\
P_{avg,gated} &= 10.12 \times 0.2 + 0.0012 \times 0.8 = 2.024 \text{ mW} \\
P_{avg,ungated} &= 10.12 \text{ mW} \\
\text{Savings} &= \frac{10.12 - 2.024}{10.12} = 80\%
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MTCMOS Power Gating Architecture (Header-Type)

The following block-level functional architecture shows the standard MTCMOS arrangement with a header (PMOS) sleep transistor, the gated logic block, the wake-up controller, and the retention register file.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VDD Global Supply Rail] --> B[Sleep Control Signal SLT]
    B --> C{PMOS Header Switch HS}
    C -->|Gate driven by SLT bar| D[Virtual VDD Rail VVDD]
    D --> E[Logic Block LB Standard Cells]
    E --> F[VSS Ground Rail]
    G[Wake Up Controller WUC] --> B
    H[Retention Register File RRF] --> E
    I[Daisy Chain Stagger Control DCS] --> G
    J[Isolation Clamp Cells ICC] --> E
    K[Sleep Mode Acknowledge SMA] --> G
    L[Power State Machine PSM] --> G
    M[Clock Gating Cell CGC] --> E
    A --> H
    F --> J
```

**Node-by-node functional description:**

- **VDD Global Supply Rail (A):** The chip-wide power distribution net.
- **Sleep Control Signal SLT (B):** Active-high digital signal driven by the power management unit.
- **PMOS Header Switch HS (C):** High-V<sub>t</sub> PMOS transistor that gates the supply. It is ON when SLT = 0 (active mode) and OFF when SLT = 1 (sleep mode).
- **Virtual VDD Rail VVDD (D):** The internal rail whose voltage collapses to near 0 V during sleep.
- **Logic Block LB (E):** The actual functional CMOS logic (low-V<sub>t</sub> for speed).
- **VSS Ground Rail (F):** Common return path.
- **Wake Up Controller WUC (G):** Sequences the power-up and asserts readiness.
- **Retention Register File RRF (H):** Always-ON (or balloon) latches that preserve state during sleep.
- **Daisy Chain Stagger Control DCS (I):** Ensures sequential wake-up of multiple power domains to limit rush current.
- **Isolation Clamp Cells ICC (J):** Prevent floating inputs of always-ON blocks receiving signals from the gated block.
- **Sleep Mode Acknowledge SMA (K):** Handshake signal to the system controller.
- **Power State Machine PSM (L):** Top-level finite state machine governing power modes.
- **Clock Gating Cell CGC (M):** Must be coordinated with power gating to prevent data loss.

## 4.2 Sleep Mode State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACTIVE
    ACTIVE --> DROWSY: drowsy signal asserted
    DROWSY --> SHUTDOWN: sleep signal asserted
    SHUTDOWN --> WAKEUP: wakeup request received
    WAKEUP --> DROWSY: rail reaches 0.9 VDD
    DROWSY --> ACTIVE: full performance required
    SHUTDOWN --> [*]: power off request
```

## 4.3 Footer-Type (NMOS) Power Gating Variant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[VDD Supply] --> Q[Logic Block LB]
    Q --> R{NMOS Footer FS}
    R --> S[VSS Ground]
    T[Sleep Signal SLT] --> R
    U[Bias Generator BG] --> R
    V[Body Bias Controller BBC] --> R
    P --> W[Retention Memory RM]
    W --> Q
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define power gating. List any two advantages of power gating in CMOS VLSI circuits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Power gating is a low-power design technique used to reduce standby (leakage) power dissipation by disconnecting a circuit block from the power supply rail (V<sub>DD</sub>) or the ground rail (V<sub>SS</sub>) using a high-threshold-voltage "sleep transistor" (PMOS header or NMOS footer). The technique is also called MTCMOS (Multi-Threshold CMOS).

**Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Drastic leakage reduction:** Sub-threshold and gate leakage currents are reduced by 2 to 4 orders of magnitude during sleep mode, making it highly effective in nanometer technologies.
2. **Compatibility with existing designs:** Power gating can be introduced at the synthesis or physical design stage without altering the functional logic of the block.

*(Acceptable bonus point: It enables hierarchical power management and supports multiple voltage domains.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is a sleep transistor? Differentiate between a header and a footer sleep transistor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sleep Transistor (1 mark):** A sleep transistor is a high-V<sub>th</sub> transistor (PMOS or NMOS) inserted between the actual power rail and the logic block to cut off leakage current when the block is idle.

**Header vs Footer (2 marks — 1 each):**

| Aspect | Header (PMOS) | Footer (NMOS) |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Position | Between V<sub>DD</sub> and virtual V<sub>DD</sub> | Between virtual V<sub>SS</sub> and V<sub>SS</sub> |
| ON condition | Gate = 0 (active mode) | Gate = 1 (active mode) |
| Body effect | Source-to-body voltage rises during operation (mild body effect) | No body effect — stronger drive per unit width |
| Drive strength | Lower (PMOS mobility ~2.5× less than NMOS) | Higher current capability |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the concept of power gating with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of sleep transistors in reducing static power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the average power dissipation of a power-gated circuit block. Compute the percentage power saving for a block with active power 15 mW, sleep power 50 µW, and duty cycle 0.15. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Power gating is a leakage reduction technique wherein high-V<sub>t</sub> sleep transistors are inserted in series with the supply rails of CMOS logic blocks. The logic uses low-V<sub>t</sub> transistors for speed; the sleep transistors use high-V<sub>t</sub> to minimize their own leakage. When the block is idle, the sleep transistor is switched OFF, breaking the leakage path.

**Block Diagram description (2 marks):**

The architecture consists of:
- Global V<sub>DD</sub> rail feeding the source of the PMOS sleep transistor.
- Drain of the sleep transistor forms the *virtual V<sub>DD</sub>* rail.
- Virtual V<sub>DD</sub> powers the standard-cell logic block.
- A sleep control signal drives the gate of the sleep transistor.
- A wake-up controller sequences the power-up.
- Retention registers preserve state during sleep.
- Isolation cells prevent floating signals on always-ON logic.

**Role of sleep transistor (3 marks):**
1. **Leakage suppression:** High-V<sub>t</sub> ensures I<sub>sub</sub> through the sleep transistor is exponentially lower than in the logic.
2. **Stack effect:** When the sleep transistor is OFF, the stacked off transistors in the logic block experience a self-reverse-bias (DIBL effect) that further reduces leakage.
3. **Virtual rail collapse:** During sleep, the virtual V<sub>DD</sub> decays to a value that reverse-biases internal junctions, multiplying leakage reduction.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Derivation 2 in Section 3.2 above.

$$P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot d + P_{sleep} \cdot (1-d)$$

**Numerical computation (3 marks):**

Given: P<sub>active</sub> = 15 mW, P<sub>sleep</sub> = 50 µW = 0.05 mW, d = 0.15.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{avg,gated} &= 15 \times 0.15 + 0.05 \times 0.85 \\
P_{avg,gated} &= 2.25 + 0.0425 = 2.2925 \text{ mW} \\
P_{avg,ungated} &= 15 \text{ mW} \\
\text{Savings} &= \frac{15 - 2.2925}{15} \times 100 = 84.72\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the different power gating techniques: fine-grained, coarse-grained, and cluster-based. Compare their area and wake-up latency trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the challenges of power gating such as rush current, wake-up latency, and state retention. Suggest suitable design techniques to mitigate them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Fine-Grained Power Gating (2.5 marks):**
- A small sleep transistor is embedded inside every standard cell (e.g., every flip-flop or gate).
- **Advantage:** Maximum control granularity; can shut down individual cells.
- **Disadvantage:** Large area overhead (15–25% area increase); complex sleep signal routing.
- **Wake-up latency:** Very low (sub-ns) because each transistor is small.

**Coarse-Grained Power Gating (2.5 marks):**
- A single large sleep transistor gates an entire functional block (e.g., a whole DSP core).
- **Advantage:** Minimal area overhead; simple control.
- **Disadvantage:** Larger rush current at wake-up; longer wake-up latency.
- **Wake-up latency:** 10–100 ns depending on block size.

**Cluster-Based (2 marks):**
- Block is partitioned into clusters; each cluster has its own sleep transistor.
- Strikes a balance between area, flexibility, and rush current control.
- **Wake-up latency:** Moderate (5–30 ns).

**Comparison table (inserted within the explanation):**

| Metric | Fine-Grained | Cluster-Based | Coarse-Grained |
|--------|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| Area overhead | High | Medium | Low |
| Wake-up latency | Low | Medium | High |
| Rush current | Low | Medium | High |
| Control complexity | High | Medium | Low |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Challenge 1 — Rush Current (2 marks):** When the sleep transistor turns ON, the entire block capacitance charges simultaneously, causing a large inrush current that disturbs the global V<sub>DD</sub> (IR drop). *Mitigation:* Use daisy-chained staggered wake-up where sleep transistors in different clusters are turned ON with a small delay between them (typically 1–5 ns).

**Challenge 2 — Wake-up Latency (2 marks):** The logic block is unusable until the virtual rail reaches ~90% of V<sub>DD</sub>. *Mitigation:* (i) Increase sleep transistor width (sacrificing area); (ii) Use forward body bias on the sleep transistor to lower V<sub>th</sub> during wake-up; (iii) Use multi-stage wake-up where the rail is pre-charged to 70% quickly, then ramped to 100%.

**Challenge 3 — State Retention (2 marks):** Flip-flops lose data when the virtual rail collapses. *Mitigation:* Use *retention registers* — special flip-flops that are always powered by a separate, smaller (balloon) supply and can save/restore the main register state. Alternative: Move critical state to software-readable shadow registers in always-ON memory.

**Synthesis statement (1 mark):** Modern SoCs combine all three mitigation techniques — staggered wake-up, retention registers, and adaptive body bias — to make power gating transparent to the application.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing power gating with clock gating:** Many students interchange these in exams. *Clock gating* reduces *dynamic* power by stopping transitions; *power gating* reduces *static* (leakage) power by cutting the supply.
> 2. **Forgetting the rush-current mitigation:** A 14-mark question on power gating challenges that does *not* mention rush current or staggered wake-up will lose at least 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping units in derivations:** KTU examiners explicitly allocate 0.5–1 mark for "stating units" in numerical problems. Always write Watts, Volts, Farads, etc.
> 4. **Not drawing the virtual rail:** When asked for a diagram, students often draw only the logic block. The *virtual V<sub>DD</sub>* rail is a KTU-favorite marking point worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting isolation cells:** Power gating answers that omit isolation/level-shifter cells on cross-domain signals lose marks in the "challenges" section.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power gating = leakage killer** for nanometer CMOS; implemented via MTCMOS with high-V<sub>t</sub> sleep transistors.
- **Header (PMOS) vs Footer (NMOS):** NMOS footers give better drive per unit area; PMOS headers offer noise isolation.
- **Three flavors:** Fine-grained, coarse-grained, cluster-based — trade area vs flexibility vs wake-up latency.
- **Core formula:** $P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot d + P_{sleep} \cdot (1-d)$ — the **duty cycle** is the master knob for power savings.
- **Sleep transistor sizing:** $W_{sleep} = \dfrac{I_{max}}{k_n \cdot V_{dsat}}$ — larger W means faster wake-up but more area.
- **Wake-up energy:** $E_{wake} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{block} V_{DD}^2$ — must be amortized over the sleep duration to be worthwhile.
- **Three big challenges:** rush current, wake-up latency, state retention — all have well-known mitigation techniques (staggered wake-up, balloon latches, isolation cells).
- **KTU board favorite:** Questions almost always pair power gating with a *numerical duty-cycle problem* and ask for state-retention discussion.
- **Never confuse:** Power gating ≠ Clock gating. Power gating addresses *leakage*; clock gating addresses *dynamic switching*.
- **Modern context:** Always-ON domains, retention flops, and multi-rail chips in 28 nm, 16 nm, 7 nm FinFET nodes are all built upon these fundamentals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clock Gating
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clock Gating

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Gating** is a sequential and combinational dynamic power-management technique in synchronous digital CMOS VLSI circuits wherein the clock signal supplied to a sequential element (flip-flop, register, latch, or an entire functional block) is *conditionally* disabled (gated) when the element is not required to perform a useful state transition. By suppressing unnecessary clock transitions, the technique directly reduces the **switching (dynamic) capacitance charging activity** of the local clock-distribution network and the internal nodes of the gated register, thereby lowering the overall **dynamic power dissipation** of the chip.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Clock Gating is classified as a **Dynamic Power Reduction** technique (as opposed to static techniques such as voltage scaling, threshold modulation, or clock-frequency reduction). It is one of the *most widely deployed* power-optimization strategies in industry, used universally in ARM Cortex, Intel Core, Qualcomm Snapdragon, and almost every modern ASIC/SoC.

The fundamental power equation that Clock Gating targets is:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **switching activity factor** of the node, $C_L$ is the **load capacitance**, $V_{DD}$ is the **supply voltage**, and $f_{clk}$ is the **operating clock frequency**. Clock Gating effectively drives $\alpha \to 0$ for the gated nodes during idle periods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Clock Gating):** Students are expected to understand *why* clock toggling dominates power, the difference between combinational (latch-free) and sequential (latch-based) gating styles, the issues of **glitches, runt pulses, and clock skew**, and the role of dedicated clock-gating cells (e.g., integrated clock-gating, **ICG**, library cells).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine a large corporate office building at night. The lights, fans, and computers in *every* cubicle are still drawing electricity even though only 3 employees are working overtime. The naive solution would be to switch off the *entire building's* main supply — but that would also kill the lights in the 3 active cubicles.

A smarter solution: install a **smart switch** at each cubicle that detects whether anyone is sitting there. If the cubicle is empty, the switch *automatically cuts off* the power to that cubicle's lights, fan, and computer, while the rest of the building remains fully powered. The smart switch itself consumes a tiny amount of power, but the savings are massive because one office worker (a flip-flop) was previously drawing power 24/7 even when doing nothing useful.

**The Mapping to VLSI:**
- *Building main supply*  →  Global Clock Network ($f_{clk}$)
- *Cubicle*  →  Flip-Flop / Register
- *Smart switch*  →  Clock-Gating Logic (AND/OR gate + optional latch)
- *Empty cubicle*  →  Idle sequential state (no useful data transition)
- *Office worker*  →  Active data computation

When a flip-flop's data input equals its current output (i.e., $D = Q$), toggling the clock is *useless* — the state does not change, yet energy is wasted charging and discharging internal capacitances every cycle. Clock Gating identifies such "wasted cycles" and freezes the clock to that flop, saving the power that would have been spent on a no-op transition.

## 1.3 Why Clock Gating Matters — Quantitative Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **clock distribution network** in a synchronous VLSI chip is one of the largest single consumers of dynamic power. Industry data consistently shows:
> - **30 % – 50 %** of the *total* dynamic power of a high-performance ASIC is dissipated by the clock tree and the clock pins of flip-flops.
> - The clock toggles **every cycle** at the highest activity factor ($\alpha \approx 1$) on the chip.
> - In modern billion-transistor SoCs, the clock network may contain **millions of buffers and inverters**, each toggling at $f_{clk}$.

This makes the clock network the *single largest "fixed-cost" power consumer* in the design. Clock Gating is therefore the most effective first-line attack on dynamic power, often yielding **20 % – 70 %** power reduction in real designs depending on the workload's idle profile.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard CMOS process and design parameters are referenced throughout this note:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (90 nm) | Typical Value (45 nm) | Typical Value (16 nm) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.8 | V |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_{th}$ | 0.25 | 0.30 | 0.35 | V |
| Gate Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox}$ | 8.5 | 12 | 18 | fF/µm² |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | 100 – 1000 | 500 – 2000 | 1000 – 4000 | MHz |
| Junction Temperature | $T_j$ | 85 – 125 | 85 – 105 | 65 – 95 | °C |
| Activity Factor (clock) | $\alpha_{clk}$ | ≈ 1.0 | ≈ 1.0 | ≈ 1.0 | — |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Clock Power Problem — Why Bother Gating?

A synchronous digital circuit operates under a global clock that drives the **clock pin (CLK)** of every flip-flop. In a conventional design:

1. **Every** flip-flop receives a clock edge on every cycle.
2. The clock edge causes the flip-flop's **internal master and slave latches** to switch state (charge and discharge internal node capacitances).
3. The clock tree buffers (H-tree or mesh) drive the *enormous* total capacitance of all clock pins, clock buffers, and clock routing wires.
4. The **data input ($D$)** of the flip-flop may be the *same* as the current output ($Q$), making the transition a "wasted" one from a functional standpoint, yet energy is still spent.

The dynamic power dissipated in any node of capacitance $C$ switching at activity $\alpha$ at frequency $f$ is:

$$P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$$

The clock node has $\alpha \approx 1$ (worst case), and the global clock network capacitance $C_{clk}$ is one of the *largest* on the chip. Hence $P_{clk}$ is dominant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why does clock gating work?", do **not** just write "it reduces dynamic power." The examiner expects you to mention that it specifically reduces the **switching activity factor $\alpha_{clk}$** from its nominal value of ≈ 1 down to a workload-dependent value that reflects only the *useful* state-changing cycles.

## 2.2 Classification of Clock-Gating Styles

KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes between two major implementation styles:

### 2.2.1 Latch-Free (Combinational) Clock Gating

- The enable signal $EN$ is directly AND-ed (or OR-ed) with the clock.
- Simplest possible implementation: a single **AND gate** inserted between the global clock and the local clock pin.

**Operation:**
- When $EN = 1$: $GCLK = CLK$  (active clock passes through)
- When $EN = 0$: $GCLK = 0$  (clock suppressed, output held low)

**Problem — Runt Pulses & Glitches:**
- AND-gating of an active-high clock with an asynchronous enable signal can produce **narrow runt pulses** at the falling edge of $CLK$ if $EN$ transitions near the clock edge. These runt pulses may be **shorter than the flip-flop's minimum pulse-width** requirement, causing metastability and functional failure.
- Also, $EN$ changing close to the clock edge can cause **glitches** on the gated clock that propagate as spurious clock edges to the downstream flops.

### 2.2.2 Latch-Based (Sequential) Clock Gating

- The enable signal $EN$ is **latched** by a negative-level-sensitive (or positive-level-sensitive) latch so that $EN$ is *guaranteed* to be stable during the active phase of the clock.
- The latched enable, $EN_{L}$, is then AND-ed with $CLK$ to produce $GCLK$.

**Operation (negative-level-sensitive latch, also called "seamless" or "Master-Slave" style):**
- When $CLK = 1$ (high): latch is **transparent** — $EN$ passes through to the AND gate.
- When $CLK = 0$ (low): latch is **opaque** — the last value of $EN$ before the falling clock edge is held and applied to the AND gate, ensuring $GCLK$ is glitch-free during the high phase of $CLK$.

This is the **industry-standard** clock-gating cell (also called **Integrated Clock Gate, ICG**, or sometimes **Clock-Gating Cell, CGC**), and every standard-cell library from Synopsys, Cadence, TSMC, and Intel contains pre-characterized versions.

### 2.2.3 XOR-Based / Data-Driven Clock Gating (Advanced)

A more aggressive variant uses XOR-comparison of the data input $D$ with the current output $Q$ to generate the enable:

$$EN = D \oplus Q$$

The flip-flop is clocked *only* when $D \neq Q$. This is rarely used in practice because:
- It requires reading the *current* $Q$ (creates a feedback timing loop).
- The savings are small compared to coarse block-level gating.
- It adds logic depth to the clock path.

KTU syllabus mentions this briefly under "data-driven clock gating" or "auto-gating".

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power of a node | Core equation for power |
| 2 | $P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$ | Clock network power | Clock-power dominance |
| 3 | $E_{clk} = C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2}$ | Energy per clock transition | Per-cycle clock energy |
| 4 | $P_{gated} = \alpha_{gated} \, C_{clk} \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f_{clk}$ | Gated clock power | After clock-gating |
| 5 | $S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha_{gated}}{\alpha_{orig}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Power-savings percentage | KTU numerical problems |
| 6 | $\alpha_{orig} \approx 1$ (always) | Original clock activity | Reference value |
| 7 | $\alpha_{gated} = \dfrac{N_{active}}{N_{total}}$ | Gated activity ratio | Idle vs active cycles |
| 8 | $f_{max,\,gated} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk} + t_{AND} + t_{setup}}$ | Max frequency with gating | Timing-budget impact |
| 9 | $P_{ICG} = C_{ICG} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{gating}$ | Power overhead of the ICG cell | Net-savings calculation |
| 10 | $P_{net} = P_{orig} - P_{gated} - P_{ICG}$ | Net power saved | Optimization decision |
| 11 | $N_{gates} \approx N_{flops} \cdot p_{idle}$ | Number of flops gated | Architectural analysis |
| 12 | $\Delta t_{skew} \approx t_{pd,\,ICG} - t_{pd,\,buf}$ | Added clock skew | Timing closure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold constants to memorize for KTU:** $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V (nominal), $\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$ (ungated), $P_{clk} / P_{total} \approx 30\% - 50\%$ (typical industry range), $P_{short\,circuit} \approx 10\% - 20\%$ of $P_{dyn}$ (often neglected unless asked).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Clock gating is the *backbone* of nearly every power-aware design in industry:

1. **Microprocessors (ARM, Intel, AMD, RISC-V cores):** Each functional unit (ALU, FPU, branch predictor, cache controller) is gated when not in use. The Cortex-A series reports **40 – 60 %** dynamic power savings from aggressive clock gating.
2. **Mobile SoCs (Snapdragon, Exynos, MediaTek Dimensity):** Multiple clock domains, each with its own clock-gating controller, allowing fine-grained gating per IP block.
3. **Networking ASICs (Cisco, Broadcom, Marvell):** Packet-processing pipelines gate entire packet queues during idle periods.
4. **FPGA fabrics (Xilinx, Intel/Altera):** Modern FPGAs include "clock enables" on every register and dedicated hardware clock buffers with built-in gating (BUFGCE primitive in Xilinx).
5. **Memory subsystems:** SDRAM controllers gate the clock to inactive banks/ranks.
6. **IoT / ultra-low-power MCUs (ARM Cortex-M0+, MSP430):** Use clock gating as the *primary* low-power mechanism (in conjunction with clock frequency scaling).

## 2.5 Clock Gating vs. Related Techniques

| Technique | Targets | Mechanism | Granularity | Overhead |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Clock Gating** | Dynamic power | Disable clock | Per-flop / per-block | Low (1 ICG per group) |
| Clock Frequency Scaling | Dynamic power | Reduce $f_{clk}$ | Global | Medium (PLL/DCM) |
| Voltage Scaling (DVS) | Dynamic + static | Reduce $V_{DD}$ | Global / per-domain | High (regulators) |
| Power Gating | Static (leakage) power | Cut $V_{DD}$ via sleep transistor | Per-block | High (retention flops) |
| Multi-Vt (MVT) | Static power | Use high-$V_t$ cells | Per-cell | Low (process choice) |
| Operand Isolation | Dynamic power | Freeze inputs to idle block | Per-block | Medium (extra muxes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Clock Gating** (reduces *dynamic* power by suppressing clock transitions) with **Power Gating** (reduces *static/leakage* power by cutting $V_{DD}$ via a header/footer transistor). The two are *complementary* and often used together, but they target *different* power components. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Power Savings from Clock Gating

**Given:** A synchronous digital block contains $N_{FF}$ flip-flops, each with a clock-pin capacitance $C_{cp}$. The global clock tree has a total capacitance $C_{tree}$ (including buffers, wires, and clock pins of ungated flops). The block is active in only $N_{active}$ out of every $N_{total}$ clock cycles, with $N_{idle} = N_{total} - N_{active}$.

**Find:** The fractional power saved by clock-gating this block.

---

### Step 1 — Original (ungated) dynamic power of the clock network

The clock network toggles on every single cycle, so the activity factor $\alpha_{orig} = 1$.

The total effective clock capacitance seen by the supply is:

$$C_{clk,\,tot} = C_{tree} + N_{FF} \cdot C_{cp}$$

The original (ungated) dynamic power dissipated in the clock network is:

$$P_{orig} = \alpha_{orig} \cdot C_{clk,\,tot} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

Since $\alpha_{orig} = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$P_{orig} = C_{clk,\,tot} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

---

### Step 2 — Gated dynamic power of the clock network

After clock gating, the gated portion of the clock network toggles only during the *active* cycles. Define the effective activity factor of the gated clock as:

$$\alpha_{gated} = \frac{N_{active}}{N_{total}}$$

The gated block's contribution to the clock power becomes:

$$P_{gated} = \alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{clk,\,block} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

where $C_{clk,\,block}$ is the total clock capacitance of the gated block (its clock pins + the ICG cell + the gated clock routing).

---

### Step 3 — Power saved (gross)

The gross power saved by clock gating is the difference between the original and gated clock powers:

$$P_{saved} = P_{orig} - P_{gated} = C_{clk,\,tot} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} - \alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{clk,\,block} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

Factoring out the common term $V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$:

$$P_{saved} = V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} \left[ C_{clk,\,tot} - \alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{clk,\,block} \right]$$

---

### Step 4 — Power overhead of the ICG cell

The ICG cell itself consumes power, both static (leakage) and dynamic (its own clocked latch + AND gate switch). The dynamic overhead is:

$$P_{ICG} = \alpha_{ICG} \cdot C_{ICG} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{gating}$$

where $C_{ICG}$ is the total switched capacitance of the ICG cell, and $\alpha_{ICG} \cdot f_{gating}$ is the rate at which the ICG's internal nodes toggle (typically equal to $f_{clk}$ if the latch is updated every cycle, but can be lower if $EN$ is stable).

> **Conversion Logic:** The ICG cell must be factored in because it is an *additional* load on the clock tree. Without this overhead, the savings calculation would be unrealistically optimistic.

---

### Step 5 — Net power savings

$$P_{net} = P_{saved} - P_{ICG} = V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} \left[ C_{clk,\,tot} - \alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{clk,\,block} - C_{ICG} \right]$$

---

### Step 6 — Fractional savings (the KTU-favorite quantity)

$$S_{\%} = \frac{P_{net}}{P_{orig}} \times 100\% = \left( 1 - \frac{\alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{clk,\,block} + C_{ICG}}{C_{clk,\,tot}} \right) \times 100\%$$

If $C_{ICG} \ll C_{clk,\,block}$ (typical, because the ICG is small relative to the block) and $\alpha_{gated} \ll 1$ (block is mostly idle), the savings approach:

$$S_{\%} \approx \left( 1 - \alpha_{gated} \cdot \frac{C_{clk,\,block}}{C_{clk,\,tot}} \right) \times 100\%$$

---

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem statement:** A 32-bit register file contains $N_{FF} = 32 \times 8 = 256$ flip-flops. Each flop has a clock-pin capacitance of $C_{cp} = 5$ fF. The global clock tree delivers the clock at $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V. The block is active only 20 % of the cycles ($\alpha_{gated} = 0.2$). The ICG cell adds $C_{ICG} = 20$ fF of clock capacitance. The ungated clock tree has $C_{tree} = 50$ pF (representing the rest of the chip). Find (a) original clock power, (b) net power saved.

**Step (a) — Original clock power:**

$$C_{clk,\,tot} = C_{tree} + N_{FF} \cdot C_{cp} = 50 \text{ pF} + 256 \times 5 \text{ fF}$$

$$= 50\,000 \text{ fF} + 1\,280 \text{ fF} = 51\,280 \text{ fF} = 51.28 \text{ pF}$$

$$P_{orig} = C_{clk,\,tot} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} = 51.28 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0^2 \times 500 \times 10^6$$

$$= 51.28 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{8} = 25.64 \times 10^{-3} = 25.64 \text{ mW}$$

**Step (b) — Net power saved:**

$$P_{saved} = V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} \cdot \left[ C_{clk,\,block} - \alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{clk,\,block} \right]$$

where $C_{clk,\,block} = N_{FF} \cdot C_{cp} = 256 \times 5 \text{ fF} = 1.28 \text{ pF}$.

$$= 1.0^2 \times 500 \times 10^6 \times \left[ 1.28 \times 10^{-12} - 0.2 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-12} \right]$$

$$= 5 \times 10^{8} \times 1.024 \times 10^{-12} = 5.12 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 0.512 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{ICG} = \alpha_{ICG} \cdot C_{ICG} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} \approx 1 \times 20 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0 \times 500 \times 10^6 = 10 \text{ µW} = 0.01 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{net} = P_{saved} - P_{ICG} = 0.512 - 0.01 = 0.502 \text{ mW}$$

**Fractional savings:**

$$S_{\%} = \frac{0.502}{25.64} \times 100\% \approx 1.96\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The savings look small *in absolute terms* but remember: this is *only* the savings from clock-gating the register file. In a real SoC with hundreds of such blocks, cumulative savings routinely exceed **30 – 50 %** of the total dynamic power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Number of Flops Justifying an ICG Cell

**Question:** When is it economically worthwhile to add an ICG cell to gate a group of $N$ flip-flops?

The ICG cell itself has a fixed cost $C_{ICG}$ (e.g., 20 fF). The savings per gated flop is $(1 - \alpha_{gated}) \cdot C_{cp}$. The break-even point is when the savings *equal* the overhead:

$$(1 - \alpha_{gated}) \cdot N \cdot C_{cp} = C_{ICG}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{C_{ICG}}{(1 - \alpha_{gated}) \cdot C_{cp}}$$

**Plug in numbers:** $C_{ICG} = 20$ fF, $C_{cp} = 5$ fF, $\alpha_{gated} = 0.2$ (active 20 % of time):

$$N_{min} = \frac{20}{(1 - 0.2) \times 5} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ flip-flops}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This explains *why* ICG cells are always inserted to gate *groups* of flops (typically 8 to 64) rather than individual flops — the ICG overhead is amortized.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trade-Offs Across Gating Styles

| Gating Style | Power Savings | Glitch-Free? | Area Overhead | Timing Impact | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Latch-free AND gate | High (theoretical) | **No** — runt pulses possible | 1 AND gate | Minimal | Asynchronous, low-frequency designs only |
| Latch-free OR gate | High (theoretical) | **No** — runt pulses possible | 1 OR gate | Minimal | Active-low clock domains |
| Latch-based (ICG) | High (practical) | **Yes** — guaranteed glitch-free | 1 latch + 1 AND | Small skew penalty | **All modern synchronous designs** |
| XOR data-driven | Very high (theoretical) | Conditional | 1 XOR + control | Larger | Speculative, low-fanout flops |
| Hierarchy / Block-level | Moderate | **Yes** | Clock-gating controller | Small | Coarse power-down modes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Latch-Based (Sequential) Clock Gating

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the standard, glitch-free, latch-based clock-gating cell (ICG) used in every modern ASIC.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clkNode[CLK]:::clk
    enNode[EN]:::en
    latchNode[Negative Level Sensitive Latch]:::latch
    andNode[AND Gate]:::and
    gclkNode[GCLK]:::gclk
    flopNode[Flip Flop 1]:::flop
    flop2Node[Flip Flop 2]:::flop
    flop3Node[Flip Flop N]:::flop

    clkNode -->|active high phase| latchNode
    enNode -->|data| latchNode
    latchNode -->|latched EN| andNode
    clkNode -->|passes through| andNode
    andNode -->|gated clock| gclkNode
    gclkNode --> flopNode
    gclkNode --> flop2Node
    gclkNode --> flop3Node

    classDef clk fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef latch fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef and fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gclk fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flop fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **EN** (enable) signal enters a **negative-level-sensitive latch**.
- When $CLK$ is HIGH (active phase), the latch is **transparent** — $EN$ flows through.
- When $CLK$ falls LOW, the latch **freezes** the last value of $EN$, holding it constant throughout the low phase of $CLK$ and the next rising edge.
- The latched enable is then **AND-ed** with $CLK$ to produce the **GCLK** (gated clock).
- The result: $GCLK$ is **glitch-free** because the AND gate's second input cannot change while $CLK$ is HIGH.

---

## 4.2 Architecture Flow — Latch-Free (Combinational) Clock Gating

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clkIn[Global CLK]:::clk
    enSig[Enable EN]:::en
    andBasic[AND Gate]:::and
    gclkOut[Gated CLK]:::gclk
    flopA[FF 1]:::flop
    flopB[FF 2]:::flop
    flopC[FF N]:::flop

    clkIn --> andBasic
    enSig --> andBasic
    andBasic --> gclkOut
    gclkOut --> flopA
    gclkOut --> flopB
    gclkOut --> flopC

    classDef clk fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef and fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gclk fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flop fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The simplest possible clock gating. Drawback: any glitch or transition on $EN$ near a $CLK$ edge passes through the AND gate and appears on $GCLK$, possibly creating a **runt pulse** shorter than the flip-flop's minimum pulse-width. This can cause **metastability** — a hard-to-debug functional failure.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Hierarchical / Block-Level Clock Gating

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootClk[Root CLK Oscillator]:::clk
    pllBlock[PLL Block]:::pll
    ctrlBlock[Power Management Controller]:::ctrl
    cgcA[ICG Cell A]:::and
    cgcB[ICG Cell B]:::and
    cgcC[ICG Cell C]:::and
    blockA[ALU Block]:::flop
    blockB[FPU Block]:::flop
    blockC[Cache Controller Block]:::flop

    rootClk --> pllBlock
    pllBlock -->|multiplied CLK| cgcA
    pllBlock -->|multiplied CLK| cgcB
    pllBlock -->|multiplied CLK| cgcC
    ctrlBlock -->|EN A| cgcA
    ctrlBlock -->|EN B| cgcB
    ctrlBlock -->|EN C| cgcC
    cgcA --> blockA
    cgcB --> blockB
    cgcC --> blockC

    classDef clk fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pll fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef and fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flop fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A **Power Management Controller** generates one enable signal per block. The ICG cells are inserted *after* the PLL (phase-locked loop) so that the clock tree sees clean, glitch-free clock transitions. Each block can be independently gated based on the system's workload profile — e.g., the FPU is gated when no floating-point instruction is in flight.

---

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Glitch-Free Operation of Latch-Based ICG

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as CLK
    participant EN as EN
    participant LATCH as Latched EN
    participant GCLK as GCLK (output)
    Note over CLK,EN: Latch transparent when CLK = 1
    Note over CLK,EN: Latch opaque when CLK = 0
    CLK->>LATCH: 0 → 1 (latch opens)
    EN->>LATCH: 1 (allowed through)
    LATCH->>GCLK: GCLK follows CLK (EN=1)
    CLK->>LATCH: 1 → 0 (latch freezes)
    LATCH->>GCLK: Latched EN = 1, GCLK stays 0
    Note over EN: EN toggles 1→0 here, but latch is OPAQUE
    EN->>LATCH: 0 (BLOCKED, latch holds previous value)
    LATCH->>GCLK: GCLK remains 0 (glitch-free)
    CLK->>LATCH: 0 → 1 (latch opens, new EN=0 passes)
    LATCH->>GCLK: GCLK = 0 (clock suppressed)
```

**Reading the timing diagram:** The latched enable is **guaranteed constant during the high phase of CLK**, which is precisely when the AND gate is "open" to clock edges. This eliminates the runt-pulse hazard inherent in the latch-free design.
