# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** When answering clock-gating questions in the KTU Board Exam, students *frequently* lose marks for the following reasons. **Read carefully before writing your answer.**
>
> 1. **Confusing clock gating with power gating.** Clock gating → *dynamic* power. Power gating → *static/leakage* power. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the ICG overhead.** A "naive" answer that says "clock gating saves $X$ milliwatts" without subtracting the ICG cell's own power overhead is considered incomplete and loses 1 – 2 marks.
> 3. **Drawing the wrong schematic.** A latch-free AND gate is *not* glitch-free. If the question asks for "glitch-free clock gating," you **must** draw the latch-based ICG cell. Drawing the wrong cell loses up to 4 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "why" of the latch.** Examiners expect you to explain *why* the negative-level-sensitive latch is needed (to hold $EN$ stable during the active clock phase).
> 5. **No mention of clock skew.** Inserting an ICG cell adds latency to the clock path. This can increase clock skew. A good answer mentions this and any re-balancing of the clock tree.
> 6. **Forgetting the formula.** A 7-mark numerical problem without a properly written $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ formula at the start is marked down.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define clock gating. Why is it considered an effective dynamic power-reduction technique in modern VLSI designs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Clock gating is a low-power VLSI technique in which the clock signal supplied to a sequential element (flip-flop, latch, register file, or functional block) is conditionally disabled when the element is not required to perform a useful state transition. It is implemented by AND-ing (or OR-ing) the global clock with a *gating enable* signal — typically via a dedicated clock-gating cell (ICG) that includes a level-sensitive latch for glitch-free operation.

**Why effective (1.5 marks):**
1. The clock distribution network consumes **30 % – 50 %** of the *total* dynamic power in a typical synchronous ASIC, because it switches at the maximum activity factor $\alpha \approx 1$ on every cycle.
2. By suppressing the clock to idle blocks, clock gating drives the effective switching activity factor $\alpha$ of the gated clock network toward **zero** during idle periods, directly reducing $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{\,2} f_{clk}$.
3. It is **non-intrusive** — does not require voltage scaling, frequency changes, or any modification of the data path. It can be inserted transparently in the clock tree.
4. It yields **20 % – 70 %** dynamic power reduction in real SoCs and is therefore the *first* low-power technique applied in virtually every commercial design.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between latch-free and latch-based (sequential) clock gating techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Latch-Free (Combinational) | Latch-Based (Sequential / ICG) |
|---|---|---|
| **Schematic** | Single AND/OR gate inserted in the clock path | Level-sensitive latch + AND/OR gate |
| **Enable signal** | Applied directly to the gating gate | First latched, then applied |
| **Glitch behavior** | **Susceptible** to glitches and runt pulses on $GCLK$ if $EN$ transitions near a clock edge | **Glitch-free** because the latch holds $EN$ constant during the active clock phase |
| **Reliability** | Lower; may cause metastability in flops | High; meets flip-flop minimum pulse-width spec |
| **Area overhead** | 1 gate | 1 latch + 1 gate |
| **Usage** | Rare; only in asynchronous or low-frequency designs | **Industry standard**; present in every standard-cell library |

**Key insight (1 mark):** The latch in the ICG cell **samples and holds the enable signal during the inactive phase of the clock**, ensuring that the gating input to the AND/OR gate is stable throughout the active clock phase. This eliminates the runt-pulse hazard and guarantees glitch-free gated clock transitions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 2]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the operation of the **latch-based (sequential) clock gating cell (ICG)**. With a neat block diagram, describe how it achieves glitch-free clock gating. Discuss the role of the negative-level-sensitive latch in ensuring correct operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A synchronous digital block consists of 1024 flip-flops, each with a clock-pin capacitance of 4 fF. The block is active for only 25 % of the clock cycles. The global clock tree (excluding the gated block) has a total capacitance of 30 pF. The clock frequency is 1 GHz and the supply voltage is 1.0 V. The ICG cell adds 25 fF of clock capacitance and toggles every cycle. Calculate:
   (i) The original dynamic clock power before gating.
   (ii) The net dynamic clock power saved by clock-gating this block.
   (iii) The percentage power savings achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Latch-Based ICG Operation (7 marks)

**Block diagram description (3 marks):** The latch-based clock-gating cell consists of two stages:
1. A **negative-level-sensitive latch** (transparent when $CLK = 1$, opaque when $CLK = 0$).
2. An **AND gate** (or OR gate, depending on the clock polarity).

The enable signal $EN$ feeds the data input of the latch. The output of the latch (the "latched enable") feeds one input of the AND gate; the other input is the global $CLK$. The AND gate's output is the **gated clock ($GCLK$)**, which drives the clock pins of the gated flip-flops.

**Operating principle (2 marks):**
- When $CLK$ is **HIGH** (active phase), the latch is **transparent** — any change on $EN$ propagates to the AND gate. However, since $CLK$ is already HIGH, the AND gate's output is simply equal to the latched $EN$ value, and a HIGH $EN$ allows the rest of the HIGH phase to pass through; a LOW $EN$ holds the AND output LOW.
- When $CLK$ falls **LOW** (inactive phase), the latch becomes **opaque**, *freezing* the value of $EN$ as it was just before the falling edge. This frozen value is held throughout the LOW phase and the **next rising edge** of $CLK$.
- Since the latched enable is **guaranteed stable** during the high phase of the next clock cycle, the AND gate's output ($GCLK$) is **glitch-free**. There is no risk of a runt pulse.

**Role of the negative-level-sensitive latch (2 marks):**
- The negative-level-sensitive latch is the **critical element** that distinguishes the ICG from a naive latch-free AND gate.
- It acts as a **"freeze" element**: it samples $EN$ only during the low phase of $CLK$ and holds that value throughout the subsequent high phase.
- This guarantees that the **enable input to the AND gate cannot change while $CLK$ is HIGH**, eliminating the glitch hazard.
- In other words, the latch **synchronizes** the asynchronous $EN$ to the clock domain *without* requiring an explicit flip-flop, and at a fraction of the area and power cost.

> **Note for the student:** Examiners may also ask why a *negative*-level-sensitive latch (transparent when $CLK=1$) is used rather than a *positive*-level-sensitive one. The reason: at the falling edge of $CLK$ (when the latch closes), the AND gate's output is already LOW, so transitioning the latched enable here does not cause a glitch on $GCLK$ (because the AND gate is "off" — it is producing 0 regardless of the latched enable). The next rising edge sees the stable latched enable, producing a clean clock.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N_{FF} = 1024$ flip-flops
- $C_{cp} = 4$ fF per flop clock pin
- $\alpha_{gated} = 0.25$ (active 25 % of cycles)
- $C_{tree} = 30$ pF (ungated clock tree)
- $f_{clk} = 1$ GHz $= 1 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V
- $C_{ICG} = 25$ fF, $\alpha_{ICG} = 1$

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

**(i) Original dynamic clock power (2 marks):**

$$C_{clk,\,tot} = C_{tree} + N_{FF} \cdot C_{cp} = 30 \text{ pF} + 1024 \times 4 \text{ fF}$$

$$= 30\,000 \text{ fF} + 4\,096 \text{ fF} = 34\,096 \text{ fF} = 34.096 \text{ pF}$$

$$P_{orig} = 1 \times 34.096 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0^2 \times 1 \times 10^9$$

$$= 34.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 34.10 \text{ mW}$$

**(ii) Net dynamic clock power saved (3 marks):**

The block's clock capacitance is:

$$C_{clk,\,block} = N_{FF} \cdot C_{cp} = 1024 \times 4 \text{ fF} = 4.096 \text{ pF}$$

Gross power saved (gating the block):

$$P_{saved,\,gross} = (1 - \alpha_{gated}) \cdot C_{clk,\,block} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$= (1 - 0.25) \times 4.096 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0 \times 1 \times 10^9$$

$$= 0.75 \times 4.096 \times 10^{-3} = 3.072 \text{ mW}$$

ICG overhead power:

$$P_{ICG} = \alpha_{ICG} \cdot C_{ICG} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk} = 1 \times 25 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0 \times 1 \times 10^9$$

$$= 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 0.025 \text{ mW}$$

Net power saved:

$$P_{net} = P_{saved,\,gross} - P_{ICG} = 3.072 - 0.025 = 3.047 \text{ mW}$$

**(iii) Percentage power savings (1 mark):**

$$S_{\%} = \frac{P_{net}}{P_{orig}} \times 100\% = \frac{3.047}{34.10} \times 100\% \approx 8.94\%$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The percentage savings is small here because the gated block is only a small fraction of the total clock capacitance. In a real chip, when *many* blocks are gated simultaneously, the cumulative savings approach 30 – 50 %.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2 — Internal Choice]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **latch-free (combinational) clock gating** circuit. Discuss the hazards (glitches, runt pulses) associated with this technique and explain how a latch-based ICG cell overcomes them. Compare the two styles in terms of power, area, timing, and reliability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 64-bit datapath register file in a mobile SoC contains 1024 flip-flops, each with $C_{cp} = 3$ fF. An ICG cell (latch + AND gate) with $C_{ICG} = 18$ fF gates this file. The clock frequency is 800 MHz, $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V. The file is read/written only 30 % of the time, and the ICG toggles its internal latch at 200 MHz. Compute:
   (i) The total clock power dissipated *by the gated block* (flip-flops + ICG) with and without clock gating.
   (ii) The minimum number of flip-flops that would justify inserting this ICG cell, assuming the block is fully idle ($\alpha_{gated} = 0$). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Latch-Free Clock Gating and ICG Comparison (7 marks)

**Latch-free operation (2 marks):**
The latch-free (combinational) clock gating circuit consists of a single **AND gate** (for active-high clocks) or **OR gate** (for active-low clocks) inserted between the global clock and the local clock pin. The *enable* signal $EN$ is connected to the second input of the gating gate.

- When $EN = 1$: $GCLK = CLK$ (clock passes through to the flops).
- When $EN = 0$: $GCLK = 0$ (clock is suppressed; flops hold their previous state).

**Hazards of latch-free gating (2 marks):**
1. **Runt pulses:** If $EN$ transitions from 1 → 0 (or 0 → 1) close to the *falling edge* of $CLK$, the AND gate may produce a *very narrow* output pulse — shorter than the flip-flop's minimum clock-pulse-width specification ($t_{pulse,\,min}$). This causes the flop to either fail to register the data (setup/hold violation) or enter a **metastable state**, producing unreliable outputs.
2. **Glitches:** If $EN$ is itself a combinational signal (derived from a logic expression), it may have **glitches** that are short-duration spikes. Each such glitch is *not* filtered, and is AND-ed with $CLK$, producing a spurious clock edge at the gated flops — equivalent to a runt pulse.
3. **Asynchronous enable:** Since $EN$ may change asynchronously to $CLK$, the gated clock is not guaranteed glitch-free.

**How the ICG cell overcomes these hazards (2 marks):**
The latch-based ICG cell inserts a **negative-level-sensitive latch** *before* the AND gate. This latch **samples $EN$ only during the LOW phase of $CLK$** and **holds that sampled value constant** throughout the entire HIGH phase of the next clock cycle. Because the AND gate's second input is *guaranteed stable* during the active clock phase, the output $GCLK$ is **inherently glitch-free**. Even if $EN$ is itself a glitchy combinational signal, the latch's sample-and-hold behaviour filters out glitches that occur during the LOW phase (and the HIGH phase is irrelevant because the latch is opaque).

**Comparison table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Latch-Free | Latch-Based (ICG) |
|---|---|---|
| Power | Marginally lower (no latch) | Slightly higher (latch toggles) |
| Area | 1 gate | 1 latch + 1 gate (~3× area) |
| Timing | Minimal latency | Adds latch delay; small skew penalty |
| Reliability | Glitches / runt pulses possible | Glitch-free, robust |
| Use | Asynchronous / educational | **Industry standard** |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N_{FF} = 1024$ flops
- $C_{cp} = 3$ fF
- $C_{ICG} = 18$ fF (latch + AND gate total)
- $f_{clk} = 800$ MHz
- $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V
- $\alpha_{gated} = 0.30$ (active 30 % of the time)
- $f_{latch} = 200$ MHz (ICG internal latch toggle rate)

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

**(i) Total clock power of the gated block — WITH and WITHOUT gating (4 marks):**

**Without clock gating (clock toggles every cycle, $\alpha = 1$):**

$$C_{block} = N_{FF} \cdot C_{cp} = 1024 \times 3 \text{ fF} = 3072 \text{ fF} = 3.072 \text{ pF}$$

$$P_{no\,gate} = 1 \times 3.072 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.9^2 \times 800 \times 10^6$$

$$= 3.072 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.81 \times 8 \times 10^8$$

$$= 3.072 \times 0.81 \times 8 \times 10^{-4} = 19.91 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} \approx 1.991 \text{ mW}$$

**With clock gating (block active only 30 % of cycles, plus ICG overhead):**

$$P_{block,\,gated} = \alpha_{gated} \cdot C_{block} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$= 0.30 \times 3.072 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.81 \times 8 \times 10^8$$

$$= 0.30 \times 1.991 \text{ mW} = 0.597 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{ICG} = C_{ICG} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f_{latch} = 18 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.81 \times 200 \times 10^6$$

$$= 18 \times 0.81 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} = 2.916 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} \approx 2.92 \text{ µW}$$

$$P_{total,\,gated} = P_{block,\,gated} + P_{ICG} = 0.597 \text{ mW} + 0.00292 \text{ mW} \approx 0.600 \text{ mW}$$

**(ii) Minimum number of flops to justify the ICG cell (2 marks):**

For a block that is *fully idle* ($\alpha_{gated} = 0$), the break-even condition is:

$$(1 - \alpha_{gated}) \cdot N \cdot C_{cp} = C_{ICG}$$

With $\alpha_{gated} = 0$:

$$1 \cdot N_{min} \cdot C_{cp} = C_{ICG} \implies N_{min} = \frac{C_{ICG}}{C_{cp}}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{18 \text{ fF}}{3 \text{ fF}} = 6 \text{ flip-flops}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Any group of **6 or more** flip-flops justifies the ICG cell, because the savings from suppressing the clock on those 6 flops exactly offset (or exceed) the ICG's own power consumption. In practice, ICG cells are inserted for groups of 8 to 64 flops to provide a comfortable margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Clock Gating (Module 2)**

- **Core idea (1 line):** Stop the clock to flip-flops that aren't doing useful work, saving dynamic power.
- **Dynamic power equation:** $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^{\,2} f$ — Clock Gating reduces $\alpha$.
- **Clock power fraction:** 30 – 50 % of total dynamic power (huge target).
- **Two styles:** Latch-free (combinational, glitchy) vs. Latch-based (sequential, glitch-free).
- **Industry standard:** Latch-based ICG (Integrated Clock Gate) cell — pre-built in every cell library.
- **Negative-level-sensitive latch:** Transparent when $CLK = 1$, opaque when $CLK = 0$. It samples $EN$ during the low phase of $CLK$ and holds it stable during the high phase, ensuring glitch-free $GCLK$.
- **ICG overhead:** Latch + AND gate (typically 15 – 30 fF of extra clock capacitance). Must be subtracted from gross savings.
- **Break-even:** An ICG cell is justified for ≥ 6 – 10 flops (in 45 – 90 nm); in advanced nodes, may need ≥ 20 flops.
- **Hierarchical gating:** Coarse block-level gating (entire IP cores) plus fine-grained gating (per register group). Multi-level hierarchy is the norm in SoCs.
- **Clock skew impact:** Inserting an ICG adds latency to the clock path → small skew penalty. Clock tree synthesis must re-balance the tree.
- **Difference from power gating:** Clock gating → *dynamic* power (clock toggling). Power gating → *static/leakage* power ($V_{DD}$ cut-off). Complementary techniques.
- **Difference from clock frequency scaling:** Clock gating → reduces $\alpha$ (active). Frequency scaling → reduces $f$ (uniformly). Both reduce $P_{dyn}$.
- **Difference from operand isolation:** Clock gating freezes *output state*. Operand isolation freezes *combinational input activity*. Both are dynamic-power techniques and often co-deployed.
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** Confusing clock and power gating; drawing the wrong gating cell; forgetting ICG overhead; not mentioning clock-skew impact; not writing the $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ formula explicitly.
- **Key numbers to memorize:** $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$, clock-power fraction = 30 – 50 %, typical savings = 20 – 70 %.
- **Worked example recap:** $P_{orig} = C V^2 f$ (full clock), $P_{gated} = \alpha_{gated} C V^2 f + C_{ICG} V^2 f_{latch}$, $S_{\%} = 1 - P_{gated}/P_{orig}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor and Gate Sizing for Dynamic and Leakage Power Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transistor and Gate Sizing for Dynamic and Leakage Power Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transistor and Gate Sizing** is the deliberate engineering of the **Width-to-Length ratio (W/L)** of MOS transistors — and consequently the **drive strength, parasitic capacitance, and leakage profile** of a CMOS logic gate — to achieve a specified balance between **propagation delay, dynamic switching power, and static (leakage) power**, while respecting the **Logical Effort** framework introduced by Sutherland & Sproull and adopted by the KTU 2024 Low-Power VLSI syllabus.

Formally, every CMOS gate is characterized by:

- **Input capacitance** $C_{in} = C_{ox} \cdot (W_n L_n + W_p L_p)$
- **Output (load) capacitance** $C_{out} = C_{int} + C_{wire} + C_{next}$
- **Effective on-resistance** $R_{eff} \propto 1/[\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)]$
- **Logical effort** $g = C_{in,g} / C_{in,inv}$ (normalized to a minimum-size inverter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEAET752, Module 2):** The student is expected to derive sizing for **equal rise/fall delay**, evaluate the **path logical effort**, size **inverter chains** for minimum delay, and extend the analysis to **leakage reduction** through **stacking, multi-V_T, multi-L, and reverse body-bias** techniques.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Pipe Network" Model

Imagine a CMOS circuit as a network of **water pipes** that must fill and drain a set of **cylindrical tanks** (the load capacitances):

| CMOS Quantity | Water-Pipe Analogy | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| W (transistor width) | **Diameter of the pipe** | Wider pipe → more current (drive strength) |
| L (channel length) | **Pipe length** | Longer L → higher resistance (slower flow) |
| $C_L$ (load cap) | **Tank volume to fill** | Larger C → more energy to charge/discharge |
| $V_{DD}$ | **Water pressure** | Higher pressure → faster fill, but quadratic energy cost |
| $V_T$ (threshold) | **Pipe friction threshold** | Higher $V_T$ → less leakage, but slower start |

Just as a plumber chooses pipe diameters to deliver water fast enough without wasting material, a VLSI designer chooses **W/L ratios** to deliver current fast enough without wasting silicon area, dynamic power, or leakage power.

> [!TIP]
> **Big Picture Intuition:** Sizing is the **single most powerful knob** a low-power designer can turn *without* changing the technology node or supply voltage. It costs no extra masks, no extra process steps — only **silicon real estate** and **routing complexity**.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Standard KTU Board Markables)

- **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f_{clk}$  
- **Static (Leakage) Power:** $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$
- **Propagation Delay:** $t_p \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$
- **Energy per Transition:** $E = C_L \, V_{DD}^{2}$
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p$  *(Joules)*
- **Energy-Delay Product (EDP):** $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$  *(Joule·seconds)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to remember (Kerala Board-Examiner Favourite):**
> - Permittivity of SiO₂: $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm
> - $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ (typically 5–25 fF/µm²)
> - Mobility ratio: $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2$ to **3** in standard CMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Size Transistors? — The "Why" Hierarchy

1. **Speed (Performance):** A larger W means more drive current, so the load capacitance charges faster.  
2. **Dynamic Power:** A larger W means **larger input capacitance**, so the *preceding* gate must drive a bigger load → more dynamic power.  
3. **Leakage Power:** A larger W means a **wider transistor channel** → larger sub-threshold and gate-tunneling leakage.  
4. **Area / Wirelength:** Bigger transistors → larger cell → longer wires → more wire capacitance.  

> The art of low-power sizing is to **meet the timing constraint with the *minimum* sized devices**.

### 2.2 The Logical-Effort Framework (Sutherland–Sproull)

Every logic gate has a **logical effort** $g$ that captures its intrinsic capacitance relative to a minimum inverter, and a **parasitic delay** $p$ that captures its intrinsic output capacitance.

- The **stage effort** is $f = C_{out}/C_{in}$.
- The **delay** of a single stage is $d = f + g$ (in units of $\tau$, the inverter delay).
- For an $N$-stage path, the total delay is $D = \sum_i (f_i + g_i) = N \cdot F^{1/N} + P$ where $F$ is the path electrical effort and $P$ is the path parasitic delay.

For an **inverter chain** driving a load $C_L$ from a driver of size $x$ (normalized to minimum), the **optimal taper factor** is:

$$ f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718 $$

and the optimum number of stages is:

$$ N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in}) \big/ \ln(e) = \ln(C_{out}/C_{in}) $$

### 2.3 Sizing for Equal Rise and Fall Delay (Ratioed CMOS)

For a symmetric CMOS inverter, the **pull-up (PMOS)** must be wider than the **pull-down (NMOS)** because hole mobility is roughly **2–3× lower** than electron mobility.

To make $t_{pLH} = t_{pHL}$:

$$ \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \approx 2 \text{ to } 3 \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n $$

This is the **standard sizing ratio** used throughout KTU 2024 board problems.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLDEN RULES:** These formulas are the *minimum* expected at the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the **form, the meaning of every symbol, and the units**.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f$ | Switching power | $\alpha \le 1$ (activity factor) |
| 2 | $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \, R_{eq,n} \, C_L$ | NMOS pull-down delay | $R_{eq} = 1/[\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_n (V_{DD}-V_{Tn})]$ |
| 3 | $t_{pLH} = 0.69 \, R_{eq,p} \, C_L$ | PMOS pull-up delay | PMOS $R_{eq} \approx 2\text{–}3 \times$ NMOS |
| 4 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \, e^{(V_{GS}-V_T)/(nV_T)}$ | Sub-threshold leakage | $V_T$ = thermal voltage (kT/q) |
| 5 | $I_{gate} = A \cdot (V_{DD}/t_{ox})^{2} \, e^{-B \cdot t_{ox}/V_{DD}}$ | Gate-tunneling leakage | Dominant for $t_{ox} < 1.5$ nm |
| 6 | $I_{leak,stack} \approx I_0 \cdot e^{-V_T/(nV_T)}$ | Stack-effect leakage reduction | Each extra OFF transistor stacks |
| 7 | $f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$ | Optimal inverter-chain taper | For minimum delay |
| 8 | $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$ | Optimal number of stages | Inverter-chain buffer |
| 9 | $R_{eff} \propto L/[W \cdot \mu C_{ox}(V_{DD}-V_T)]$ | Long-channel on-resistance | $\alpha$-power law: $I \propto (V_{DD}-V_T)^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 1.3$ |
| 10 | $PDP = C_L V_{DD}^{2} \cdot 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$ | Energy–delay trade-off | Minimizing PDP ≠ minimizing delay |
| 11 | $g = C_{in,gate}/C_{in,inv}$ | Logical effort (per input) | NAND2 $g = 4/3$, NOR2 $g = 5/3$ |
| 12 | $C_{in,total} = \sum_i C_{in,i}$ for series NMOS | Series-stacked input cap | NAND uses $2/3$ cap per input |

> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):** Transistor sizing is the **first-pass** tool used by every industrial RTL-to-GDSII flow (Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus). Without good initial sizing, downstream **clock-tree synthesis** and **dynamic-voltage-scaling** engines cannot converge on timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Propagation Delay of a CMOS Inverter (α-Power Model)

We start with the **α-power law** for short-channel MOSFETs (Sakurai–Newton, 1990):

$$ I_D = K' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)^{\alpha} $$

where $\alpha \approx 1.3$ for 90–180 nm and $\alpha \to 1$ for long-channel devices.

**Step 1 — Output capacitance to be charged:**

$$ C_L = C_{out,int} + C_{wire} + C_{next} $$

**Step 2 — Average charging current (assuming saturated operation):**

$$ I_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K' \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)^{\alpha} $$

**Step 3 — Propagation delay (time to charge 50% of $V_{DD}$):**

$$ t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{avg}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{\tfrac{1}{2} K' \tfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD}-V_T)^{\alpha}} $$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$ \boxed{\, t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{K' \cdot \tfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)^{\alpha}} \,} $$

This is the **single most tested equation** on KTU 2024 board papers for delay analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Energy per Switching Transition

**Step 1 — Charge delivered from supply to load:**

$$ Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD} $$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn from supply (NOT stored in capacitor — half is dissipated in PMOS):**

$$ E_{drawn} = V_{DD} \cdot \int_0^{\infty} i(t) \, dt = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 3 — Energy stored in the load capacitor:**

$$ E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor (during charging):**

$$ E_{diss} = E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 5 — Energy dissipated in NMOS during discharge:**

$$ E_{diss,NMOS} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 6 — Total dynamic energy per cycle:**

$$ \boxed{\, E_{tot} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \,} $$

This is independent of transistor size! **Sizing alone cannot reduce the per-transition energy** — only $V_{DD}$ reduction or activity-factor reduction can.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Power-Delay Product (PDP) and Optimum Sizing

The PDP is the **figure of merit** for energy-efficient switching.

**Step 1 — Multiply $P_{dyn}$ and $t_p$:**

$$ PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p = \alpha f \, C_L V_{DD}^{2} \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{K' (W/L)(V_{DD}-V_T)^{\alpha}} $$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$ PDP = \frac{\alpha f \, C_L^{2} \, V_{DD}^{3}}{K' \, (W/L) \, (V_{DD}-V_T)^{\alpha}} $$

**Step 3 — Key Insight:** PDP **decreases as W increases**, but only as $1/W$ (not as $1/W^2$). This means:

- Doubling W → halves delay (good)
- Doubling W → halves PDP (better)
- Doubling W → **doubles** dynamic power (bad, for the *driver* itself, because input C doubles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters (KTU Board Trick):** The "optimum" size for *minimum energy* is **not** the same as the "optimum" size for *minimum delay*. Designers use the **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** to balance both.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Inverter Chain for Minimum Delay (Buffer Insertion)

A minimum-size inverter (input cap $C_{in,1}$) must drive a huge load $C_L$. We insert $N-1$ intermediate inverters of geometrically-increasing size.

**Step 1 — Define taper factor:** $f = (C_L / C_{in,1})^{1/N}$

**Step 2 — Delay of one stage (Logical Effort form):**

$$ d_i = f + g = f + 1 \quad \text{(since } g_{inv} = 1\text{)} $$

**Step 3 — Total path delay:**

$$ D = N(f + 1) = N \cdot \left[ \left(\tfrac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right)^{1/N} + 1 \right] $$

**Step 4 — Minimize with respect to $N$.** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$ \frac{\partial D}{\partial N} = \left(\tfrac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right)^{1/N} \cdot \left[ 1 - \tfrac{1}{N} \ln\left(\tfrac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right) \right] - 1 = 0 $$

**Step 5 — Solving (using Lambert W) gives:**

$$ \boxed{\, N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in,1}) \,} $$

and the **optimal taper factor** is:

$$ \boxed{\, f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718 \,} $$

### 3.5 Derivation V — Stack Effect on Sub-Threshold Leakage

When two NMOS transistors are stacked with both OFF, the intermediate node voltage rises due to leakage balancing. This **negative $V_{GS}$** for the top transistor exponentially reduces its leakage.

**Step 1 — Sub-threshold current of a single OFF transistor:**

$$ I_{sub} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_T}{n V_T}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right] $$

**Step 2 — For a 2-stack, $V_{GS,top} \approx -V_T/2$ (approx).** Substituting:

$$ I_{stack} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_T/2 - V_T}{n V_T}\right) = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{-3V_T}{2n V_T}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Ratio of single vs. stack:**

$$ \frac{I_{stack}}{I_{single}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_T}{n V_T}\right) \approx 10^{-2} \text{ to } 10^{-3} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Pearl:** The KTU 2024 board often asks for the **order-of-magnitude leakage reduction** of a 2-stack vs 3-stack. A useful rule of thumb: **each extra stacked OFF transistor reduces leakage by ~10×**.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sizing Trade-off Summary

| Technique | Dynamic Power | Leakage Power | Delay | Area | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ W (upsize) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Critical path timing failure |
| ↓ W (downsize) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Non-critical path, idle blocks |
| Stack OFF transistors | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | — | Sleep mode, idle blocks |
| Multi-$V_T$ (LVT/HVT) | same | ↓↓ | ↑ | — | Mixed-criticality paths |
| Long-channel L (L×2) | same | ↓↓ | ↑ | — | Leakage-dominant (older nodes) |
| Reverse Body Bias | same | ↓ | ↑ | — | Standby / sleep mode |
| Multi-$V_{DD}$ | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↑ (for low $V_{DD}$) | ↑ | Voltage islands |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Sizing a Symmetric CMOS Inverter (Ratioed Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin] --> B[Gn gate]
    C[VDD] --> D[Gp gate]
    B --> E[NMOS W/L = 1]
    D --> F[PMOS W/L = 2 to 3]
    E --> G[Output Vout]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Load Capacitance CL]
    I[GND] --> E

    classDef nmos fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pmos fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#662200,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#e6f5e6,stroke:#225522,color:#000

    class E nmos
    class F pmos
    class A,G,H node
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The PMOS is intentionally **2–3× wider** than the NMOS so that the *hole current* (slower) matches the *electron current* (faster), making $t_{pLH} = t_{pHL}$.

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Inverter Chain Buffer Insertion (Tapered Buffer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source: Small Inverter x1] --> ST1[Stage1 Inverter x3]
    ST1 --> ST2[Stage2 Inverter x8]
    ST2 --> ST3[Stage3 Inverter x22]
    ST3 --> ST4[Stage4 Inverter x60]
    ST4 --> LD[Load Capacitance CL]

    classDef stage1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#803300,color:#000
    classDef stage3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#803300,color:#000
    classDef stage4 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#803300,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#d0d0d0,stroke:#333,color:#000

    class S,ST1 stage1
    class ST2 stage2
    class ST3 stage3
    class ST4 stage4
    class LD endnode
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each stage's size grows by the **taper factor $f \approx e \approx 2.7$**. Total stages $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$ for minimum delay.

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Stack Effect for Leakage Reduction (NAND2 Sleep Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD[VDD Rail] --> M1[PMOS M1 - OFF]
    M1 --> MID[Internal Node Vm rises to approx VDD/2]
    MID --> M2[PMOS M2 - OFF]
    M2 --> OUT[Output Node - floating]

    GND[GND Rail] --> M3[NMOS M3 - OFF]
    M3 --> MID2[Internal Node Vx approx 0.1V]
    MID2 --> M4[NMOS M4 - OFF]
    M4 --> IN2[Input B = 0 OFF]

    classDef pmos fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#662200,color:#000
    classDef nmos fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef nodel fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#806000,color:#000

    class M1,M2 pmos
    class M3,M4 nmos
    class MID,MID2 nodel
```

> **Reading the diagram:** With **both NMOS OFF** and the input at 0 V, the intermediate node $V_x$ floats up to a small positive value (≈100 mV). This creates a **negative $V_{GS}$ for the top NMOS**, exponentially cutting its sub-threshold leakage.

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Decision Flowchart: Which Sizing Technique to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Block to Size] --> B{Is it on critical path?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is leakage dominant?}
    C -- No --> D[Upsize W to meet timing]
    D --> E[Keep VDD as rated]
    C -- Yes --> F[Use LVT or longer L at fixed W]
    B -- No --> G{During sleep mode?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply stack effect + RBB]
    G -- No --> I[Downsize W to save dynamic power]
    I --> J[Verify slack still positive]

    classDef yes fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef no fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#806000,color:#000

    class D,F yes
    class H,I,J no
    class A,B,C,E,G node
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

> **Define *logical effort* of a CMOS logic gate. Why is the logical effort of a 2-input NAND gate $g = 4/3$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Logical effort $g$ is the ratio of the input capacitance of a gate to the input capacitance of a minimum-sized inverter that delivers equal output current.

$$ g = \frac{C_{in,\text{gate}}}{C_{in,\text{inv}}} $$

**For 2-input NAND (1.5 marks):** The NAND has **two parallel PMOS** (each of size 2, to give equal drive) and **two series NMOS** (each of size 2). Thus:

- PMOS cap per input: $2 + 2 = 4$ units (when one input is held constant, the other sees 2 PMOS in parallel as its driver).
- NMOS cap per input: $2$ units.
- Total per input: $4 + 2 = 6$ units in arbitrary "inverter-equivalent" units.
- Normalizing to one inverter input (which is 3 units = 1 NMOS + 2 PMOS): $g = 6/4.5$... the **standard KTU board answer** is **$g = 4/3$** because the standard sizing (with series-NMOS of W/L=2) and PMOS of W/L=4 give $C_{in} = 4\cdot C_{ox}W_{min}$, while the inverter's input is $3\cdot C_{ox}W_{min}$. Thus $g = 4/3$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **State and explain the *stack effect* in CMOS. How is it used to reduce sub-threshold leakage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The *stack effect* is the exponential reduction in sub-threshold leakage current when two or more OFF transistors are stacked in series between the supply rail and the output node.

**Explanation (2 marks):** In a stacked OFF configuration (e.g., NAND2 in sleep mode), the intermediate node voltage rises to a small positive value (≈100 mV) due to leakage balancing. This produces a **negative $V_{GS}$** on the top transistor, which by the sub-threshold current equation:

$$ I_{sub} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_T}{nV_T}\right) $$

gives an exponential drop in $I_{sub}$ (typically **10× per extra stacked OFF transistor**). It is widely used in **sleep transistors, MTCMOS power-gating**, and idle-block leakage suppression.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each carries **14 marks** (7 + 7).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B, Q7(b)]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 500$ fF. The minimum-sized inverter has an input capacitance of $C_{in,1} = 10$ fF. Find the **optimum number of stages** $N_{opt}$ and the **sizing of each stage** for minimum propagation delay. Assume an inverter logical effort of $g = 1$ and parasitic delay $p = 1$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A circuit block has 4 identical stages in a critical path, with each stage having logical effort $g = 2$ and parasitic delay $p = 1.5$. The path electrical effort is $F = 32$. Determine the **optimal stage effort** $f_{opt}$, the **minimum path delay** $D_{min}$, and the **input capacitance of the first stage** if the load capacitance is $C_L = 1$ pF.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Inverter Chain Sizing [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Optimum number of stages [2 marks]:**

$$ N_{opt} = \ln\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{500}{10}\right) = \ln(50) \approx 3.912 $$

Round to **$N = 4$ stages** (must be integer).

**Step 2 — Optimum taper factor [1 mark]:**

$$ f_{opt} = \left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right)^{1/N} = 50^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{50}} \approx 2.659 $$

**Step 3 — Size of each stage [2 marks]:**

| Stage | Size (relative to minimum) | Input cap (fF) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1$ | 10 |
| 2 | $f = 2.66$ | 26.6 |
| 3 | $f^2 = 7.07$ | 70.7 |
| 4 | $f^3 = 18.8$ | 188 |
| Load (after stage 4) | $f^4 = 50$ | 500 |

**Step 4 — Verification [2 marks]:** Each stage sees fan-out $f = 2.66$ → uniform per-stage delay $d = f + g = 2.66 + 1 = 3.66\tau$ → total path delay $D = 4 \times 3.66\tau = 14.64\tau$.

---

**Part (b) — Path Logical Effort [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Path logical effort and path parasitic delay [1 mark]:**

$$ G = \prod_{i=1}^{4} g_i = 2^4 = 16 $$
$$ P = \sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i = 4 \times 1.5 = 6 $$

**Step 2 — Optimal stage effort [2 marks]:**

$$ f_{opt} = (F \cdot G)^{1/N} = (32 \cdot 16)^{1/4} = (512)^{1/4} = 4.76 $$

**Step 3 — Minimum path delay [2 marks]:**

$$ D_{min} = N \cdot f_{opt} + P = 4 \times 4.76 + 6 = 19.04 + 6 = 25.04 \, \tau $$

**Step 4 — First-stage input capacitance [2 marks]:**

The first-stage input cap is found from the total path effort:

$$ \text{Total path effort } H = G \cdot F \cdot B = 16 \cdot 32 \cdot 1 = 512 $$

$$ C_{in,1} = \frac{C_L \cdot G}{H} = \frac{C_L}{F} = \frac{1000\,\text{fF}}{32} = 31.25\,\text{fF} $$

> **Note:** With branching $B = 1$, the formula simplifies elegantly. The first stage should be sized to **31.25 fF**, the second to $31.25 \times f/g_1 = 31.25 \times 4.76/2 = 74.4$ fF, etc.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set C, Q8(a)]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **expression for the optimum taper factor** of a tapered CMOS inverter chain driving a load $C_L$ from a minimum-sized driver with input cap $C_{in,1}$. Show clearly that $f_{opt} = e$ for an *infinite* chain.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a CMOS NAND2 gate with both NMOS in stack. If the leakage of a single OFF NMOS is $I_0 = 100$ nA at 25 °C, and the stack reduces the effective $V_{GS}$ by 0.2 V, calculate the **stacked leakage current** at 27 °C. Use $n = 1.4$ and $V_T(\text{thermal}) = kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 300 K.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of Optimum Taper Factor [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Delay of an $N$-stage inverter chain [2 marks]:**

Each stage has a fan-out $f$ (= ratio of its load to its input cap). The delay of one stage is $d_i = f + g$ (with $g = 1$ for inverter). Total delay:

$$ D = N \cdot (f + 1) $$

where $f^N = C_L / C_{in,1}$ (since the size grows by $f$ each stage).

**Step 2 — Express $f$ in terms of $N$ [1 mark]:**

$$ f = \left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right)^{1/N} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $D$ [1 mark]:**

$$ D = N \left[ \left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right)^{1/N} + 1 \right] $$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $N$ [2 marks]:**

Let $X = (C_L/C_{in,1})^{1/N}$. Then $\ln X = \frac{1}{N}\ln(C_L/C_{in,1})$, so $X = e^{\ln(C_L/C_{in,1})/N}$.

$$ \frac{\partial D}{\partial N} = X + N \cdot \frac{\partial X}{\partial N} + 1 = 0 $$

$$ \frac{\partial X}{\partial N} = X \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{N^{2}}\right) \ln\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right) = -\frac{X \ln(C_L/C_{in,1})}{N^{2}} $$

**Step 5 — Set to zero, then **take the limit** $C_L / C_{in,1} \to \infty$ [1 mark]:**

For very large $C_L/C_{in,1}$, the dominant term gives:

$$ N_{opt} = \ln\!\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in,1}}\right), \quad f_{opt} = e^{1} = e \approx 2.718 $$

**QED.**

---

**Part (b) — Stack-Effect Leakage Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters [1 mark]:**

- $I_0 = 100$ nA (leakage of single OFF NMOS at $V_{GS} = 0$)
- $\Delta V_{GS} = -0.2$ V (more negative for top NMOS due to stack)
- $n = 1.4$, $V_T = 25.85$ mV
- $T = 27$ °C = 300 K

**Step 2 — Recompute thermal voltage at 300 K [1 mark]:**

$$ V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 25.875 \,\text{mV} $$

**Step 3 — Sub-threshold current equation for the stacked top NMOS [2 marks]:**

$$ I_{stack} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_{GS} - V_T^{\text{device}}}{n V_T}\right) $$

Assuming the device threshold $V_T^{\text{device}} = 0.4$ V (typical), and $\Delta V_{GS} = -0.2$ V:

$$ \frac{\Delta V_{GS}}{n V_T} = \frac{-0.2}{1.4 \times 0.02585} = \frac{-0.2}{0.03619} \approx -5.526 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponential [1 mark]:**

$$ \exp(-5.526) \approx 3.985 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 5 — Compute stacked leakage [1 mark]:**

$$ I_{stack} = 100\,\text{nA} \times 3.985 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.3985\,\text{nA} \approx 400\,\text{pA} $$

**Step 6 — Conclusion [1 mark]:** The 2-stack reduces leakage from **100 nA → ~0.4 nA**, a **~250× reduction**, confirming the well-known order-of-magnitude benefit of stacking.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students *commonly* lose marks (Module 2):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$ with $N_{opt} = \log_{10}(C_L/C_{in})$** — use **natural log**, not base-10! (Examiner deducts 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting that $N_{opt}$ must be rounded to an *integer*** — show the rounding explicitly and verify that the rounded $N$ still gives lower delay than $N-1$ or $N+1$.
> 3. **In stack-effect problems, omitting the negative sign on $\Delta V_{GS}$** — the entire exponent depends on it.
> 4. **Sizing NAND/NOR without specifying *per-input* logical effort** — the KTU board wants $g_{\text{per-input}}$, not the aggregate.
> 5. **Mixing up $f_{opt} = e$ (inverter chain, infinite load) with $f_{opt} = 4$ (NAND chain)** — the formula $f_{opt} = (F \cdot G)^{1/N}$ is **general**; $e$ is a special case.
> 6. **Not mentioning the trade-off**: every leakage-reduction technique costs *delay*. Always state the trade-off explicitly to score full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this *just before* walking into the exam hall.**

- **Definition** — Sizing = choosing $W/L$ of every transistor to balance **speed, power, area**.
- **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — **independent of W**, but W affects $\alpha$ and $C_{in}$.
- **Static Power:** $P_{leak} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ — **rises with W** (more channel width → more leakage paths).
- **Delay:** $t_p \propto C_L / [W \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)^{\alpha}]$ — **falls as $1/W$**.
- **Inverter chain** — optimum stages $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$; optimum taper $f_{opt} = e \approx 2.7$.
- **Logical Effort** — $g = C_{in,gate}/C_{in,inv}$; $g_{NAND2} = 4/3$, $g_{NOR2} = 5/3$, $g_{inv} = 1$.
- **Path delay** — $D = \sum_i (f_i + p_i) = N f + P$ (when all stages matched).
- **Path effort method** — $H = GFB$, then size each stage by $C_{in,i} = (G \cdot C_{out,i-1})/f_i$.
- **Stack effect** — extra OFF transistors → negative $V_{GS}$ → ~10× leakage reduction per stack.
- **Multi-V_T** — LVT (low $V_T$) for critical paths; HVT (high $V_T$) for non-critical paths.
- **Multi-L** — doubling $L$ halves leakage but adds ~10% delay; standard **leakage-reduction knob at 90–180 nm**.
- **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** — increases effective $V_T$ → cuts leakage; used in *standby/sleep* mode.
- **Sizing ratio** for symmetric rise/fall — $(W/L)_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p)(W/L)_n \approx 2$ to $3 \times (W/L)_n$.
- **Critical Pitfall** — sizing *reduces* $P_{dyn}$ of the *driver* but *increases* $P_{dyn}$ of the *preceding stage* (larger $C_{in}$). Always check the **global** power impact, not just local.
- **Order-of-magnitude rules** — 2-stack → 10–250× leakage reduction; 3-stack → 1000×+; every 100 mV $V_T$ increase → ~10× leakage reduction at 300 K.
- **Exam mantra** — "PDP scales as $1/W$; EDP scales as $1/W^2$. Choose $W$ to *just* meet the timing constraint."
- **Long-channel R** — $R_{eff} \propto L / [W \mu C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_T)]$ — keep this formula at your fingertips.

> **Final KTU Board Tip (2024 Scheme):** Always end a sizing question with a **one-line trade-off statement** — e.g., *"Upsizing reduces delay at the cost of higher input capacitance and leakage, which is justified here because the path is timing-critical."* Examiners reward the *engineering judgment* as much as the math.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Design Styles; Clocked Design Style - Basic Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Circuit Design Styles; Clocked Design Style - Basic Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Circuit Design Styles — The Big Picture

In CMOS VLSI design, the **transistor-level topology** chosen to realize a Boolean function is called a **Circuit Design Style** (also referred to as a *logic style* or *logic family*). The choice of design style directly dictates the **area, speed, power dissipation, noise margin, and robustness** of the synthesized digital block.

KTU 2024 Scheme classifies CMOS circuit design styles into two broad families:

| Family | Key Idea | Examples |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Static Design Styles** | Output is always connected to $V_{DD}$ or GND through a conducting path | Static CMOS, Pseudo-NMOS, Pass Transistor Logic, Transmission Gates |
| **Dynamic (Clocked) Design Styles** | Output state is held on a parasitic capacitance, refreshed by a clock | Clocked CMOS (C²MOS), Dynamic CMOS, Domino Logic, NORA Logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — KTU 2024):** The syllabus explicitly demands a *basic concept treatment* of clocked design styles. You must clearly explain *why a clock is needed*, *how precharge/evaluation phases work*, and *how clock distribution itself becomes a power bottleneck*.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Clocked (Dynamic) Design Style

A **Clocked Design Style** is a CMOS logic implementation in which a global (or local) **clock signal $\phi$** is used as an additional control input to predefine the operation of the circuit in two mutually exclusive phases:

- **Precharge Phase (when $\phi = 0$):** The output node is *unconditionally* connected to $V_{DD}$ (or GND) and precharged to a known logic level.
- **Evaluate Phase (when $\phi = 1$):** The output node is conditionally discharged (or charged) based on the logic inputs through a pull-down (or pull-up) network.

The output is **not driven continuously** — instead, it is **temporally stored** on the parasitic capacitance of the output node ($C_L$).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Synchronized Bucket Brigade"

Imagine a row of buckets (the logic gates) connected by a one-way valve. A traffic controller (the **clock**) gives two alternating instructions:

1. **"FILL!"** (Precharge, $\phi = 0$): Every bucket is forcibly filled with water from a common reservoir ($V_{DD}$).
2. **"CHECK & POUR!"** (Evaluate, $\phi = 1$): Each bucket *decides* whether to tip its contents into the next bucket based on the inputs.

The buckets themselves do not need a permanently-open tap; the controller orchestrates everything. This is exactly how a clocked CMOS gate operates — saving the energy cost of always maintaining a conducting path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Recap:**
> - **Clocked Design Style** = Logic evaluation governed by a periodic clock with distinct **precharge (PC)** and **evaluate (EV)** phases.
> - The clock is **mandatory**; without it, the circuit has no defined output state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of CMOS Design Styles (Module 3 Mapping)

KTU Module 3 expects students to recognize at least the following five canonical styles:

### 2.1.1 Static CMOS (Complementary CMOS)
- **Topology:** Pull-Up Network (PUN, pMOS) + Pull-Down Network (PDN, nMOS).
- **Properties:**
  - *Ratio-less* (no sizing constraints).
  - Rail-to-rail output swing.
  - **Zero static power** (no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND).
  - But: high input capacitance ($C_{in} \approx 3 \cdot C_{ox}$ per input).

### 2.1.2 Pseudo-NMOS Logic
- **Topology:** PDN (nMOS) + a single always-on pMOS load (gate tied to GND).
- **Properties:**
  - **Static power dissipation** when output is LOW (pMOS + nMOS both conduct).
  - Reduced transistor count (N+1 vs. 2N).
  - Used in NOR-heavy critical paths (e.g., address decoders).

### 2.1.3 Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) & Transmission Gates
- Single nMOS or parallel nMOS-pMOS pair passes the input to the output.
- **Threshold drop** problem with single nMOS.

### 2.1.4 Dynamic (Clocked) CMOS — **Module Focus**
- **Topology:** pMOS precharge transistor + nMOS PDN controlled by clock $\phi$.
- Phases:
  - **Precharge** ($\phi = 0$): pMOS ON → Output → $V_{DD}$.
  - **Evaluate** ($\phi = 1$): pMOS OFF; PDN conditionally discharges the output.

### 2.1.5 Domino Logic (Cascaded Dynamic)
- Dynamic gate followed by a static CMOS inverter → non-inverting domino stage.
- Allows **cascading** of dynamic gates safely.

## 2.2 Basic Concept of Clocked Design Style — The "Why"

### 2.2.1 The Fundamental Need for a Clock
Static CMOS has a **duality redundancy**: every Boolean function is realized twice — once as a PUN and once as a PDN. This guarantees *always-defined* output but doubles the transistor count and increases parasitic capacitance.

Dynamic/clocked logic **breaks this redundancy**. The clock assumes one of the two logic values temporarily, and the transistors only realize the function in one direction (typically discharge to GND).

### 2.2.2 Operating Phases in Detail

Let $\phi$ be the clock and $\overline{\phi}$ its complement.

**Phase 1 — Precharge ($\phi = 0$, $\overline{\phi} = 1$):**
- The **precharge pMOS** (with gate tied to $\phi$) turns ON.
- The **evaluate nMOS** (with gate tied to $\phi$) in series with the PDN turns OFF.
- The output capacitance $C_L$ charges to $V_{DD}$ through the pMOS.
- The output node is **HIGH** regardless of inputs.

**Phase 2 — Evaluate ($\phi = 1$, $\overline{\phi} = 0$):**
- The precharge pMOS turns OFF (isolating $V_{DD}$).
- The evaluate nMOS turns ON, enabling the PDN.
- If the inputs evaluate to a **TRUE** condition in the PDN, a discharge path forms, and $C_L$ discharges toward GND → Output goes **LOW**.
- If the PDN remains OFF, $C_L$ holds its charge (HIGH).

## 2.3 Key Advantages of Clocked Design Style

1. **Reduced transistor count:** Only N+1 (or N+2) transistors vs. 2N for static CMOS.
2. **Lower input capacitance:** Inputs drive only nMOS transistors in the PDN.
3. **Faster switching:** No PUN to fight against during discharge.
4. **Zero static power** (when in precharge or idle).
5. **Higher logic density** — preferred for critical, high-speed datapath blocks (ALUs, register files).

## 2.4 Critical Disadvantages

1. **Clock distribution overhead:** Every gate needs a clock wire → power cost in the clock tree.
2. **Charge sharing / redistribution** with internal node capacitances can corrupt the output.
3. **Leakage** during the evaluate phase for floating nodes.
4. **Cascading restriction** (solved by *Domino logic*).
5. **Race conditions** during clock transitions (clock feedthrough, charge injection).
6. **Minimum clock frequency** required to refresh leaked charge (for pure dynamic logic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Clocked dynamic logic is the workhorse of **high-performance microprocessors** (e.g., the critical 64-bit integer ALU in early Pentium / Alpha processors, where fan-in-heavy operations are realized using dynamic domino gates for speed).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Boundary | Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Dynamic Power Dissipation** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts (W) | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency |
| 2 | **Static Power** | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Watts (W) | $I_{leak}$ includes subthreshold + gate leakage |
| 3 | **Total Power** | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} + P_{short}$ | Watts (W) | $P_{short}$ is the crowbar short-circuit power |
| 4 | **Energy per Transition** | $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Joules (J) | Energy stored on output capacitor |
| 5 | **Clock Power (Tree)** | $P_{clk} = \alpha_{clk} \cdot C_{tree} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts (W) | $\alpha_{clk}$ typically = 1 for clock nets (toggles every cycle) |
| 6 | **Propagation Delay (RC model)** | $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | Seconds (s) | First-order Elmore approximation |
| 7 | **Precharge Time** | $t_{PC} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{pMOS} \cdot C_L$ | Seconds (s) | Time to charge $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$ |
| 8 | **Evaluate Discharge Time** | $t_{EV} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{PDN} \cdot C_L$ | Seconds (s) | Time for PDN to discharge $C_L$ |
| 9 | **Charge Sharing Voltage** | $V_{final} = V_{pre} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$ | Volts (V) | If $C_{int}$ is the internal parasitic |
| 10 | **Subthreshold Leakage Current** | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)$ | Amperes (A) | $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at 300 K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the golden constant:** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at room temperature ($T = 300\ K$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ J/K$).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dynamic Power Dissipation in a Clocked CMOS Gate

We derive the energy consumed by a clocked gate during one complete precharge-evaluate cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify the charging event.**
During the precharge phase ($\phi = 0$), the output capacitance $C_L$ is charged from $0\ V$ to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{drawn} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot dQ = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV$$

$$E_{drawn} = C_L \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \, dV = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored on the capacitor.**

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated as heat in the pMOS (during charging).**
By conservation of energy:

$$E_{dissipated,\,PC} = E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4 — Discharging phase contribution.**
During evaluation, $C_L$ discharges through the PDN. The energy stored ($\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$) is dissipated entirely in the nMOS PDN — **no energy is returned to the supply**.

$$E_{dissipated,\,EV} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total energy per switching event.**
Summing both halves:

$$E_{total} = E_{PC} + E_{EV} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6 — Convert to power.**
If the gate switches $\alpha \cdot f_{clk}$ times per second (where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$):

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Insight:** Note that $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat on *every* full transition. This is the fundamental reason **low-power VLSI** aggressively scales down $V_{DD}$ — power scales with $V_{DD}^2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Clock Tree Power Consumption

The clock signal is unique: it toggles **every cycle** (i.e., $\alpha_{clk} = 1$) and fans out to *every* sequential element on the chip.

**Step 1 — Total clock-tree capacitance.**
The clock wire and the gate capacitance of all clocked transistors form a lumped capacitance:

$$C_{tree} = C_{wire} + N_{clk} \cdot C_{gate,clk}$$

where $N_{clk}$ is the total number of clocked transistors on the chip.

**Step 2 — Activity factor.**
By definition, $\alpha_{clk} = 1$ (the clock rises and falls every period).

**Step 3 — Clock power equation.**
Substituting into the dynamic power formula:

$$\boxed{P_{clk} = f_{clk} \cdot C_{tree} \cdot V_{DD}^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Industry Reality Check:** In modern high-performance ICs (e.g., server-class CPUs), $P_{clk}$ can account for **30%–40%** of total chip power. This is why *clock gating* — a low-power technique — is a Module 3 staple.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge Sharing Voltage Drop

A notorious failure mode of dynamic logic.

**Setup:** During precharge, the output node $C_L$ is charged to $V_{DD}$. An internal node $C_{int}$ (e.g., drain capacitance of an intermediate transistor in the PDN) is also precharged (typically to $V_{DD}$ via some path, or remains at $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$).

**Step 1 — Initial charges.**

$$Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) V_{DD}$$

**Step 2 — After charge redistribution (isolated from $V_{DD}$).**
When the evaluate phase begins and the internal node connects to $C_L$ via an ON transistor, charge redistributes. Total capacitance becomes $C_L + C_{int}$.

$$Q_{final} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$$

**Step 3 — Conservation of charge ($Q_{initial} = Q_{final}$).**

$$(C_L + C_{int}) V_{final} = (C_L + C_{int}) V_{DD}$$

Wait — this is trivially $V_{final} = V_{DD}$. The charge sharing effect becomes **destructive** when the internal node $C_{int}$ was *previously discharged* to 0 V (because the PDN had been activated earlier). Let us redo the case where $V_{int}(0) = 0\ V$ and $V_L(0) = V_{DD}$.

**Redone Step 1 — Initial charges.**

$$Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = C_L V_{DD}$$

**Redone Step 3 — Conservation.**

$$(C_L + C_{int}) V_{final} = C_L V_{DD}$$

$$\boxed{V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}}$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case drop.**

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** If $C_{int} > C_L$, the output voltage can drop below the noise margin and corrupt the logic value. This is why precharge transistors are sometimes added on internal nodes (in *fully dynamic* logic) — a mitigation strategy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Clocked Style vs Static CMOS

| Performance Metric | Static CMOS | Clocked (Dynamic) CMOS | Implication |
|--------------------|-------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| **Transistor count** | 2N | N + 2 (1 clk + 1 PDN) | Dynamic wins on density |
| **Static Power** | **0** | ~0 (ideal) | Both excellent |
| **Dynamic Power per gate** | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Equal — but *clock tree* adds overhead |
| **Clock loading** | None | Yes (clk on every gate) | Dynamic penalized |
| **Cascadability** | Yes, unlimited | Restricted (needs buffer) | Domino fixes this |
| **Noise immunity** | Excellent | Poor (charge sharing, leakage) | Static is robust |
| **Logic function** | Inverting + Non-inverting | Inverting only (basic dynamic) | Needs inverter to make non-inverting |
| **Min clock freq.** | None | Required (refresh leakage) | Static is freer |

**Reading the diagram:**

## 4.2 Phase Timing Diagram — Clocked CMOS Behavior

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Phi as Clock phi
    participant Mp1 as pMOS Precharge
    participant Mn1 as nMOS Evaluate
    participant PDN as Pull-Down Network
    participant OUT as Output Node
    participant Y as Buffered Output

    Note over Phi,Y: PHASE 1 - PRECHARGE (phi = 0)
    Phi->>Mp1: phi = 0 -> pMOS turns ON
    Mp1->>OUT: Charges CL from 0 V to VDD
    Phi->>Mn1: phi = 0 -> nMOS turns OFF
    Note over OUT: OUT held at VDD (HIGH)

    Note over Phi,Y: PHASE 2 - EVALUATE (phi = 1)
    Phi->>Mp1: phi = 1 -> pMOS turns OFF
    Phi->>Mn1: phi = 1 -> nMOS turns ON
    PDN->>OUT: If inputs TRUE -> discharge to GND (LOW)
    PDN-->>OUT: If inputs FALSE -> CL holds VDD (HIGH)

    OUT->>Y: Buffered through static inverter
    Note over Y: Y is non-inverting logic output
```

**Interpretation:** The clock generated by the PLL is distributed via a low-skew H-tree to local buffers, which then drive individual clocked logic blocks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Clocked CMOS Design Style. Mention any two advantages.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Clocked CMOS Design Style** (also called dynamic CMOS logic) is a CMOS logic implementation in which a periodic clock signal $\phi$ controls the operation of the circuit in two phases — **Precharge phase ($\phi = 0$)** and **Evaluate phase ($\phi = 1$)**. During precharge, the output capacitance is charged to $V_{DD}$ via a clock-controlled pMOS transistor. During evaluation, the output is conditionally discharged to GND through the nMOS pull-down network (PDN) based on the logic inputs.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Reduced transistor count** — only N+1 or N+2 transistors vs. 2N for static CMOS (e.g., a 4-input NAND needs 8 transistors in static CMOS but only 5 in dynamic CMOS).
2. **Lower input capacitance** — inputs connect only to nMOS gates in the PDN, resulting in faster switching and lower dynamic power on input nets.

**Mark Distribution (for student reference):**

|-----------|-------|
| Definition of clocked style | 1.5 |
| Mentioning two advantages | 1.5 |

### **Q2. Differentiate between Static CMOS and Dynamic (Clocked) CMOS logic.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static CMOS | Dynamic (Clocked) CMOS |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Clock required?** | No | Yes |
| **Transistor count for N-input NAND** | 2N | N + 1 |
| **Logic function realized** | Both PUN and PDN | Only PDN (pull-down) |
| **Output state** | Always driven (no floating) | Temporarily stored on $C_L$ |
| **Charge sharing problem** | Not present | Present |
| **Noise margin** | High | Low (sensitive to leakage & noise) |
| **Power — static** | ~0 | ~0 |
| **Power — clock tree** | 0 | Significant |
| **Cascadability** | Unlimited | Needs buffer (Domino) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must attempt exactly one full question (either Q.A or Q.B).

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **basic Clocked CMOS Inverter** during precharge and evaluate phases. Define the role of the clock signal and the precharge transistor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **any three advantages and three disadvantages** of clocked CMOS design style over static CMOS. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Clocked CMOS Inverter — Circuit and Operation**

A basic clocked CMOS inverter (dynamic inverter) consists of:
- One **pMOS precharge transistor (Mp)** with gate connected to clock $\phi$.
- One **nMOS evaluate transistor (Mn)** with gate connected to clock $\phi$ (placed in series with the PDN).
- One **input nMOS (Min)** whose gate is the data input *IN*.
- An **output capacitance $C_L$** representing the load (next stage + wiring).

During precharge ($\phi = 0$):
- pMOS Mp is **ON** (since $V_{GS} = 0 - V_{DD} = -V_{DD} < V_{tp}$).
- nMOS Mn is **OFF**.
- $C_L$ charges to $V_{DD}$ through Mp → **Output = HIGH**.

During evaluate ($\phi = 1$):
- pMOS Mp is **OFF** (isolating $V_{DD}$).
- nMOS Mn is **ON**.
- If IN = HIGH: Min is ON; a conducting path GND → Mn → Min → Output exists → $C_L$ discharges → **Output = LOW**.
- If IN = LOW: Min is OFF; no discharge path → $C_L$ holds its precharged value → **Output = HIGH**.

This realizes the **NOT** function dynamically, requiring only **2 transistors + clock input** versus **2 transistors (no clock)** in static CMOS — but with the trade-off of clock loading.

**Marks Breakdown (7):**
| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| Circuit diagram with labels | 2.5 |
| Precharge phase explanation | 1.5 |
| Evaluate phase explanation | 2.0 |
| Role of clock + precharge transistor | 1.0 |

**(b) Three Advantages and Three Disadvantages**

**Advantages:**
1. **Lower transistor count** (N+2 vs 2N) → higher integration density.
2. **Reduced input capacitance** (inputs only see nMOS gates) → faster logic, less input-driving power.
3. **No static power consumption** (no DC path between $V_{DD}$ and GND) → ideal for low-power VLSI.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Clock distribution overhead** — every gate loads the clock net, consuming $P_{clk} = f C_{tree} V_{DD}^2$.
2. **Charge sharing & leakage** can corrupt the floating output during long evaluate phases.
3. **Cascading restriction** — basic dynamic gates cannot directly drive other dynamic gates without buffering (needs *Domino*).

**Marks Breakdown (7):**
| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| Any 3 valid advantages | 3.5 |
| Any 3 valid disadvantages | 3.5 |

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **dynamic power dissipation** in a CMOS circuit. Explain why clock power becomes a dominant contributor in clocked design styles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **charge sharing problem** in dynamic CMOS logic with a suitable expression. Suggest one mitigation technique. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Dynamic Power Dissipation — Derivation**

The dynamic power is dissipated when the output capacitance $C_L$ of a CMOS gate is charged and discharged through the supply rail.

**Charging (Precharge) Phase:**
Energy drawn from $V_{DD}$:

$$E_{drawn} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot dQ = C_L \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

Energy stored on $C_L$ = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.
Energy dissipated in pMOS = $E_{drawn} - E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

**Discharging (Evaluate) Phase:**
The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is fully dissipated as heat in the nMOS PDN.

**Total energy per switching cycle:**

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Power** = Energy per cycle × switching frequency:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot f_{clk} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

where $\alpha$ is the switching activity ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$).

**Why Clock Power Dominates:**
The clock is the most heavily loaded net in the chip — it drives **every sequential element and every clocked gate**. Its activity factor $\alpha_{clk} = 1$ (toggles every cycle), so:

$$P_{clk} = C_{tree} V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$$

This can account for 30–40% of total chip power in modern processors, making clock gating and clock-tree optimization (H-tree, mesh, multi-domain) critical low-power techniques.

**Marks Breakdown (7):**
| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| Charging energy derivation | 2.0 |
| Discharging energy derivation | 1.5 |
| Final $P_{dyn}$ formula | 1.0 |
| Clock power explanation | 2.5 |

**(b) Charge Sharing in Dynamic CMOS**

When the evaluate phase begins and the PDN turns ON, charge initially stored on $C_L$ may redistribute with internal parasitic capacitances ($C_{int}$) of the PDN transistors. If $C_{int}$ was at $0\ V$ before evaluation, the output voltage drops.

**Conservation of Charge:**

$$C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$$

$$\boxed{V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}}$$

**Voltage Drop:**

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

If $\Delta V > V_{noise\,margin}$, the output is misinterpreted by the next stage — a **logic failure**.

**Mitigation Technique:**
**Precharge internal nodes too.** Add small pMOS "bleeder" transistors (clocked by $\phi$ or $\overline{\phi}$) at internal PDN nodes so that $C_{int}$ is also precharged to $V_{DD}$. This eliminates the charge-sharing voltage drop because $V_{int}(0) = V_{DD}$, and after sharing:

$$V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L + C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} = V_{DD}$$

(no drop!).

**Marks Breakdown (7):**
| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| Charge sharing explanation | 2.0 |
| $V_{final}$ derivation | 2.5 |
| One mitigation technique | 2.5 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Mark-loss trap in Part B derivations:** Students often write $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ as the *total* energy — this is wrong. The total is $C_L V_{DD}^2$ because **both** charge and discharge dissipate energy. Examiners specifically deduct for missing the second $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ term.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Clocked" with "Clock Distribution":** Many students write clocked logic needs a clock, but fail to explain *where the clock is connected* (gate of precharge pMOS + gate of evaluate nMOS). Always draw the clock wire to **both** transistors.
>
> 3. **Charge sharing direction confusion:** When asked about charge sharing, students frequently assume the internal node was at $V_{DD}$ and compute a trivial drop of zero. **Always clarify** the initial conditions of $C_{int}$ (typically $0\ V$ for a realistic failure mode).
>
> 4. **Forgetting $\alpha$ in the power formula:** $P_{dyn} = C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ is incomplete without the **switching activity factor** $\alpha$. Examiners award 0.5 marks for the $\alpha$ term.
>
> 5. **Confusing Static vs Dynamic power:** Static power is the *leakage* power ($V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$), NOT zero in real transistors. Don't write "Static power = 0" without qualifying it as *ideal subthreshold*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Clocked Design Style**

- ☐ **Clocked Design Style** = Logic driven by a clock $\phi$ in **precharge** + **evaluate** phases.
- ☐ **Precharge phase:** $\phi = 0$ → output capacitor $C_L$ charges to $V_{DD}$ via pMOS.
- ☐ **Evaluate phase:** $\phi = 1$ → output conditionally discharges through nMOS PDN.
- ☐ **Transistor count for N-input gate:** 2N (static) vs N+1 or N+2 (dynamic).
- ☐ **Output is stored on $C_L$** — no continuous driving path.
- ☐ **Dynamic power formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$.
- ☐ **Total energy per switch:** $E_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ (NOT $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$).
- ☐ **Clock power:** $P_{clk} = C_{tree} V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ — can be 30–40% of total chip power.
- ☐ **Charge sharing equation:** $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$.
- ☐ **Three failure modes:** charge sharing, leakage, clock feedthrough.
- ☐ **Three advantages:** fewer transistors, lower $C_{in}$, no static current.
- ☐ **Three disadvantages:** clock overhead, charge sharing, cascading limitation.
- ☐ **Cascading fix:** use **Domino logic** (dynamic + static inverter buffer).
- ☐ **Thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ mV$** at 300 K — relevant for leakage.
- ☐ **Noise margin** of clocked style is *poorer* than static CMOS — a frequent exam comparison point.
- ☐ **Pseudo-NMOS** is **not** clocked — it has *static* power; do not confuse with dynamic logic.
- ☐ **Static CMOS** has **no clock** and **no floating nodes** — the polar opposite of clocked logic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Domino Logic (Domino NAND Gate)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Domino Logic (Domino NAND Gate)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Domino Logic** is a high-speed, clocked dynamic CMOS logic style introduced by Krambeck, Lee, and Law in 1982. It belongs to the family of *dynamic CMOS logic circuits* where a periodic clock signal $\phi$ (or $\overline{\phi}$) controls two non-overlapping operating phases: the **Precharge Phase** and the **Evaluation Phase**. Unlike static CMOS, the output node is *not* driven by a complementary pull-up (PMOS) tree and pull-down (NMOS) tree simultaneously. Instead, a single precharge PMOS charges the internal dynamic node to $V_{DD}$ during the precharge phase, and a single NMOS pull-down network conditionally discharges the same node during the evaluation phase.

A **Domino NAND Gate** is the canonical Domino Logic primitive that realizes the Boolean function:

$$Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$$

The circuit consists of three key sub-blocks:
1. A **precharge PMOS transistor** ($M_p$) gated by the clock $\phi$.
2. An **NMOS pull-down network** implementing the AND function (NMOS devices in series for inputs A and B).
3. A **static CMOS inverter** that buffers the dynamic node to produce a full-rail, non-inverting output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Domino Logic is listed explicitly under the topic *"Dynamic CMOS Logic Styles"* in Module 3 of PEAET752. The Domino NAND is the **mandatory** pedagogical primitive because it overcomes the well-known speed penalty of static CMOS NAND gates (where series PMOS devices severely degrade the pull-up drive).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Falling Dominoes in a Row":**
> Imagine a row of standing domino tiles, all initially upright. A clock is like a *mechanical pusher arm* that resets (precharges) all the tiles back to standing position every cycle. When the pusher retracts (evaluation begins), only the tile directly pushed by an incoming weight (a logic HIGH on the inputs) falls. Critically, when one tile falls, the *kinetic energy* of its fall is transmitted to its neighbour, causing it to fall too — but **only if the neighbour is free to fall** (i.e., its inputs are HIGH). This cascading, unidirectional wave of "falls" is exactly the *domino effect* in dynamic CMOS: each gate's output transition triggers the next stage's evaluation, propagating in a single, monotonic direction (from HIGH to LOW) per cycle.

In the Domino NAND:
- The "upright tile" is the dynamic node precharged to $V_{DD}$.
- The "incoming weight" is the input combination (A = B = 1).
- The "falling tile" is the dynamic node discharging to GND.
- The "cascading effect" is why this logic is named *Domino*.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, the following standard metrics must be **memorized in bold**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **0.7 V – 1.2 V** (modern nanoscale) |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **MHz – GHz range** |
| Precharge Time | $t_{pre}$ | **nanoseconds** |
| Evaluation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | **picoseconds** |
| Dynamic Node Capacitance | $C_L$ | **femtofarads (fF)** |
| Noise Margin (Low) | $NM_L$ | **reduced vs. static CMOS** |
| Keeper Ratio | $K_r$ | **0.1 – 0.3** (weak keeper) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Keeper Ratio ($K_r$):** $K_r = \dfrac{(W/L)_{keeper}}{(W/L)_{pull\text{-}down}}$. A small $K_r$ ensures the keeper does not fight the pull-down network during a legitimate evaluation discharge, but is strong enough to counter sub-threshold leakage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of the Domino NAND Gate

The Domino NAND operates under a two-phase clocking scheme. We assume a clock signal $\phi$ that is HIGH during the evaluation phase and LOW during the precharge phase (for active-HIGH precharge style).

### **Phase 1 — Precharge Phase ($\phi = 0$)**

- $M_p$ is **ON** (PMOS gate at LOW).
- The dynamic node $X$ is charged to $V_{DD}$ through $M_p$.
- The static inverter sees $V_{DD}$ at its input, so the output $Y = 0$ (LOW).
- The pull-down network is *guaranteed OFF* because either (a) at least one of A, B is LOW, or (b) an explicit evaluation NMOS gated by $\overline{\phi}$ disconnects the network from GND.
- **Energy stored on $C_L$:** $E_{pre} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why precharge is essential:** During precharge, *all* internal nodes are reset to a known state. This eliminates the memory-effect hazard and prevents short-circuit currents that would otherwise flow in static CMOS for certain input combinations.

### **Phase 2 — Evaluation Phase ($\phi = 1$)**

- $M_p$ is **OFF** (PMOS gate at HIGH).
- The dynamic node $X$ is *conditionally* discharged through the NMOS pull-down network.
- **Case 1:** If A = 1 AND B = 1 → Series NMOS path conducts → $X$ discharges to GND → Inverter input is LOW → $Y = 1$ (HIGH).
- **Case 2:** If A = 0 OR B = 0 → At least one NMOS is OFF → $X$ remains at $V_{DD}$ → Inverter input is HIGH → $Y = 0$ (LOW).
- This realizes the NAND truth table: $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

### **Logic Summary Table**

| $\phi$ (Clock) | A | B | Dynamic Node $X$ | Output $Y$ | Phase |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | x | x | $V_{DD}$ | 0 | Precharge |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $V_{DD}$ | 0 | Evaluate |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | $V_{DD}$ | 0 | Evaluate |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | $V_{DD}$ | 0 | Evaluate |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | GND | 1 | Evaluate |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a static CMOS inverter at the output of every Domino gate?**
> 1. **Full-rail restoration:** The dynamic node $X$ suffers from charge sharing, leakage droop, and capacitive coupling noise. The static inverter restores a clean $0$ or $V_{DD}$ at the output.
> 2. **Non-inverting logic:** Dynamic logic is naturally inverting. The static inverter adds one inversion, making Domino *non-inverting*, which allows direct cascading without an extra inverter.
> 3. **Drive strength:** The static inverter provides strong drive for fan-out and interconnect capacitance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Domino faster than static CMOS NAND?**
> - In static CMOS NAND, two PMOS transistors are stacked in series for the pull-up, giving an effective drive resistance of $2 R_p$. Pull-up time $\tau_{pu} = 2 R_p C_L$.
> - In Domino NAND, only **one** PMOS ($M_p$) charges $C_L$, giving $\tau_{pu,Domino} = R_p C_L$ — theoretically **2× faster** pull-up.
