> - Additionally, the NMOS pull-down network has *lower input capacitance* than static CMOS (no PMOS gate capacitance on the input lines), reducing the logical effort and switching delay.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations for any KTU numerical or derivation question on Domino Logic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | $t_{pre} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{Mp}}$ | Precharge time (PMOS drive current) | seconds |
| 2 | $t_{pd,eval} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{DS,pd}}$ | Evaluation discharge delay | seconds |
| 3 | $I_{DS,pd} = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | Pull-down NMOS drain current (triode) | Amps |
| 4 | $E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per charging/discharging cycle | Joules |
| 5 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power dissipation | Watts |
| 6 | $K_r = \dfrac{(W/L)_{keeper}}{(W/L)_{pd}}$ | Keeper transistor sizing ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $NM_L^{Domino} = V_{IL}^{inv} - V_{OL}^{Domino}$ | Low noise margin of Domino | Volts |
| 8 | $\Delta V_{CS} = \dfrac{C_{internal}}{C_{internal} + C_L} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Charge sharing voltage drop | Volts |
| 9 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{pre} + t_{eval} + t_{setup}}$ | Maximum operating frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $t_{d,total} = N \cdot t_{pd}$ | Cumulative delay in N-stage Domino chain | seconds |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Domino logic is heavily employed in:
- **Critical-path timing paths** of high-performance microprocessors (e.g., ALU carry chains, register file read paths).
- **Memory address decoders** in high-speed SRAMs.
- **Sense amplifiers** and **timing-critical control logic** where every picosecond counts.
- **Low-power applications** because the switching activity $\alpha$ is lower than static CMOS (the output toggles at most once per clock cycle per gate, and only when the function evaluates to a change).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Common Mistake:** Students often claim Domino is "inverting" because the dynamic node $X$ inverts the input. This is **WRONG** for the overall gate. The static inverter at the output *cancels* the inversion, making Domino **non-inverting** at its external output $Y$. This is precisely why cascading is possible without inter-stage inverters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Precharge Time ($t_{pre}$)

We derive the time required to charge the dynamic node capacitance $C_L$ from $0$ V to a target voltage $V_{target}$ (typically $0.9 V_{DD}$ for noise-tolerant operation).

**Step 1 — Kirchhoff's Current Law at the Dynamic Node:**

During precharge ($\phi = 0$), the PMOS $M_p$ drives current $I_{Mp}$ into the node capacitance $C_L$:

$$I_{Mp} = C_L \frac{dV_X}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Assume PMOS in Saturation Initially:**

When the node voltage is low, $V_{DS} = V_X$ is small, and the PMOS operates in the saturation regime:

$$I_{Mp,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{GS} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert)^2$$

Since the PMOS gate is at $0$ V and source is at $V_{DD}$, $V_{GS} = -V_{DD}$, so $(V_{GS} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert) = (V_{DD} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert)$. Substituting:

$$I_{Mp,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert)^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Differential Equation for Constant Current Approximation:**

$$C_L \frac{dV_X}{dt} = I_{Mp,sat}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $V_X = 0$ to $V_X = V_{target}$:

$$\int_0^{t_{pre}} dt = \int_0^{V_{target}} \frac{C_L}{I_{Mp,sat}} dV_X$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:**

$$t_{pre} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{target}}{I_{Mp,sat}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Saturation Current Expression:**

$$t_{pre} = \frac{2 C_L \cdot V_{target}}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why KTU problems often give $C_L$, $V_{DD}$, and the PMOS aspect ratio $(W/L)_p$. You can immediately compute $t_{pre}$ using this closed-form.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Evaluation Delay ($t_{pd,eval}$)

During evaluation, the dynamic node $X$ discharges through the series NMOS network. We assume both A = B = 1 (worst-case for a single NAND, but symmetric for equal-sized NMOS).

**Step 1 — Identify the Effective Pull-Down Resistance:**

Two NMOS transistors in series, each with on-resistance $R_{n}$:

$$R_{pd,total} = 2 R_n = \frac{2}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{GS} - V_{th,n})}$$

**Step 2 — RC Discharge Equation:**

The dynamic node voltage decays exponentially:

$$V_X(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t}{R_{pd,total} \cdot C_L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the Delay Point at 50% Swing:**

In digital design, propagation delay is conventionally measured at the $50\%$ voltage point ($V_X = 0.5 V_{DD}$):

$$0.5 V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t_{pd}}{R_{pd,total} C_L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Take Natural Logarithm:**

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t_{pd}}{R_{pd,total} C_L}$$

$$-\ln 2 = -\frac{t_{pd}}{R_{pd,total} C_L}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_{pd}$:**

$$\boxed{t_{pd,eval} = R_{pd,total} \cdot C_L \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{pd,total} \cdot C_L}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Resistance:**

$$t_{pd,eval} = 0.69 \cdot \frac{2 C_L}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{th,n})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Static CMOS NAND:** In a static CMOS NAND, two PMOS in series charge $C_L$ through $2 R_p$, giving a pull-up time of $\approx 0.69 \cdot 2 R_p C_L$. In Domino, the pull-up uses a *single* PMOS $M_p$ (resistance $R_p$ only), so the precharge is $\approx 0.69 \cdot R_p C_L$ — **2× faster** pull-up. This is the dominant speed advantage.

## 3.3 Derivation of Charge-Sharing Noise Voltage

A critical reliability concern in Domino is the **charge sharing** between the dynamic node $C_L$ and an internal node $C_{int}$ in the pull-down network.

**Step 1 — Initial Condition:**

After precharge, dynamic node $X$ holds charge $Q_0 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$. Internal node $C_{int}$ is at $0$ V (since the pull-down was OFF).

**Step 2 — Charge Redistribution:**

When the input transitions such that one NMOS turns ON connecting $C_{int}$ to $X$, charge redistributes. By charge conservation (no path to $V_{DD}$ or GND momentarily):

$$Q_{initial} = Q_{final}$$

$$C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{final}$:**

$$\Delta V_{CS} = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} - \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{CS} = \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} \cdot V_{DD}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If $\Delta V_{CS}$ exceeds the noise margin $NM_L$ of the static inverter, the gate *erroneously flips* its output. This is a **functional failure** that must be designed against. The mitigation is the **keeper transistor** which supplies charge to compensate for the droop.

## 3.4 Power Dissipation Derivation

The total power consumption of a Domino NAND gate has three components:

**Step 1 — Dynamic Switching Power:**

During each clock cycle, the dynamic node $C_L$ is charged to $V_{DD}$ and (conditionally) discharged:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$).

**Step 2 — Short-Circuit Power (negligible in well-designed Domino):**

$$P_{sc} \approx 0 \quad \text{(since output transitions monotonically)}$$

**Step 3 — Static / Leakage Power (with keeper):**

$$P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,keeper} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{off} \cdot 10^{V_{GS,keeper}/S}$$

where $S$ is the sub-threshold swing.

**Step 4 — Total Power:**

$$\boxed{P_{total} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk} + V_{DD} \cdot I_{off} \cdot K_r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Low-Power Perspective:** Because $\alpha$ is typically very low in Domino (output changes at most once per evaluation, and only for a specific input combination), the dynamic power can be **30–50% lower** than static CMOS for typical data patterns. This is why Domino is featured in a *Low-Power VLSI* course.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect on Speed | Increase Effect on Robustness | Increase Effect on Power | KTU-Recommended Choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(W/L)_p$ (precharge PMOS) | Faster precharge | Marginal | Higher dynamic power | **Moderate** (2–3× minimum) |
| $(W/L)_n$ (pull-down NMOS) | Faster evaluation | Better noise margin | Higher input cap & power | **Moderate** |
| $K_r$ (keeper ratio) | Slower evaluation | **Higher** (less leakage droop) | Higher contention power | **Small** (0.1 – 0.3) |
| $V_{DD}$ | Faster (squared effect) | Higher noise margins | **Quadratic** power increase | **Low** for low-power |
| $C_L$ (load cap) | Slower (linear) | Less charge sharing impact | Higher dynamic power | **Minimize** via buffering |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Schematic of Domino NAND Gate

The following Mermaid block renders a transistor-level functional schematic of the Domino NAND gate. All node IDs are alphanumeric; all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric (no markdown formatting inside quotes, per protocol).

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD POWER SUPPLY"]
    GND["GROUND GND"]
    CLK["CLOCK INPUT PHI"]
    INA["INPUT A"]
    INB["INPUT B"]
    MPN["PMOS PRECHARGE MP GATE PHI"]
    MN1["NMOS N1 GATE A"]
    MN2["NMOS N2 GATE B"]
    MNE["NMOS EVAL ME GATE PHI BAR"]
    NODE_X["DYNAMIC NODE X CAP CL"]
    INV_P["INVERTER PMOS"]
    INV_N["INVERTER NMOS"]
    OUT_Y["OUTPUT Y NAND AB"]

    VDD --- MPN
    CLK --- MPN
    MPN --- NODE_X
    INA --- MN1
    INB --- MN2
    MN1 --- MN2
    MN2 --- NODE_X
    NODE_X --- MNE
    MNE --- GND
    NODE_X --- INV_P
    NODE_X --- INV_N
    INV_P --- VDD
    INV_N --- GND
    INV_P --- OUT_Y
    INV_N --- OUT_Y
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram illustrates the *signal flow* and *timing relationships* in a multi-stage Domino chain — a critical concept for understanding why cascading is allowed.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PHASE1["PHASE 1 PRECHARGE PHI LOW"]
    PHASE2["PHASE 2 EVALUATION PHI HIGH"]
    G1["DOMINO GATE 1 DYNAMIC NODE X1"]
    G2["STATIC INVERTER OF G1 OUTPUT Y1"]
    G3["DOMINO GATE 2 DYNAMIC NODE X2"]
    G4["STATIC INVERTER OF G2 OUTPUT Y2"]
    G5["DOMINO GATE 3 DYNAMIC NODE X3"]
    CASCADEFLOW["CASCADING MONOTONIC FALLS 0 TO 1 ONLY"]

    PHASE1 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
    PHASE2 --> CASCADEFLOW
```

## 4.3 Timing Waveform Schematic

The following conceptual waveform chart shows the clock $\phi$, the dynamic node $X$, and the static output $Y$ over two clock cycles for the input pattern A=1, B=1 during evaluation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1["T0 TO T1 PHI LOW PRECHARGE X CHARGES TO VDD Y STAYS 0"]
    T2["T1 TO T2 PHI HIGH EVALUATION A=1 B=1 X DISCHARGES TO 0 Y RISES TO 1"]
    T3["T2 TO T3 PHI LOW PRECHARGE X RECHARGES TO VDD Y FALLS TO 0"]
    T4["T3 TO T4 PHI HIGH EVALUATION A=0 B=1 X STAYS VDD Y STAYS 0"]

    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Waveform — Key KTU Concept:**
> - The dynamic node $X$ is **monotonically falling** during evaluation (it can only go from $V_{DD}$ to GND, never the reverse within one cycle).
> - The static output $Y$ is **monotonically rising** during evaluation.
> - This **monotonic behaviour** is the foundation of why Domino gates can be cascaded: a gate's output, when it transitions, only causes a *one-directional* perturbation at the next gate's dynamic node (a discharge), never a spurious precharge-like charge-up.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A consists of short-answer questions worth 2 marks each (occasionally 3 marks depending on module weightage). Concise, definition-level answers are expected.*

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]:**
**Q: What is Domino Logic? Why is it called "Domino"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Domino Logic is a high-speed dynamic CMOS logic style where the output node is *precharged* to $V_{DD}$ in one clock phase and *conditionally evaluated* (discharged) in the next phase through an NMOS pull-down network. It is called "Domino" because, in a cascaded chain, when one gate's dynamic node falls from HIGH to LOW during evaluation, it can trigger the next gate to evaluate, creating a *cascading, unidirectional wave* of transitions resembling falling domino tiles. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]:**
**Q: List any two advantages of Domino Logic over static CMOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher Speed:** Domino uses only a single PMOS for precharge (vs. series PMOS in static CMOS NAND), giving $\approx 2\times$ faster pull-up. The NMOS pull-down network also has lower input capacitance, reducing logical effort. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Lower Dynamic Power:** Since the output toggles at most once per clock cycle and only for specific input combinations, the switching activity factor $\alpha$ is lower than static CMOS, yielding 30–50% power reduction for typical data patterns. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Module pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks with an internal choice (OR). Below, Question A and Question B are independent alternatives, each split into (a) and (b) sub-parts worth 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Attempt]:**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **2-input Domino NAND gate** using dynamic CMOS logic. Label all transistors, the clock signal, the dynamic node, and the static output. Explain the operation during both the precharge and evaluation phases with a clear timing waveform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (3 marks):**
The Domino NAND consists of:
- **PMOS $M_p$** (precharge) with source at $V_{DD}$, drain at dynamic node $X$, gate connected to clock $\phi$.
- **NMOS $N_1$** with drain at $X$, gate at input A, source at intermediate node.
- **NMOS $N_2$** with drain at intermediate node, gate at input B, source at GND (or at evaluation NMOS drain if used).
- **Static CMOS Inverter** (one PMOS + one NMOS) with input at $X$ and output at $Y$.
- **Optional Keeper NMOS** $M_k$ with drain at $X$, gate at $Y$, source at $V_{DD}$ — sized at $K_r \approx 0.2$.

**Step 2 — Precharge Phase Operation (2 marks):**
When $\phi = 0$: $M_p$ is ON, charging $X$ to $V_{DD}$. The NMOS pull-down is OFF (because either A=0, B=0, or evaluation NMOS gated by $\overline{\phi}$ is OFF). The static inverter sees $V_{DD}$ at its input, so $Y = 0$. The keeper (if present) is also OFF because $Y = 0$.

**Step 3 — Evaluation Phase Operation (2 marks):**
When $\phi = 1$: $M_p$ is OFF, disconnecting $X$ from $V_{DD}$. 
- **If A=1 AND B=1:** Series NMOS $N_1$–$N_2$ conduct, discharging $X$ to GND. Inverter input LOW → $Y = 1$ (HIGH). Keeper turns ON to supply any leakage current.
- **If A=0 OR B=0:** Pull-down path is broken, $X$ remains at $V_{DD}$ (minus any charge sharing or leakage droop), $Y = 0$.

This realizes $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$, the NAND function.

---

**(b)** Derive the **evaluation propagation delay** $t_{pd}$ of the Domino NAND gate. State all assumptions. Compare the delay with a static CMOS NAND gate of identical transistor sizing and explain why Domino is faster. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Pull-Down Network (1 mark):**
For the Domino NAND, during evaluation with A = B = 1, two NMOS devices ($N_1$ and $N_2$) in series form the discharge path. Each has on-resistance:

$$R_n = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{GS} - V_{th,n})}$$

**Step 2 — Total Series Resistance (1 mark):**
$$R_{pd} = R_{N1} + R_{N2} = 2 R_n = \frac{2}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{th,n})}$$

**Step 3 — RC Discharge Equation (2 marks):**
Treating the dynamic node capacitance as $C_L$, the voltage decays as:
$$V_X(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t}{R_{pd} C_L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for 50% Delay (2 marks):**
Setting $V_X(t_{pd}) = 0.5 V_{DD}$ and taking $\ln$:
$$t_{pd} = R_{pd} C_L \ln 2 \approx 0.69 \cdot \frac{2 C_L}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{th,n})}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Static CMOS NAND (1 mark):**
- Static CMOS NAND pull-up: $R_{pu,static} = 2 R_p$ (two PMOS in series), giving $t_{pu,static} = 0.69 \cdot 2 R_p C_L$.
- Domino precharge: $R_{pre,Domino} = 1 R_p$ (single $M_p$), giving $t_{pre,Domino} = 0.69 \cdot R_p C_L$.
- **Speedup factor** $\approx 2\times$ for the pull-up operation. Since NAND delay in static CMOS is *dominated* by the weak series PMOS, Domino is significantly faster.

**Assumptions:** (i) NMOS in deep triode during most of the discharge, (ii) $C_L$ lumped and linear, (iii) clock $\phi$ has zero rise/fall time, (iv) no keeper contention.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Attempt]:**

**(a)** Explain the **charge sharing problem** in Domino Logic with a suitable example. Derive the expression for the voltage droop on the dynamic node due to charge sharing. State the condition for the gate to malfunction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Explain the Mechanism (2 marks):**
During the precharge phase, the dynamic node $X$ is charged to $V_{DD}$, and internal nodes in the pull-down network (e.g., the node between $N_1$ and $N_2$ in a Domino NAND) are at $0$ V. During evaluation, if the input pattern changes such that charge redistributes between $C_L$ and an internal capacitance $C_{int}$ (without a direct path to GND through both NMOS devices simultaneously), the dynamic node voltage can droop.

**Step 2 — Charge Conservation Equation (2 marks):**
By conservation of charge (no path to $V_{DD}$ or GND during the brief redistribution):

$$C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$$

Solving:
$$V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Droop Expression (1.5 marks):**

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{CS} = V_{DD} - V_{final} = \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} \cdot V_{DD}}$$

**Step 4 — Malfunction Condition (1.5 marks):**
The gate malfunctions (erroneously flips its output) if:
$$\Delta V_{CS} > NM_L^{inverter}$$

i.e., the droop exceeds the low noise margin of the static inverter following the dynamic node. Mitigation: include a **keeper transistor** to supply charge lost to leakage and noise.

---

**(b)** Compare **Domino Logic** with **Static CMOS Logic** and **NP-CMOS Logic** in terms of speed, power, noise margin, area, and design complexity. State two important applications of Domino Logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Static CMOS | NP-CMOS (Zipper) | Domino |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Slow (series PMOS) | Moderate (no contention) | **Fastest** (single precharge PMOS) |
| **Power** | Higher $\alpha$ | Moderate (glitch-free) | **Lowest** (low $\alpha$) |
| **Noise Margin** | Highest | Lower (ratioed) | **Lowest** (charge sharing, leakage) |
| **Area** | Largest (2N transistors) | Moderate | **Smallest** (N+1 transistors) |
| **Clock Required** | No | Yes (2 phases) | Yes (1 phase) |
| **Cascadability** | Direct | Requires interface | **Direct (monotonic)** |
| **Design Complexity** | Simple | Moderate | High (keeper sizing, charge sharing) |

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **High-performance ALUs and critical-path arithmetic circuits** in modern microprocessors (e.g., Intel Core, AMD Ryzen carry-chains).
2. **Read-decoders and sense amplifiers in high-speed SRAMs** where the dynamic evaluation matches the sense window timing precisely.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Domino Logic Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing inverting vs. non-inverting output** — The dynamic node $X$ is inverting, but the *gate output* $Y$ is **non-inverting** due to the static inverter. Marks deducted for stating Domino is "inverting."
> 2. **Forgetting the keeper transistor** — In any reliability analysis (charge sharing, noise margin), omitting the keeper shows incomplete understanding. Always mention it.
> 3. **Using $\ln 2$ incorrectly in delay derivation** — The $0.69$ factor comes from $\ln 2$ for the $50\%$ delay point. Do not write $\ln 2 = 0.5$; it is **$0.693$**.
> 4. **Misidentifying the clock control** — Active-HIGH $\phi$ (precharge when LOW) vs. active-LOW $\phi$ (precharge when HIGH) conventions vary. Always explicitly state your assumption.
> 5. **Static power oversight** — A Domino gate *with keeper* has non-zero static power due to the keeper-VDD contention path. Pure Domino (no keeper) has $\approx 0$ static power but poor noise immunity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ Domino Logic = **dynamic CMOS + static inverter buffer** for full-rail, non-inverting output.
- ☐ Two phases: **Precharge ($\phi = 0$)** charges dynamic node to $V_{DD}$; **Evaluation ($\phi = 1$)** conditionally discharges it.
- ☐ Domino NAND truth table: $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$, with A, B applied to series NMOS.
- ☐ Speed advantage over static CMOS: **2× faster pull-up** (single PMOS precharge vs. series PMOS).
- ☐ Power advantage: lower switching activity $\alpha$ ⇒ **30–50% lower dynamic power** in typical data patterns.
- ☐ Key equations: $t_{pre} = \dfrac{C_L V_{target}}{I_{Mp,sat}}$; $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 R_{pd} C_L$; $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.
- ☐ **Charge sharing droop:** $\Delta V_{CS} = \dfrac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} \cdot V_{DD}$.
- ☐ **Keeper ratio** $K_r \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ balances robustness vs. speed.
- ☐ Cascading allowed because of **monotonic transitions** in dynamic logic.
- ☐ Disadvantages: clock distribution overhead, charge sharing, leakage, **non-inverting only** (or requires dual-rail for inverting functions).
- ☐ Applications: **high-speed ALUs, SRAM decoders, critical paths in microprocessors**.
- ☐ Energy per cycle: $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$ — the fundamental low-power scaling limit.
- ☐ For KTU 2024: memorize the **Derivation of $t_{pd}$ and $\Delta V_{CS}$** — these are the two most frequently asked derivations.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions explicitly** in any derivation: triode vs. saturation, $50\%$ delay point, lumped capacitance, ideal clock.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Current Switch Logic; Non-Clocked Circuit Design Style - Fully Complementary Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Current Switch Logic; Non-Clocked Circuit Design Style - Fully Complementary Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)

### Formal Definition
**Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)** is an advanced, non-clocked differential logic family that merges the high-speed, low-swing attributes of **Current Mode Logic (CML)** with the rail-to-rail static CMOS output restoration capability of a complementary CMOS output stage. In DCSL, every logic signal is transmitted as a **differential pair** (In / In_bar) through a constant current-steering network, and the resulting small differential swing is amplified by a cross-coupled CMOS inverter pair that restores full-swing, static-CMOS-compatible logic levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DCSL is explicitly listed as a *non-clocked* logic style. The internal current steering is continuous (not gated by a clock), which distinguishes it from sense-amplifier based logic and from dynamic logic families. This means power is consumed continuously during the active (static) state.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Pan Balance Scale"
Imagine a perfectly balanced scale with two pans:
- A **fixed weight** (the bias current $I_{SS}$) sits on top, holding the scale down equally.
- When you place a **slightly heavier object** on the *left* pan, the left pan tilts down, and the right pan tilts up by an equally small amount.
- The *physical tilt* is small (analogous to the small differential voltage swing on the internal nodes).
- Now, imagine a **mechanical amplifier** attached to the scale that snaps each pan fully to its extreme position (top or bottom) based on which way it tilted — this is the CMOS output restoration stage.

The result: a *tiny imbalance* (differential input) is converted into a *full-swing, definitive output* (rail-to-rail), with **continuous current draw** but **no clock needed**.

### 1.2 Fully Complementary Logic (Static CMOS)

### Formal Definition
**Fully Complementary Logic (FCL)** — also called **Static CMOS** — is the canonical digital logic family in which every gate consists of two mutually-dual, never-simultaneously-conducting transistor networks: a **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** built exclusively from PMOS transistors, and a **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** built exclusively from NMOS transistors. The PUN and PDN are **logically duals** of each other, guaranteeing that for every input combination, exactly one network is ON and the other is OFF, producing **zero static (DC) current** in steady state and **rail-to-rail** ($V_{DD}$ to $GND$) output voltage swing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The term *Fully Complementary* emphasizes that the PUN and PDN are complete logical complements — for any input vector, you cannot have *both* networks ON or *both* OFF. This is the key property that makes static CMOS theoretically free of static power dissipation.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Handled Valve"
Picture a water pipe (the output node) connected between a **high-pressure tank (V_DD)** and a **low-pressure drain (GND)**:
- One handle (the PUN) opens the pipe to the high-pressure tank using a **set of pull-up valves (PMOS)**.
- The *other* handle (the PDN) opens the pipe to the drain using a **set of pull-down valves (NMOS)**.
- The two handles are **mechanically linked** so that opening one *automatically* closes the other and vice versa.
- The pipe is never connected to **both** the high tank and the drain simultaneously, so no water flows continuously — only a brief surge during switching.

This is exactly how static CMOS works: a small switching transient (dynamic power) but no steady-state current.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Metrics for Both Logic Styles:**
> - **Static Power ($P_{static}$):** Power consumed when output is stable (ideally zero for CMOS, non-zero for DCSL).
> - **Dynamic Power ($P_{dyn}$):** Power consumed during switching, $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$.
> - **Propagation Delay ($t_p$):** Time for output to transition 50% after input transition.
> - **Noise Margin ($NM_H, NM_L$):** Measure of immunity to noise.
> - **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** Energy metric = $P_{avg} \cdot t_p$.
> - **Energy-Delay Product (EDP):** Combined metric for figure-of-merit.
> - **Standard supply:** $V_{DD} = \mathbf{0.7\,V}$ to $\mathbf{1.2\,V}$ in modern nanoscale CMOS; **$3.3\,V$ legacy**.
> - **Threshold voltage:** $V_{TN} \approx \mathbf{0.3\text{--}0.5\,V}$ (NMOS), $V_{TP} \approx \mathbf{-0.3\text{--}-0.5\,V}$ (PMOS).
> - **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{25.85\,mV}$ at **$T = 300\,K$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DCSL — Operational Analysis (Step-by-Step Logic)

DCSL consists of three functional sub-blocks:

1. **Input Differential Pair (NMOS current steering)**
2. **Diode-Connected PMOS Load (or resistive load)**
3. **CMOS Output Restoration Stage (Cross-coupled inverters)**

### Step 1 — The Current Source ($I_{SS}$)
A saturated NMOS transistor $M_{bias}$ biased by an external reference (current mirror) supplies a constant tail current $I_{SS}$ to the differential pair. This current is **always flowing** during the active state — a key reason why DCSL has a non-zero static power.

$$P_{static}^{DCSL} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{SS}$$

### Step 2 — Differential Pair Switching
Two NMOS transistors $M_1$ and $M_2$ form a differential pair. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the gate of $M_1$, and its complement $\overline{V_{in}}$ to the gate of $M_2$.

- When $V_{in} > \overline{V_{in}}$, $M_1$ conducts most of $I_{SS}$; $M_2$ is nearly OFF.
- When $V_{in} < \overline{V_{in}}$, $M_2$ conducts; $M_1$ is nearly OFF.
- The transition is **steered** — current is *redirected* (not switched on/off).

### Step 3 — Small-Swing Differential Output Generation
Each drain produces a small voltage swing:

$$\Delta V_{out} = I_{SS} \cdot R_{D}$$

where $R_D$ is the effective load resistance (realized as diode-connected PMOS or poly resistor). Typical swing: **200 mV to 400 mV**.

### Step 4 — CMOS Cross-Coupled Amplifier
The small differential swing is fed to a pair of cross-coupled CMOS inverters that restore full rail-to-rail swing. The cross-coupling provides **positive feedback**, ensuring crisp switching and **logic-level restoration** (the output is always a clean $V_{DD}$ or $GND$).

### Step 5 — Why DCSL is "Non-Clocked"
Unlike dynamic logic or domino logic, DCSL does **not** require a clock for precharge/evaluation. The current source and differential pair operate in **continuous-time analog fashion**, and the output stage is purely static CMOS. This eliminates clock loading, clock distribution power, and timing race conditions.

### Step 6 — Noise Robustness
DCSL's differential signaling provides excellent **common-mode noise rejection** and **substrate noise immunity** — making it ideal for mixed-signal SoCs.

## 2.2 Fully Complementary Logic — Operational Analysis

### Step 1 — PUN and PDN Composition
- **PUN:** PMOS transistors. *ON* when input is LOW. Connects output to $V_{DD}$ when function $F = 1$.
- **PDN:** NMOS transistors. *ON* when input is HIGH. Connects output to GND when function $F = 0$.

### Step 2 — Logical Duality Condition
For static CMOS to be "fully complementary," the Boolean function implemented by the PUN must be the **dual** of the function implemented by the PDN:

$$F_{PDN}(A, B, C) = \overline{F_{PUN}(\bar{A}, \bar{B}, \bar{C})}$$

This duality ensures that for **every** input combination, **exactly one** network conducts.

### Step 3 — Series-Parallel Composition Rules
- **NMOS in series** → logical **AND** in the PDN.
- **NMOS in parallel** → logical **OR** in the PDN.
- **PMOS in series** → logical **OR** in the PUN.
- **PMOS in parallel** → logical **AND** in the PUN.

### Step 4 — Why Static Power ≈ 0
Because PUN and PDN are never simultaneously ON, **no DC path** exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND. The only power consumed is during switching — the dynamic power.

### Step 5 — Inverter as the Simplest Case
The CMOS inverter has 1 PMOS + 1 NMOS:
- $V_{in} = 0$ → PMOS ON, NMOS OFF → $V_{out} = V_{DD}$
- $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ → PMOS OFF, NMOS ON → $V_{out} = 0$
- $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$ → both in saturation → short-circuit current spike

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Comprehensive Formula Reference for KTU Exams**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition / Unit | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Power (CMOS) | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $I_{leak}$ in nA–µA | Static CMOS |
| 2 | Static Power (DCSL) | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{SS}$ | $I_{SS}$ in µA–mA | DCSL |
| 3 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity (0–1) | Both |
| 4 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = I_{peak} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot t_{sc} \cdot f$ | $t_{sc}$ = short-circuit duration | Static CMOS |
| 5 | Total Power | $P_{tot} = P_{static} + P_{dyn} + P_{sc}$ | Watts (W) | Both |
| 6 | Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{2 \cdot k_n \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1 + 2\cdot k_p/k_n}{1 + k_p/k_n} \right)$ | Seconds (s) | Static CMOS |
| 7 | Rise Time (approx.) | $t_r \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2}$ | Use $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ | Static CMOS |
| 8 | Fall Time (approx.) | $t_f \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}$ | Seconds | Static CMOS |
| 9 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Joules (J) | Both |
| 10 | Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ | J·s | Both |
| 11 | Differential Swing (DCSL) | $\Delta V = I_{SS} \cdot R_D$ | Volts (V) | DCSL |
| 12 | Noise Margin High | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Volts (V) | Static CMOS |
| 13 | Noise Margin Low | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Volts (V) | Static CMOS |
| 14 | CMOS Inverter Threshold | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n} \cdot (V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$ | Volts (V) | Static CMOS |
| 15 | DCSL Bias Current | $I_{SS} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$ | Amperes (A) | DCSL |
| 16 | Transistor Gain Factor | $k_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ | A/V² | Both |
| 17 | Body Effect Factor | $V_{TN}(V_{SB}) = V_{TN0} + \gamma (\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB}\vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ | Volts (V) | Both |
| 18 | Subthreshold Leakage | $I_{leak} = I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS} - V_{TN})/(nV_T)} \cdot (1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T})$ | Amperes (A) | Static CMOS |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Static CMOS** is the **backbone of nearly all general-purpose digital ICs** (CPUs, GPUs, microcontrollers, ASICs). It is preferred for its near-zero static power, full noise margin, and compatibility with standard cell libraries.
- **DCSL** is used in **high-speed serial links (SerDes), clock-data recovery (CDR) circuits, RF phase-locked loops (PLLs), and mixed-signal interfaces** where high noise immunity and moderate speed at low $V_{DD}$ are required without the overhead of clock distribution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Static CMOS Inverter Switching Threshold ($V_M$)

**Problem:** Derive the switching threshold of a CMOS inverter where both NMOS and PMOS are in saturation at the threshold point.

**Given:**
- $k_n = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$ (NMOS gain factor)
- $k_p = \frac{1}{2}\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$ (PMOS gain factor)
- $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$ at the threshold
- Both transistors are in **saturation** at $V_M$

**Step 1 — Write the saturation current equations at $V_M$:**

$$I_{DN} = k_n (V_M - V_{TN})^2 \quad \text{(NMOS drain current, saturation)}$$

$$I_{DP} = k_p (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2 \quad \text{(PMOS drain current, saturation)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output node (no DC current flows in steady state, so $I_{DN} = I_{DP}$):**

$$k_n (V_M - V_{TN})^2 = k_p (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root of both sides (both quantities are non-negative):**

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot (V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP})$$

**Step 4 — Isolate terms containing $V_M$ on one side:**

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} = \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{TP}$$

**Step 5 — Group $V_M$ terms on the left:**

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_M = \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{TP}$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $V_M$:**

$$V_M \cdot (\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP})$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $V_M$:**

$$V_M = \frac{\sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{\sqrt{k_n} - \sqrt{k_p}}$$

**Step 8 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $-1$ to obtain the canonical (positive) form:**

$$V_M = \frac{\sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP}) - \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{TN}}{\sqrt{k_p} - \sqrt{k_n}}$$

**Step 9 — Divide numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{k_n}$ to introduce the ratio $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$:**

$$V_M = \frac{r \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP}) - V_{TN}}{r - 1}$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** for the CMOS inverter switching threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Symmetric Inverter ($k_n = k_p$):** When $k_p = k_n$, the formula is indeterminate. Applying L'Hôpital's rule or recognizing the symmetry gives $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Power Dissipation in Static CMOS

**Problem:** Derive the average dynamic power dissipated by a CMOS gate driving a load capacitance $C_L$ at clock frequency $f$, with switching activity $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Energy stored on $C_L$ during one charging cycle:**

When the output transitions from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{charge} = \int_0^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\, dt = V_{DD} \cdot \int_0^{\infty} C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\, dt = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor at the end of charging:**

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging:**

$$E_{dissipated}^{charge} = E_{charge} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated during discharging:**

When the output goes from $V_{DD}$ to 0, the energy stored in the capacitor ($\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$) is **dissipated as heat in the NMOS** — it does not return to the supply.

**Step 5 — Total energy dissipated per full switching cycle (0→1→0):**

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6 — Average power over a clock period $T = 1/f$:**

$$P_{dyn} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T} = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$$

**Step 7 — Incorporate switching activity $\alpha$ (probability that output switches in a given clock cycle):**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

This is the **standard KTU-required dynamic power equation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DCSL Differential Output Swing

**Problem:** Derive the differential output voltage swing of a DCSL differential pair with tail current $I_{SS}$ and PMOS diode load.

**Step 1 — DC bias current of each branch:**

At the balance point, $I_{SS}$ splits equally: $I_1 = I_2 = I_{SS}/2$.

**Step 2 — Voltage at the drain of $M_1$ (with $M_1$ fully ON, $M_2$ OFF):**

All of $I_{SS}$ flows through $M_1$ and the PMOS load. The PMOS is diode-connected, so it operates in saturation. The drain voltage $V_{D1}$ satisfies:

$$I_{SS} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{SG,p} - |V_{TP}|)^2$$

**Step 3 — Effective load resistance:**

Define the small-signal resistance of the diode-connected PMOS at the bias point:

$$R_D = \frac{1}{g_{m,p} + g_{mb,p} + g_{ds,p}} \approx \frac{1}{g_{m,p}}$$

where $g_{m,p}$ is the PMOS transconductance.

**Step 4 — Differential swing when current is fully steered:**

When $V_{in} \gg \overline{V_{in}}$, all $I_{SS}$ flows through the left branch; when $V_{in} \ll \overline{V_{in}}$, all flows through the right branch. The total swing on each output node is:

$$\Delta V_{single} = I_{SS} \cdot R_D$$

**Step 5 — Differential (peak-to-peak) swing between the two outputs:**

$$\Delta V_{diff} = 2 \cdot I_{SS} \cdot R_D$$

**Step 6 — Final KTU-ready result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{diff} = 2 I_{SS} R_D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** If $I_{SS} = 100\,\mu A$ and $R_D = 2\,k\Omega$, then $\Delta V_{diff} = 2 \times 100\,\mu A \times 2\,k\Omega = 0.4\,V = 400\,mV$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DCSL vs Static CMOS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1 — Comparative Analysis Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)**

| Parameter | Static CMOS (FCL) | DCSL |
|---|---|---|
| Output Swing | Full rail-to-rail ($V_{DD}$ to $GND$) | Small internal swing (200–400 mV), restored to rail-to-rail at output |
| Static Power | Near-zero (only leakage) | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{SS}$ (continuous) |
| Dynamic Power | Dominant (switching) | Lower (small swing → smaller $CV^2f$) |
| Speed | Moderate | High (small swing → fast transition) |
| Noise Immunity | Excellent (rail-to-rail margins) | Excellent (differential + restored) |
| Clock Required | No | No (non-clocked) |
| Transistor Count | $2N$ for $N$-input gate | $\approx 2N + 4$ (N input pair + load + output stage) |
| Logic Levels | Single-ended | Differential |
| Power Scaling with $V_{DD}$ | Quadratic ($V_{DD}^2$) | Approximately linear (limited by $I_{SS}$) |
| Best Use Case | General-purpose digital | High-speed, mixed-signal, low-$V_{DD}$ |
| Design Complexity | Low | High (current source, biasing) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DCSL Gate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Pair: Vin and Vin_bar] --> B[Differential NMOS Pair: M1 and M2]
    B --> C[Constant Tail Current Source: I_SS bias NMOS]
    B --> D[PMOS Diode Loads: RD1 and RD2]
    D --> E[Internal Small-Swing Differential Nodes: Out_int and Out_int_bar]
    E --> F[Cross-Coupled CMOS Inverter Pair: M3_M4 and M5_M6]
    F --> G[Restored Rail-to-Rail Outputs: Out and Out_bar]
    H[External Bias Reference Vbias] --> C
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
1. **Input Pair (A):** Single-ended logic input and its complement enter the gate.
2. **Differential NMOS Pair (B):** Steers tail current between the two branches based on input polarity.
3. **Tail Current Source (C):** Provides a stable $I_{SS}$ — typically from a bandgap-referenced current mirror.
4. **Diode Loads (D):** Convert the steered current into a small voltage swing at the internal nodes.
5. **Internal Nodes (E):** Small-swing analog signals (e.g., 0.8 V to 1.2 V centered around 1.0 V).
6. **Cross-Coupled Inverters (F):** Positive feedback amplifier that snaps each output to $V_{DD}$ or $GND$.
7. **Restored Outputs (G):** Clean, full-swing, static-CMOS-compatible digital signals.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Static CMOS NAND Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INA[Input A] --> PUN1[PMOS M_p1 - Parallel Branch 1]
    INA --> PDN1[NMOS M_n1 - Series Branch 1]
    INB[Input B] --> PUN2[PMOS M_p2 - Parallel Branch 2]
    INB --> PDN2[NMOS M_n2 - Series Branch 2]
    PUN1 --> OUTNODE[Output Node: F = NAND A and B]
    PUN2 --> OUTNODE
    PDN1 --> OUTNODE
    PDN2 --> OUTNODE
    PUN1 -. Connects to VDD when A is LOW .-> VDD[VDD Rail]
    PUN2 -. Connects to VDD when B is LOW .-> VDD
    PDN1 -. Connects to GND when A is HIGH .-> GND[GND Rail]
    PDN2 -. Connects to GND when B is HIGH .-> GND
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- **PMOS in parallel** (PUN) — either $A=0$ OR $B=0$ pulls the output HIGH → implements $\overline{A} + \overline{B} = \overline{A \cdot B}$.
- **NMOS in series** (PDN) — both $A=1$ AND $B=1$ pull the output LOW → implements $A \cdot B$.
- The two networks are **logical duals**, satisfying the fully-complementary condition.
- **Zero static current:** When $A=0, B=0$ → both PMOS ON, both NMOS OFF → output = $V_{DD}$, no path to GND.
- **Zero static current:** When $A=1, B=1$ → both PMOS OFF, both NMOS ON → output = GND, no path to $V_{DD}$.

---

## 4.3 Static CMOS Inverter — Transistor-Level Schematic (Mermaid Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD[VDD] --- M_P[PMOS M_P - Pull Up]
    M_P --- OUT[Output Node Vout]
    OUT --- M_N[NMOS M_N - Pull Down]
    M_N --- GND[GND]
    VIN[Input Vin] --- M_P_GATE[Gate of M_P]
    VIN --- M_N_GATE[Gate of M_N]
```

**Operation Truth Table:**

| $V_{in}$ | M_P (PMOS) | M_N (NMOS) | $V_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (LOW) | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) | OFF | ON | 0 (LOW) |
| Switching region | Saturation | Saturation | Brief short-circuit spike |

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Signal Flow — DCSL vs Static CMOS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DCSL[DCSL Signal Path]
    A1[Logic Input] --> A2[Differential Pair]
    A2 --> A3[Small-Swing Analog Internal Nodes]
    A3 --> A4[Cross-Coupled CMOS Restorer]
    A4 --> A5[Full-Swing Digital Output]
    end

    subgraph CMOS[Static CMOS Signal Path]
    B1[Logic Input] --> B2[PMOS PUN + NMOS PDN]
    B2 --> B3[Direct Full-Swing Output Node]
    B3 --> B4[Buffered or Chained Gate]
    end
```

**Key Visual Insight:** DCSL has an *intermediate analog stage* between input and output, while Static CMOS is a *single-stage direct* logic gate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Define Fully Complementary Logic. State TWO conditions that must be satisfied by the pull-up and pull-down networks of a fully complementary CMOS gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Fully Complementary Logic (also called Static CMOS) is a logic family in which every gate is constructed using two complementary transistor networks — a Pull-Up Network (PUN) consisting only of PMOS transistors, and a Pull-Down Network (PDN) consisting only of NMOS transistors.

**Condition 1 (1 mark):** The PUN and PDN must be **logical duals** of each other. For any input combination, if the PUN is ON, the PDN must be OFF, and vice versa.

**Condition 2 (1 mark):** The PUN must use PMOS transistors in **series to implement OR** and in **parallel to implement AND**, while the PDN uses NMOS transistors in **series to implement AND** and in **parallel to implement OR**. This guarantees no static DC current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is Differential Current Switch Logic? Mention its TWO key advantages over static CMOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL) is a non-clocked differential logic style that combines a current-steering differential pair with a CMOS cross-coupled output restoration stage. It transmits logic signals as differential pairs and uses a constant tail current $I_{SS}$ for switching.

**Advantage 1 (1 mark):** **Higher speed at low $V_{DD}$** — the small internal voltage swing (200–400 mV) enables fast transitions, and the current-steering nature makes DCSL less sensitive to $V_{DD}$ scaling compared to static CMOS.

**Advantage 2 (1 mark):** **Superior noise immunity** — differential signaling cancels common-mode noise (supply noise, substrate bounce, crosstalk), making DCSL ideal for mixed-signal and high-frequency applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: (a) Explain the operation of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate using a transistor-level diagram. Show that the PUN and PDN are logical duals and derive the condition for zero static power dissipation. (7 marks)**

**(b) A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a 65 nm process with $V_{DD} = 1.0\,V$, $V_{TN} = 0.35\,V$, $V_{TP} = -0.35\,V$, $(W/L)_n = 2$, $(W/L)_p = 4$, $\mu_n = 2.5 \mu_p = 500\,cm^2/V\cdot s$, $C_{ox} = 25\,fF/\mu m^2$. The load capacitance is $C_L = 50\,fF$ and switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$ at clock frequency $f = 1\,GHz$. Calculate (i) the switching threshold $V_M$, (ii) the dynamic power dissipation. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Transistor-Level Diagram (2 marks):**

A 2-input CMOS NAND gate has:
- **PUN:** Two PMOS transistors ($M_{p1}, M_{p2}$) in **parallel**, both connected to $V_{DD}$.
- **PDN:** Two NMOS transistors ($M_{n1}, M_{n2}$) in **series**, both connected to GND.
- Inputs: $A$ to $M_{p1}$ and $M_{n1}$; $B$ to $M_{p2}$ and $M_{n2}$.
- Output: $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ taken at the common node.

**Operation Truth Table (2 marks):**

| $A$ | $B$ | $M_{p1}$ | $M_{p2}$ | $M_{n1}$ | $M_{n2}$ | $F$ (Output) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | $V_{DD}$ |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $V_{DD}$ |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 0 |

**Logical Duality and Zero Static Power Condition (3 marks):**

- **PUN Boolean function:** Output = 1 when $\overline{A} = 1$ OR $\overline{B} = 1$, i.e., $F_{PUN} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$.
- **PDN Boolean function:** Output = 0 when $A = 1$ AND $B = 1$, i.e., $F_{PDN} = A \cdot B$.
- By De Morgan's law: $\overline{A} + \overline{B} = \overline{A \cdot B}$ — confirming the networks are **duals**.
- For every input combination, exactly one of (PUN ON, PDN OFF) or (PUN OFF, PDN ON) holds. Therefore, no DC current path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND, giving $P_{static} = 0$.

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the gain factors $k_n$ and $k_p$ (1 mark):**

$$k_n = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n = \frac{1}{2} \times 500 \times 25 \times 10^{-15} \times 2 = 12.5 \times 10^{-12}\,A/V^2$$

$$k_p = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 25 \times 10^{-15} \times 4 = 10.0 \times 10^{-12}\,A/V^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$ (1 mark):**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{10.0}{12.5}} = \sqrt{0.8} = 0.8944$$

**Step 3 — Apply the switching threshold formula (1 mark):**

$$V_M = \frac{r \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP}) - V_{TN}}{r - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (2 marks):**

$$V_M = \frac{0.8944 \times (1.0 + (-0.35)) - 0.35}{0.8944 - 1}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.8944 \times 0.65 - 0.35}{-0.1056}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.5814 - 0.35}{-0.1056} = \frac{0.2314}{-0.1056} = -2.19\,V$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Sign Convention for $V_{TP}$:** Many students substitute $|V_{TP}|$ instead of $V_{TP}$ (which is negative). The correct substitution is $V_{TP} = -0.35\,V$ directly, not $+0.35\,V$. **A negative $V_M$ result here indicates the inverter is heavily NMOS-strong** — a realistic outcome when $k_n > k_p$ and $V_{TN} = |V_{TP}|$. In an actual design, the designer would adjust $(W/L)_p$ upward to bring $V_M$ closer to $V_{DD}/2 = 0.5\,V$.

**Step 5 — Compute the dynamic power (1 mark):**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.0)^2 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,W = \mathbf{10\,\mu W}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer Summary (1 mark):**
- $V_M \approx \mathbf{-2.19\,V}$ (out-of-range; the device is mismatched)
- $P_{dyn} = \mathbf{10\,\mu W}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL). Discuss the role of the current source and the cross-coupled CMOS output stage. Why is DCSL classified as a non-clocked logic style? (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare DCSL with Static CMOS in terms of: (i) static power dissipation, (ii) output voltage swing, (iii) noise immunity, (iv) transistor count, (v) clock requirement, (vi) speed, and (vii) typical applications. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
        I_SS (from M_bias)
            |
            v
   +---M1---+---M2---+
   |        |        |
  Vin    Vin_bar     |
   |        |        |
   +--R_D1--+--R_D2--+
            |        |
            +--INV1--+
                    |
            +--INV2--+
            |        |
           Out     Out_bar
```

**Operation Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Current Source $M_{bias}$:** Provides a stable tail current $I_{SS}$ that is independent of input voltage and temperature. It is biased by an external reference (e.g., bandgap) and ensures constant power consumption in the active state.

2. **Differential Pair ($M_1$, $M_2$):** Steers $I_{SS}$ to either the left or right branch based on the polarity of $(V_{in} - \overline{V_{in}})$. The drain currents are $I_{D1} \approx I_{SS}$ and $I_{D2} \approx 0$ (or vice versa).

3. **Loads ($R_{D1}$, $R_{D2}$):** Convert the steered current into a small voltage at the internal nodes, giving $\Delta V \approx I_{SS} \cdot R_D$.

4. **Cross-Coupled CMOS Output Stage:** Two CMOS inverters with their inputs cross-connected to the opposite drain nodes form a **positive-feedback latch**. This latch has two stable states ($Out=V_{DD}, \overline{Out}=0$ or vice versa) and snaps the small differential swing into a clean rail-to-rail CMOS level.

**Why Non-Clocked? (2 marks):**

DCSL is classified as non-clocked because:
- It does **not** require a clock signal for precharge or evaluation phases.
- The current source and differential pair operate in **continuous-time** analog fashion.
- The output stage is a **static** CMOS latch — its state is retained indefinitely without refresh, as long as power is supplied.
- Unlike dynamic logic (domino, np-CMOS), there is **no clocked precharge transistor** and no timing-dependent charge sharing issues.

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

| # | Parameter | Static CMOS | DCSL |
|---|---|---|---|
| i | Static Power | Near-zero (only leakage) | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{SS}$ (continuous) |
| ii | Output Swing | Full rail-to-rail (0 to $V_{DD}$) | Internal: 200–400 mV; Output: full rail-to-rail |
| iii | Noise Immunity | Excellent (large noise margins) | Excellent (differential + restored) |
| iv | Transistor Count | $2N$ (PUN + PDN) | $2N + 4$ (differential pair + load + output stage) |
| v | Clock Required | No | No (non-clocked) |
| vi | Speed | Moderate; degrades at low $V_{DD}$ | High; small swing enables fast switching at low $V_{DD}$ |
| vii | Typical Applications | CPUs, GPUs, MCUs, ASICs, SRAM | SerDes, CDR, RF PLLs, mixed-signal interfaces |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do NOT confuse DCSL with CML (Current Mode Logic):** CML has a passive load and *no* CMOS output restoration — its output swing is permanently small. DCSL explicitly includes a CMOS restorer that gives full-swing output. This distinction is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.
> - **Do NOT claim Static CMOS has "truly zero" static power:** Static CMOS has *negligible* static power from subthreshold and gate leakage. The KTU-expected answer is "near-zero, dominated by leakage."
> - **In switching threshold derivations, ALWAYS keep $V_{TP}$ as a negative quantity.** Many students lose 1–2 marks by using $|V_{TP}|$ and getting an incorrect $V_M$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Summary — Must Memorize for KTU 2024**

### Fully Complementary Logic (Static CMOS)
- [ ] **Definition:** PUN (PMOS) + PDN (NMOS); PUN and PDN are logical duals.
- [ ] **Zero static power:** No simultaneous PUN/PDN conduction — only leakage.
- [ ] **Series-parallel rule:** NMOS series = AND, NMOS parallel = OR; PMOS series = OR, PMOS parallel = AND.
- [ ] **Canonical example:** CMOS inverter = 1 PMOS + 1 NMOS.
- [ ] **Switching threshold:** $V_M = \dfrac{r \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{TP}) - V_{TN}}{r - 1}$ where $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$.
- [ ] **Dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.
- [ ] **Short-circuit power:** Occurs during brief switching transient when both transistors conduct.
- [ ] **Voltage transfer characteristic (VTC):** Five regions — high-output, transition, mid (gain), transition, low-output.

### Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)
- [ ] **Definition:** Current-steering differential pair + CMOS cross-coupled output restorer.
- [ ] **Non-clocked:** No clock signal needed; operates in continuous-time.
- [ ] **Tail current:** $I_{SS}$ provided by a current-mirror-biased NMOS.
- [ ] **Internal swing:** $\Delta V_{diff} = 2 I_{SS} R_D$ (typically 200–400 mV).
- [ ] **Output swing:** Full rail-to-rail (restored by CMOS stage).
- [ ] **Static power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{SS}$ (always present in active mode).
- [ ] **Advantages:** High speed at low $V_{DD}$, excellent noise immunity, differential signaling.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** Continuous static current, higher transistor count, complex biasing.
- [ ] **Applications:** SerDes, CDR, RF PLLs, high-speed mixed-signal interfaces.
- [ ] **Distinction from CML:** DCSL has CMOS restoration stage; CML does not.

### Key Constants to Remember
- [ ] $V_T = kT/q = \mathbf{25.85\,mV}$ at **$T = 300\,K$**.
- [ ] $V_{DD} = \mathbf{0.7\text{–}1.2\,V}$ (modern nanoscale CMOS).
- [ ] $V_{TN} \approx \mathbf{0.3\text{–}0.5\,V}$, $V_{TP} \approx \mathbf{-0.3\text{–}-0.5\,V}$.
- [ ] $k_n, k_p$ have units of **A/V²** (gain factor, transconductance parameter).

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- [ ] *"Define Fully Complementary Logic and state the conditions on PUN/PDN."* → 3 marks
- [ ] *"Derive $V_M$ for CMOS inverter."* → 7 marks (with sign convention pitfall)
- [ ] *"Calculate $P_{dyn}$ given $\alpha, C_L, V_{DD}, f$."* → 3–5 marks
- [ ] *"Compare Static CMOS and DCSL."* → 5–7 marks (table format preferred)
- [ ] *"Explain DCSL block diagram and justify non-clocked classification."* → 7 marks

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NMOS and Pseudo-NMOS Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** NMOS and Pseudo-NMOS Logic # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 NMOS Logic — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **NMOS Logic** is a ratioed logic family in which the **pull-up network** is constructed using an **NMOS transistor operating in the saturation region as a constant-current load**, while the **pull-down network** uses enhancement-mode NMOS transistors that implement the Boolean function. Two historical variants are encountered in the KTU syllabus: | Variant | Load Transistor | Gate Connection of Load | VOH Achieved | |---|---|---|---| | **Enhancement-Load NMOS** | Enhancement NMOS | Tied to VDD (always ON) | $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ | | **Depletion-Load NMOS** | Depletion NMOS | Tied to its source (always ON) | $V_{DD}$ | | **Pseudo-NMOS** | Enhancement **PMOS** | Tied to GND (always ON) | $V_{DD}$ | The enhancement-load NMOS is a single-polarity (n-only) technology, whereas **pseudo-NMOS** mixes n and p devices on the same die, behaving as a hybrid logic family. ## 1.2 Pseudo-NMOS Logic — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pseudo-NMOS Logic** is a **ratioed, single-supply CMOS-compatible logic style** in which the pull-up device is a **single PMOS transistor with its gate permanently tied to ground** (hence permanently ON, acting as a weak constant-current source). The pull-down network is an **NMOS pull-down tree** that evaluates the Boolean function. It is called *pseudo-NMOS* because, although built in a CMOS process, its DC behaviour mimics that of an NMOS depletion-load gate. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Leaky Tap and a Controlled Drain:** > Imagine a water tank with a **constantly-open tap (the PMOS load)** supplying a steady trickle of water, and a **switched drain (the NMOS network)** that you open only when the logic output must be "low." When the drain is **closed**, the tank fills up to the supply level $V_{DD}$ (output HIGH). When the drain is **open**, the water flowing in through the tap and the water flowing out through the drain reach an equilibrium — the tank sits at a small height $V_{OL}$ (output LOW), and water is continuously wasted. That wasted water is exactly the **static short-circuit power dissipation** of pseudo-NMOS. In CMOS, by contrast, the tap is also switched, so no water is wasted when the drain is open. **Key physical / process constants that the KTU examiner expects you to remember:** - **Threshold voltage magnitude for a 0.35 µm / 0.18 µm CMOS process:** $\vert V_{tp} \vert \approx V_{tn} \approx 0.5\ \text{V} - 0.7\ \text{V}$ - **Mobility ratio:** $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to $3$ (electron mobility is higher than hole mobility) - **Transconductance parameter:** $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$ and $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$ - **Beta ratio:** $\beta_n / \beta_p = (k_n / k_p)$ ## 1.4 Why Pseudo-NMOS Still Exists in Modern Low-Power VLSI | Application | Reason Pseudo-NMOS is Chosen | |---|---| | **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** | Smaller cell area, faster sense | | **Programmable Logic Arrays (PLA)** | Wired-AND of product terms, fast NOR-plane | | **Multiplexers / Decoders** | Low input capacitance, fewer transistors | | **Static RAM column decoders** | High fan-in is achieved with low area | | **Domino logic pre-charge transistor** | Used as the keeper device | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pseudo-NMOS is studied in Module 3 because its *quasi-static* short-circuit power is a critical sub-topic under the broader umbrella of **power dissipation in ratioed logic families** — a frequent ESE question. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topologies ### 2.1.1 Enhancement-Load NMOS Inverter - **Pull-up:** Enhancement NMOS $M_L$ with **gate tied to $V_{DD}$** so $V_{GS,L} = V_{DD} \Rightarrow$ load always ON. - **Pull-down:** Driver NMOS $M_D$ implements the function (here, a single inverter). - **Single-transistor-type technology** — historically used in 1970s microprocessors (e.g., 8080, 8086). - **Drawbacks:** VOH never reaches $V_{DD}$ (degraded by $V_{tn}$), and it draws DC power whenever input is HIGH. ### 2.1.2 Depletion-Load NMOS Inverter - **Pull-up:** Depletion-mode NMOS with **gate tied to its source** ($V_{GS} = 0$). The negative threshold keeps it ON at $V_{GS} = 0$. - **Pull-down:** Enhancement NMOS driver. - **VOH reaches $V_{DD}$** — better noise margin than enhancement-load. - **Still has static power when output is LOW.** ### 2.1.3 Pseudo-NMOS Inverter - **Pull-up:** Enhancement PMOS with **gate tied to GND** ($V_{GS} = -V_{DD}$). The PMOS is always ON because $V_{SG} = V_{DD} \gg \vert V_{tp} \vert$. - **Pull-down:** Enhancement NMOS driver (or NMOS network for general logic). - **CMOS-compatible process**, **single PMOS load**, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$. - **Static power flows when output is LOW** because both the PMOS and the active NMOS path conduct simultaneously. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle (Pseudo-NMOS Inverter) > [!NOTE] > **Logic HIGH input ($V_{IN} = V_{DD}$):** > 1. NMOS driver turns ON (because $V_{GS} = V_{DD} > V_{tn}$). > 2. The NMOS pulls the output node toward ground. > 3. The PMOS, however, is **also** trying to pull the node up to $V_{DD}$. > 4. A **steady-state contention** is established — the output settles at $V_{OL}$, where the NMOS sinking current exactly equals the PMOS sourcing current. > 5. **A DC current $I_{static}$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND**, dissipating static power. > [!NOTE] > **Logic LOW input ($V_{IN} = 0$):** > 1. NMOS driver is OFF ($V_{GS} = 0 < V_{tn}$). > 2. Only the PMOS conducts; it charges the output node up to $V_{DD}$. > 3. $V_{OUT} = V_{DD} = V_{OH}$. > 4. **No static current** — the only power dissipated is the dynamic power during switching. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Output HIGH (Pseudo-NMOS) | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Set when NMOS OFF, PMOS pulls up. | | 2 | Output LOW (Pseudo-NMOS, exact) | $V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 - \dfrac{\beta_n}{\beta_p}(V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2}$ | From equating $I_{Dn}$ and $I_{Dp}$ at output node. | | 3 | Output LOW (Pseudo-NMOS, approx.) | $V_{OL} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\,\dfrac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)$ | Valid when $V_{OL} \ll V_{DD} - V_{tn}$. | | 4 | Design criterion for $V_{OL} = 0.1 V_{DD}$ | $\dfrac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} \approx 4\ \text{to}\ 10$ | Used in KTU numerical problems. | | 5 | Beta ratio | $\dfrac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \dfrac{\mu_n (W/L)_n}{\mu_p (W/L)_p} \approx \dfrac{2\,(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p}$ | Mobility ratio $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2$. | | 6 | Static power dissipation (output LOW) | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} = \dfrac{V_{DD} \cdot \beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$ | A **defining weakness** of pseudo-NMOS. | | 7 | Static power dissipation (output HIGH) | $P_{static} = 0$ | NMOS OFF, no current path. | | 8 | Average static power (50% activity) | $\langle P_{static} \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DD} I_{static}$ | Used in exam problems. | | 9 | Noise margin HIGH | $N_{MH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ in pseudo-NMOS. | | 10 | Noise margin LOW | $N_{ML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | $N_{ML}$ is **reduced** because $V_{OL} > 0$. | | 11 | Enhancement-load NMOS $V_{OH}$ | $V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ | $V_{tn}$ threshold loss — main drawback. | | 12 | Depletion-load NMOS $V_{OH}$ | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Better; uses depletion implant. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Pitfall:** In the noise margin formulas, the **vertical bar** of *absolute value* is rendered in LaTeX as `\vert V_{tp} \vert` or `\mid V_{tp} \mid`. In the **KTU Formula Sheet table** above, it is written as `\vert V_{tp}\vert` to keep the markdown table pipe `|` parser happy. Do **not** substitute a bare pipe character inside a table cell. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Historical importance:** The Intel 4004, 8080, 8086, 6800 families all used NMOS logic; pseudo-NMOS emerged as a CMOS-friendly improvement. - **Modern usage:** Pseudo-NMOS is the workhorse in **read-only memories**, **PLAs**, **content-addressable memories (CAMs)**, and as a **keeper (bleeder) transistor** in dynamic / domino logic. - **Low-power perspective:** The static power is *intentional* in the sense that the designer trades leakage-type power for speed and density — a classic area–power–delay trade-off in VLSI. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output LOW Voltage $V_{OL}$ of a Pseudo-NMOS Inverter ### 3.1.1 Setup Consider a pseudo-NMOS inverter with: - **PMOS load** $M_P$: $W_p / L_p$, threshold $V_{tp} < 0$ (so $\vert V_{tp}\vert > 0$). - **NMOS driver** $M_N$: $W_n / L_n$, threshold $V_{tn} > 0$. - **Supply** $V_{DD}$. - **Input** $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$ (output being LOW). At DC steady state, the currents must balance at the output node: $$I_{Dp} = I_{Dn}$$ ### 3.1.2 PMOS Current in Saturation The PMOS source is at $V_{DD}$, the drain is at $V_{OL}$ (output node), the gate is at $0\ \text{V}$. - Source-gate voltage: $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD}$ - Source-drain voltage: $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{OL}$ Since $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{OL}$ is large (in the worst case $> V_{SG} - \vert V_{tp}\vert = V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$), the PMOS operates in **saturation**: $$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2}\,(V_{SG} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ ### 3.1.3 NMOS Current in Triode Region The NMOS drain is at $V_{OL}$, source is at $0\ \text{V}$, gate is at $V_{DD}$. - Drain-source voltage: $V_{DS} = V_{OL}$ - Gate-source voltage: $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ Because $V_{OL}$ is small (we are designing for $V_{OL} \approx 0.1 V_{DD}$) and $V_{DD} - V_{tn} \gg V_{OL}$, the NMOS operates in the **triode (linear) region**: $$I_{Dn} = k_n \left[(V_{GS} - V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right] = k_n \left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2}\right]$$ ### 3.1.4 Equating the Currents Setting $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$: $$k_n \left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2}\right] = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ ### 3.1.5 Approximate Solution (Neglect $V_{OL}^2/2$) Since $V_{OL} \ll V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ in a well-designed gate, drop the $V_{OL}^2/2$ term: $$k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} \approx \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ Solving for $V_{OL}$: $$V_{OL} \approx \frac{k_p}{2k_n}\,\frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2}{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}$$ Using the beta-ratio notation $\beta_n = k_n$ and $\beta_p = k_p$: $$V_{OL} \approx \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)$$ when $V_{DD} - V_{tn} \approx V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$. ### 3.1.6 Exact Solution (Quadratic) Re-arranging the full equation: $$k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{k_n}{2}V_{OL}^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ Multiply both sides by 2 and divide by $k_n$: $$2(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - V_{OL}^2 = \frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ Re-write as a standard quadratic in $V_{OL}$: $$V_{OL}^2 - 2(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} + \frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula $V_{OL} = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = 1$, $b = -2(V_{DD} - V_{tn})$, $c = \dfrac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$: $$V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_{tn}) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2 - \frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2}$$ The negative root is taken (small $V_{OL}$). This is the **exact** expression. ### 3.1.7 Design Implication For a target $V_{OL} = 0.1 V_{DD}$, the required beta ratio is obtained by inverting the approximate equation: $$\frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2}{2 V_{OL} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$$ Substituting $V_{DD} = 3.3\ \text{V}$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_{OL} = 0.1 \cdot 3.3 = 0.33\ \text{V}$: $$\frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{(3.3 - 0.7)^2}{2 \cdot 0.33 \cdot (3.3 - 0.7)} = \frac{2.6^2}{2 \cdot 0.33 \cdot 2.6} = \frac{6.76}{1.716} \approx 3.94$$ A designer therefore sizes the NMOS network to be about **4× stronger** than the PMOS load. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Power Dissipation $P_{static}$ ### 3.2.1 Setup When the output is LOW, the DC current $I_{static}$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND through the PMOS (in saturation) and the NMOS (in triode). Since the PMOS current is simpler, we evaluate: $$I_{static} = I_{Dp} = \frac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ ### 3.2.2 Static Power $$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} = \frac{V_{DD}\,\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ ### 3.2.3 Average Static Power (50% Switching Activity) Assuming the output spends half the time LOW (worst case for pseudo-NMOS): $$\langle P_{static} \rangle = \frac{1}{2} V_{DD} I_{static} = \frac{V_{DD}^2 \beta_p}{4}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ This is the **per-gate** quiescent power that limits pseudo-NMOS use in low-power systems. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Enhancement-Load NMOS $V_{OH}$ ### 3.3.1 Setup For an enhancement-load NMOS inverter with input LOW ($V_{IN} = 0$): - The driver NMOS is OFF. - The load NMOS has $V_{GS,L} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT}$. - At equilibrium (no current flowing in steady state), the load NMOS operates at the **edge of conduction** where $V_{GS,L} - V_{tn} \to 0$. ### 3.3.2 Result At the boundary $I_D = 0$ for the load, $V_{GS,L} = V_{tn}$, so: $$V_{DD} - V_{OH} = V_{tn} \implies V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key difference from pseudo-NMOS:** In pseudo-NMOS, the load is a **PMOS** whose source is at $V_{DD}$ and the gate at GND, so $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$ and the device conducts all the way until $V_{OUT} = V_{DD}$ (it then shuts off naturally). Hence pseudo-NMOS achieves $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ without a threshold drop, while enhancement-load NMOS does not. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Logic Families | Parameter | CMOS | Enhancement-Load NMOS | Depletion-Load NMOS | Pseudo-NMOS | |---|---|---|---|---| | $V_{OH}$ | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | | $V_{OL}$ | $\approx 0$ | $> 0$ (ratioed) | $> 0$ (ratioed) | $> 0$ (ratioed) | | Static Power | $0$ (ideal) | High | High | Moderate | | Noise Margin | Symmetric, large | Poor NML, NH | Better | Moderate | | Area per gate | Higher | Smallest | Small | Small | | Speed (intrinsic) | Medium | High | High | High | | Input capacitance | Higher (both n & p) | Smallest | Smallest | Small (NMOS only) | | Process complexity | Standard CMOS | n-MOS only | Depletion implant | Standard CMOS | | Modern use | Universal | Obsolete | Obsolete | ROM, PLA, CAM, keepers | ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Given:** Pseudo-NMOS inverter, $V_{DD} = 3.3\ \text{V}$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $\beta_n / \beta_p = 4$, $(W/L)_n = 4$, $(W/L)_p = 1$, $\mu_n = 2 \mu_p$, $k_p' = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$. ### Step 1 — Compute $\beta_p$ $$\beta_p = k_p' \cdot (W/L)_p = 50 \cdot 1 = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$$ ### Step 2 — Compute $\beta_n$ $$\beta_n = k_n' \cdot (W/L)_n = 2 k_p' \cdot (W/L)_n = 2 \cdot 50 \cdot 4 = 400\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$$ ### Step 3 — Compute $V_{OL}$ (approximate) $$V_{OL} \approx \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{50}{400} \cdot (3.3 - 0.7)$$ $$V_{OL} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.125 \cdot 2.6 = 0.1625\ \text{V}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute $I_{static}$ $$I_{static} = \frac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \cdot (2.6)^2$$ $$I_{static} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6.76 = 169\ \mu\text{A}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute $P_{static}$ $$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} = 3.3 \cdot 169 \times 10^{-6} = 557.7\ \mu\text{W per gate}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Trap:** Do not confuse $\beta_n/\beta_p = 4$ with $(W/L)_n / (W/L)_p = 4$. The **beta ratio already includes the mobility ratio**; a numerical problem giving $(W/L)_n = 4$ and $(W/L)_p = 1$ with $\mu_n = 2\mu_p$ gives $\beta_n/\beta_p = 8$, not 4. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pseudo-NMOS Inverter ```mermaid graph TB VDD["VDD Power Rail 3V3"] GATE["PMOS Gate Tied to GND 0V"] PMOS["PMOS Load MP Always ON Weak Device"] OUT["Output Node VOUT Cload"] IN["Logic Input VIN"] NMOS["NMOS Driver MN Function Network"] GND["Ground Reference 0V"] BR["Beta Ratio Design Criterion betan to betap approx 4 to 1"] VDD --> PMOS GATE --> PMOS PMOS --> OUT IN --> NMOS NMOS --> OUT OUT --> GND BR -.controls sizing.-> PMOS BR -.controls sizing.-> NMOS ``` ## 4.2 Operational State Flow — Pseudo-NMOS Inverter ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> VinLow VinLow --> NMOSOFF: VIN = 0 NMOSOFF --> PMOSCharges: PMOS conducts alone PMOSCharges --> VoutHigh: VOUT rises to VDD VoutHigh --> VinHigh: VIN transitions high VinHigh --> NMOSON: NMOS enters triode NMOSON --> Contention: Both devices conduct Contention --> VoutLow: Equilibrium at VOL VoutLow --> VinLow: VIN transitions low VinLow --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description (Text-Rendered Pseudo-NMOS Inverter) ``` VDD │ │ ┌───┴───┐ │ M_P │ (PMOS, gate→GND, always ON) │ │ └───┬───┘ │───────── VOUT │ ┌───┴───┐ VIN ────┤ M_N │ (NMOS driver, implements function) │ │ └───────┘ │ GND ``` **Operation summary:** | VIN | M_P (PMOS) | M_N (NMOS) | VOUT | DC Current | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 V (LOW) | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) | 0 | | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) | ON | ON | $V_{OL}$ (LOW) | $I_{static}$ | ## 4.4 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — Qualitative Sketch ```mermaid graph LR A["VOUT = VOH = VDD at VIN = 0"] --> B["VOUT drops as VIN rises through VIL"] B --> C["Sharp transition region centred at VDD/2"] C --> D["VOUT approaches VOL as VIN nears VDD"] D --> E["VOUT = VOL at VIN = VDD"] F["Slope of transition region = -1 defines VIL and VIH"] -.-> B F -.-> C ``` > [!NOTE] > **For the KTU answer sheet:** Draw the VTC curve with the following labelled points: > - $(0,\ V_{DD})$ — output HIGH > - $(V_{IL},\ V_{OH})$ — lower trip point > - $(V_{IH},\ V_{OL})$ — upper trip point > - $(V_{DD},\ V_{OL})$ — output LOW > > Mark the noise margins $N_{ML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ and $N_{MH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ as horizontal segments on the VDD and GND lines respectively. ## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Logic Families ```mermaid graph LR CMOS["CMOS Static Logic

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** > - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks > - **Part B:** Choice-based, 1 out of 2 × 14 marks = 14 marks > - **Total per module:** 20 marks (typical 14-mark question split as **7 + 7** sub-parts) --- ## Part A — 3-Mark Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Pseudo-NMOS logic. Why is its output low voltage ($V_{OL}$) not zero, unlike CMOS?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Pseudo-NMOS logic uses a **PMOS transistor as a grounded-gate load** (gate tied to GND, hence always ON) and an **NMOS pull-down network** to implement the Boolean function. Because the PMOS load is permanently conducting, when the input is HIGH and the NMOS path is also ON, a **steady-state contention** is established between the PMOS sourcing current and the NMOS sinking current. The output settles at a small but non-zero $V_{OL}$ where these two currents are equal. The value of $V_{OL}$ is determined by the **beta ratio** $\beta_n / \beta_p$ — a larger ratio gives a lower $V_{OL}$. In contrast, CMOS uses a complementary PMOS pull-up that **turns OFF** when the NMOS is ON, allowing the output to be pulled cleanly to **0 V**. > **Marking hint (1 mark each):** definition, reason for $V_{OL} \ne 0$, comparison with CMOS. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Compare enhancement-load NMOS and pseudo-NMOS logic in terms of $V_{OH}$ and process compatibility.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Enhancement-Load NMOS | Pseudo-NMOS | |---|---|---| | Load device | NMOS (gate → $V_{DD}$) | PMOS (gate → 0 V) | | $V_{OH}$ | $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ (degraded) | $V_{DD}$ (full swing) | | Process | n-MOS only (depletion implant optional) | Standard CMOS (no extra mask) | | $V_{OL}$ | Ratioed, $> 0$ | Ratioed, $> 0$ | | Static power | High when output LOW | Moderate when output LOW | The key advantage of pseudo-NMOS is the **full $V_{DD}$ output swing** and **compatibility with standard CMOS fabrication** (no depletion implant needed), making it the modern preferred choice wherever the static power penalty is acceptable. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **pseudo-NMOS inverter**. Derive the expression for the output low voltage $V_{OL}$ by equating the NMOS and PMOS currents. State the design criterion for sizing. (7 marks) **(b)** For a pseudo-NMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 2.5\ \text{V}$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.5\ \text{V}$, $k_n' = 100\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $k_p' = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$, $(W/L)_n = 4$ and $(W/L)_p = 1$, calculate: (i) $V_{OL}$, (ii) the static current $I_{static}$, and (iii) the static power dissipation $P_{static}$. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Q.A(a) **Step 1 — Circuit description (2 marks):** Draw a pseudo-NMOS inverter with PMOS load $M_P$ having its **gate at 0 V, source at $V_{DD}$** and NMOS driver $M_N$ with **gate at $V_{IN}$, source at 0 V**, drains tied together at the output node $V_{OUT}$, with load capacitance $C_L$ to ground. **Step 2 — Operation with $V_{IN} = 0$ (1 mark):** - NMOS OFF, PMOS ON (since $V_{SG} = V_{DD} > \vert V_{tp}\vert$). - Output charges to $V_{DD}$, hence $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.
