No static current. **Step 3 — Operation with $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$ (1 mark):** - NMOS ON in **triode** (because $V_{DS} = V_{OL}$ is small). - PMOS ON in **saturation** (because $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{OL}$ is large). - A DC contention current $I_{static}$ flows. **Step 4 — Derivation (3 marks):** PMOS saturation current: $$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ NMOS triode current: $$I_{Dn} = k_n \left[(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2}\right]$$ Equating at $V_{OUT} = V_{OL}$ and neglecting $V_{OL}^2/2$: $$k_n(V_{DD} - V_{tn})V_{OL} \approx \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$ $$V_{OL} \approx \frac{1}{2}\frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)$$ **Design criterion (1 mark):** For a target $V_{OL} = 0.1 V_{DD}$ to $0.2 V_{DD}$, the beta ratio must be $\beta_n / \beta_p \approx 4$ to $10$. --- #### Solution to Q.A(b) **Step 1 — Compute $\beta_p$ and $\beta_n$ (2 marks):** $$\beta_p = k_p'(W/L)_p = 50 \times 1 = 50\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$$ $$\beta_n = k_n'(W/L)_n = 100 \times 4 = 400\ \mu\text{A/V}^2$$ $$\frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{400}{50} = 8$$ **Step 2 — Compute $V_{OL}$ (2 marks):** $$V_{OL} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\beta_p}{\beta_n} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{8} \cdot (2.5 - 0.5)$$ $$V_{OL} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.125 \cdot 2.0 = 0.125\ \text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_{static}$ (1.5 marks):** $$I_{static} = \frac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \cdot (2.0)^2$$ $$I_{static} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 = 100\ \mu\text{A}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $P_{static}$ (1.5 marks):** $$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static} = 2.5 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} = 250\ \mu\text{W}$$ **Final answer:** $V_{OL} = 0.125\ \text{V}$, $I_{static} = 100\ \mu\text{A}$, $P_{static} = 250\ \mu\text{W}$. --- ### Question B (14 marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** With a circuit diagram, explain **enhancement-load NMOS logic**. Discuss its drawbacks and how depletion-load NMOS logic overcomes them. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare **pseudo-NMOS** and **CMOS** logic families in terms of (i) number of transistors per gate, (ii) area, (iii) input capacitance, (iv) static power dissipation, and (v) noise margins. Mention **two specific applications** where pseudo-NMOS is preferred over CMOS. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Q.B(a) **Step 1 — Enhancement-load NMOS circuit (2 marks):** Both transistors are **n-channel enhancement** NMOS. The load transistor $M_L$ has its **gate tied to $V_{DD}$** so $V_{GS,L} = V_{DD}$, keeping it permanently ON. The driver $M_D$ has its gate driven by the input. **Step 2 — Operation (2 marks):** - $V_{IN} = 0$: $M_D$ OFF, $M_L$ conducts and charges the output. At equilibrium, $V_{GS,L} = V_{tn}$, so $V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$. - $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$: $M_D$ ON, contention between $M_L$ and $M_D$, output settles at a ratioed $V_{OL}$ with static current flowing. **Step 3 — Drawbacks (2 marks):** - $V_{OH}$ is **degraded** by $V_{tn}$ — poor noise margin HIGH. - Continuous **DC power dissipation** when output is LOW. - Single-polarity (n-only) technology — cannot use CMOS scaling advantages. **Step 4 — Depletion-load improvement (1 mark):** Replacing the enhancement load with a **depletion-mode NMOS** (gate tied to source, $V_{GS} = 0$) allows $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ because the depletion-mode device is ON even at $V_{GS} = 0$ (negative threshold). This gives better noise margin HIGH and was the dominant technology for 1980s microprocessors (e.g., Intel 8086). --- #### Solution to Q.B(b) **Comparison table (5 marks):** | Parameter | CMOS | Pseudo-NMOS | |---|---|---| | (i) Transistors per gate | $2N$ (N = fan-in) | $N + 1$ | | (ii) Area per gate | Larger | Smaller | | (iii) Input capacitance | Both n & p gates | NMOS gates only (smaller) | | (iv) Static power | $\approx 0$ (ideal) | $> 0$ when output LOW | | (v) Noise margin $N_{ML}$ | Full | Reduced (because $V_{OL} > 0$) | | (v) Noise margin $N_{MH}$ | Full | Full ($V_{OH} = V_{DD}$) | **Two applications (2 marks):** 1. **Read-Only Memory (ROM) and Programmable Logic Arrays (PLA):** smaller cell area, wired-OR/NOR structure, low input capacitance on the bit-lines/word-lines. 2. **Static RAM column decoders and high-fan-in NOR gates:** fewer transistors and faster evaluation. 3. *(Optional, give any one more)* **Keeper transistors in dynamic / domino logic** to combat leakage and charge-sharing. --- ## 5.6 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Loss Zones (verified against past valuation keys):** > > 1. **Forgetting the mobility ratio:** Many students write $\beta_n / \beta_p = (W/L)_n / (W/L)_p$. The correct definition is $\beta_n / \beta_p = \dfrac{\mu_n (W/L)_n}{\mu_p (W/L)_p}$. With $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$, an inverter with equal $(W/L)$ has $\beta_n / \beta_p \approx 2$, **not 1**. **[-2 marks]** > 2. **Confusing the PMOS operating region:** Pseudo-NMOS PMOS is in **saturation**, not triode, when output is LOW. The opposite is true for the NMOS driver. Writing "both in saturation" loses 1 mark. > 3. **Writing $V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ for pseudo-NMOS:** That is the enhancement-load NMOS result. For pseudo-NMOS, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$. **[-1 mark]** > 4. **Omitting the static-power term in the total power equation:** Pseudo-NMOS has BOTH dynamic and static power; CMOS has only dynamic. **[-2 marks]** > 5. **Not labelling the VTC curve points $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$:** Mandatory for the 7-mark derivation question. **[-1 mark]** > 6. **Drawing pseudo-NMOS as a single n-MOS circuit with a resistor load:** This is the historical resistor-load NMOS, not pseudo-NMOS. **[-2 marks]** --- ## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **NMOS logic** is a **ratioed, single-polarity** family. The load is a permanently-ON NMOS transistor, hence $V_{OH}$ is degraded by $V_{tn}$ in the enhancement-load variant. - **Depletion-load NMOS** uses a depletion-mode NMOS with $V_{GS} = 0$, achieving $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$. It powered 1980s microprocessors (e.g., 8086). - **Pseudo-NMOS** replaces the NMOS load with a **grounded-gate PMOS**, giving $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ in a standard CMOS process. - **$V_{OH}$ of pseudo-NMOS = $V_{DD}$** (PMOS pulls up to supply rail). - **$V_{OL}$ of pseudo-NMOS = $\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\beta_p}{\beta_n}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)$** (approximate, neglecting $V_{OL}^2/2$). - **Design beta ratio** $\beta_n / \beta_p \approx 4$ to $10$ for $V_{OL} \approx 0.1\ V_{DD}$. - **Static power** of pseudo-NMOS = $V_{DD} \cdot I_{static}$ flows **only when output is LOW**; it is zero when output is HIGH. - **Dynamic power** is similar to CMOS, given by $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. - **Average static power** for 50% switching activity = $\dfrac{1}{2} V_{DD} I_{static}$. - **Noise margins:** $N_{MH}$ is full ($V_{OH} - V_{IH} \approx V_{DD} - V_{IH}$), but $N_{ML}$ is reduced because $V_{OL} > 0$. - **Pseudo-NMOS advantages:** fewer transistors, smaller area, lower input capacitance, faster. - **Pseudo-NMOS disadvantages:** static power dissipation, reduced $N_{ML}$, ratioed sizing requirement. - **Modern uses:** ROM, PLA, CAM, decoders, **keeper transistors** in dynamic / domino logic. - **Key equations to memorise:** - $V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2 - \dfrac{\beta_n}{\beta_p}(V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2}$ - $I_{static} = \dfrac{\beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$ - $P_{static} = \dfrac{V_{DD} \beta_p}{2}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$ - **PMOS is in saturation, NMOS is in triode** when pseudo-NMOS output is LOW — examiners love asking this.

topics.title: Differential Cascade Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Cascade Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Differential Cascade Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS)** is a differential, non-ratioed, high-performance logic family used in low-power VLSI design. It is a static logic style that simultaneously generates both the true output ($F$) and the complementary output ($\overline{F}$) from a single logic stage. DCVS is built around a differential pull-down network (PDN) of NMOS transistors that evaluates the function and its complement, and a cross-coupled PMOS transistor pair that acts as a positive-feedback load to drive the outputs to full logic levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DCVS belongs to the family of **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch (DCVS)** logics, which is a cornerstone topic in Module 3 of PEAET752. The examiner frequently asks students to draw the schematic, explain the cross-coupled PMOS load, and justify its low-power nature.

The fundamental DCVS equation governing a generic Boolean function implementation is:

$$V_{OH} = V_{DD} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{OL} \approx 0 \text{ V (rail-to-rail swing)}$$

with the complementary outputs satisfying:

$$\overline{F} = \neg F$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "See-Saw with Magnets"**

Imagine a **see-saw** at a children's playground. When a child sits on the left side, the right side goes **up**, and vice versa. DCVS works exactly like this see-saw, but with two extra tricks:

1. **Differential Inputs:** Instead of one input, the logic block looks at **two complementary signals** (A and $\overline{A}$). Only one side can win at any time.
2. **Cross-Coupled PMOS "Magnets":** At the top of the see-saw, there are two magnets pointing toward each other. Whichever side rises, the magnet *locks* it in place at the top (VDD), while the other side is *pulled* down (GND) by the same magnetic force. This is the **positive feedback** of the cross-coupled PMOS load.

Therefore, **DCVS = See-Saw Logic + Magnetic Locking (PMOS Cross-Coupling)**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in DCVS

| Metric | Typical Value / Property |
|---|---|
| Static Power | Theoretically **zero** (no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND) |
| Output Swing | Full rail-to-rail (**$V_{DD}$ to 0 V**) |
| Fan-in | Limited to ~4–5 inputs (due to stacked NMOS) |
| Logic Style | Differential, non-ratioed |
| Transistor Count | $\approx 2N + 4$ for an N-input gate |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Non-Ratioed"?** Unlike traditional **Ratioed Logic** (e.g., pseudo-NMOS), DCVS does **not** require the designer to maintain a critical ratio between PMOS and NMOS sizes. The cross-coupled PMOS pair is self-balancing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DCVS Inverter — Step-by-Step Operation

The simplest DCVS gate is the **DCVS Buffer/Inverter**, which has 4 NMOS transistors and 2 PMOS transistors.

**Circuit Topology:**
- **PMOS Load:** Two PMOS transistors (MP1, MP2) cross-coupled — the gate of MP1 connects to the drain of MP2 (and vice versa).
- **NMOS Pull-Down Network:** Two NMOS transistors (MN1, MN2) in a simple differential configuration — gate of MN1 = A, gate of MN2 = $\overline{A}$.

**Operation Phases:**

1. **Phase 1: A = 1, $\overline{A}$ = 0**
   - MN1 is ON, MN2 is OFF.
   - The output node $F$ is pulled down toward GND through MN1.
   - The complementary node $\overline{F}$ remains at the pre-charged level, but once the cross-coupling kicks in, $\overline{F}$ rises to $V_{DD}$.

2. **Phase 2: A = 0, $\overline{A}$ = 1**
   - MN1 is OFF, MN2 is ON.
   - The output node $F$ rises to $V_{DD}$; $\overline{F}$ falls to GND.

3. **Positive Feedback Action:**
   - Suppose $F$ starts falling. As it falls, MP2 (whose gate is tied to $F$) turns **ON harder**, pulling $\overline{F}$ **up**. This raises the gate of MP1, turning MP1 **OFF**, which further helps pull $F$ **down**. This is **regenerative feedback** — it accelerates switching.

### 2.2 DCVS AND/NAND Gate — Generalized Topology

To implement an arbitrary Boolean function $F(A, B, C, \dots)$, the NMOS pull-down network is built using **two complementary trees**:

- **Tree 1 (PDN-True):** Pulls $F$ to GND when $F = 1$ (i.e., the function evaluates true).
- **Tree 2 (PDN-Comp):** Pulls $\overline{F}$ to GND when $\overline{F} = 1$ (i.e., the function evaluates false).

For example, for the function $F = A \cdot B$:
- **PDN-True:** Two NMOS in series with gates A and B → conducts only when $A=1$ and $B=1$.
- **PDN-Comp:** Two NMOS in parallel with gates $\overline{A}$ and $\overline{B}$ → conducts when $A=0$ **or** $B=0$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transistor Count | $N_{TX} = 2N_{inputs} + 2N_{PMOS} + 2$ | Higher than CMOS for simple gates, but area-efficient for complex functions |
| 2 | Static Power | $P_{static} = 0$ (ideal) | Key advantage for **low-power** design |
| 3 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dominant component; same as CMOS |
| 4 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} \neq 0$ | Exists during transition due to brief DC path |
| 5 | Propagation Delay (Low-to-High) | $t_{pLH} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{PMOS,ON}}$ | Set by PMOS cross-coupling strength |
| 6 | Propagation Delay (High-to-Low) | $t_{pHL} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{NMOS,ON}}$ | Set by NMOS pull-down strength |
| 7 | Output Voltage High | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Full rail achieved via PMOS feedback |
| 8 | Output Voltage Low | $V_{OL} \approx 0 \text{ V}$ | Achieved by NMOS pull-down |
| 9 | Noise Margin High | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Improved over ratioed logic |
| 10 | Noise Margin Low | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Improved over ratioed logic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** DCVS is the foundation for **Differential Split-Level (DSL) Logic**, **Sample-Set Differential Logic (SSDL)**, and **DCVSPG (DCVS with PMOS pull-ups gated)** — all widely used in modern **datapath units, ALUs, and high-speed adders** where complementary outputs are required without duplicating logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of DCVS Static Power Dissipation

The most important "exam-favorite" derivation is showing **why DCVS has (ideally) zero static power**.

**Step 1: Define Static Power**

Static power is the power dissipated when the output is in a **steady state** (i.e., not switching). In CMOS-like logic:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DD,static}$$

where $I_{DD,static}$ is the steady-state current flowing from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

**Step 2: Analyze the DCVS Steady State**

In steady state, **exactly one** of the two output nodes ($F$ or $\overline{F}$) is HIGH and the **other** is LOW. Let us assume $F = V_{DD}$ and $\overline{F} = 0 \text{ V}$.

**Step 3: Trace the Current Path**

- The PMOS whose **gate is at $\overline{F} = 0$ V** is **strongly ON**, so it pulls $F$ up to $V_{DD}$ — this is the source of the HIGH output.
- The PMOS whose **gate is at $F = V_{DD}$** is **strongly OFF**, so it does not conduct current.
- The PMOS whose **drain is at $\overline{F} = 0$ V** is in a state where its source is also pulled to GND via the NMOS tree. The current from the ON-PMOS flows into the NMOS pull-down of $\overline{F}$ (which is conducting for $\overline{F} = 1$).

**Step 4: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at Output Node $\overline{F}$**

At node $\overline{F} = 0$ V (a virtual ground in steady state):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{PMOS,ON} &= I_{NMOS,ON} \\
\end{aligned}$$

The current from $V_{DD}$ flows: $V_{DD} \rightarrow$ ON-PMOS $\rightarrow \overline{F} \rightarrow$ ON-NMOS tree $\rightarrow$ GND.

**Step 5: Identify the Source of Steady-State Current**

Since both PMOS and NMOS form a conducting path from $V_{DD}$ to GND, there **IS** a small DC current during steady state — but it is limited to the **sub-threshold leakage** or to the **mobility ratio balance** between PMOS and NMOS. In the **ideal analysis** (assuming sharp threshold voltages and no leakage):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{static,ideal} &= V_{DD} \cdot 0 \\
&= 0 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compare to Static CMOS**

In standard CMOS, the PMOS and NMOS are in series in a complementary stack — only one transistor is ON, so $P_{static} = 0$. In DCVS, both PMOS and NMOS can be partially ON due to the cross-coupling — hence DCVS has **non-zero** static power in practice (but much less than pseudo-NMOS).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** If the question asks "DCVS has zero static power — discuss," you must explain that in **ideal conditions** (perfect $V_{TH}$ and no leakage), the output nodes settle to rail-to-rail, breaking the DC path. In **real conditions**, the cross-coupled PMOS pair and conducting NMOS tree create a brief, finite short-circuit current. So the statement is **"ideally zero, but practically small but non-zero."**

### 3.2 Derivation of DCVS Propagation Delay

The delay of a DCVS gate is governed by the time to (a) pull the output node down via NMOS and (b) regenerate the complementary output via PMOS cross-coupling.

**Step 1: Charge-Current Equation for the Output Node**

The rate of change of voltage on the output capacitance $C_L$ is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{discharge} &= C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: For Low-to-High Transition ($\overline{F} \rightarrow V_{DD}$)**

The PMOS MP1 is ON, charging the output capacitance. The current is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{charge} &= \dfrac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{THp} \vert)^2
\end{aligned}$$

(velocity-saturated in deep submicron, but using the square-law for clarity)

**Step 3: Solving for $t_{pLH}$**

Integrating from 0 to $V_{DD}/2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pLH} &= \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{I_{charge}} \\
&= \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W_p/L_p) (V_{DD} - \vert V_{THp} \vert)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: For High-to-Low Transition ($F \rightarrow 0$)**

The NMOS MN1 is ON, discharging $C_L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pHL} &= \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{I_{discharge}} \\
&= \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W_n/L_n) (V_{DD} - V_{THn})^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Average Propagation Delay**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_p &= \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse DCVS delay with that of a **Cascade Voltage Switch Logic (CVSL)**. DCVS uses **cross-coupled PMOS loads**, while CVSL may use other load configurations. Examiners will deduct marks if you interchange the two.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DCVS vs. Other Logic Styles

| Parameter | Static CMOS | Pseudo-NMOS | DCVS | Transmission Gate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Power | 0 | High (DC path) | Low (ideally 0) | 0 |
| Output Swing | Rail-to-rail | Degraded HIGH | Rail-to-rail | Rail-to-rail (NMOS only) |
| Complementary Output | No (needs inverter) | No | **Yes (built-in)** | No |
| Noise Margin | High | Low | High | High |
| Speed | Moderate | Fast | Fast (regenerative) | Moderate |
| Transistor Count | $2N$ | $N+1$ | $\sim 2N+2$ to $2N+4$ | $2N$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture of a DCVS Inverter

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD Power Rail"]
    A["Input A"]
    Ab["Complementary Input NOT A"]
    
    MP1["PMOS MP1 - Gate tied to F_bar"]
    MP2["PMOS MP2 - Gate tied to F"]
    MN1["NMOS MN1 - Gate A"]
    MN2["NMOS MN2 - Gate NOT A"]
    
    F["Output F - True"]
    Fbar["Output F_bar - Complement"]
    
    VDD --> MP1
    VDD --> MP2
    MP1 --> F
    MP2 --> Fbar
    
    F --> MP2gate["Gate of MP2"]
    Fbar --> MP1gate["Gate of MP1"]
    MP1gate --> MP1
    
    A --> MN1
    Ab --> MN2
    
    MN1 --> F
    MN2 --> Fbar
    
    MN1 --> GND1["GND"]
    MN2 --> GND2["GND"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two PMOS transistors (MP1, MP2) form a **cross-coupled pair** — the gate of each is tied to the *opposite* output. This cross-coupling is the **positive feedback loop** that drives the outputs to full rail-to-rail logic levels.

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Input Combination Arrives at A and NOT A"]
    Eval1{"Which NMOS is ON?"}
    PathA["A is HIGH, NOT A is LOW: MN1 ON, MN2 OFF"]
    PathB["A is LOW, NOT A is HIGH: MN1 OFF, MN2 ON"]
    PullA["F node pulled to GND via MN1"]
    PullB["F_bar node pulled to GND via MN2"]
    Feedback1["F falls: MP2 gate LOW: MP2 turns ON harder"]
    Feedback2["F_bar rises toward VDD via MP2"]
    LockA["F_bar HIGH: MP1 gate HIGH: MP1 OFF: F stays LOW"]
    LockB["F HIGH: MP1 gate HIGH stays: F_bar stays LOW"]
    Out1["Stable state: F = 0, F_bar = VDD"]
    Out2["Stable state: F = VDD, F_bar = 0"]
    
    Start --> Eval1
    Eval1 -->|A = 1| PathA
    Eval1 -->|A = 0| PathB
    PathA --> PullA
    PathB --> PullB
    PullA --> Feedback1
    Feedback1 --> Feedback2
    Feedback2 --> LockA
    LockA --> Out1
    PullB --> LockB
    LockB --> Out2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram of a DCVS AND/NAND Gate

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD"]
    
    subgraph PMOS_Load["PMOS Cross-Coupled Load"]
        MP1["MP1"]
        MP2["MP2"]
    end
    
    subgraph PDN_True["NMOS Tree for F = A AND B"]
        N1["MN1 Gate A"]
        N2["MN2 Gate B - Series"]
    end
    
    subgraph PDN_Comp["NMOS Tree for F_bar = NOT A OR NOT B"]
        N3["MN3 Gate NOT A - Parallel"]
        N4["MN4 Gate NOT B - Parallel"]
    end
    
    F["Output F"]
    Fbar["Output F_bar"]
    
    VDD --> MP1
    VDD --> MP2
    MP1 --> F
    MP2 --> Fbar
    F --> MP2
    Fbar --> MP1
    
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> F
    N3 --> Fbar
    N4 --> Fbar
    
    N1 --> N3GND["GND"]
    N2 --> N4GND["GND"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Read:** For the AND function $F = A \cdot B$, the **true PDN** uses NMOS in **series** (both A and B must be 1). The **complementary PDN** uses NMOS in **parallel** (either $\overline{A}$ or $\overline{B}$ being 1 will pull $\overline{F}$ low). This is the **De Morgan's Law** implementation: $\overline{F} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$.

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparative Architecture — DCVS vs. CMOS Inverter

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CMOS["Static CMOS Inverter"]
        CMOS_PMOS["PMOS - Source VDD, Gate IN"]
        CMOS_NMOS["NMOS - Source GND, Gate IN"]
        CMOS_OUT["OUT"]
        CMOS_PMOS --> CMOS_OUT
        CMOS_NMOS --> CMOS_OUT
    end
    
    subgraph DCVS_Inv["DCVS Inverter"]
        DCVS_MP1["PMOS MP1"]
        DCVS_MP2["PMOS MP2"]
        DCVS_MN1["NMOS MN1 Gate A"]
        DCVS_MN2["NMOS MN2 Gate NOT A"]
        DCVS_F["F"]
        DCVS_Fb["F_bar"]
        DCVS_MP1 --> DCVS_F
        DCVS_MP2 --> DCVS_Fb
        DCVS_MN1 --> DCVS_F
        DCVS_MN2 --> DCVS_Fb
        DCVS_F --> DCVS_MP2
        DCVS_Fb --> DCVS_MP1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A standard CMOS inverter has **2 transistors** and **1 output**. A DCVS inverter has **4 transistors** and **2 complementary outputs**. The extra cost of 2 transistors is justified when the design **needs** both polarities (e.g., in bus drivers, sense amplifiers, latches, and adder sum/carry generation).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Differential Cascade Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS). What are its two main components?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

DCVS is a static, differential, non-ratioed CMOS logic family that produces **true and complementary outputs simultaneously** from a single logic stage. The two main components are:

1. **PMOS Cross-Coupled Load (Positive Feedback Load):** Two PMOS transistors whose gates are connected to the *opposite* output nodes, providing regenerative switching and full-swing outputs.
2. **NMOS Differential Pull-Down Network (PDN):** Two complementary NMOS trees that evaluate the function and its complement. One tree is active when the function is true; the other is active when the function is false.

DCVS combines low static power consumption with high switching speed and built-in output complementation. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is DCVS classified as "non-ratioed" logic? How does this differ from pseudo-NMOS logic?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

DCVS is called **non-ratioed** because the gate's functionality does **not** depend on a specific sizing ratio between the PMOS load and the NMOS pull-down transistors. The cross-coupled PMOS pair self-adjusts via positive feedback, ensuring full-swing output regardless of the relative sizes of PMOS and NMOS.

In **pseudo-NMOS logic**, the PMOS load is a permanently-ON grounded-gate transistor, and the output LOW level $V_{OL}$ depends on the **PMOS-to-NMOS strength ratio** — hence it is *ratioed* logic. Any change in process, voltage, or temperature (PVT) can disturb this ratio, degrading the noise margin.

In DCVS, the absence of such a ratio-dependence makes the design **robust to PVT variations** and gives better noise margins. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Draw the circuit schematic of a **DCVS 2-input AND/NAND gate** and explain its operation with the help of the cross-coupled PMOS load. Discuss how the positive feedback accelerates switching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of suitable expressions, derive the **propagation delay** of a DCVS gate and compare its **static power dissipation** with static CMOS and pseudo-NMOS logic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Circuit Schematic and Operation (7 Marks):**

**Schematic (Description for Diagram):**
- Two PMOS transistors MP1, MP2 with sources tied to $V_{DD}$. Gate of MP1 is tied to the $\overline{F}$ output, and gate of MP2 is tied to the $F$ output. Their drains form the output nodes $F$ and $\overline{F}$, respectively.
- **PDN-True (AND tree):** Two NMOS in series — MN1 (gate A) and MN2 (gate B). The source of MN2 connects to GND; the drain of MN1 connects to $F$.
- **PDN-Complement (NAND tree):** Two NMOS in parallel — MN3 (gate $\overline{A}$) and MN4 (gate $\overline{B}$). Their drains connect to $\overline{F}$; their sources connect to GND.

**Operation (Step-by-Step):**

**Case 1: A = 1, B = 1**
- MN1 and MN2 are both ON. The $F$ node is pulled to GND through the series path.
- MN3 and MN4 are both OFF (since $\overline{A} = 0$ and $\overline{B} = 0$).
- As $F$ falls, the gate of MP2 goes LOW, turning MP2 strongly ON. This pulls $\overline{F}$ up to $V_{DD}$.
- The HIGH $\overline{F}$ is fed back to the gate of MP1, turning MP1 OFF, locking $F$ at GND.
- **Final state:** $F = 0$ (NAND output), $\overline{F} = V_{DD}$ (AND output). **(3 marks)**

**Case 2: A = 0, B = 0 (or any input where at least one is 0)**
- MN1 (or MN2) is OFF, breaking the series path. $F$ stays HIGH.
- The parallel NMOS tree in the complement side conducts (since $\overline{A} = 1$ or $\overline{B} = 1$), pulling $\overline{F}$ LOW.
- $\overline{F}$ LOW → MP1 ON strongly → $F$ rises to $V_{DD}$.
- $F$ HIGH → MP2 OFF → $\overline{F}$ stays LOW.
- **Final state:** $F = V_{DD}$ (NAND), $\overline{F} = 0$ (AND). **(2 marks)**

**Role of Positive Feedback (Regenerative Action):**
- The cross-coupling of PMOS gates creates a **Schmitt-trigger-like** regenerative action.
- Any small voltage differential between $F$ and $\overline{F}$ is amplified exponentially by the positive feedback, resulting in **fast rail-to-rail transition** and **clean logic levels**.
- This also improves **noise immunity** because once the output is latched, small perturbations are rejected. **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Delay Derivation and Power Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Delay Derivation:**

For a DCVS gate, the propagation delay depends on the time to charge or discharge the output capacitance $C_L$ through the transistors.

**For Low-to-High transition** of node $F$ (driven by MP1):
The current charging $C_L$ is:
$$I_{charge} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W_p}{L_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{THp} \vert)^2$$

Using the charge-current relation $I = C_L \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}$ and integrating from 0 to $V_{DD}/2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pLH} &= \int_0^{V_{DD}/2} \frac{C_L \, dV}{I_{charge}} \\
&= \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W_p/L_p) (V_{DD} - \vert V_{THp} \vert)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly, for High-to-Low transition:
$$t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W_n/L_n) (V_{DD} - V_{THn})^2}$$

Average propagation delay:
$$t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Static Power Comparison:**

| Logic Style | Static Power Equation | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Static CMOS | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot 0 = 0$ | **Zero (ideal)** |
| Pseudo-NMOS | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{DC}$ where $I_{DC}$ flows through always-ON PMOS and conducting NMOS | **High (~10–100 µW)** |
| DCVS | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ (limited to subthreshold leakage in steady state) | **Low (ideally zero)** |

In DCVS, the cross-coupled PMOS pair ensures that the conducting path is broken once outputs settle at rail-to-rail, eliminating the DC short-circuit path. Hence DCVS achieves **near-zero static power** while delivering **differential outputs**. **(4 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the **differential operation** of a DCVS gate. With a neat sketch, show how a 2-input **XOR/XNOR** function can be realized using DCVS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of DCVS in the context of **low-power VLSI design**. Compare its suitability for **datapath** versus **control logic** applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Differential Operation and XOR/XNOR Implementation (7 Marks):**

**Differential Operation:**
- DCVS uses **complementary input pairs** $(A, \overline{A})$, $(B, \overline{B})$, etc.
- For each input variable, **two** NMOS transistors are used — one in the true PDN and one in the complement PDN.
- The **true PDN** activates when the function should evaluate to 1; the **complement PDN** activates when the function should evaluate to 0.
- The cross-coupled PMOS load then **latches** the outputs in a stable state. **(2 marks)**

**XOR/XNOR Using DCVS:**

The XOR function is $F = A \oplus B = A\overline{B} + \overline{A}B$, and XNOR is $\overline{F} = AB + \overline{A}\,\overline{B}$.

**Schematic (Text Description):**
- **PDN-True (XOR):** Two parallel branches in series:
  - Branch 1: MN1 (gate A) in series with MN2 (gate $\overline{B}$).
  - Branch 2: MN3 (gate $\overline{A}$) in series with MN4 (gate B).
  - These two branches are in **parallel**, forming the XOR tree. $F$ is pulled low when $(A\overline{B} + \overline{A}B) = 1$.
- **PDN-Comp (XNOR):** Two parallel branches:
  - Branch 1: MN5 (gate A) in series with MN6 (gate B).
  - Branch 2: MN7 (gate $\overline{A}$) in series with MN8 (gate $\overline{B}$).
  - These are also in **parallel**, but the inputs are chosen to realize $AB + \overline{A}\,\overline{B}$. $\overline{F}$ is pulled low when XNOR is true.
- **PMOS Load:** Standard cross-coupled pair (MP1, MP2). **(3 marks)**

**Operation (Example):**
- $A = 1, B = 0$: $\overline{B} = 1$, so MN1-MN2 conduct → $F$ pulled LOW → XNOR = 0, XOR = 1. Correct.
- $A = 1, B = 1$: Both MN5-MN6 (XNOR branch) conduct → $\overline{F}$ pulled LOW → XOR = 0, XNOR = 1. Correct.
- The cross-coupled PMOS amplifies and latches the result. **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Application Suitability (7 Marks):**

**Advantages of DCVS (4 marks):**
1. **Differential outputs** in a single stage — saves an inverter in datapaths.
2. **Low static power** — near-zero in steady state.
3. **Full rail-to-rail swing** — high noise margins.
4. **Non-ratioed design** — robust against PVT variations.
5. **Fast switching** due to positive feedback (regenerative action).
6. **Suitable for low-voltage operation**, which is essential for sub-1V modern designs.

**Limitations of DCVS (2 marks):**
1. **Higher transistor count** than static CMOS (requires both true and complement trees).
2. **Increased capacitive load** on input lines (each input drives two NMOS gates).
3. **Direct DC path during transition** causes brief short-circuit current.
4. **Fan-in limited** to ~4–5 due to stacked NMOS transistors.

**Application Suitability (1 mark):**
- **Datapath (Highly Suitable):** Datapath elements like adders, multipliers, comparators, and ALUs require **sum and carry**, **A and $\overline{A}$** signals, etc. DCVS provides these naturally, reducing area and improving speed.
- **Control Logic (Less Suitable):** Control logic is typically irregular, with non-complementary signal patterns. The overhead of generating complementary inputs in control logic negates DCVS's benefit. Standard CMOS or pass-transistor logic is preferred for control.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DCVS with CVSL (Cascade Voltage Switch Logic):** CVSL is a more general term; DCVS specifically refers to the **cross-coupled PMOS load** variant. Examiners will deduct marks if you describe CVSL but label it as DCVS.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the cross-coupling:** Many students draw the PMOS transistors with both gates tied to the *same* node. The **cross-coupling is the heart of DCVS** — without it, the circuit is just a differential pair, not DCVS.
> 
> 3. **Stating "static power = 0" without qualification:** In ideal conditions yes, but in real silicon with subthreshold leakage, it is non-zero. Use the phrase **"ideally zero"** or **"near-zero"** to be safe.
> 
> 4. **Misapplying De Morgan's Law for the complement tree:** The complement PDN must implement $\overline{F}$, not $F$. Always verify by writing out the Boolean function.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the limitation on fan-in:** The stacked NMOS transistors in the true tree slow down as the number of inputs increases. This is a **favourite 2-mark sub-question**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **DCVS = NMOS Differential Tree + Cross-Coupled PMOS Load.** Remember the cross-coupling — gate of MP1 ↔ drain of MP2.
- 🔑 **It is non-ratioed**, hence robust to PVT variations.
- 🔑 **Static power is ideally zero** but non-zero in practice due to leakage.
- 🔑 **Output is differential (F and $\overline{F}$)** — single-stage generation.
- 🔑 **Full rail-to-rail output swing** is achieved through **positive feedback**.
- 🔑 **Fan-in is limited to ~4–5** due to NMOS stacking.
- 🔑 **Transistor count ≈ 2N + 2 to 2N + 4**, higher than static CMOS.
- 🔑 **Best suited for datapath units**, **ALUs**, and **adders** where complementary signals are required.
- 🔑 **The complement PDN is built using De Morgan's Law** of the function implemented in the true PDN.
- 🔑 **Cross-coupled PMOS** acts as a **regenerative amplifier**, accelerating switching and improving noise margin.
- 🔑 **DCVS is a foundation** for more advanced logics: **DCVSPG**, **DSL**, **SSDL**.
- 🔑 **Do not confuse with pseudo-NMOS:** pseudo-NMOS has always-ON PMOS load (ratioed); DCVS has cross-coupled PMOS load (non-ratioed).
- 🔑 **Short-circuit power** exists briefly during transitions in DCVS.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Switching; Adiabatic Charging
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Switching; Adiabatic Charging

# 🔋 Adiabatic Switching & Adiabatic Charging in Low-Power VLSI

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adiabatic Switching** is a low-power VLSI design methodology in which the transition of a circuit node voltage (i.e., charging or discharging a load capacitance) is achieved by a *time-varying, gradually-rising/falling power-clock waveform* instead of a sudden step voltage. The word *adiabatic* (Greek: *adiabatos* — "impassable") refers to a thermodynamic process in which no heat is exchanged with the surroundings; in CMOS terms, it implies that *no energy is dissipated* if the switching is made infinitely slow and the circuit is reversible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adiabatic Charging** is the specific physical process by which the load capacitance $C_L$ of a CMOS logic gate is charged through a controlled, time-varying voltage source $V_s(t)$ (typically a ramp, trapezoid, or staircase waveform), thereby reducing the energy dissipated as $I^2R$ heat in the on-resistance of the charging transistor.

In conventional CMOS, the charging of load capacitance is the *dominant* source of dynamic power dissipation:

$$P_{conv} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f_{clk}$$

Adiabatic techniques aim to suppress the $V_{DD}^2$ term by replacing the abrupt step with a slow, recoverable ramp — trading **speed** for **energy efficiency**.

---

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Water-Tank Analogy

| Conventional CMOS (Step Charging) | Adiabatic Charging (Ramp Charging) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 🚿 *Imagine opening a fire-hose to fill a small bucket.* The water hammers the bottom; when you slam the valve shut, a destructive pressure wave (a "water-hammer") dissipates kinetic energy as heat/sound. Most energy is **lost as turbulence**. | 💧 *Imagine gently pouring water from a thin, controlled stream into the same bucket.* The water enters smoothly, the level rises predictably, and when you reverse the flow, you can **siphon almost all the water back** into the source. |
| Energy lost: $\frac{1}{2} C V^2$ — fixed, **independent of how fast** the switch operates. | Energy lost: $\frac{RC}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ — *decreases* as charging time $T$ increases. |

> [!TIP]
> The deep insight: **Conventional charging wastes energy because the current is impulsive; adiabatic charging keeps current small and constant by spreading the voltage transition over a longer time.**

---

### 1.3 Energy Recovery — The Key Idea

> [!NOTE]
> A true adiabatic system is *reversible*. When the supply voltage $V_s(t)$ ramps **down**, the energy stored in $C_L$ is *returned* to the power-clock generator through the same transistor channel (now acting as a "return valve"). This **partial energy recovery** is what makes adiabatic logic families (e.g., **2N-2N2P, ECRL, PFAL, SCAL**) achieve > 90% energy savings at low clock frequencies.

> **Key Metric:** *Power-Clock Generator Efficiency* (often **70–90%** in practical adiabatic designs) — this is the *real-world bottleneck* preventing adiabatic logic from becoming mainstream in synchronous digital VLSI.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conventional CMOS Charging — The Baseline

In a standard CMOS inverter, when the PMOS pulls the output node from **0 → V_DD** through the on-resistance $R_{on}$:

- The capacitor voltage rises as: $v_C(t) = V_{DD} \,(1 - e^{-t/R_{on}C_L})$
- The instantaneous current decays as: $i(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{R_{on}} \, e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}$
- **Half** the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ ends up *stored* in $C_L$; the **other half** is *dissipated* in $R_{on}$ as $I^2R$ heat.

$$E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical fact for KTU board exams:** This $\frac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ dissipation is **fixed** — it is *not* reduced by making the transistor "faster" or "slower". This is why aggressive voltage scaling and clock-gating alone have a *fundamental floor* that only adiabatic or near-threshold techniques can break.

---

### 2.2 Adiabatic Ramp Charging — The Reversible Limit

Replace the step-voltage $V_{DD}$ with a *ramp* $V_s(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{T} \cdot t$ for $0 \le t \le T$.

- Since $V_s(t)$ rises *slowly* compared to the $R_{on}C_L$ time constant, the capacitor voltage *tracks* the source.
- Current stays nearly **constant**: $i(t) = C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV_s}{dt} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}$
- The instantaneous $I^2R$ dissipation is now *spread* over the entire ramp duration $T$.

**Result (derived in Section 3):**

$$E_{ramp} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{ramp} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{2}\,}$$

When $T \gg R_{on}C_L$, $E_{ramp} \to 0$ — the **adiabatic ideal**.

---

### 2.3 Stepwise Charging — Practical Approximation

A true ramp is hard to generate on-chip. The practical approximation is an **N-step staircase** waveform with step height $\Delta V = V_{DD}/N$.

$$E_{step} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{N}$$

Each successive step has *less* dissipation because the voltage swing per step shrinks. As $N \to \infty$, stepwise → ramp → true adiabatic.

---

### 2.4 📋 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Energy, conventional step charging** | $E_{conv} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | *Independent* of $R_{on}, T, f_{clk}$ |
| 2 | **Energy, adiabatic ramp charging** | $E_{ramp} = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Valid for **linear ramp** $0 \le t \le T$ |
| 3 | **Energy, stepwise charging (N steps)** | $E_{step} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{N}$ | Uniform staircase, no resistor losses |
| 4 | **Average current during ramp** | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}$ | Constant for ideal ramp |
| 5 | **Adiabatic efficiency** | $\eta_{ad} = 1 - \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T}$ | $\to 1$ as $T \gg R_{on} C_L$ |
| 6 | **Throughput / clock period** | $T_{clk} \ge k \cdot R_{on} C_L$ | Trade-off: lower energy ⇒ slower clock |
| 7 | **Energy-delay product (EDP)** | $EDP = E \cdot T_{delay}$ | Adiabatic favours *energy* over *delay* |
| 8 | **4-phase power-clock** | $V_{\phi}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \mathrm{triangular}(t)$ | Used in 2N-2N2P, ECRL families |
| 9 | **Capacitor voltage tracking** | $v_C(t) \approx V_s(t)$ | When $T \gg R_{on} C_L$ (quasi-adiabatic) |
| 10 | **Energy stored in $C_L$ at end of ramp** | $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | *Recoverable* on discharge ramp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU board numericals:** When asked "derive the energy in adiabatic charging", the **expected final form** is $E_{ramp} = (RC/T) \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$. The examiner specifically looks for the $\frac{RC}{T}$ factor — leaving it as $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ (the conventional answer) results in **0/7 marks** for that part.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Adiabatic? |
| :--- | :--- |
| 📱 *Wearable / IoT sensors* | Battery life extension when operating at 100 Hz – 1 MHz |
| 🪪 *Passive RFID / smart cards* | Energy harvested from RF field; every nano-joule counts |
| 🧠 *Neuromorphic / brain-inspired chips* | Spike rates are inherently slow (Hz–kHz) — ideal for adiabatic |
| 🔬 *Cryogenic CMOS (quantum control)* | Adiabatic drivers minimise on-chip heat near qubits |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1 — Energy Dissipated in Conventional CMOS Charging

**Circuit model:** A switch closes at $t = 0$ connecting a step source $V_{DD}$ to a capacitor $C_L$ through a channel resistance $R_{on}$.

**Step 1 — Write the differential equation using KVL:**

$$V_{DD} = i(t) R_{on} + v_C(t), \quad i(t) = C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

This gives the first-order linear ODE:

$$R_{on} C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t) = V_{DD}$$

**Step 2 — Solve with initial condition $v_C(0) = 0$:**

$$v_C(t) = V_{DD} \left(1 - e^{-t / R_{on} C_L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Express the current:**

$$i(t) = C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} = \frac{V_{DD}}{R_{on}} e^{-t / R_{on} C_L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the instantaneous power dissipated in $R_{on}$:**

$$P_R(t) = i^2(t) R_{on} = \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R_{on}} \, e^{-2t / R_{on} C_L}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t = \infty$ (full charge cycle):**

$$E_R = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R_{on}} \, e^{-2t / R_{on} C_L} \, dt$$

$$E_R = \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R_{on}} \cdot \left[-\frac{R_{on} C_L}{2} \, e^{-2t / R_{on} C_L}\right]_0^{\infty}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the limit:**

$$E_R = \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R_{on}} \cdot \frac{R_{on} C_L}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,E_{conv} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2\,}$$

**Sanity check:** This is exactly *half* the total energy delivered by the source ($E_{src} = Q V_{DD} = C V_{DD}^2$), the other half being stored in the capacitor. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2 — Energy Dissipated in Adiabatic Ramp Charging

**Circuit model:** Same RC network, but the source is now a *linear ramp*:

$$V_s(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{T} \, t, \quad 0 \le t \le T$$

**Step 1 — Write the ODE:**

$$V_s(t) = R_{on} C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{T} t = R_{on} C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

**Step 2 — Quasi-adiabatic assumption ($T \gg R_{on}C_L$):** the capacitor voltage tracks the source almost perfectly, so $v_C(t) \approx V_s(t)$.

$$\frac{dv_C}{dt} \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Current through the resistor:**

$$i(t) = C_L \frac{dv_C}{dt} \approx \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}$$

> The current is **constant** — a critical observation. The impulsive current of conventional charging is replaced by a *small, sustained* current.

**Step 4 — Instantaneous power in $R_{on}$:**

$$P_R(t) = i^2(t) R_{on} = \left(\frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}\right)^2 R_{on} = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the ramp duration $T$:**

$$E_R = \int_0^T P_R(t) \, dt = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2} \cdot T$$

$$E_R = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2$$

**Step 6 — Final tidy form:**

$$\boxed{\,E_{ramp} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Define the dimensionless factor $\alpha = R_{on}C_L / T$. Then:
- If $T = R_{on}C_L$ (i.e., the ramp is *as fast* as the RC time constant): $E_{ramp} = E_{conv}$. No benefit.
- If $T = 10 \, R_{on}C_L$: $E_{ramp} = 0.1 \cdot E_{conv}$. **10× energy reduction**.
- If $T = 100 \, R_{on}C_L$: $E_{ramp} = 0.01 \cdot E_{conv}$. **100× energy reduction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry note:** During the *discharge* ramp (when $V_s$ falls from $V_{DD}$ back to 0), the same amount of energy is dissipated in $R_{on}$ — but most of the energy stored in $C_L$ flows *back* into the source. The *net* dissipation per full charge–discharge cycle is therefore $2 \times E_{ramp}$, not $C_L V_{DD}^2$ as in the conventional case.

---

### 3.3 Derivation #3 — Stepwise Charging (N-Step Approximation)

A discrete staircase of $N$ steps, each of height $\Delta V = V_{DD}/N$, is the most practical on-chip approximation to a ramp.

**Step 1 — Energy dissipated in the $k$-th step (charging from $(k-1)\Delta V$ to $k\Delta V$):**

For a step transition of magnitude $\Delta V$ on a capacitor that *already* holds voltage $(k-1)\Delta V$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} C_L (\Delta V)^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{N}\right)^2$$

> The energy dissipated in each step depends *only* on the step size, not on the absolute voltage — because the resistive loss is purely from the $\Delta V$ transition.

**Step 2 — Sum over all $N$ steps:**

$$E_{step} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} E_k = N \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{N}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$E_{step} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{step} = \frac{E_{conv}}{N}\,}$$

**Comparison with ramp result:** For $N$ steps of equal duration $\Delta t = T/N$:

$$E_{ramp} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{N \cdot \Delta t} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

If $\Delta t = R_{on}C_L$ (each step fully settles), then $E_{ramp} = E_{step} = E_{conv}/N$. ✓ — both views agree in the *quasi-static* limit.

---

### 3.4 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Conventional CMOS | Adiabatic Ramp | Adiabatic Stepwise |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Source waveform | Step $V_{DD}$ | Linear ramp | N-step staircase |
| Peak current | $V_{DD}/R_{on}$ (very high) | $C_L V_{DD}/T$ (small) | Stepwise-bounded |
| Energy dissipated | $\frac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ (fixed) | $\frac{RC}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ | $\frac{1}{2N} CV_{DD}^2$ |
| Energy recovery | ❌ None | ✅ Partial (on discharge) | ✅ Partial (on ramp-down) |
| Speed | High (ns) | Low (µs–ms) | Low–medium |
| Clock network | Single $V_{DD}$ rail | Multi-phase power-clock | Multi-phase power-clock |
| Design complexity | Low | High | Medium |
| Energy–Delay trade | Fixed | Tunable by $T$ | Tunable by $N$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Adiabatic Charging Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCG["Power-Clock\nGenerator\n(4-phase)"] -->|"Φ(t): ramp up"| TX1["PMOS\nDriver"]
    PCG2["Power-Clock\nReturn Path"] -->|"Φ(t): ramp down"| TX2["NMOS\nDriver"]
    TX1 --> CL["Load Capacitance\nC_L (logic node)"]
    TX2 --> CL
    CL -->|"Recovered energy"| PCG2
    PCG -.->|"Energy recovery\n(> 70%)"| PS["Power Supply\nV_DD"]

    subgraph LOGIC_BLOCK [Adiabatic Logic Gate: 2N-2N2P / ECRL / PFAL]
        TX1
        TX2
        CL
    end

    style PCG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style PCG2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style CL fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    style LOGIC_BLOCK fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

**Reading the diagram:** The power-clock generator produces a *4-phase trapezoidal/ramp* waveform that *simultaneously* acts as both supply and clock. During the **ramp-up** phase, energy flows from generator → through the PMOS driver → into $C_L$. During the **ramp-down** phase, the NMOS driver turns on, and the stored charge in $C_L$ flows *back* into the power-clock line, partially recharging the supply capacitance.

---

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Conventional vs Adiabatic Charging Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Output node\nat 0 V, C_L discharged"] --> B{"Switching\nTechnique?"}

    B -->|Conventional CMOS| C1["Step voltage V_DD\napplied via PMOS"]
    C1 --> D1["Impulsive current\ni = V_DD / R_on (peak)"]
    D1 --> E1["i²R heat dissipated\nin channel resistance"]
    E1 --> F1["Final: v_C = V_DD\nEnergy stored: ½CV²"]
    F1 --> G1["Discharge: ½CV²\ndissipated in NMOS\n(no recovery)"]
    G1 --> H1["Net dissipation\nper cycle = CV²"]
    H1 --> I1["ENERGY WASTED ❌"]

    B -->|Adiabatic Ramp| C2["Ramp voltage\nV_s(t) = V_DD·t/T"]
    C2 --> D2["Small constant current\ni = C_L·V_DD / T"]
    D2 --> E2["Small i²R loss\nspread over time T"]
    E2 --> F2["Final: v_C = V_DD\nEnergy stored: ½CV²"]
    F2 --> G2["Discharge: ramp down\nEnergy flows BACK\nto power-clock"]
    G2 --> H2["Net dissipation\nper cycle = 2·RC/T · ½CV²"]
    H2 --> I2["ENERGY RECOVERED ✅"]

    style C1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style I1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style I2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Stepwise Charging Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N_STEPS [N-Step Staircase Power Clock]
        S1["Step 1\n0 → V_DD/N"] --> S2["Step 2\nV_DD/N → 2V_DD/N"]
        S2 --> S3["Step 3\n2V_DD/N → 3V_DD/N"]
        S3 --> Sdots["..."]
        Sdots --> SN["Step N\n(N-1)V_DD/N → V_DD"]
    end

    S1 --> CHARGE["Charge C_L\nE₁ = ½C(V_DD/N)²"]
    S2 --> CHARGE
    S3 --> CHARGE
    SN --> CHARGE

    CHARGE --> RESULT["Total Energy\nE_step = ½CV²_DD / N"]
    RESULT --> COMPARE{"Compare"}
    COMPARE -->|"N = 1"| CONV["E_conv = ½CV²_DD\n(conventional)"]
    COMPARE -->|"N → ∞"| IDEAL["E_ideal → 0\n(true adiabatic)"]

    style N_STEPS fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B
    style RESULT fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    style CONV fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style IDEAL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET752, Module 4]`
> **Define adiabatic switching. How does it differ from conventional CMOS switching in terms of energy dissipation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Adiabatic switching** is a low-power CMOS design technique in which the voltage transition at a capacitive node is driven by a *time-varying, gradually-changing power-clock waveform* (typically a ramp or staircase) instead of an abrupt step voltage $V_{DD}$.
>
> **(1 mark) Key difference in energy dissipation:**
> - **Conventional switching:** $E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ — *fixed* and *independent* of switching speed.
> - **Adiabatic switching:** $E_{ramp} = \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ — *inversely proportional* to the charging time $T$.
>
> **(1 mark) Reversibility:** Adiabatic switching allows the energy stored in the load capacitor to be *recovered* back into the power-clock supply during the discharge ramp, which is impossible in conventional CMOS.
>
> **(1 mark) Trade-off:** Energy reduction comes at the cost of *reduced switching speed* — the clock period $T$ must be much larger than the $R_{on}C_L$ time constant.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET752, Module 4]`
> **With a neat waveform, explain the concept of a power-clock used in adiabatic logic circuits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **power-clock** is a time-varying supply voltage (typically a *4-phase trapezoidal* or *ramp* waveform) that simultaneously serves as both the *power supply* and the *clock signal* for adiabatic logic gates.
>
> **(1 mark) Waveform description:** Each phase consists of four sub-periods — *ramp-up*, *hold-high*, *ramp-down*, and *hold-low* — repeated sequentially across four wires ($\Phi_1, \Phi_2, \Phi_3, \Phi_4$), each phase-shifted by $90°$.
>
> **(1 mark) Working principle:** During *ramp-up*, current flows from the power-clock line into the load capacitance $C_L$ (charging). During *ramp-down*, current flows *back* into the power-clock line (energy recovery). During *hold-high*/*hold-low*, the node is stable and evaluation occurs.
>
> **(1 mark) Significance:** The slow, controlled transitions minimise the $I^2R$ dissipation, and the energy-return path during ramp-down enables *partial energy recovery*, making adiabatic logic ultra-low-power at low clock frequencies (sub-MHz regime).

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Internal Choice*

> **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET752, Module 4]`
>
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the energy dissipated in charging a load capacitance $C_L$ through a channel resistance $R$ using **(i) a step voltage $V_{DD}$** and **(ii) a linear ramp voltage $V_s(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{T} t$. Comment on the limiting cases. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the principle of adiabatic charging with a stepwise approximation. Derive the expression for energy dissipation when $N$ equal steps are used and show how it relates to the ramp result. Discuss the energy-delay trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a)(i) — Step Voltage Charging [3.5 marks]

> The detailed derivation is given in **Section 3.1** above. Final result:
> $$\boxed{E_{conv} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2}$$
> **Limiting cases:**
> - *Fast switch* ($R \to 0$): The current impulse becomes infinite, but $\int i^2 R \, dt$ remains $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ — energy loss is *invariant* of $R$. This is the **charge-redistribution paradox**.
> - *Slow switch* ($R$ large): $E_{conv}$ is unchanged; only the *time* to charge changes.

### Part (a)(ii) — Linear Ramp Charging [3.5 marks]

> The detailed derivation is in **Section 3.2** above. Final result:
> $$\boxed{E_{ramp} = \frac{R\,C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2}$$
> **Limiting cases:**
> - *Fast ramp* ($T \ll R C_L$): $E_{ramp} \gg E_{conv}$ — the source outruns the capacitor and most of the energy is dropped across $R$ (this is the *non-adiabatic* regime where ramp charging is *worse* than step charging).
> - *Slow ramp* ($T \gg R C_L$): $E_{ramp} \to 0$ — the **true adiabatic limit** with complete energy recovery.
> - *Matched ramp* ($T = R C_L$): $E_{ramp} = E_{conv}$ — break-even point.

### Part (b) — Stepwise Approximation & Energy-Delay Trade-off [7 marks]

> Detailed derivation in **Section 3.3**. Final result:
> $$\boxed{E_{step} = \frac{1}{2 N} C_L V_{DD}^2}$$
> **Relating stepwise to ramp:** If $N$ steps of equal duration $\Delta t$ are used, total time $T = N \cdot \Delta t$. In the quasi-static limit $\Delta t = R C_L$:
> $$E_{ramp} = \frac{R C_L}{N \cdot R C_L} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2N} C V_{DD}^2 = E_{step} \checkmark$$
> **Energy–Delay trade-off:**
> - *Energy $\propto 1/T$* (for ramp) and *Energy $\propto 1/N$* (for stepwise).
> - *Delay $\propto T$* — every doubling of $T$ halves the energy loss.
> - **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is *not* improved by adiabatic switching; EDP is *the same order* as conventional CMOS. Adiabatic logic is therefore preferred only when **ultra-low static power** dominates, not high-throughput operation.

---

> **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET752, Module 4]`
>
> **(a)** With the help of a circuit schematic, explain the operation of an adiabatic charging circuit using a ramped power-clock. Derive the energy stored in the load capacitance and the energy dissipated in the switching transistor. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the energy dissipation of conventional CMOS charging and adiabatic charging for $C_L = 50\,\text{fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$, $R_{on} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $T = 5\,\text{ns}$. Compute the percentage energy saving. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Adiabatic Circuit Operation [7 marks]

**Schematic (re-drawn as text):**

```
       V_s(t) = (V_DD/T)·t       (ramped power-clock)
           │
           ├──[ PMOS, R_on ]──┬──── Output node
           │                  │
          GND               C_L ──── (load capacitance)
                              │
                             GND
```

**Operation:**

1. **(1 mark)** The PMOS transistor is turned on with a *constant* gate voltage such that it operates in the *triode/linear* region, behaving as a small resistor $R_{on}$.
2. **(1 mark)** The source $V_s(t)$ ramps linearly from 0 to $V_{DD}$ over time $T \gg R_{on}C_L$.
3. **(1 mark)** The capacitor voltage $v_C(t) \approx V_s(t)$ *tracks* the source. The current through the PMOS is approximately constant: $i(t) \approx C_L V_{DD}/T$.
4. **(1 mark)** Energy delivered by source during ramp-up: $E_{src} = \int_0^T V_s(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt = \int_0^T \frac{V_{DD}}{T} t \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T} dt = \frac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{2}$.
5. **(1 mark)** Energy stored in $C_L$ at the end: $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.
6. **(1 mark)** Energy dissipated in $R_{on}$: $E_{R} = E_{src} - E_{stored} = 0$ *in the ideal limit*; in practice, $E_R = (R_{on} C_L / T) \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.
7. **(1 mark)** During ramp-down, $C_L$ discharges *back* into $V_s(t)$, recovering the stored energy.

### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison [7 marks]

**Given:** $C_L = 50\,\text{fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$, $R_{on} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega = 10^3\,\Omega$, $T = 5\,\text{ns} = 5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Conventional charging energy:**

$$E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2$$

$$E_{conv} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 = 36 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{J} = 36\,\text{fJ}$$

**Step 2 — Adiabatic ramp energy:**

$$E_{ramp} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

Compute the dimensionless factor:

$$\frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} = \frac{10^3 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.01$$

Therefore:

$$E_{ramp} = 0.01 \times 36\,\text{fJ} = 0.36\,\text{fJ}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage energy saving:**

$$\%\text{ Savings} = \frac{E_{conv} - E_{ramp}}{E_{conv}} \times 100\% = \frac{36 - 0.36}{36} \times 100\% = 99\%$$

**Final Answer:** **Adiabatic charging achieves a 99% energy reduction** in this example — a savings of **35.64 fJ per switching event**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam tip:** Always compute $R_{on}C_L/T$ first; if it is < 0.1, the adiabatic approach is *strongly justified*. Also, when $R_{on}C_L/T \ge 1$, adiabatic charging is *worse* than conventional — this is the *faster-than-RC trap*.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in adiabatic-charging derivations (and how marks are lost):**
>
> 1. **Writing $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ for the ramp result** — the *whole point* of the derivation is to show the $(R_{on}C_L/T)$ factor. Losing this = losing 4 of 7 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in $E_{stored}$** — the energy stored in the capacitor is $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$, NOT $CV^2$. Mixing the two costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing "adiabatic" with "isothermal"** — adiabatic means *no heat exchange with surroundings* (all energy that leaves the source enters the capacitor or is recovered); isothermal is a different thermodynamic concept and is irrelevant here.
>
> 4. **Skipping the integration limits** — the ramp integral must explicitly be from $0$ to $T$, not from $0$ to $\infty$. The ramp *ends* at $t = T$.
>
> 5. **Writing the stepwise answer as $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$** instead of $E = \frac{1}{2N}CV^2$ — forgetting the $N$ in the denominator is the #1 stepwise-charging error.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the energy-recovery phase** — adiabatic circuits have *two* ramps per cycle (charge + discharge). The *net* dissipation per cycle is $2 \times E_{ramp}$, not $E_{ramp}$. A 14-mark answer that only analyses the charging ramp without mentioning recovery is capped at 9/14.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for "Adiabatic Switching & Adiabatic Charging":**

- 🔑 **Adiabatic switching** uses a *time-varying* supply (ramp/staircase) to charge $C_L$ gradually, minimising $I^2R$ dissipation.
- 🔑 **Conventional charging** wastes a fixed $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ per cycle — *independent* of switching speed.
- 🔑 **Adiabatic ramp charging** wastes $E_{ramp} = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ — *inversely proportional* to ramp time $T$.
- 🔑 **Stepwise charging** with $N$ steps: $E_{step} = \dfrac{1}{2N} C_L V_{DD}^2$. Each step has equal step-size $\Delta V = V_{DD}/N$.
- 🔑 **The break-even point** is at $T = R_{on}C_L$. Below this, ramp charging is *worse*; above this, it is *better*.
- 🔑 **Energy recovery** during the discharge ramp is what makes adiabatic logic fundamentally different — charge flows *back* to the power-clock.
- 🔑 **Power-clock generator efficiency** (typically **70–90%**) is the practical limit; it caps the theoretical energy savings.
- 🔑 **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is *not* improved by adiabatic switching — these circuits trade speed for energy.
- 🔑 **Current profile** in ramp charging is *constant*: $i(t) = C_L V_{DD}/T$ (versus impulsive exponential decay in conventional).
- 🔑 **Quasi-adiabatic assumption** — $T \gg R_{on}C_L$ — is required for the simple ramp result to hold.
- 🔑 **Adiabatic logic families** to know by name: *2N-2N2P, ECRL (Efficient Charge Recovery Logic), PFAL (Pass-transistor Adiabatic Logic), SCAL (Split-level Charge Recovery Logic), CAL (Clocked Adiabatic Logic)*.
- 🔑 **Applications** — RFID, smart cards, IoT sensors, cryogenic CMOS, neuromorphic chips — anywhere *low frequency* + *ultra-low power* is required.
- 🔑 **Numerical format expected in KTU exams:** State given values → compute $R_{on}C_L/T$ factor first → substitute → compute $E_{conv}$ → compute $E_{ramp}$ → compute % savings. Always show units in **fJ** or **pJ**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Amplification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Amplification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adiabatic Amplification** is a class of low-power circuit techniques in which a small input signal (typically a differential voltage developed on a sense node or bit-line) is amplified to a full-rail logic level by *recovering* the energy associated with the output node capacitance back to a slowly-ramped (quasi-static) power-clock supply, rather than dissipating it irreversibly as heat in a resistive pull-up/pull-down path.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, an **adiabatic amplifier** combines two principles:

1. **Adiabatic (reversible) charging** – the output capacitance $C_L$ is charged through a controlled on-resistance using a *trapezoidal / sinusoidal / stepped power-clock* of period $T \gg RC$, so that the current drawn from the supply is bounded and energy is returned to the supply during the discharge phase.
2. **Amplification** – a sense-amplifier-like regenerative or differential structure converts a small $\Delta V$ on the input pair into a full-swing $V_{DD}$ output, while the charging/discharging of the load capacitance remains adiabatic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key syllabus keyword – "Adiabatic":** Borrowed from thermodynamics, meaning *no exchange of heat/energy with the surroundings*. In VLSI, we interpret it as "no (or minimal) irreversible energy dissipation on the output node."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recycled Water Wheel"

Imagine filling a bucket (the load capacitor $C_L$) with water (electrical charge) from a raised tank (the power supply $V_{DD}$).

- **Conventional CMOS way**: You punch a hole at the bottom of the tank. Water gushes out violently and splashes on the ground — half the water is wasted as splash (heat dissipation $\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$). Every cycle, fresh water must be brought in.
- **Adiabatic way**: A small pipe (the on-transistor acting as a *controlled resistor*) connects the tank to the bucket, and the tank is **lowered very slowly**. Water trickles in gently — almost no splash. When emptying, the water is poured *back* into the tank, which rises back up. The water is **reused** next cycle. The slower you lower/raise the tank (longer $T$), the less is lost.

The "amplifier" part adds a small auxiliary tap (a differential pair) that *decides* which bucket fills and which empties, using a tiny amount of water (the input $\Delta V$) — but the heavy lifting is done adiabatically.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|
| Power-clock period | $T_{PCK}$ | $\geq 10\,RC$ |
| Activity factor | $\alpha$ | $0 \to 1$ (low for sparse data) |
| Energy per transition | $E_{ad}$ | $\ll \tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ |
| Supply ramp slope | $dV_{PCK}/dt$ | Slow & monotonic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adiabatic ≠ Quasi-static forever.** True *reversible* adiabatic operation requires $T_{PCK} \to \infty$, which is impractical. In KTU exam answers, you almost always write "quasi-adiabatic" when $T_{PCK} \gg RC$ but finite.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle — Adiabatic Charging of a Capacitor

Consider the canonical adiabatic charging circuit:

- A capacitor $C_L$ is connected through an on-resistance $R_{on}$ to a *time-varying* supply $V_{PCK}(t)$.
- We assume the *step-wise constant current* or *ramped* supply model.

### 2.1.1 Conventional (Non-Adiabatic) Charging (Recap)

For an **ideal step** $V_{DD}$ applied through resistance $R$:

$$E_{diss,conv} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$$

Half is stored on $C_L$, the other half is *burned* in $R$ as heat — independent of how fast you switch.

### 2.1.2 Adiabatic (Ramped) Charging

If the supply rises **linearly** from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ over a time $T$:

$$V_{PCK}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{T}\,t,\qquad 0 \le t \le T$$

The current is bounded:

$$i(t) = C_L \frac{dV_{PCK}}{dt} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}$$

and the *instantaneous* power dissipated in $R_{on}$ is:

$$P(t) = i^2(t) R_{on} = \left(\frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}\right)^2 R_{on}$$

Integrating over $T$:

$$E_{diss,ad} \;=\; \int_0^T P(t)\,dt \;=\; \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}\cdot T \;=\; \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The famous result:**
> $$E_{diss,ad} = \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{\tau}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$$
> Compare to conventional $E_{diss,conv} = \tfrac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$. As $T \to \infty$, dissipation vanishes — the *defining* property of adiabatic switching.

## 2.2 Adding the "Amplifier" — Sense-Amplifier Action

Adiabatic *amplification* uses a regenerative cross-coupled (latch) or differential pair to *detect* and *amplify* a small $\Delta V_{in}$ to a rail-to-rail swing. There are three canonical families KTU expects you to know:

| Family | Core Idea | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diode-connected adiabatic amp.** | Two diode-connected PMOS/NMOS in series with a slow ramp | Simple, fully reversible | Low gain, slow |
| **Cross-coupled latch amplifier** | Two cross-coupled inverters triggered by a delayed power-clock | High gain, fast regeneration | Not strictly reversible (regeneration loses energy) |
| **Differential pair + AC-coupled sense** | Small $\Delta V$ sensed; full swing restored on power-clock | True adiabatic, good noise margin | Needs matched pair, large area |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this table** — it covers ~70% of numerical problems asked in Module 4.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conventional switching energy | $E_{conv}=\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ | Joules | Step input |
| 2 | Adiabatic dissipation (linear ramp) | $E_{ad}=\dfrac{RC}{T}\,CV_{DD}^2$ | Joules | $T \gg RC$ |
| 3 | Quasi-adiabatic bound | $E_{ad}\ll E_{conv}$ when $T\gg 2RC$ | — | Design rule |
| 4 | Time constant | $\tau = R_{on}C_L$ | seconds | $R_{on}$ = on-resistance of charging device |
| 5 | Stored energy (ideal cap) | $E_{store}=\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ | Joules | Recoverable |
| 6 | Returned (recovered) energy | $E_{rec}=CV_{DD}^2 - E_{ad}$ | Joules | Reversible fraction |
| 7 | Average adiabatic power | $P_{ad}=\dfrac{RC}{T^2}\,CV_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{sw}$ | W | $f_{sw}$ switching freq. |
| 8 | Sense-amp small-signal gain (diff. pair) | $A_v = g_m R_{out}$ | dimensionless | $g_m$ = transconductance |
| 9 | Energy-delay product (ADP) | $ADP = E_{ad}\cdot T_{delay}$ | J·s | Metric for trade-off |
| 10 | Thermodynamic limit | $E_{min}=k_B T \ln 2$ per bit | J | Landauer's bound |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DRAM bit-line sense amplifiers** in ultra-low-power mobile and IoT SRAM macros.
- **Energy-recovery CMOS** (ER-CMOS) pipelines in deep-space/medical implants (e.g., pacemakers) where battery life is paramount.
- **Reversible logic** building blocks for quantum-computing interface circuits.
- **Sub-threshold adiabatic** design for sensor nodes harvesting ambient energy.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step (KTU Marker Comments)

1. *Why ramp the supply?* — Limits the **peak current** to $C_L V_{DD}/T$, so $i^2R$ losses are *quadratically* reduced by increasing $T$.
2. *Why cross-couple?* — A small $\Delta V$ cannot be amplified linearly with low energy; regeneration provides *positive feedback* and rail-to-rail swing. (Strictly, regeneration itself is *not* fully adiabatic — exam answers must note this trade-off.)
3. *Why use multiple phases?* — 2-phase / 4-phase clocking ensures the output is sampled only after the ramp completes, preventing shoot-through.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Dissipated in Linear-Ramp Adiabatic Charging

**Given:**
- Load capacitance $C_L$
- Charging resistance $R_{on}$
- Power-clock: $V_{PCK}(t)=\dfrac{V_{DD}}{T}\,t$ for $0\le t\le T$

**To find:** Total energy dissipated in $R_{on}$ during one ramp.

### Step 1 — Express the current.

The capacitor voltage must follow the supply (quasi-static assumption $T\gg R_{on}C_L$):

$$V_C(t) \approx V_{PCK}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{T}\,t$$

The current through the resistor is the capacitor current:

$$i(t) = C_L\,\frac{dV_C}{dt} = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{T}$$

### Step 2 — Compute instantaneous power in $R_{on}$.

$$P_R(t) = i^2(t)\,R_{on} = \left(\frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}\right)^2 R_{on} = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate over the ramp interval $[0,T]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{diss} &= \int_0^T P_R(t)\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \int_0^T \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}\cdot\Big[t\Big]_0^T \\[4pt]
&= \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}\cdot T \\[4pt]
&= \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}\cdot C_L V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final compact form.

$$\boxed{\,E_{diss,ad} = \frac{\tau}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2\,}$$

> **Logic of the conversion:** We pulled out $1/T^2$, multiplied by $T$ to get $1/T$ (i.e., energy $\propto 1/T$), and recognised $R_{on}C_L = \tau$.

### Step 5 — Comparison ratio.

$$\frac{E_{diss,ad}}{E_{diss,conv}} = \frac{2R_{on}C_L}{T} = \frac{2\tau}{T}$$

For adiabatic operation we need $T \gg 2\tau$ — a **design rule of thumb** worth memorising.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Recovery Efficiency $\eta$

**Definition:** The fraction of charging energy returned to the supply.

### Step 1 — Energy delivered by supply.

The supply provides:

$$E_{sup} = \int_0^T V_{PCK}(t)\,i(t)\,dt = \int_0^T \frac{V_{DD}}{T}t \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}\,dt$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sup} &= \frac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{T^2}\int_0^T t\,dt \\[4pt]
&= \frac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{T^2}\cdot\frac{T^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,C_L V_{DD}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Energy dissipated.

Already derived: $E_{diss,ad}=\dfrac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$

### Step 3 — Recovered energy.

By conservation:

$$E_{rec} = E_{sup} - E_{diss,ad} = \frac{1}{2}\,C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{E_{rec}}{E_{sup}} = 1 - \frac{2R_{on}C_L}{T} = 1 - \frac{2\tau}{T}\,}$$

> **Logic:** As $T\to\infty$, $\eta\to 1$ — *perfect* energy recovery (the reversible limit).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Small-Signal Gain of the Differential Adiabatic Sense Pair

**Given:** Differential pair with transconductance $g_m$, load $R_L$, supply ramp $V_{PCK}(t)$.

### Step 1 — Differential input.

$$\Delta V_{in} = V_{in}^+ - V_{in}^-$$

### Step 2 — Differential output current.

$$i_{out} = g_m\,\Delta V_{in}$$

### Step 3 — Output voltage (linear regime, before regeneration).

$$V_{out} = g_m\,R_L \cdot \Delta V_{in}$$

### Step 4 — Effective gain.

$$\boxed{\,A_v = g_m R_L\,}$$

For a CMOS pair in sub-threshold, $g_m = I_D/(n V_T)$, so even nano-ampere bias currents yield usable gain — this is *why* adiabatic amps work well in sub- $V_T$ regimes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Adiabatic Amplifier to Use?"

| Criterion | Diode-connected | Cross-coupled Latch | Diff-pair + AC sense |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy recovery | Excellent (90 %+) | Moderate (70–85 %) | Excellent (90 %+) |
| Speed | Slow | Fast | Medium |
| Noise margin | Low | High | High |
| Design complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| KTU exam frequency | High | Very High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Adiabatic Amplifier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Input Voltage ΔVin] --> B[Differential Sense Pair]
    B --> C{Regenerative Cross-Coupled Latch}
    C --> D[Quasi-Static Output Node CL]
    E[Slow Power-Clock VPCK ramped over T] --> F[On-Resistance Ron device]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Energy Recovery to VPCK on ramp-down]
    C --> H[Full-Swing Rail-to-Rail Output Vout]
    style A fill:#E8F4FD
    style B fill:#FDECE8
    style C fill:#FFF4E6
    style D fill:#E8FDF0
    style E fill:#F0E8FD
    style F fill:#FDECE8
    style G fill:#E8FDF0
    style H fill:#FFF4E6
```

> **Reading guide:** The blue path carries the tiny input signal; the orange/red path is the *regeneration* (amplification); the green loop represents the *adiabatic* charge/discharge through $R_{on}$ to the slowly-ramped $V_{PCK}$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram — 4-Phase Power-Clock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Phase 1 Evaluate]
        A1[VPCK rises 0 to VDD]
    end
    subgraph P2[Phase 2 Hold]
        A2[VPCK flat at VDD]
    end
    subgraph P3[Phase 3 Recover]
        A3[VPCK falls VDD to 0]
    end
    subgraph P4[Phase 4 Wait]
        A4[VPCK flat at 0]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P1
    style A1 fill:#FFF4E6
    style A2 fill:#E8FDF0
    style A3 fill:#FDECE8
    style A4 fill:#E8F4FD
```

> **Reading guide:** Energy is delivered in **Phase 1**, sampled in **Phase 2**, *recovered* in **Phase 3**, and the circuit idles in **Phase 4**. This is the canonical "evaluate-hold-recover-wait" adiabatic clocking.

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic-Level Flow — Cross-Coupled Adiabatic Sense Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Left_Branch[Left Half-Cell]
        L1[PMOS M1 gate tied to OUT_bar] --> L2[Drain = OUT node]
        L3[NMOS M3 gate tied to OUT] --> L4[Drain = OUT node]
    end
    subgraph Right_Branch[Right Half-Cell]
        R1[PMOS M2 gate tied to OUT] --> R2[Drain = OUT_bar node]
        R3[NMOS M4 gate tied to OUT_bar] --> R4[Drain = OUT_bar node]
    end
    VPCK[Slow Power-Clock VPCK] --> L2
    VPCK --> R2
    L4 --> GND1[Ground]
    R4 --> GND2[Ground]
    IN_pos[Bit-line +] --> L1
    IN_neg[Bit-line -] --> R1
    OUT[Full-Swing Output Vout] --- L1
    OUT_bar[Complementary Output Vout_bar] --- R1
    style VPCK fill:#F0E8FD
    style IN_pos fill:#E8F4FD
    style IN_neg fill:#E8F4FD
    style OUT fill:#FFF4E6
    style OUT_bar fill:#FFF4E6
```

> **Reading guide:** The two half-cells form a **cross-coupled latch**; positive feedback between the M1/M2 (PMOS) and M3/M4 (NMOS) pairs ensures that even a millivolt-level $\Delta V_{BL}$ on the bit-lines is regenerated to a full $V_{DD}$ swing, while the load capacitance on the output nodes is charged through the channel resistance from the slow $V_{PCK}$ — giving adiabatic operation.

## 4.4 Energy vs. Time Comparison (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional CMOS] --> B[Square-step: 1/2 CV² lost per transition, independent of time]
    C[Adiabatic CMOS] --> D[Ramped: RC/T * CV² lost, decreases as 1/T]
    style A fill:#FDECE8
    style B fill:#FDECE8
    style C fill:#E8FDF0
    style D fill:#E8FDF0
```

> **Reading guide:** Conventional charging has a *time-independent* energy loss. Adiabatic charging is *fundamentally a speed–power trade-off*: slower clocks ⇒ less dissipation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

### Q1. Define adiabatic switching in CMOS circuits. Why is it called "adiabatic"?
