**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adiabatic switching is a low-power VLSI technique in which a load capacitance is charged or discharged through a *time-varying, slowly-ramped* power supply rather than a step voltage, so that the current through the charging transistor is kept small. Because the supply is ramped quasi-statically (over a period $T \gg R_{on}C_L$), the energy stored in the capacitance can be returned to the supply on the falling edge. The term *adiabatic* is borrowed from thermodynamics, meaning *no heat exchange with the surroundings*; in CMOS, it implies *no (or minimal) irreversible energy dissipation* on the output node. **(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – ramp/current concept, 1 mark – etymology and reversible energy return.)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

### Q2. Compare conventional CMOS energy dissipation with adiabatic dissipation per switching event. State the design condition for adiabatic operation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Conventional: $E_{conv}=\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$, *independent* of switching time.
- Adiabatic (linear ramp): $E_{ad}=\dfrac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,CV_{DD}^2$, *inversely proportional* to ramp time $T$.
- Design condition (quasi-adiabatic regime): $T \gg 2 R_{on} C_L$ (i.e., the power-clock period must be much larger than the RC time constant of the charging path). **(1 mark each.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit and waveforms, explain the operation of a **2-phase adiabatic amplifier using a cross-coupled sense pair**. Derive the expression for energy dissipated per cycle.

**(b)** An adiabatic amplifier uses a charging transistor with $R_{on}=1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and a load capacitance $C_L=10\,\text{pF}$. The power-clock is a linear ramp of $V_{DD}=1.8\,\text{V}$ over $T=20\,\text{ns}$.

- (i) Calculate the conventional switching energy.
- (ii) Calculate the adiabatic dissipation per cycle.
- (iii) Find the energy-recovery efficiency $\eta$.
- (iv) Comment on the speed–power trade-off.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Circuit description (2 marks):** Two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1, M2, M3, M4) sharing a common *slow* power-clock $V_{PCK}$ at their sources/drains. The bit-lines $BL$ and $\overline{BL}$ provide the small differential input. The output nodes $OUT$ and $\overline{OUT}$ are loaded by $C_L$.
2. **Operation (3 marks):**
   - **Phase $\phi_1$ (Evaluate):** $V_{PCK}$ ramps from 0 to $V_{DD}$ slowly. The bit-line pair delivers a small $\Delta V$. The half-cell whose output is "winning" (higher gate-source on the NMOS / lower on the PMOS) conducts more, but the *ramp* keeps current small.
   - **Phase $\phi_2$ (Hold):** $V_{PCK}$ flat at $V_{DD}$. The latch regenerates — the small imbalance amplifies to a full rail.
   - **Phase $\phi_1'$ (Recover):** $V_{PCK}$ ramps from $V_{DD}$ back to 0. Charge on $C_L$ flows *back* to the supply through the same on-resistance.
3. **Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1,
$$E_{diss,ad}=\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $R_{on}=1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_L=10\,\text{pF}$, $V_{DD}=1.8\,\text{V}$, $T=20\,\text{ns}$.

**(i) Conventional switching energy:**

$$E_{conv}=\tfrac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 = 0.5 \times 10\times 10^{-12} \times (1.8)^2$$

$$= 0.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.24 = 1.62 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J} = 16.2\,\text{pJ}$$

**(ii) Adiabatic dissipation:**

First compute $\tau = R_{on}C_L = 10^3 \times 10^{-11} = 10^{-8}\,\text{s} = 10\,\text{ns}$.

$$E_{ad}=\frac{\tau}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{10\,\text{ns}}{20\,\text{ns}} \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.8)^2$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1.62 \times 10^{-11} = 8.1 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{J} = 8.1\,\text{pJ}$$

**(iii) Recovery efficiency:**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{2\tau}{T} = 1 - \frac{2 \times 10}{20} = 1 - 1.0 = 0$$

> **⚠ Examiner's note:** Here $T=2\tau$, so efficiency is **zero** — the circuit is on the boundary of the adiabatic regime. To get $\eta>0$, we need $T>2\tau$, e.g., $T=40\,\text{ns}$ would give $\eta=0.5$.

**(iv) Speed–power trade-off (2 marks):** Doubling $T$ from 20 ns to 40 ns halves $E_{ad}$ from 8.1 pJ to ≈ 4.05 pJ, but doubles latency. There is an **inverse linear** relationship between switching time and dissipated energy, making the energy-delay product ($ADP = E_{ad}\cdot T$) a constant ≈ $8.1\times 10^{-12}\times 20\times 10^{-9} = 1.62\times 10^{-19}$ J·s for this design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Alt Set]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **energy recovery** in adiabatic logic. With the help of a ramped supply, derive the condition under which adiabatic dissipation is less than $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of the conventional energy loss.

**(b)** A differential adiabatic sense amplifier has a transconductance $g_m = 200\,\mu\text{A/V}$, load resistance $R_L=10\,\text{k}\Omega$, and is biased such that the effective charging resistance is $R_{on}=500\,\Omega$. The output capacitance is $C_L=5\,\text{pF}$, supply $V_{DD}=1.2\,\text{V}$, ramp time $T=50\,\text{ns}$. Find:
- (i) Small-signal voltage gain $A_v$.
- (ii) RC time constant $\tau$.
- (iii) Adiabatic dissipation per transition.
- (iv) The minimum ramp time $T_{min}$ such that $E_{ad} \le \tfrac{1}{4} E_{conv}$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Energy recovery concept (2 marks):** In conventional CMOS, when a capacitor is discharged, the stored energy $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ is *dissipated* in the NMOS pull-down network as heat and lost forever. In adiabatic logic, the discharge happens through a controlled on-resistance into a *falling* power-clock, so the charge returns to the supply, which *recovers* the energy.
2. **Ramped-supply model (2 marks):** With linear ramp $V_{PCK}(t)=(V_{DD}/T)\,t$, the current is constant $i=C_L V_{DD}/T$, and the *recoverable* energy after one cycle is $E_{rec}=CV_{DD}^2-E_{ad}$.
3. **Condition derivation (3 marks):** We want
$$E_{ad} \le \frac{1}{4}E_{conv} = \frac{1}{8}C_L V_{DD}^2$$
Substituting $E_{ad}=\dfrac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2$:
$$\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2 \le \frac{1}{8}C_L V_{DD}^2$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} \le \frac{1}{8}$$
$$\boxed{\,T \ge 8 R_{on} C_L = 8\tau\,}$$
So the **design condition** is that the power-clock period must be **at least 8 RC time constants**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Voltage gain:**

$$A_v = g_m R_L = (200\times 10^{-6}) \times (10\times 10^3) = 2$$

**(ii) RC time constant:**

$$\tau = R_{on} C_L = 500 \times 5\times 10^{-12} = 2.5\times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} = 2.5\,\text{ns}$$

**(iii) Adiabatic dissipation:**

$$E_{ad} = \frac{\tau}{T}\,C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{2.5\,\text{ns}}{50\,\text{ns}} \times 5\times 10^{-12} \times (1.2)^2$$

$$= 0.05 \times 5\times 10^{-12} \times 1.44 = 3.6 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{J} = 0.36\,\text{pJ}$$

**(iv) Minimum ramp time:**

From $T \ge 8\tau$:

$$T_{min} = 8 \times 2.5\,\text{ns} = 20\,\text{ns}$$

Verification: at $T=20$ ns,
$$E_{ad}=\frac{2.5}{20}\times 5\times 10^{-12}\times 1.44 = 0.125 \times 7.2\,\text{pJ} = 0.9\,\text{pJ}$$
$$E_{conv}=\tfrac{1}{2}\times 5\times 10^{-12}\times 1.44 = 3.6\,\text{pJ}$$
$$E_{ad}/E_{conv}=0.9/3.6 = 0.25 = \tfrac{1}{4}\;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor:** Many students write $E_{conv}=CV_{DD}^2$. The correct per-cycle loss is $\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$. Losing **½ mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $R_{on}$ and $R_L$:** $R_{on}$ is the *channel resistance of the charging device* (used in $E_{ad}$), while $R_L$ is the *load resistance* (used in $A_v$). Mixing them is a common error worth **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring the ramp-down phase:** Energy recovery requires a *falling* ramp too. Always state "evaluate + recover" in two-phase answers.
> 4. **Claiming 100 % reversibility:** Strictly, regeneration in a cross-coupled latch is *not* fully adiabatic. Acknowledge this nuance for full marks in design-trace questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Adiabatic amplification = *reversible* charging of $C_L$ + small-signal amplification via sense pair.
- [x] **Conventional energy:** $E_{conv}=\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ — *time-independent*.
- [x] **Adiabatic energy:** $E_{ad}=\dfrac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\,CV_{DD}^2 = \dfrac{\tau}{T}\,CV_{DD}^2$ — *inversely proportional to ramp time*.
- [x] **Adiabatic condition:** $T \gg R_{on}C_L$ (quasi-adiabatic); strictly, $T \to \infty$ for true reversibility.
- [x] **Energy recovery efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{2\tau}{T}$.
- [x] **4-phase clocking:** Evaluate → Hold → Recover → Wait.
- [x] **Gain of diff-pair sense amp:** $A_v = g_m R_L$.
- [x] **Speed–power trade-off:** $ADP = E_{ad}\cdot T_{delay} = $ constant for given $CV^2$.
- [x] **Landauer limit:** $E_{min}=k_B T \ln 2 \approx 2.8\times 10^{-21}$ J/bit at 300 K.
- [x] **Three families:** Diode-connected, Cross-coupled latch, Differential + AC sense — know the *pros/cons*.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** distinguish $R_{on}$ (channel) vs $R_L$ (load).
- [x] **Real-world use cases:** DRAM sense amps, IoT/sensor nodes, medical implants, reversible/quantum interface.
- [x] **Regeneration is *not* fully adiabatic** — must mention this in design answers for full marks.

> 🎯 **Pro tip:** When a KTU question asks "compare conventional and adiabatic", present a *table* with three columns (Energy, Time-dependence, Recovery). Examiners reward structured answers with **0.5–1 bonus mark** for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One Stage and Two Stage Adiabatic Buffer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET752 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** One Stage and Two Stage Adiabatic Buffer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Adiabatic Buffer?

An **adiabatic buffer** is a low-power CMOS logic gate (typically an inverter) that uses a time-varying **power-clock (PC)** supply instead of a constant DC V_DD rail, in order to transfer energy to and from the load capacitance gradually rather than abruptly. The word *adiabatic* is borrowed from thermodynamics, where it means "no heat loss to the surroundings." In VLSI, it implies that the energy drawn from the supply to charge the output node is theoretically **recoverable** back into the supply when the node discharges, instead of being dissipated as heat across the resistive transistors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Adiabatic logic* is a class of low-power digital circuit techniques where energy transfer between the power supply and the load capacitance is carried out in a slow, quasi-static manner through a dedicated AC power-clock waveform, thereby reducing the energy dissipated per switching event to a value proportional to (RC/T) rather than the conventional ½CV².

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tower"

Imagine filling a bucket from a tap:

- **Conventional CMOS** is like a tap turned **ON at full force** — water (energy) slams into the bucket, splashes out (dissipated as heat through the transistor "resistance"), and only half of the energy you paid for actually stays in the bucket (½CV²).
- **Adiabatic charging** is like **slowly trickling water** through a thin pipe — almost all the water you supplied can be **siphoned back out** into the tank when the bucket is emptied, because the flow was slow enough to avoid turbulence (heat loss).

The **slower** you charge (larger ramp time T), the **less** energy is wasted, because the current through the resistive channel stays small (I = C·V/T).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Energy dissipation in adiabatic logic scales as **E ∝ (RC/T) · CV²**. If T ≫ RC, the dissipation can be made **arbitrarily small** — this is the fundamental advantage over static CMOS, where dissipation is fixed at **½CV²** regardless of switching speed.

### 1.3 Why Adiabatic Buffers?

Buffer stages (chain of inverters) are essential for:
1. **Driving large capacitive loads** (clock trees, long interconnects, output pads).
2. **Signal restoration** in pipelined adiabatic pipelines.
3. **Tapering the drive strength** to minimize overall propagation delay.

However, the *last* buffer in a conventional chain that drives a huge load C_L dissipates the dominant share of dynamic power. Replacing this buffer with an **adiabatic buffer** can yield power savings of 5×–10× in the critical high-fanout stages.

> [!TIP]
> **Typical KTU Exam Hook:** Whenever a question asks "Why use adiabatic buffers instead of CMOS buffers?" the answer must emphasize *energy recovery* + *linear dependence on RC/T* (rather than the fixed ½CV² of CMOS).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Dissipation in Conventional CMOS Revisited

In a static CMOS inverter driving a load C_L from 0 → V_DD:
- A transistor channel resistance R_ch is present.
- Charging current i(t) = C_L · dV/dt is non-zero across the transistor.
- Instantaneous power p(t) = i²(t) · R_ch.
- Integrating over the charging interval gives:

$$
E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

This energy is **always lost as heat** in the PMOS channel, **independent of the rise time** of the input.

## 2.2 Quasi-Adiabatic Charging

When the supply itself is a **slow ramp** V_s(t) = (V_DD/T)·t, the current through the PMOS is held constant:

$$
i(t) = C_L \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}
$$

The voltage across the transistor V_x(t) = V_s(t) − V_C(t) = 0 (quasi-static) and the **small-signal** dissipation is:

$$
P_{diss} = i^{2}(t) \cdot R = \frac{C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T^{2}} \cdot R
$$

Energy dissipated per ramp (charging) cycle:

$$
E_{adiabatic} = P_{diss} \cdot T = \frac{R C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T} = \frac{R C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Making T very large reduces E_adiabatic, but it also reduces operating frequency f ∝ 1/T. There is an **optimal T** at which total energy-delay-product (EDP) is minimized.

## 2.3 The One-Stage Adiabatic Buffer (Adiabatic Inverter)

A one-stage adiabatic buffer is structurally **identical to a CMOS inverter**, with one modification:

| Terminal | Conventional CMOS | Adiabatic Buffer |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| V_DD rail | Constant DC V_DD | AC power-clock PC(t) |
| GND rail | Constant 0 V | Constant 0 V |
| Input (gate of M_P, M_N) | Logic input IN | Logic input IN |
| Output (drain) | OUT to load C_L | OUT to load C_L |

**Operating principle:**
- When IN = 0, PMOS turns ON, NMOS turns OFF. Current flows from PC(t) through PMOS to charge C_L up to V_DD.
- When IN = 1, PMOS turns OFF, NMOS turns ON. C_L discharges through NMOS back into PC(t), **recovering** the stored charge.

The crucial requirement is that **PC(t) must be high *before* IN goes low, and low *after* IN goes high** — the timing between input and power-clock determines correct evaluation.

### 2.4 The Two-Stage Adiabatic Buffer

A single adiabatic inverter has two shortcomings:
1. Its output swings are degraded by threshold-voltage drop (V_T) due to the slow PMOS/NMOS turn-on.
2. It cannot drive very large loads while still being energy-efficient.

A **two-stage (cascaded) adiabatic buffer** uses two adiabatic inverters in series, with the second (larger) stage sized to drive C_L. This provides:
- **Signal restoration** (full V_DD swing at the output).
- **Drive-strength tapering** — minimizes overall delay.
- **Phase shifting** — the second stage is clocked on a delayed power-clock.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the following table. It covers ~80% of numerical problems on adiabatic buffers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Conventional switching energy | $E_{conv} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$ | Joules | Fixed, independent of T |
| 2 | Adiabatic energy per transition | $E_{ad} = \dfrac{R C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$ | Joules | T = ramp period |
| 3 | Average adiabatic power | $P_{ad} = \dfrac{R C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$ | Watts | f = switching frequency |
| 4 | Power reduction factor | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{ad}}{E_{conv}} = \dfrac{2 R C_L}{T}$ | Dimensionless | T ≫ RC → η → 0 |
| 5 | Quasi-adiabatic condition | $T \gg R C_L$ | Seconds | Required for adiabatic behaviour |
| 6 | Stage-ratio (tapering) | $\beta_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$ | — | For minimum delay chain |
| 7 | Energy per unit time per stage | $P_n = \dfrac{R_n C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T}$ | Watts | n = stage index |
| 8 | Output voltage (one-stage, with threshold drop) | $V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{Tp}$ | Volts | After one stage only |
| 9 | Restored output (two-stage) | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Volts | After two stages |
| 10 | Minimum EDP frequency | $f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{T_{opt}} \propto \dfrac{1}{R C_L}$ | Hz | Where EDP is minimized |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-character pitfall:** When writing `|x|` (absolute value) in markdown tables, NEVER use the bare vertical bar. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to prevent breaking the table renderer. Example: $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ is correct; `|V_Tp|` will corrupt the table.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Adiabatic buffer chains are deployed in:
- **Ultra-low-power sensor nodes** (IoT, RFID) where battery life dominates.
- **Energy-recovery clock distribution networks** in high-performance CPUs.
- **Cryptographic hardware** where side-channel power analysis must be minimized.
- **Quasi-static pipelines** (2N2P, 2N-2N2P, ECRL, PFAL logic families) — adiabatic buffers form the **interface stage** between the adiabatic core and external CMOS logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Dissipated in a One-Stage Adiabatic Buffer

**Setup:** A PMOS transistor with channel resistance R_ch charges a load capacitance C_L from a linearly ramping power-clock:

$$
V_{PC}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{T}\, t \quad ; \quad 0 \le t \le T
$$

**Step 1 — Write the KVL equation across the PMOS channel:**

$$
V_{PC}(t) = i(t) \cdot R_{ch} + V_C(t)
$$

where V_C(t) is the voltage across C_L.

**Step 2 — Capacitor current relation:**

$$
i(t) = C_L \frac{dV_C}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (2) into (1):**

$$
V_{PC}(t) = R_{ch}\, C_L \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)
$$

**Step 4 — For quasi-adiabatic operation, assume V_C(t) tracks V_PC(t) closely**, so:

$$
V_C(t) \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{T}\, t
$$

This gives a constant current:

$$
i(t) = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{T} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage drop across the PMOS (the dissipative element):**

$$
V_{PMOS}(t) = V_{PC}(t) - V_C(t) = i(t) \cdot R_{ch} = \frac{R_{ch} C_L V_{DD}}{T}
$$

**Step 6 — Instantaneous power dissipated in PMOS:**

$$
p(t) = i(t) \cdot V_{PMOS}(t) = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T} \cdot \frac{R_{ch} C_L V_{DD}}{T} = \frac{R_{ch} C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Total energy dissipated per ramp (integrate over T):**

$$
E_{ad} = \int_{0}^{T} p(t)\, dt = \frac{R_{ch} C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T^{2}} \cdot T = \frac{R_{ch} C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{ad} = \frac{R_{ch} C_L}{T}\, C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Ratio of adiabatic to conventional energy:**

$$
\frac{E_{ad}}{E_{conv}} = \frac{(R_{ch} C_L / T)\, C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}}{\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}} = \frac{2 R_{ch} C_L}{T}
$$

This is the **adiabatic gain factor** — it falls linearly as T grows.

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Power in a Two-Stage Adiabatic Buffer

**Setup:** Two cascaded adiabatic inverters (stages 1 and 2), each of which is energized once per full power-clock cycle. Let f = 1/T_cyc be the clock frequency.

**Step 1 — Energy per transition (per stage), from §3.1:**

$$
E_{ad}^{(1)} = \frac{R_1 C_1^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T} \quad ; \quad E_{ad}^{(2)} = \frac{R_2 C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T}
$$

where C_1 is the input cap of stage 2, and C_L is the final load.

**Step 2 — Total energy per clock cycle (sum of two stages):**

$$
E_{total} = E_{ad}^{(1)} + E_{ad}^{(2)} = \frac{V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T} \left( R_1 C_1^{\,2} + R_2 C_L^{\,2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Average power:**

$$
P_{avg} = E_{total} \cdot f = \frac{V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T^2} \left( R_1 C_1^{\,2} + R_2 C_L^{\,2} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{avg} = \frac{R_1 C_1^{\,2} + R_2 C_L^{\,2}}{T^{2}} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimal stage sizing (minimum delay under iso-energy).** Using the buffer-tapering result with ratio β = C_2 / C_1:

$$
C_1 = \sqrt{\frac{C_L}{\beta}} \quad ; \quad R_1 = \beta R_2
$$

Substituting and minimizing P_avg with respect to β:

$$
\frac{d P_{avg}}{d\beta} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_{opt} = e \approx 2.718
$$

> [!TIP]
> The famous *e ≈ 2.72* tapering ratio appears in **both** conventional and adiabatic buffer design — the difference is that adiabatic buffers are evaluated under the AC power-clock constraint, not DC.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — One-Stage vs Two-Stage Comparison

| Parameter | One-Stage | Two-Stage |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Swing at output | $V_{DD} - V_{Tp}$ (degraded) | $V_{DD}$ (restored) |
| Energy per cycle | $\tfrac{R_1 C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T}$ | $\tfrac{R_1 C_1^{\,2} + R_2 C_L^{\,2}}{T} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2}$ |
| Static current path | One PMOS + one NMOS | Two PMOS + two NMOS |
| Suitable load | Small to medium C_L | Large C_L |
| Delay | Single τ = R_C_L | Sum of two stage delays |
| Restoration | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Power-clock phase | Single PC | Two PCs (often phase-shifted) |
| Energy efficiency gain | Moderate | Higher for large C_L |
| Complexity (transistors) | 2 | 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 One-Stage Adiabatic Buffer — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of a one-stage adiabatic buffer, showing the power-clock input, the CMOS pair, and the load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1["Adiabatic Buffer Stage 1"]
        PC1["Power Clock PC1 trapezoidal waveform"]:::pc
        IN1["Logic Input IN from upstream adiabatic stage"]:::in
        MP1["PMOS transistor M1 source tied to PC1"]:::pmos
        MN1["NMOS transistor M2 source tied to GND"]:::nmos
        OUT1["Output node OUT1 driving load C1"]:::out
    end

    PC1 --> MP1
    IN1 -- "gate of M1 and M2" --> MP1
    IN1 -- "gate of M1 and M2" --> MN1
    MP1 -- "drain" --> OUT1
    MN1 -- "drain" --> OUT1
    OUT1 -. "energy stored" .-> C1[("Load C1")]
    C1 -. "recovered when MN1 ON" .-> PC1

    classDef pc fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pmos fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef nmos fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Two-Stage Adiabatic Buffer — Cascaded Architecture

The two-stage version uses two adiabatic inverters in series. The second stage is **sized larger** (W/L increased) to drive C_L, and may use a **phase-shifted power-clock** for sequential evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Stage 1 small driver"]
        PC1["PC1 ramp from 0 to VDD"]:::pc
        IN1["IN input from adiabatic core"]:::in
        G1["CMOS pair M1 M2 sized small"]:::pmos
        OUT1["Intermediate node N1"]:::out
    end

    subgraph S2["Stage 2 large driver"]
        PC2["PC2 delayed by quarter cycle"]:::pc
        IN2["N1 fed as input to stage 2"]:::in
        G2["CMOS pair M3 M4 sized large to drive CL"]:::pmos
        OUT2["Final output OUT to load CL"]:::out
    end

    PC1 --> G1
    IN1 --> G1
    G1 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> IN2
    PC2 --> G2
    G2 --> OUT2

    CL[("Large Load CL")] --> OUT2
    CL -. "recovered charge" .-> PC2

    classDef pc fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pmos fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Power-Clock and Input/Output Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Power Clock
    participant IN as Input IN
    participant N1 as Node N1
    participant OUT as Output OUT

    Note over PC,IN: Phase 1 Evaluate and Hold
    PC->>PC: ramps from 0 to VDD slowly
    IN->>IN: pulled LOW turns PMOS ON
    PC-->>N1: charges N1 to VDD minus VTP
    N1->>OUT: drives stage 2 input HIGH
    Note over N1,OUT: N1 reaches VDD minus VTP

    Note over PC,IN: Phase 2 Hold and Recover
    PC->>PC: ramps from VDD back to 0
    IN->>IN: pulled HIGH turns NMOS ON
    N1-->>PC: charge flows back to PC
    OUT->>OUT: discharged to 0 V
    Note over OUT: Energy returned to supply
```

## 4.4 Energy-Flow Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1["Energy Source Power Clock PC"]:::pc
    V2["Transistor Channel Resistance Rch"]:::r
    V3["Load Capacitance CL stores energy 0.5 CL VDD squared"]:::c
    V4["Heat dissipation in channel"]:::heat
    V5["Recovered energy back to PC"]:::rec

    V1 -- "current through Rch" --> V2
    V2 -- "small drop Vx" --> V4
    V2 -- "majority flows" --> V3
    V3 -- "during discharge" --> V5
    V5 -- "returns to" --> V1

    classDef pc fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef c fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef heat fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef rec fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "adiabatic switching" in the context of low-power VLSI design. Why is it termed "adiabatic"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Adiabatic switching is a low-power circuit technique in which the energy transfer between the power supply and the load capacitance is performed **gradually** using a time-varying (trapezoidal or sinusoidal) power-clock, instead of an abrupt DC V_DD step.

It is termed "adiabatic" because, in the ideal limit where the charging time T → ∞, the current through the resistive channel approaches zero and the dissipation in the channel becomes negligible — analogous to a **thermally adiabatic** process in which no heat is exchanged with the surroundings. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for gradual transfer, 1 mark for the thermodynamic analogy.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Write the expression for the energy dissipated per switching event in (i) a conventional CMOS inverter, and (ii) a one-stage adiabatic buffer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(i) Conventional CMOS: $E_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$  *(1½ marks)*

(ii) One-stage adiabatic buffer: $E_{ad} = \dfrac{R_{ch} C_L}{T} \, C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$  *(1½ marks)*

where R_ch is the on-resistance of the PMOS, C_L is the load capacitance, and T is the rise time of the power-clock. The key difference is that E_conv is **independent of T**, while E_ad scales as 1/T.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **one-stage adiabatic buffer**. Derive the expression for the energy dissipated per charging cycle. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A one-stage adiabatic buffer drives a load C_L = 50 fF from a 1.8 V power-clock that ramps linearly over T = 10 ns. The PMOS on-resistance is R_ch = 200 Ω. Calculate (i) the energy dissipated per ramp, and (ii) compare it with the energy that a conventional CMOS buffer would dissipate. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Circuit (1 mark):** Draw a CMOS inverter — PMOS source tied to PC(t), PMOS drain = output node, NMOS drain = output node, NMOS source = GND, both gates tied to input IN, output node connected to load C_L.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- When IN = 0, PMOS is ON and NMOS is OFF. PC(t) is in its ramp-up phase, so current flows from PC through the PMOS into C_L, gradually raising V_OUT.
- When IN = 1, PMOS is OFF and NMOS is ON. PC(t) is in its ramp-down phase, so C_L discharges through the NMOS back into the PC line — the stored charge is **recovered**.
- Timing: IN must transition to 0 *before* PC begins its ramp up, and transition to 1 *before* PC begins its ramp down.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Assume V_PC(t) = (V_DD/T)·t and C_L charges quasi-statically, so V_C(t) ≈ V_PC(t).

Current: $i(t) = C_L \dfrac{V_{DD}}{T}$ (constant).

Voltage across PMOS: $V_{PMOS} = i \cdot R_{ch} = \dfrac{R_{ch} C_L V_{DD}}{T}$.

Instantaneous dissipation: $p(t) = i^2 R_{ch} = \dfrac{R_{ch} C_L^{\,2} V_{DD}^{\,2}}{T^{2}}$.

Energy per ramp:

$$
E_{ad} = \int_{0}^{T} p(t)\, dt = \frac{R_{ch} C_L}{T} \, C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** C_L = 50 fF = 50 × 10⁻¹⁵ F, V_DD = 1.8 V, T = 10 ns = 10 × 10⁻⁹ s, R_ch = 200 Ω.

**(i) Adiabatic energy (3.5 marks):**

$$
E_{ad} = \frac{R_{ch} C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

$$
E_{ad} = \frac{200 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^{2}
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$
\frac{R_{ch} C_L}{T} = \frac{200 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-8}} = \frac{10^{-11}}{10^{-8}} = 10^{-3} \; (\text{dimensionless factor})
$$

$$
C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 = 162 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 162 \; \text{fJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{ad} = 10^{-3} \times 162 \; \text{fJ} = 0.162 \; \text{fJ}}
$$

**(ii) Conventional CMOS energy (3.5 marks):**

$$
E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{conv} = 81 \; \text{fJ}}
$$

**Comparison:** E_ad / E_conv = 0.162 / 81 = 0.002 = **0.2 %**. The adiabatic buffer dissipates **~500× less** energy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Draw the block diagram of a **two-stage adiabatic buffer** and explain how it achieves signal restoration. Why is the second stage normally larger than the first? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A two-stage adiabatic buffer chain has the following parameters: stage-1 on-resistance R_1 = 1 kΩ, stage-2 on-resistance R_2 = 200 Ω, inter-stage capacitance C_1 = 20 fF, load C_L = 200 fF, V_DD = 1.5 V, ramp time T = 5 ns. Compute the **total energy dissipated per clock cycle** and the **average power** at f = 100 MHz. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (2 marks):**
PC1 → [Stage 1: small CMOS pair] → Node N1 → [Stage 2: large CMOS pair] → OUT → C_L; PC2 (delayed) feeds Stage 2.

**Signal restoration (3 marks):**
- A one-stage adiabatic buffer loses one V_Tp in its high level because the PMOS turns off when V_GS falls below V_Tp. Hence V_OH = V_DD − V_Tp.
- The second stage is identical in structure but is **driven from a low-impedance previous node N1**. When N1 goes HIGH, it overdrives the PMOS gate strongly (V_GS = 0, full V_DD across GS), so the PMOS conducts fully and pulls the output up to **V_DD** (or V_DD − V_Tp at worst, but the next stage of a pipeline restores it further).
- More importantly, two cascaded gain stages overcome the V_T drop progressively: each stage's high output is V_DD − V_T, fed into the next stage's input which only needs to be > V_T to switch — hence a full rail-to-rail swing is obtained at the final output.

**Why stage 2 is larger (2 marks):**
- The load C_L is typically very large. To drive it within a time T without violating the quasi-adiabatic condition, the on-resistance R_2 of the output transistors must be small (since T ≫ R_2 · C_L is required).
- A larger W/L reduces R_2 at the cost of larger C_1 (input cap of stage 2), which the smaller stage 1 must drive.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** R_1 = 1 kΩ, R_2 = 200 Ω, C_1 = 20 fF, C_L = 200 fF, V_DD = 1.5 V, T = 5 ns, f = 100 MHz.

**Step 1 — Energy in stage 1 (2 marks):**

$$
E_1 = \frac{R_1 C_1}{T} \cdot C_1 V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

$$
\frac{R_1 C_1}{T} = \frac{1000 \times 20 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-11}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_1 V_{DD}^{\,2} = 20 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.25 = 45 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 45 \; \text{fJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_1 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 45 \; \text{fJ} = 0.18 \; \text{fJ}}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy in stage 2 (2 marks):**

$$
E_2 = \frac{R_2 C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}
$$

$$
\frac{R_2 C_L}{T} = \frac{200 \times 200 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-11}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 8 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} = 200 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.25 = 450 \; \text{fJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_2 = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 450 \; \text{fJ} = 3.6 \; \text{fJ}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy per cycle (1.5 marks):**

$$
\boxed{E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 = 0.18 + 3.6 = 3.78 \; \text{fJ}}
$$

**Step 4 — Average power at 100 MHz (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{avg} = E_{total} \cdot f = 3.78 \times 10^{-15} \times 100 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{avg} = 3.78 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W} = 0.378 \; \mu\text{W}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor R·C_L/T.** Many students write E_ad = C_L·V²_DD (the conventional expression), which is worth **zero marks**. Always include the (R·C_L/T) factor.
> 2. **Unit mismatches.** Always convert C_L to farads (fF × 10⁻¹⁵) and T to seconds (ns × 10⁻⁹) before substituting.
> 3. **Confusing ramp time T with clock period T_cyc.** If the power-clock is trapezoidal, the ramp occupies only a fraction of T_cyc — be careful in energy-delay problems.
> 4. **Ignoring the V_T drop.** A one-stage buffer output is V_DD − V_Tp, not V_DD. State this explicitly whenever a question asks about output swing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — One-Stage and Two-Stage Adiabatic Buffer**

- ☐ **Adiabatic** = "no heat loss" — gradual charging allows energy recovery.
- ☐ One-stage buffer = CMOS inverter whose V_DD terminal is replaced by a **power-clock PC(t)**.
- ☐ Charging path: PC → PMOS → C_L; discharging path: C_L → NMOS → PC (charge recovered).
- ☐ Energy per transition: $E_{ad} = (R C_L / T) \cdot C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$  (vs ½C_L V_DD² for CMOS).
- ☐ Power-reduction factor: $2 R C_L / T$ — the larger the T, the smaller the dissipation.
- ☐ Quasi-adiabatic condition: $T \gg R C_L$ (typically T ≥ 10 R C_L).
- ☐ One-stage suffers from V_T drop; **two-stage is needed for full output swing restoration**.
- ☐ Two-stage sizing rule: $\beta_{opt} = C_{in,\,n+1} / C_{out,\,n} \approx e \approx 2.72$.
- ☐ Adiabatic buffers sit at the **output** of adiabatic pipelines to drive large C_L energy-efficiently.
- ☐ Trade-off: Power savings ↓ as frequency ↑; adiabatic is best for low-to-medium frequency (< 200 MHz) designs.
- ☐ Real applications: RFID tags, IoT sensor nodes, energy-recovery clock trees, side-channel-attack-resistant crypto blocks.
- ☐ Common KTU pitfall: writing ½C_L V²_DD in adiabatic derivations — always carry the (RC/T) factor.
- ☐ Always state the quasi-static assumption explicitly when deriving E_ad.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Adiabatic Logic Gates — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adiabatic Logic Gates** are a class of low-power CMOS circuits in which the energy stored in the load capacitance is **recycled back to the power supply** (instead of being dissipated as heat) by using a **time-varying ramp/clocked power supply** and switching the circuit nodes **sufficiently slowly**.

The term *adiabatic* is borrowed from thermodynamics, where an adiabatic process is one in which **no heat is exchanged with the surroundings**. In the VLSI context, it does not mean truly zero dissipation — it means that the energy that would normally be lost as heat in a conventional CMOS gate is instead **guided back** to a reservoir (the power-clock generator) for reuse.

In a conventional CMOS inverter, the energy lost per switching event is:

$$E_{conv} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_L \, V_{dd}^{2}$$

In an adiabatic gate, this is replaced by an energy dissipation that scales with the **ratio of the time-constant** $\tau = R \, C_L$ to the **transition time $T$** of the power-clock:

$$E_{adiabatic} \;=\; \frac{R \, C_L}{T} \, C_L \, V_{dd}^{2}$$

Hence, by making $T \gg R \, C_L$, the dissipation can in principle be made **arbitrarily small**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Wheel vs Waterfall" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine filling a bucket of water:**
> - **Conventional CMOS** is like dumping the entire bucket from a height in one instant → the water hits the ground and all its potential energy is converted to **heat (irrecoverable loss)**. This corresponds to the abrupt $\tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ dissipation in the pull-up PMOS during a hard switch.
> - **Adiabatic Logic** is like pouring the water *slowly* down a water-wheel, where the wheel turns a generator that pumps most of the water *back up* to the original tank. Only a small fraction of the energy is lost to friction (resistance). The slower you pour (larger $T$), the closer the loss approaches zero.

This analogy captures the three pillars of adiabatic design:
1. A **slow, controlled transition** (the ramp).
2. A **resistive charging path** through which the current is bounded.
3. A **return path** for the charge to flow back into the supply.

## 1.3 Salient Features of Adiabatic Gates

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Power-clock** | A trapezoidal / sinusoidal supply that ramps up and down |
| **Energy recovery** | Charge flows back to the supply during the falling ramp |
| **Loss scaling** | $E_{loss} \propto (R C_L \, / \, T)$ — slower ⇒ less loss |
| **No static current** | Theoretically zero DC path from $V_{dd}$ to ground |
| **Speed–power trade-off** | Aggressive pipelining gives high throughput *and* low energy per operation |
| **Activity-independent** | Power ≈ constant per clock cycle (capacitance-driven) |

## 1.4 Why Conventional CMOS is Fundamentally Energy-Wasteful

In a static CMOS gate, when the output node capacitance $C_L$ is pulled from $0$ to $V_{dd}$ through the PMOS transistor (modelled as a switch of resistance $R_{on}$), the supply delivers an energy of $C_L V_{dd}^{2}$. Half of this ($\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}$) is **dissipated in the PMOS channel**; the other half is stored on the capacitor. On the next transition, this stored energy is dumped to ground through the NMOS — again losing $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}$. Hence the **irrecoverable loss per switching cycle is $C_L V_{dd}^{2}$**, independent of the transistor resistance.

Adiabatic logic breaks this paradigm by **never abruptly shorting a charged capacitor to either rail**; the current is always bounded by a finite resistance, and the energy is shuttled back and forth between the supply and the load capacitance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Adiabatic Logic Gates — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy-Recovery Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Replace the constant DC supply $V_{dd}$** with a **time-varying power-clock** $V_{pc}(t)$ that ramps linearly from $0$ to $V_{dd}$ and back, on a period $T$.
2. **Charge the load capacitor $C_L$ slowly** through the on-resistance $R$ of the pull-up network. The charging current is

   $$i(t) \;=\; C_L \, \frac{dV_{pc}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{T} \quad \text{(constant for a linear ramp)}.$$

3. **The instantaneous power dissipated in $R$** is $P(t) = i^{2}(t) R = (C_L V_{dd}/T)^{2} R$.
4. **The total energy dissipated in one ramp-up phase** is

   $$E_{diss} \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt \;=\; \frac{R C_{L}^{2} V_{dd}^{2}}{T}.$$

5. **Recognition step:** Writing $C_L^{2} V_{dd}^{2}/T = (C_L V_{dd}^{2}) \cdot (C_L/T)$, the dissipation can also be expressed as

   $$E_{diss} \;=\; \frac{R C_L}{T} \, C_L V_{dd}^{2}.$$

6. **Asymptotic behaviour:** if $T \gg R C_L$, the factor $(R C_L / T) \to 0$, so the dissipation per transition can be made arbitrarily small. This is the **fundamental adiabatic gain** over conventional CMOS.
7. **Discharge phase:** during the falling ramp, the same charge flows *back* through the same resistance toward the supply, recovering the stored energy. Thus, only the small term $E_{diss}$ is actually lost.

## 2.2 Power-Clock Phasing (4-Phase Operation)

Adiabatic pipelines use a **4-phase trapezoidal clock** $V_{pc}(t)$ — $\Phi_{1}, \Phi_{2}, \Phi_{3}, \Phi_{4}$ — each shifted by $T/4$:

| Phase | Action on the logic stage |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\Phi_{1}$ — **Evaluate** | Power-clock ramps $0 \to V_{dd}$; output node charges through the ON-path; logic decision is made. |
| $\Phi_{2}$ — **Hold** | Power-clock flat at $V_{dd}$; previous stage is held while next stage evaluates. |
| $\Phi_{3}$ — **Recover** | Power-clock ramps $V_{dd} \to 0$; charge returns to the supply (energy recovery). |
| $\Phi_{4}$ — **Wait** | Power-clock flat at $0$; stage idle until the next $\Phi_{1}$. |

This four-phase clocking makes the pipeline **naturally adiabatic and pipelined** — every stage is always doing something useful, and at any instant some stages are evaluating while others are recovering.

## 2.3 Classification of Adiabatic Logic Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests the distinction between *Quasi-Adiabatic* and *Fully-Adiabatic* logic. Memorize the two bullets below.**

- **Quasi-Adiabatic Logic:** uses diodes or diode-connected transistors to provide the return path for charge. *Partial* energy recovery. Simpler, but a small residual loss of $\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{tp}^{2}$ (threshold drop) occurs per cycle. Examples: **2N-2N2P, 2N-2N2N, ECRL, PFAL, CAL, NERL, TSEL**.
- **Fully-Adiabatic Logic:** uses cross-coupled latch structures (e.g., split-level, reversible) so that *all* the energy is theoretically recoverable. More complex, but the asymptotic dissipation scales as $(RC/T) C V^{2}$ for *all* nodes, including the internal ones. Examples: **Split-level Charge Recovery Logic (SCRL), Reversible Adiabatic Logic**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the canonical formulas you must know for any adiabatic-logic problem. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute value to keep the markdown table safe.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Energy dissipated in **conventional** charging | $E_{conv} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ | J, per charging transition |
| 2 | Total energy lost per **full switching cycle** (conv.) | $E_{cycle,conv} \;=\; C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ | J, charge + discharge |
| 3 | Charging **current** in adiabatic ramp | $i(t) \;=\; C_L \, dV/dt \;=\; C_L V_{dd}/T$ | A, constant for linear ramp |
| 4 | Instantaneous **power** in charging resistor | $P(t) \;=\; i^{2}(t) R \;=\; R \, C_L^{2} V_{dd}^{2}/T^{2}$ | W |
| 5 | **Energy dissipated** in one adiabatic ramp | $E_{adiabatic} \;=\; (R C_L / T) \, C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ | J, valid for linear ramp |
| 6 | **Adiabatic gain** factor | $G \;=\; E_{adiabatic}/E_{conv} \;=\; 2 R C_L / T$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Asymptotic limit | $E_{adiabatic} \to 0$ as $T \gg R C_L$ | ideal condition |
| 8 | **Average power** at clock frequency $f$ | $P_{avg} \;=\; C_L^{2} V_{dd}^{2} \, R f / T$ | W |
| 9 | Quasi-adiabatic **residual loss per cycle** | $E_{quasi} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{tp}^{2}$ | J, where $V_{tp}$ is the threshold |
| 10 | Voltage across a charging cap at time $t$ | $V_C(t) \;=\; V_{dd} \, t / T$ | V, linear ramp assumption |
| 11 | Charge delivered per transition | $Q \;=\; C_L V_{dd}$ | C |
| 12 | Activity factor of a CMOS gate | $\alpha$ | dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 13 | Conventional **dynamic power** | $P_{dyn} \;=\; \alpha C_L V_{dd}^{2} f$ | W |
| 14 | Adiabatic **dynamic power** | $P_{ad} \;=\; (R C_L / T) \, \alpha C_L V_{dd}^{2} f$ | W |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Adiabatic Logic

- **Ultra-low-power sensor nodes** and **energy-harvesting IoT** systems where the available energy is on the order of micro-watts.
- **RFIDs and biomedical implants** — the energy recovered per cycle directly extends battery life or removes the need for a battery.
- **Secure / side-channel-resistant hardware** — quasi-adiabatic switching has nearly constant current draw, making power-analysis attacks (e.g., DPA) significantly harder.
- **Cryogenic computing & reversible computing research** — adiabatic CMOS is one of the few physical implementations of Landauer's principle-friendly logic.
- **Asynchronous adiabatic pipelines** — used in research prototypes of MIPS-like processors demonstrating sub-1 fJ/operation energy.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Adiabatic Logic Gates — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Lost in Conventional CMOS Charging

**Goal:** Show that abruptly charging $C_L$ from $0$ to $V_{dd}$ through a non-zero resistance $R$ dissipates exactly $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ in that resistance.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL across the loop.** The supply $V_{dd}$, the resistor $R$ and the capacitor $C_L$ form a series loop, so at every instant

$$V_{dd} \;=\; V_R(t) \;+\; V_C(t) \;=\; i(t) R \;+\; \frac{1}{C_L}\int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau.$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the KVL equation with respect to time** to eliminate the integral:

$$0 \;=\; R \, \frac{di}{dt} \;+\; \frac{i(t)}{C_L} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{di}{dt} \;+\; \frac{i}{R C_L} \;=\; 0.$$

**Step 3 — Solve this first-order ODE** with the initial condition $i(0) = V_{dd}/R$ (capacitor initially uncharged, full supply voltage across $R$):

$$i(t) \;=\; \frac{V_{dd}}{R} \, e^{-t/(R C_L)}.$$

**Step 4 — Compute the energy dissipated in the resistor** by integrating $i^{2}(t) R$ over the entire discharge of the supply:

$$E_{R} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} i^{2}(t) R \, dt \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{V_{dd}^{2}}{R} e^{-2t/(R C_L)} \, dt.$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.** Using $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-a t}\,dt = 1/a$ with $a = 2/(R C_L)$:

$$E_{R} \;=\; \frac{V_{dd}^{2}}{R} \cdot \frac{R C_L}{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}.$$

**Step 6 — Energy balance check.** The total energy delivered by the supply is

$$E_{supplied} \;=\; V_{dd} \int_{0}^{\infty} i(t)\,dt \;=\; V_{dd} \cdot C_L V_{dd} \;=\; C_L V_{dd}^{2}.$$

The difference $E_{supplied} - E_{R} = C_L V_{dd}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ is the energy **stored on the capacitor**, which is **dumped to ground** through the NMOS in the next phase. Therefore the **total energy lost per full cycle is $C_L V_{dd}^{2}$**, independent of $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** the dissipation in conventional CMOS is *independent* of the on-resistance. That is why simply reducing $R$ (faster transistors) does **not** lower the dynamic power — the dissipation is set by $C_L$ and $V_{dd}$ alone.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Lost in Adiabatic (Linear-Ramp) Charging

**Goal:** Show that, when the supply is a *slow* ramp $V_{pc}(t) = (V_{dd}/T) \, t$ for $0 \le t \le T$, the dissipation in $R$ scales as $1/T$.

**Step 1 — Write the power-clock voltage.**

$$V_{pc}(t) \;=\; \frac{V_{dd}}{T} \, t, \quad 0 \le t \le T.$$

**Step 2 — Compute the current.** The capacitor voltage tracks the supply (otherwise a non-zero voltage would develop across $R$, producing a current). With the capacitor following the ramp:

$$V_C(t) \;\approx\; V_{pc}(t) \quad\Rightarrow\quad i(t) \;=\; C_L \, \frac{dV_C}{dt} \;=\; C_L \, \frac{V_{dd}}{T}.$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that the current is *constant*** throughout the ramp — this is the central feature of adiabatic charging. By contrast, the conventional case had $i(t)$ decaying exponentially.

**Step 4 — Compute the energy delivered by the ramp** over the full transition:

$$E_{supplied} \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} V_{pc}(t)\,i(t)\,dt \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} \frac{V_{dd}}{T} t \cdot \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{T}\, dt \;=\; \frac{C_L V_{dd}^{2}}{T^{2}} \int_{0}^{T} t\,dt.$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$E_{supplied} \;=\; \frac{C_L V_{dd}^{2}}{T^{2}} \cdot \frac{T^{2}}{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}.$$

**Step 6 — Compute the energy stored on the capacitor at $t = T$.**

$$E_{C} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}.$$

**Step 7 — Compute the energy dissipated in $R$ by energy balance:**

$$E_{R} \;=\; E_{supplied} \;-\; E_{C} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2} \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2} \;=\; 0 \;\; \text{(idealised)}.$$

This says that with a *perfectly tracking* ramp, the dissipation is **zero**. In practice, the finite on-resistance $R$ produces a *small* voltage drop $V_R = i R$, so the capacitor voltage lags the supply by $V_R$. Repeating the calculation with this lag:

**Step 8 — Realistic case.** Let $V_C(t) = V_{pc}(t) - i R$ and $i = C_L (V_{dd}/T)$:

$$V_R \;=\; i R \;=\; \frac{R C_L V_{dd}}{T}, \quad P_{R} \;=\; i^{2} R \;=\; \frac{R C_{L}^{2} V_{dd}^{2}}{T^{2}}.$$

**Step 9 — Integrate to get the energy dissipated in $R$ over the ramp:**

$$E_{R} \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} P_{R}\, dt \;=\; \frac{R C_{L}^{2} V_{dd}^{2}}{T^{2}} \cdot T \;=\; \frac{R C_{L}^{2} V_{dd}^{2}}{T}.$$

**Step 10 — Final compact form.** Re-arranging:

$$\boxed{\,E_{adiabatic} \;=\; \frac{R C_L}{T} \, C_L V_{dd}^{2}\,}$$

**Step 11 — Adiabatic gain compared to conventional charging:**

$$G \;=\; \frac{E_{adiabatic}}{E_{conv}} \;=\; \frac{(R C_L / T) C_L V_{dd}^{2}}{\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2 R C_L}{T}.$$

When $T = 2 R C_L$, the adiabatic gate dissipates the *same* as a conventional one. When $T = 20 R C_L$, the dissipation is reduced by a factor of **10** — the classic energy-delay trade-off.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quasi-Adiabatic Residual Loss

> [!NOTE]
> Quasi-adiabatic families (ECRL, PFAL, 2N-2N2P, etc.) return charge to the supply through **diode-connected transistors**, which always drop a threshold voltage $V_{tp}$. This drop is the source of the residual loss.

**Step 1 — During the recovery phase, the output node discharges from $V_{dd}$ to roughly $V_{dd} - V_{tp}$ before the diode can conduct** the return current.

**Step 2 — The energy recovered** is therefore $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L (V_{dd} - V_{tp})^{2}$ instead of the ideal $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2}$.

**Step 3 — The unrecovered residual loss** is the difference:

$$E_{quasi,residual} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^{2} \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L (V_{dd} - V_{tp})^{2}.$$

**Step 4 — Expand using the identity** $(a-b)^{2} = a^{2} - 2ab + b^{2}$:

$$E_{quasi,residual} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L \bigl[V_{dd}^{2} - (V_{dd}^{2} - 2 V_{dd} V_{tp} + V_{tp}^{2})\bigr] \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L (2 V_{dd} V_{tp} - V_{tp}^{2}).$$

**Step 5 — Since $V_{tp} \ll V_{dd}$ in typical CMOS (e.g., 0.4 V vs. 1.2 V)**, the $V_{tp}^{2}$ term is negligible, and we obtain the **standard textbook result**:

$$\boxed{\,E_{quasi,residual} \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd} V_{tp}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental lower bound** on the energy dissipation of a quasi-adiabatic gate per cycle — it cannot be reduced by slowing the ramp, because it is set by the diode drop, not by the ramp slope.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conventional vs Adiabatic (Quantified)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is the **single most important comparison** for KTU 14-mark questions. Commit it to memory.

| Parameter | Conventional CMOS | Quasi-Adiabatic | Fully Adiabatic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy per cycle | $C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd} V_{tp} + (R C_L/T) C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ | $(R C_L/T) C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ |
| Power-clock required | No (DC supply) | Yes (trapezoidal) | Yes (trapezoidal) |
| Energy recovered | 0 % | ~70–85 % | ~95–99 % |
| Static current path | None (static CMOS) | None | None |
| Delay penalty | 1× | 3–10× | 5–20× |
| Threshold sensitivity | Low | High (residual loss) | Low |
| Area overhead | 1× | 1.5–2× | 2–3× |
| Logic style | Complementary | Cross-coupled / diode-recovery | Reversible split-level |
| Example families | Static CMOS, DCVSL | ECRL, PFAL, 2N-2N2P, CAL | SCRL, Reversible |

# Adiabatic Logic Gates — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Flow Block Diagram — Conventional vs Adiabatic

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates how charge and energy move through the two logic styles.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph convBlock[Conventional CMOS Charging]
        nodeV1[DC Supply Vdd] -- hard switch --> nodeR1[PMOS R_on]
        nodeR1 -- step charging current --> nodeC1[Load Capacitor CL]
        nodeC1 -- half CV squared stored, half lost as heat --> nodeGND1[Dissipation Node]
    end
    subgraph adBlock[Adiabatic Charging with Power Clock]
        nodeV2[Power Clock Vpc t] -- slow ramp --> nodeR2[Logic Network R_on]
        nodeR2 -- bounded constant current --> nodeC2[Load Capacitor CL]
        nodeC2 -- reverse path during ramp down --> nodeV2
    end
    nodeV1 --- nodeV2
```

> **Reading the diagram.** In the conventional branch (left), charge leaves the supply, half of $C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ is irrecoverably dumped to ground. In the adiabatic branch (right), the same charge loops back to the supply; only the term $(R C_L / T) C_L V_{dd}^{2}$ is lost.

## 4.2 4-Phase Power-Clock Timing Diagram

The trapezoidal power-clock and the four pipeline phases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Phi 1 Evaluate] -- ramp 0 to Vdd --> nodeP2[Phi 2 Hold flat Vdd]
    nodeP2 -- ramp Vdd to 0 --> nodeP3[Phi 3 Recover]
    nodeP3 -- flat 0 --> nodeP4[Phi 4 Wait]
    nodeP4 -- next cycle --> nodeP1
```

| Phase | Voltage shape | Energy flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\Phi_1$ Evaluate | Ramp $0 \to V_{dd}$ | Supply $\to$ Load (charging) |
| $\Phi_2$ Hold | Flat $V_{dd}$ | None (steady) |
| $\Phi_3$ Recover | Ramp $V_{dd} \to 0$ | Load $\to$ Supply (recovery) |
| $\Phi_4$ Wait | Flat $0$ | None (idle) |

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Generic Quasi-Adiabatic Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInA[Input A] --> nodeLogic
    nodeInB[Input B] --> nodeLogic
    nodeInC[Input C] --> nodeLogic
    nodeLogic[NMOS Logic Tree F] -- drives --> nodeOut1[Output Out]
    nodeOut1 -- diode coupled to --> nodeOut2[Output Out-bar]
    nodeOut2 -- diode coupled to --> nodeOut1
    nodeVdd[Power Clock Vpc] -- drives --> nodeOut1
    nodeVdd -- drives --> nodeOut2
    nodeLogic --> nodeGND2[Ground]
```

**Block description (read top-to-bottom):**

1. **Inputs A, B, C** feed the **NMOS logic tree**, which evaluates a function $F(A,B,C)$ in the nMOS pull-down network.
2. The two **complementary outputs** `Out` and `Out-bar` are formed by a pair of diode-connected transistors (or cross-coupled PMOS in PFAL), giving the energy-recovery path.
3. The shared **power-clock $V_{pc}$** drives both outputs so that charging and recovery are symmetric.
4. The nMOS logic tree sinks charge to ground only when the output should be pulled low, *slowly*, during the evaluate phase.

## 4.4 Generic 2N-2N2P Gate — Connection-Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input A] --> nodeN1A
    nodeB[Input B] --> nodeN1B
    nodeN1A[N1A NMOS series] --> nodeJunction
    nodeN1B[N1B NMOS series] --> nodeJunction
    nodeJunction[Output Node Out] --> nodeP1[P1 PMOS latch]
    nodeJunction --> nodeP2[P2 PMOS latch]
    nodeP1 --> nodeOut2[Out-bar]
    nodeP2 --> nodeJunction
    nodeOut2 --> nodeGND3[Logic Tree NMOS]
    nodeGND3 --> nodeGND4[Ground]
    nodeVdd2[Power Clock Vpc] --> nodeP1
    nodeVdd2 --> nodeP2
```

> **Naming convention decoded.**
> - The first **2N** = the two NMOS transistors (`N1A`, `N1B`) that form the differential input pair.
> - The second **2N** = the two NMOS transistors that form the pull-down logic tree.
> - The **2P** = the two cross-coupled PMOS transistors (`P1`, `P2`) that form the energy-recovery latch, allowing charge to flow back into the power-clock during the falling ramp.

## 4.5 Architecture of ECRL (Efficient Charge Recovery Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA2[In] --> nodeN3A[N3A in pull-down tree]
    nodeB2[In-bar] --> nodeN3B[N3B in pull-down tree]
    nodeVdd3[Power Clock Vpc] --> nodeP3A[Diode PMOS P3A]
    nodeVdd3 --> nodeP3B[Diode PMOS P3B]
    nodeP3A --> nodeOutA[Out]
    nodeP3B --> nodeOutB[Out-bar]
    nodeN3A --> nodeOutA
    nodeN3B --> nodeOutB
    nodeOutA --> nodeCross[Cross-coupled latch]
    nodeOutB --> nodeCross
    nodeOutA --> nodeLogicOut[To next stage]
    nodeOutB --> nodeLogicOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **ECRL distinguishing feature:** it uses **two nMOS pull-down logic trees** (one for each complementary output) and a pair of **diode-connected PMOS** that perform the energy-recovery charging. Output is always taken from the nMOS tree, ensuring level-restored, ratioed logic.
